**Step 3:** Normalised single-overhead form (amortised per process):

$$
U_{eff} = \frac{T_b}{T_b + T_{cs}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration:**
If $T_b = 100\,\mu s$ and $T_{cs} = 10\,\mu s$:

$$
U_{eff} = \frac{100}{100 + 10} = \frac{100}{110} \approx 0.909 = 90.9\%
$$

Overhead percentage $= 9.1\%$.

**Step 5 — System-wide view over N switches in a workload period $T_w$:**

$$
T_{overhead} = N \cdot T_{cs}
$$

$$
U_{sys} = 1 - \frac{N \cdot T_{cs}}{T_w}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget the factor of 2 in the basic derivation. State whether the question's $T_{cs}$ is per-switch (both save + load) or per-direction (just save or just load). Always read the question's notation carefully.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Process Operations:**

**1. Process Creation:**
Events that trigger process creation: user login, OS boot, batch job submission, a running process explicitly spawning a child (e.g., `fork()` in Unix). The OS performs:

- Assigns a unique PID.
- Allocates and initialises the PCB.
- Allocates memory for the process image (text, data, stack, heap).
- Loads the program code into the text segment.
- Sets up default open file descriptors (stdin, stdout, stderr).
- Places the new PCB in the Ready queue.
- Returns control to the parent (which may wait or continue).

**2. Process Execution:**
The OS multiplexes the process on the CPU via the short-term scheduler. The process may:

- Issue system calls (e.g., `read`, `write`, `open`).
- Cause interrupts (e.g., divide by zero, page fault).
- Cooperate with other processes via IPC (pipes, sockets, shared memory, signals).
- Fork child processes, creating a tree.

**3. Process Termination:**
Triggers: normal exit (last statement or `exit()`), fatal error, killed by another process (e.g., `kill` signal). The OS performs:

- Closes all open file descriptors and releases file locks.
- Releases allocated memory, devices, and I/O buffers.
- Sends termination status to the parent (via `wait()`).
- Deallocates the PCB.
- Removes the process from all queues.
- If the parent is gone, the process is re-parented to `init` (PID 1) to avoid zombies.

**Process Hierarchy:**

- Each process has a **parent** (the creator) and may have **children**.
- The structure forms a **process tree** rooted at `init` (PID 1 in Unix).
- A process's `ppid` (parent PID) is stored in its PCB.
- Terminating a parent may orphan or orphan-and-reparent its children.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse *process termination* (the OS reclaims resources) with *process exit* (the process invokes `exit()`). The former is a kernel action, the latter a process syscall. Examiners want both explained.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Processes and Process States**

- **Process = program in execution**; the same program can create multiple independent processes.
- A process is identified and managed through its **PCB**, which holds *all* its context.
- A process has **three core regions** in memory: **text (code), data, stack**; the **heap** is optional/auxiliary.
- The **seven states** are: New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated, Suspended Ready, Suspended Blocked.
- **Ready** processes are in **main memory** and runnable; **Suspended** processes are on **disk**.
- A process can only move to **Running** from **Ready** — never directly from New or Blocked.
- A process can only move to **Terminated** from **Running** — there is no other entry point.
- **Running → Blocked** is initiated by the *process itself* (it issues I/O); **Running → Ready** is initiated by the *scheduler* (preemption).
- The **three schedulers** — long-term (admission), short-term (CPU), medium-term (swapper) — operate at different timescales.
- **Context switch** costs $T_{cs}$ per switch; effective CPU utilisation $U_{eff} = T_b \big/ (T_b + T_{cs})$.
- **Little's Law** $L = \lambda \cdot W$ applies to any queueing subsystem, including the ready and blocked queues.
- **PCB fields** to remember: PID, State, PC, Registers, Memory info, Scheduling info, Open files, Accounting info.
- A **process tree** is rooted at `init` (PID 1) in Unix/Linux; zombies are terminated children awaiting `wait()`.
- The **suspended states** exist purely for **memory management** in systems that use swapping.
- **In a single-processor system, only ONE process can be in the Running state at a time** — this is a classic short-answer question.

---

**End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: Processes — Process States**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Control Block (PCB)** — A **kernel-level data structure** maintained by the Operating System for **every active process**, that encapsulates all the information required to manage, schedule, identify, and resume the process during its lifetime in the system.

The PCB is also referred to in literature as the **Task Control Block (TCB)**, **Process Descriptor**, or **Process Table Entry**. It is created when a process is admitted into the system and destroyed only when the process terminates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The PCB is the single most critical data structure in the process management subsystem. Loss or corruption of a PCB leads to a **zombie process** or system crash.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Student Master File"

Imagine a university registrar's office. For every enrolled student, the registrar maintains a **Master File** containing:

| Registrar's File Field | Operating System Equivalent |
|---|---|
| University Roll Number | **Process ID (PID)** |
| Current Semester | **Process State** |
| Attendance & Internal Marks | **CPU Registers & Program Counter** |
| Branch & Section | **Memory Management Info** |
| Fee Receipt Number | **I/O Status Information** |
| Scholarship Priority | **Scheduling Priority** |
| Parent's Name & Address | **Pointer to Parent Process** |

Just as the registrar cannot track a student without this file, the OS cannot manage a process without its PCB. When a student migrates between classes, the file travels with them; similarly, during a **context switch**, the PCB is the medium through which the OS saves and restores process state.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Maximum PID value (Linux 32-bit):** $32768$ (default `pid_max`)
> - **Maximum PID value (Linux 64-bit):** $4194304$ (`/proc/sys/kernel/pid_max`)
> - **PCB Residency:** Always in **kernel-protected memory** (never in user space)
> - **Pointer Size:** 4 bytes (32-bit) or **8 bytes (64-bit)** on modern systems

---

## 1.4 Role of PCB in the OS

The PCB serves **four primary functions**:

1. **Identification** — Uniquely identifies a process via its PID and PPID.
2. **State Preservation** — Stores the snapshot of CPU registers, enabling context switches.
3. **Resource Tracking** — Maintains accounting of memory, open files, and I/O devices.
4. **Scheduling Support** — Carries priority values and queue pointers used by the scheduler.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Decomposition of the PCB

The PCB is typically organized into the **three logical groupings** given below. Each grouping is described with the operational "Why" and "How".

### Group A — Process Identification Block
- **Process ID (PID):** Unique non-negative integer assigned at creation.
  - *Why:* Kernel uses PID for all system calls (e.g., `kill(pid, signal)`).
  - *How:* Allocated from a PID bitmap; recycled on process termination.
- **Parent Process ID (PPID):** PID of the creator (except `init` / `systemd`, where PPID = 0).
- **User ID & Group ID:** For permission enforcement (Unix DAC model).
- **Process Group / Session ID:** Used in job control and signal delivery.

### Group B — Processor State Block (CPU Context)
- **Program Counter (PC):** Address of the *next instruction* to execute.
- **CPU Registers:** General-purpose (AX, BX, CX, DX on x86), index, segment, stack pointer.
- **Status Word / Flags Register:** Carry, Zero, Sign, Overflow bits.
- **Stack Pointer (SP):** Top of the kernel stack for this process.
- *Why:* Required for **context switching** — without these, the process cannot resume exactly where it left off.

### Group C — Process Control Block (Scheduling & Resource Info)
- **Process State:** One of {New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated}.
- **Scheduling Parameters:** Priority, quantum, scheduling policy (RR, FCFS, SJF).
- **Queue Pointers:** `next`, `prev` pointers for the ready queue, wait queue, etc.
- **Memory Management Info:** Base/Limit registers, Page Table base pointer, Segment Table pointer.
- **I/O Status Info:** List of open file descriptors, I/O devices allocated, pending I/O requests.
- **Accounting Info:** CPU time used, user CPU time, system CPU time, wall-clock time, time limits.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the mathematical and structural definitions used in PCB-related problems.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Symbol / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| PID Uniqueness Constraint | $\vert P \vert = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{PID}_i$, all $\text{PID}_i$ distinct | $\vert P \vert$ = number of active processes |
| Context Switch Time | $T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{load}$ | seconds |
| CPU Utilization | $U_{CPU} = 1 - \frac{T_{cs}}{T_{cs} + T_{burst}}$ | fraction ($0 \leq U \leq 1$) |
| Throughput Bound | $\theta \leq \frac{1}{T_{cs}}$ | processes/sec |
| PCB Size (theoretical) | $S_{PCB} = S_{ID} + S_{CPU} + S_{MMU} + S_{IO} + S_{SCH}$ | bytes |
| Memory Overhead Ratio | $\rho = \frac{n \cdot S_{PCB}}{M_{total}}$ | dimensionless |
| Maximum Concurrent Processes | $P_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{M_{kernel}}{S_{PCB}} \right\rfloor$ | integer |

> **Boundary Conditions:**
> - $T_{cs} > 0$ always (no instantaneous switching).
> - $S_{PCB} \geq 64$ bytes (typical minimum in Unix).
> - $U_{CPU} \to 1$ when $T_{cs} \ll T_{burst}$.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

The PCB is the **backbone of multiprogramming** and is used in:

- **Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS):** VxWorks, FreeRTOS use PCBs (TCBs) to enforce hard deadlines.
- **Cloud Computing:** Each container or VM is backed by a host-OS PCB.
- **Embedded Systems:** Automotive ECUs (Engine Control Units) rely on PCB-based scheduling.
- **Cyber-Physical Systems:** The PCB's accounting fields enable **WCET (Worst-Case Execution Time)** analysis for safety certification (ISO 26262).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CPU Utilization as a Function of Context-Switch Time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **favourite KTU question** and frequently tested. Memorize the logic, not just the formula.

**Given:**
- $T_{cs}$ = time taken to perform one context switch (seconds).
- $T_{burst}$ = average CPU burst time of a process (seconds).
- Each cycle of CPU work is preceded by a context switch.

**Step 1 — Define one complete cycle.**

The CPU is busy for $T_{burst}$ seconds, but before resuming work after a context switch, it spends $T_{cs}$ seconds saving/loading the PCB. Therefore, the **total time per process execution unit** is:

$$
T_{total} = T_{cs} + T_{burst}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the wasted (overhead) fraction.**

Out of the total cycle time, the portion $T_{cs}$ is *not* useful CPU work. The fraction of time wasted is:

$$
f_{waste} = \frac{T_{cs}}{T_{cs} + T_{burst}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of CPU utilization.**

CPU utilization $U_{CPU}$ is the fraction of time the CPU is doing useful work, which is $1 - f_{waste}$:

$$
U_{CPU} = 1 - f_{waste} = 1 - \frac{T_{cs}}{T_{cs} + T_{burst}}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify by dividing numerator and denominator by $T_{cs}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{CPU} &= 1 - \frac{T_{cs}/T_{cs}}{(T_{cs} + T_{burst})/T_{cs}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \frac{T_{burst}}{T_{cs}}} \\
&= \frac{T_{burst}/T_{cs}}{1 + T_{burst}/T_{cs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU style).**

Let $T_{cs} = 0.01$ ms and $T_{burst} = 100$ ms. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{CPU} &= 1 - \frac{0.01}{0.01 + 100} \\
&= 1 - \frac{0.01}{100.01} \\
&= 1 - 0.00009999 \\
&\approx 0.99990 \\
&= 99.99\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Context-switch overhead is negligible for long CPU bursts but becomes significant in systems with many short processes (e.g., web servers handling thousands of requests per second).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Number of Concurrent Processes

The kernel reserves a fixed region of memory for the PCB array. If $M_{kernel}$ is the kernel memory allocated for PCBs and $S_{PCB}$ is the size of one PCB:

$$
P_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{M_{kernel}}{S_{PCB}} \right\rfloor
$$

**Numerical Example:**

Suppose $M_{kernel} = 64$ MB and $S_{PCB} = 1$ KB. Convert units:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max} &= \left\lfloor \frac{64 \times 1024 \text{ KB}}{1 \text{ KB}} \right\rfloor \\
&= \left\lfloor 65536 \right\rfloor \\
&= 65536 \text{ processes}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the default `threads-max` limit in older Linux kernels.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | PCB | TCB (Thread Control Block) |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Per **process** | Per **thread** |
| Contains Memory Info | Yes (page tables, segment tables) | No (shared with peer threads) |
| Contains Open File List | Yes | No (shared with process) |
| Size | Large (typically 1–4 KB) | Small (typically 100–500 bytes) |
| Context Switch Cost | High (TLB flush, cache pollution) | Low (only register save/restore) |
| Granularity | Heavyweight | Lightweight |
| Created By | `fork()`, `CreateProcess()` | `pthread_create()` |
| KTU Tag | Process-level | Thread-level |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a PCB

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **internal block structure** of a typical Process Control Block, mapped as a hierarchical block-level functional architecture (since a literal physical drawing is best expressed this way in Mermaid).

```mermaid
graph TB
    PCBNode["PROCESS CONTROL BLOCK PCB"]

    PIDNode["GROUP A PROCESS IDENTIFICATION"]
    StateNode["GROUP B PROCESSOR STATE CPU CONTEXT"]
    CtrlNode["GROUP C PROCESS CONTROL FIELDS"]
    MemNode["GROUP D MEMORY MANAGEMENT"]
    IONode["GROUP E I O STATUS"]
    AcctNode["GROUP F ACCOUNTING"]

    PCBNode --> PIDNode
    PCBNode --> StateNode
    PCBNode --> CtrlNode
    PCBNode --> MemNode
    PCBNode --> IONode
    PCBNode --> AcctNode

    PIDNode --> PID1["PID 32 bit or 64 bit"]
    PIDNode --> PPID1["PPID Parent PID"]
    PIDNode --> UID1["User ID and Group ID"]
    PIDNode --> SID1["Session and Group ID"]

    StateNode --> PC1["Program Counter"]
    StateNode --> REG1["General Purpose Registers"]
    StateNode --> SP1["Stack Pointer"]
    StateNode --> FLAGS1["Status Flags Register"]
    StateNode --> KSTK1["Kernel Stack Pointer"]

    CtrlNode --> PST1["Process State New Ready Running Wait"]
    CtrlNode --> PRI1["Priority and Scheduling Policy"]
    CtrlNode --> QP1["Queue Pointers next prev"]
    CtrlNode --> SIG1["Signal Mask Pending Signals"]

    MemNode --> BASE1["Base and Limit Registers"]
    MemNode --> PTBL1["Page Table Base Pointer"]
    MemNode -> STBL1["Segment Table Pointer"]

    IONode -> FDS1["List of Open File Descriptors"]
    IONode -> DEVS1["Allocated I O Devices"]
    IONode -> IOREQ1["Pending I O Requests"]

    AcctNode -> CTIME1["User CPU Time"]
    AcctNode -> STIME1["System CPU Time"]
    AcctNode -> TOT1["Wall Clock Time"]
    AcctNode -> LIM1["Time and Resource Limits"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The root node `PCBNode` represents the entire PCB. It branches into **six logical groups** (A–F), each containing 4–5 sub-fields. This is the canonical structure examined in KTU descriptive questions.

---

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — PCB During Context Switch

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["Process P1 RUNNING"]
    P2["Process P2 READY"]
    OS1["OS Kernel Scheduler"]
    PCB1["PCB of P1 saved to memory"]
    PCB2["PCB of P2 loaded into CPU registers"]
    P2RUN["Process P2 RUNNING"]
    P1RDY["Process P1 READY"]

    P1 -->|Interrupt or preemption| OS1
    OS1 -->|Save registers, PC, SP| PCB1
    PCB1 -->|State becomes Ready| P1RDY
    P2 -->|Scheduler dispatch| PCB2
    PCB2 -->|CPU resumes execution| P2RUN
```

> **Interpretation:** When P1 is preempted, its PCB is updated and P1 joins the ready queue. The scheduler then picks P2, loads its PCB into the CPU, and P2 begins execution. The PCB is the **only carrier of state** during this transition.

---

## 4.3 Process State Diagram (PCB-Annotated)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New : PCB created and initialized
    New --> Ready : Admitted by long term scheduler
    Ready --> Running : Short term scheduler dispatches
    Running --> Ready : Quantum expires or preempted
    Running --> Waiting : I O request or system call
    Waiting --> Ready : I O completion interrupt
    Running --> Terminated : Exit or abort signal
    Terminated --> [*] : PCB deallocated
```

> **Note:** Each transition involves a **write or update to the PCB's `state` field** and possible movement across the ready/wait queue — a high-frequency operation in any OS.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Process Control Block. List any six fields stored in it. [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023 | Model Paper KTU2024-OS-M1-Q7]`

**Model Answer:**

> A Process Control Block (PCB) is a **kernel data structure** that contains all the information about a specific process, allowing the operating system to manage, schedule, and resume its execution.

**Any six fields (1/2 mark each):**

1. **Process ID (PID)** — unique identifier
2. **Process State** — New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated
3. **Program Counter (PC)** — next instruction address
4. **CPU Registers** — general purpose and status flags
5. **Memory Management Info** — base/limit registers, page table pointer
6. **I/O Status Information** — list of open files and allocated devices
7. **Scheduling Parameters** — priority, queue pointers

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only the names without explaining their purpose. Always include a brief role description for at least 2–3 fields to secure full marks.

---

### Question 2: What is the role of the Program Counter and CPU Registers in the PCB? Why are they essential during a context switch? [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 | Model Paper KTU2024-OS-M1-Q8]`

**Model Answer:**

- The **Program Counter (PC)** holds the address of the **next instruction** to be executed by the process. It is essential because, without saving it during a context switch, the process would lose its execution position and could not resume correctly.
- **CPU Registers** (accumulator, index, segment, stack pointer, status flags) hold the **transient computational state** of the process.
- During a **context switch**, the OS saves the PC and registers of the outgoing process into its PCB and loads them from the PCB of the incoming process. This **save-and-restore mechanism** is what enables multiprogramming.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Writing "PC is a counter" without mentioning the *next instruction* concept. Examiners expect the *resume-execution* perspective, not a textbook glossary line.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Part B questions carry an **internal choice**. Two alternative questions (A and B) are given; answer **either A or B**.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the various fields of a Process Control Block in detail. [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Model Paper KTU2024-OS-M1-Q9a]`

**Model Answer (with structured sub-headings):**

A PCB is a kernel-resident data structure organized into the following logical groups:

**1. Process Identification Information (1 mark)**

- **PID:** Unique non-negative integer assigned at creation. Used by all system calls referencing the process.
- **PPID:** PID of the parent. The `init` process has PPID = 0.
- **UID / GID:** Determine file access permissions (Discretionary Access Control).

**2. Processor State Information (2 marks)**

- **Program Counter (PC):** Points to the next instruction. Critical for resumption after preemption.
- **CPU Registers:** Accumulator, base, index, general-purpose registers. Store intermediate computation results.
- **Stack Pointer (SP):** Top of the kernel stack belonging to the process.
- **Status Register / Flags:** Carry, Zero, Sign, Overflow, Interrupt-enable bits.

**3. Process Control Information (2 marks)**

- **Process State:** Current lifecycle state (Ready/Running/Waiting/...).
- **Scheduling Info:** Priority value, scheduling policy (RR, FCFS, MLFQ).
- **Queue Pointers:** `next` and `prev` pointers linking the process into the ready/wait queue.
- **Signal Information:** Pending and blocked signal masks.

**4. Memory Management Information (1 mark)**

- **Base / Limit Registers:** Define the process's address space boundaries.
- **Page Table Base Pointer:** For virtual memory translation.
- **Segment Table Pointer:** In segmented memory systems.

**5. I/O Status Information (1 mark)**

- **List of Open Files:** File descriptor table pointer.
- **I/O Devices Allocated:** Peripherals currently in use.
- **Pending I/O Requests:** Outstanding operations.

#### (b) With a neat diagram, explain the role of PCB in context switching. [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Model Paper KTU2024-OS-M1-Q9b]`

**Model Answer:**

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```
+----------+        +----------------+        +----------+
|  CPU     |  <-->  |  PCB of P_old  |        |  PCB of  |
| Registers|        +----------------+        |  P_new   |
+----------+              |                    +----------+
       |                 V                          |
       |   (1) Save state into PCB_old             |
       |--------------------------------------------|
       |   (2) Load state from PCB_new              |
       |<-------------------------------------------|
       V
   Execution resumes for P_new
```

**Explanation (4 marks):**

1. **Trigger:** A context switch is triggered by an interrupt, system call, or scheduler preemption.
2. **Save Phase:** The OS copies the **Program Counter, all CPU registers, stack pointer, and flags** of the currently running process (`P_old`) into its PCB and stores it in the kernel's process table. The state field is updated to `Ready`.
3. **Schedule Phase:** The scheduler selects a new process (`P_new`) from the ready queue based on its PCB's priority and policy fields.
4. **Load Phase:** The OS reads `P_new`'s PCB and loads the saved PC, registers, SP, and flags into the actual CPU hardware registers.
5. **Resume Phase:** Control is transferred to `P_new`, which begins (or continues) execution as if uninterrupted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** The PCB is the **only state-carrier** between a process's execution slices. It enables the OS to give the *illusion* of simultaneous execution to multiple processes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:**
> - Drawing a poor / unclear diagram — lose up to **2 marks**.
> - Confusing **context switch** with **mode switch** (kernel ↔ user mode transition). Context switch involves PCB swapping; mode switch does NOT.
> - Forgetting to mention that the **TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer)** may need flushing during a context switch (1 mark lost if missed).

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Differentiate between PCB and TCB. Explain the structure of PCB with a block diagram. [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Model Paper KTU2024-OS-M1-Q10a]`

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | PCB (Process Control Block) | TCB (Thread Control Block) |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Heavyweight (whole process) | Lightweight (single thread) |
| Memory Info | Has its own page table | Shares parent's page table |
| Open Files | Has its own FD table | Shares with process |
| Size | 1–4 KB typical | 100–500 bytes |
| Creation Cost | High (memory, file table, etc.) | Low (only registers and stack) |
| Examples | `fork()` on Linux | `pthread_create()` |

**PCB Block Diagram (3 marks):** *(Refer to SECTION 4.1 diagram — reproduce the six-group structure: Process ID, CPU State, Control Info, Memory Info, I/O Status, Accounting.)*

#### (b) Discuss how the PCB is used during process creation, execution, and termination. [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Model Paper KTU2024-OS-M1-Q10b]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Process Creation (2 marks)**

- The kernel allocates a **new PCB** in kernel memory and initializes all fields.
- A unique **PID** is assigned from the PID bitmap.
- The PCB's state field is set to `New`.
- Parent-child pointers are wired (PPID set, child list updated).
- After initialization, the state transitions to `Ready` via the long-term scheduler.

**2. Process Execution (3 marks)**

- The **short-term scheduler** dispatches the process, transitioning PCB state to `Running`.
- The PCB's accounting fields (`user_CPU_time`, `system_CPU_time`) are continuously updated by the timer interrupt handler.
- On **preemption**, all CPU registers, PC, SP, and flags are written into the PCB and the state becomes `Ready`.
- During **I/O wait**, the state changes to `Waiting` and the PCB is moved to the appropriate wait queue.
- On **I/O completion**, the device driver moves the PCB back to the ready queue.

**3. Process Termination (2 marks)**

- The process issues an `exit()` system call, or receives a fatal signal.
- The kernel records the **exit status** in the PCB.
- The PCB enters the `Terminated` (or `Zombie`) state — it remains until the parent calls `wait()`.
- The parent collects the exit status, the kernel **deallocates the PCB**, frees the PID, and removes the entry from the process table.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is to claim that the PCB is deleted *immediately* on process exit. The **zombie state** is a tested concept — the PCB persists until the parent reaps it. Missing this loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Final-exam crammer box. Tick these mentally before you submit the answer script.

- [ ] **PCB = kernel data structure** representing **one process** — it is *not* in user space.
- [ ] Three essential groupings: **Identification, CPU State, Control Info** (memory, I/O, accounting).
- [ ] **Program Counter + Registers** in PCB are the *minimum required* for resumption.
- [ ] PCB is created at process **admission**, destroyed at **reaping** (after parent `wait()`).
- [ ] During a **context switch**, save → schedule → load all operate on the PCB.
- [ ] PCB **size** grows with the process: 1 KB (typical), up to 4–8 KB (Linux `task_struct`).
- [ ] **Zombie process** = terminated but PCB still exists (parent hasn't called `wait()`).
- [ ] **Orphan process** = parent terminated first; re-parented to `init` (PID 1).
- [ ] **TCB** (Thread Control Block) is *lighter* — shares memory/files of its parent process.
- [ ] **Formula to memorize:**
$$U_{CPU} = 1 - \frac{T_{cs}}{T_{cs} + T_{burst}}, \quad P_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{M_{kernel}}{S_{PCB}} \right\rfloor$$
- [ ] **Context Switch ≠ Mode Switch.** A context switch *always* involves swapping PCBs.
- [ ] The PCB is consulted by the **scheduler, dispatcher, interrupt handler, and resource allocator** — making it the single most-used kernel object.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the 6 PCB groups:** **"I-C-M-I-S-A"** → **I**dentification, **C**PU state, **M**emory, **I**/O, **S**cheduling/accounting. Practice writing it in the order examiners expect.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **thread** (also called a *lightweight process*, **LWP**) is the basic unit of CPU utilization; it comprises a **thread ID**, a **program counter (PC)**, a **register set**, and a **stack**. It shares with other threads belonging to the same process its **code section**, **data section**, and other **OS resources** such as open files and signals. A traditional (or *heavyweight*) process — that is, a process with only one thread of control — is equivalent to a task with one thread.

A **thread pool** is a collection of pre-created worker threads that wait for tasks to be dispatched. **Implicit threading** is a class of strategies in which the compiler or runtime library, rather than the application programmer, handles the creation and management of threads (e.g., **OpenMP**, **Grand Central Dispatch (GCD)**, **Intel Threading Building Blocks (TBB)**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCERT601 — Module 1):** Threads cover *types of threads, multithreading models, thread libraries, threading issues, thread pools, and implicit threading*. A clear understanding of **user-level vs kernel-level threads** and the **three multithreading models** is mandatory for ESE questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Office Worker" Analogy** 🏢
>
> Imagine an **Office Building** as a *process* — it has a name, an address, shared meeting rooms, a pantry, and electricity (shared resources). Now, the **workers** inside that office are *threads*. They all share the same building, the same pantry, and the same electricity, but each worker has their **own desk** (register set), their **own to-do list** (program counter), and their **own personal drawer** (stack).
>
> If the office fires one worker, the building still stands. If a new worker is hired, they can immediately start using the pantry — no new electricity meter needs installation. This is precisely how threads work inside a process: **cheap to create, fast to switch, and they share the parent's address space.**

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Highlights)

- **Thread ID** — unique identifier for the thread within its process.
- **Program Counter (PC)** — points to the next instruction to execute.
- **Register Set** — the working storage private to each thread.
- **Stack / Stack Pointer** — local variables and call frames of a thread.
- **Thread-Specific Data (TSD)** — local storage accessible to only one thread.
- **Process** — the unit of resource allocation; a container for one or more threads.
- **Concurrency** — logically simultaneous progress of multiple tasks.
- **Parallelism** — physically simultaneous execution on multiple CPU cores.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Motivation: Why Threads?

Modern software demands responsiveness, resource economy, and parallelism. Threads achieve all three:

- **Responsiveness** — A single blocked thread (e.g., waiting for I/O) does not freeze the entire application; another thread keeps the UI alive (think: a web browser keeping the address bar responsive while a page loads).
- **Resource Sharing** — Threads of the same process share memory and resources by default, eliminating the costly Inter-Process Communication (IPC) mechanisms like pipes, message queues, or shared-memory setup.
- **Economy** — Creating a thread is **~10×–100× cheaper** than creating a process because there is no need to duplicate the address space.
- **Scalability / Parallelism** — On multi-core systems, threads can be distributed across cores to achieve true parallel speedup.
- **Utilization of Multiprocessor Architectures** — A single-threaded process can use at most one CPU core regardless of how many exist; multithreading exploits **N** cores.

### 2.2 Thread vs Process — The KTU Comparison

| Attribute | **Process** | **Thread** |
|---|---|---|
| **Creation Cost** | Heavy (address-space duplication) | Lightweight (~10–100× faster) |
| **Context Switch** | Expensive (TLB & cache flush) | Cheap (registers + stack only) |
| **Memory Space** | Independent address space | Shared within process |
| **Communication** | IPC (pipes, sockets, shared memory) | Direct shared variables |
| **Crash Isolation** | High (one crash $\nmid$ others) | Low (one fault may kill all peers) |
| **Owner of Resources** | Yes — owns them | No — borrows from process |
| **Granularity** | Coarse | Fine |

### 2.3 Multithreading Models (Three Canonical Models)

> [!NOTE]
> **The relationship between user threads and kernel threads is the *multithreading model*.** This is a high-yield KTU ESE topic — expect direct questions.

#### Model 1: Many-to-One (N:1)
- Many **user-level threads** are mapped to a single **kernel thread**.
- Thread management is done in **user space** by a thread-library runtime.
- If one thread makes a blocking system call, the **entire process blocks**.
- **Cannot run in parallel** on multi-core systems (only one kernel thread per process).
- Example: **GNU Portable Threads (Pth)**.

#### Model 2: One-to-One (1:1)
- Each user thread maps to a distinct kernel thread.
- Provides **true concurrency** on multiprocessors.
- Overhead: each user thread requires a corresponding kernel thread — limits the maximum number of threads.
- Example: **Windows threads**, **Linux Pthreads**.

#### Model 3: Many-to-Many (M:N)
- Multiplexes **M** user threads onto **N** kernel threads, where $M \ge N$.
- Developers can create as many user threads as needed; the kernel can schedule them efficiently.
- A common variant is the **two-level model** which binds some user threads permanently to kernel threads.
- Example: **Solaris**, **Windows ThreadFiber** (historically), **GNU Pth + scheduler activations**.

### 2.4 Thread Libraries

A *thread library* provides the API for programmers to create and manage threads. The three principal approaches are:

1. **Asynchronous (in-kernel) libraries** — implemented entirely at the kernel level; the library's code resides in kernel space. System calls trap into the kernel. *(e.g., Windows, Solaris)*
2. **Synchronous (user-level) libraries** — implemented entirely in user space without kernel support; all code and data structures live in user space. *(e.g., POSIX Pthreads when used in user-space mode, GNU Pth)*
3. **Hybrid implementations** — combine both approaches; some code in user space, some in kernel. *(e.g., modern Pthreads on Linux via glibc, which uses the `clone()` syscall)*

The three widely-used thread libraries are:

- **POSIX Pthreads** — IEEE 1003.1c standard; usable on UNIX/Linux. Functions: `pthread_create()`, `pthread_join()`, `pthread_exit()`.
- **Win32 Threads** — Microsoft's API for Windows. Functions: `CreateThread()`, `WaitForSingleObject()`.
- **Java Threads** — JVM-managed. Methods: `Thread.start()`, `Thread.join()`, `Thread.run()`.

### 2.5 Threading Issues (Critical for KTU ESE)

The following are the canonical issues a multithreaded system must solve:

- **System Calls (`fork()` vs `exec()`**)** — Should `fork()` duplicate only the calling thread, or all threads? In UNIX, `fork()` duplicates *all* threads; if the child immediately calls `exec()`, only the calling thread is relevant.
- **Cancellation** — Terminating a thread before it has finished.
  - *Asynchronous cancellation*: one thread immediately terminates the target (unsafe — may leave shared data in an inconsistent state).
  - *Deferred cancellation*: the target thread periodically checks a flag and voluntarily exits (safe).
- **Signal Handling** — Signals are system-level events. Options: deliver the signal to the thread to which it applies, deliver to every thread, deliver to certain threads, or designate a specific thread to receive all signals.
- **Thread Pools** — Pre-create a fixed number of worker threads; the server dispatches requests to idle workers. Advantages: faster service (no thread creation per request), bounded resource use, and easy task-parallel structuring.
- **Thread-Specific Data** — Useful when a procedure needs to maintain data local to each thread (e.g., transaction IDs).
- **Scheduler Activations** — A mechanism used by the M:N model where the kernel communicates with the user-thread scheduler via *upcalls*.

### 2.6 Implicit Threading

Modern languages/runtime systems **hide** thread management from the developer:

- **Thread Pools** (already discussed).
- **OpenMP** — a compiler-directive-based API for C/C++/Fortran. The compiler inserts thread creation/synchronization based on `#pragma omp parallel` directives.
- **Grand Central Dispatch (GCD)** — Apple's technology for macOS/iOS. Developers define task *blocks*; GCD dispatches them to a thread pool.
- **Intel Threading Building Blocks (TBB)** — a C++ template library for parallel programming with task-based parallelism.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Amdahl's Law (Speedup)** | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | Max speedup with **$N$** cores, **$P$** = parallel fraction | dimensionless |
| **Max Speedup Limit** | $\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \dfrac{1}{1 - P}$ | As **$N \to \infty$**; upper bound | dimensionless |
| **Cost of Creation** | $T_{\text{thread}} \approx 0.01 \cdot T_{\text{process}}$ | Thread creation is ~1% of process creation | seconds |
| **Context-Switch Time** | $T_{\text{ctx}} = T_{\text{reg}} + T_{\text{flush}}$ | Register save/restore + optional cache flush | $\mu s$ |
| **Thread Count (1:1 limit)** | $N_{\text{threads}} \le N_{\text{kernel\_resources}}$ | Limited by kernel table size | integer |
| **Concurrency** | Logical simultaneity | Independent tasks making progress | $logical$ |
| **Parallelism** | $\ge 1$ thread/core physical execution | Requires $\ge 2$ cores | $cores$ |
| **Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{\text{busy time}}{\text{total time}}$ | Fraction of CPU time used | $[0,1]$ |
| **Throughput** | $\lambda = \dfrac{\text{tasks}}{T_{\text{total}}}$ | Tasks per unit time | $tasks/s$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Threads power web servers (Apache `worker` MPM, Nginx event loops), database engines (PostgreSQL per-connection threads), GUI applications (UI thread + worker threads), and parallel computing frameworks (OpenMP on HPC clusters). Every KTU CSE graduate will write, debug, or maintain threaded code.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Amdahl's Law and Threading Speedup

**Problem Setup.** A program has total execution time $T = 1$ (normalized). A fraction $P$ of this program is *parallelizable* (can be split across threads); the remaining $(1 - P)$ is *serial* (must run on a single thread). We have $N$ CPU cores / threads available.

**Step 1 — Decompose the execution time.**

The serial part takes $(1 - P) \cdot T = (1 - P)$ time. The parallel part takes $P \cdot T = P$ time, but this can be divided among $N$ threads.

**Step 2 — Compute the parallel portion's runtime on $N$ threads.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{parallel, N cores}} &= \frac{P \cdot T}{N} = \frac{P}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum serial and parallel portions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(N) &= T_{\text{serial}} + T_{\text{parallel, N cores}} \\
T(N) &= (1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Define Speedup $S(N)$ as the ratio of original time to parallel time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(N) &= \frac{T(1)}{T(N)} = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Take the limit as $N \to \infty$ to find the upper bound.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) &= \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \lim\limits_{N \to \infty}\dfrac{P}{N}} \\
&= \frac{1}{(1 - P) + 0} \\
&= \frac{1}{1 - P}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even with **infinite** threads, the maximum speedup is bounded by $\dfrac{1}{1 - P}$. If only **90%** of the program is parallelizable ($P = 0.9$), the absolute ceiling is $\dfrac{1}{0.1} = 10\times$ speedup — no matter how many cores you throw at it.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Multithreaded Web Server

**Problem.** A web server takes an average of **$10 \text{ ms}$** to process a request. Of this, **$8 \text{ ms}$** is parallelizable (database fetch, file I/O) and **$2 \text{ ms}$** is serial (connection setup, response formatting). The server has **$4$** worker threads.

**Step 1 — Identify $P$.**

$$
P = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8
$$

**Step 2 — Compute expected runtime with 4 threads.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(4) &= (1 - P) + \frac{P}{N} = 0.2 + \frac{0.8}{4} = 0.2 + 0.2 = 0.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the speedup.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(4) &= \frac{1}{T(4)} = \frac{1}{0.4} = 2.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the theoretical maximum with infinite threads.**

$$
S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - P} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5.0
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the actual response time with 4 threads.**

$$
T_{\text{actual}} = 10 \text{ ms} \times 0.4 = 4 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Increasing $N$ from 4 to 8 yields $T(8) = 0.2 + 0.1 = 0.3$ — only a marginal improvement. The serial **$20\%$** is the bottleneck. The lesson: *optimize the serial portion first.*

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Thread Models — Pros, Cons, Use Cases

| Model | **Concurrency on Multi-core?** | **Blocking Syscall Effect** | **Max Threads** | **Overhead** | **KTU Example** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Many-to-One (N:1)** | ❌ No | Whole process blocks | Library-defined (very high) | Very low | GNU Portable Threads |
| **One-to-One (1:1)** | ✅ Yes | Only the calling thread blocks | Limited by kernel | Higher (syscall per op) | Linux Pthreads, Windows |
| **Many-to-Many (M:N)** | ✅ Yes | A kernel thread remains available | Effectively unlimited | Tunable | Solaris, modern Go runtime |
| **Two-Level (M:N + bound)** | ✅ Yes | Mixed | Effectively unlimited | Tunable | Windows ThreadFiber, TRIX |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pthread Functions (KTU Favorite)

| Function | Purpose | Return Value |
|---|---|---|
| `pthread_create(tid, attr, fn, arg)` | Spawn a new thread running `fn(arg)` | 0 on success |
| `pthread_exit(retval)` | Terminate calling thread, return `retval` | void |
| `pthread_join(tid, retval)` | Wait for thread `tid` to terminate | 0 on success |
| `pthread_yield()` | Release CPU to another ready thread | 0 on success |
| `pthread_attr_init(attr)` | Initialize a thread-attribute object | 0 on success |
| `pthread_cancel(tid)` | Send cancellation request to `tid` | 0 on success |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Thread Cancellation — Asynchronous vs Deferred

| Property | **Asynchronous** | **Deferred** |
|---|---|---|
| **When** | Immediately, on signal | At next cancellation point |
| **Safety** | Unsafe — shared state may corrupt | Safe — thread controls exit point |
| **Use Case** | Rare; emergency shutdown | Default in Pthreads |
| **Pthread Mode** | `PTHREAD_CANCEL_ASYNCHRONOUS` | `PTHREAD_CANCEL_DEFERRED` |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process vs Threads — Memory Layout

```mermaid
graph TB
    processA["PROCESS A — Independent Address Space"]
    text1["Code Section"]

    t2["Thread 2: PC, Regs, Stack"]
    t3["Thread 3: PC, Regs, Stack"]

    processA --> data1
    processA --> files1
    processA --> heap1
    processA --> t1
    processA --> t2
    processA --> t3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** Notice that all three threads point to the *same* Process A. Their individual per-thread items (PC, Registers, Stack) are unique, but the code, data, files, and heap are **shared** among them.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** Define a thread. How does it differ from a process? (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
A *thread* is a basic unit of CPU utilization that consists of a thread ID, program counter, register set, and stack, and shares code, data, and OS resources with other threads in the same process. A *process* is a heavyweight unit of resource allocation with an independent address space, while a thread is lightweight and shares the address space of its parent process. Threads are cheaper to create and switch than processes.

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** List any three benefits of multithreading. (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
1. **Responsiveness** — long-running tasks can run in the background while the UI stays alive.
2. **Resource sharing** — threads share process memory by default, avoiding complex IPC.
3. **Economy** — thread creation is much cheaper than process creation, with faster context switches.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Module-Internal Choice Format:** Answer **any one** of the two alternatives. Both alternatives carry equal marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | ESE Module 1 Choice A]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **three multithreading models** — Many-to-One, One-to-One, and Many-to-Many — with neat diagrams. Compare their advantages and limitations. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Many-to-One (N:1) Model:**
In this model, multiple user-level threads are mapped to a single kernel thread. The thread management is done entirely in user space by a thread-library runtime.
- *Advantage:* Very efficient — no kernel mode transitions for thread operations.
- *Limitation:* The entire process blocks if any thread makes a blocking system call. It cannot exploit multiprocessors — only one kernel-thread-bound execution at a time.
- *Example:* GNU Portable Threads (Pth).

**One-to-One (1:1) Model:**
Each user thread corresponds to exactly one kernel thread.
- *Advantage:* Provides true concurrency on multiprocessors; a blocking call in one thread does not block the others.
- *Limitation:* Creating a user thread requires a kernel thread — limits the number of threads; syscall overhead per thread operation.
- *Example:* Linux Pthreads, Windows Win32 threads.

**Many-to-Many (M:N) Model:**
Maps $M$ user threads to $N$ kernel threads, where $M \ge N$. The kernel scheduler activates user-level schedulers through *scheduler activations* (upcalls).
- *Advantage:* Combines the best of both — developers can spawn many user threads, and the kernel can distribute them across cores.
- *Limitation:* Complex to implement; needs kernel-user cooperation (e.g., scheduler activations).
- *Example:* Solaris threads, modern Go runtime scheduler.

A *two-level model* is a hybrid that allows some user threads to be **permanently bound** to a kernel thread while others multiplex.

*(Neat diagrams as shown in Section 4.2 should be included.)*

**Q. (b)** Discuss the various **threading issues** that must be handled by an OS supporting multithreading. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

1. **System Calls (`fork`/`exec`):** When a multithreaded process calls `fork()`, the child should ideally duplicate only the calling thread (so the child can call `exec()` immediately without dragging along unneeded state). In UNIX, `fork()` copies all threads, so the developer must be careful.

2. **Thread Cancellation:** Terminating a thread before completion. Two modes:
   - *Asynchronous:* Immediate termination — unsafe; can leave shared data in an inconsistent state.
   - *Deferred:* The target thread checks a cancellation flag at *cancellation points* and exits voluntarily — safe.

3. **Signal Handling:** Signals (e.g., `SIGINT`) can be:
   - Delivered to the thread to which the signal applies.
   - Delivered to every thread in the process.
   - Delivered to certain threads.
   - A specific *signal-handling thread* is designated to receive all signals.

4. **Thread Pools:** Pre-create a fixed number of worker threads to handle incoming requests. The pool's master thread accepts requests and dispatches them to idle workers, eliminating per-request thread creation overhead.

5. **Thread-Specific Data:** Allows each thread to maintain private data (e.g., a per-thread transaction ID) within shared procedures.

6. **Scheduler Activations:** Used by M:N models; the kernel provides upcalls to inform the user-thread scheduler about events (thread blocked/unblocked, processor allocation changes).

7. **Data Race and Synchronization:** Concurrent threads accessing shared data without synchronization produce non-deterministic results — requiring mutexes, semaphores, or condition variables.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | ESE Module 1 Choice B]`

**Q. (a)** With the help of suitable diagrams, explain the **structure of a process with multiple threads**. Discuss the components shared between threads and those that are thread-specific. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A process with $N$ threads has *one* address space, but each thread maintains its own execution context. The diagram (see Section 4.1) shows the layout.

**Shared among threads (process-wide):**
- **Code section** — instructions of the program.
- **Data section** — global and static variables.
- **Heap** — dynamically allocated memory.
- **Open files and sockets** — file descriptors.
- **Signal handlers** — process-wide.
- **Process credentials, working directory.**

**Thread-specific (per-thread):**
- **Thread ID.**
- **Program counter (PC)** — points to the next instruction of that thread.
- **Register set** — context for that thread.
- **Stack** — local variables, return addresses, function-call frames.
- **Stack pointer (SP).**
- **Thread-local storage (TLS / TSD)** — explicit per-thread data.
- **Thread-specific signal masks / errno.**

**Implication:** Because all threads share the same address space, communication is trivial — write to a shared variable. However, this also means *synchronization* is mandatory to avoid data races. The cost of a context switch between threads of the same process is much lower than between processes (no TLB flush, no address-space switch).

**Q. (b)** What is **Amdahl's Law**? A program has a parallelizable portion of **$75\%$**. Compute the maximum achievable speedup and the speedup when using **$8$** threads. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Amdahl's Law** states that the speedup of a program from parallelization is bounded by the fraction of the program that must remain serial. The formula is:

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}
$$

where $P$ is the parallel fraction and $N$ is the number of processing elements (threads / cores).

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$$
P = 0.75, \qquad N = 8, \qquad (1 - P) = 0.25
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum speedup (limit as $N \to \infty$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\max} &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.25 + 0} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4.0
\end{aligned}
$$

So the absolute ceiling is $4\times$ speedup, no matter how many threads you add.

**Step 3 — Compute the speedup with $N = 8$ threads.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(8) &= \frac{1}{0.25 + \dfrac{0.75}{8}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.25 + 0.09375} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.34375} \\
&\approx 2.91
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

With 8 threads, we get a speedup of approximately **$2.91\times$**, which is **$72.7\%$** of the theoretical maximum $4\times$. The bottleneck is the $25\%$ serial portion. Doubling the threads from 8 to 16 would yield:

$$
S(16) = \frac{1}{0.25 + \frac{0.75}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.296875} \approx 3.37
$$

a marginal gain. *Engineering takeaway:* optimize the serial portion first.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in Threads questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "thread" with "process"** — losing 2 marks by writing "thread has its own address space" (FALSE — threads *share* the address space).
> 2. **Mixing up the multithreading models** — Many-to-One vs One-to-One examples are swapped frequently. Memorize: *N:1 → no parallelism, 1:1 → full parallelism but limited count, M:N → best of both*.
> 3. **Forgetting the Pthread function signatures** — writing pseudocode-style answers rather than `pthread_create(&tid, NULL, fn, arg)` form costs you.
> 4. **Skipping the Amdahl's Law derivation** — examiners award marks for showing the limit $N \to \infty$ and identifying the serial bottleneck.
> 5. **Not labeling diagrams** — Mermaid-style diagrams without labels (`T1, T2, T3`) lose credit. Always label **shared** vs **thread-local** regions.
> 6. **Misstating cancellation modes** — confusing *asynchronous* with *deferred* cancellation costs a full sub-part mark.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **thread** = thread ID + PC + registers + stack; **shared** with peers: code, data, heap, files.
- ✅ Threads are **lightweight** (~10–100× faster to create than processes).
- ✅ **Three multithreading models**: Many-to-One (N:1), One-to-One (1:1), Many-to-Many (M:N).
- ✅ **Many-to-One** cannot use multiple cores and blocks the whole process on a syscall.
- ✅ **One-to-One** gives true parallelism but limits thread count.
- ✅ **Many-to-Many** + *two-level* = best scalability; used by Solaris, Go runtime.
- ✅ **Pthreads** (POSIX), **Win32**, and **Java** are the three dominant thread libraries.
- ✅ **Threading issues** to remember: `fork()`/`exec()`, cancellation (async vs deferred), signals, thread pools, TSD, scheduler activations, synchronization.
- ✅ **Thread pools** pre-create workers to reduce per-request overhead.
- ✅ **Implicit threading** (OpenMP, GCD, TBB) moves thread-management into the compiler/runtime.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law**: $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - P) + P / N}$, ceiling = $\dfrac{1}{1 - P}$.
- ✅ Speedup is **bounded by the serial fraction** — optimize serial code first.
- ✅ **Concurrency ≠ Parallelism** — concurrency is logical, parallelism is physical.
- ✅ **Diagrams are mandatory** in ESE answers on threading — always show shared vs per-thread regions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**CPU Scheduling** is the fundamental operating system mechanism by which the kernel selects one process from among the many that are ready to execute and allocates the central processing unit to that process. In a multiprogrammed environment, multiple processes compete for the CPU simultaneously; the **scheduler** (a component of the OS kernel) resolves this contention to optimize system performance according to a chosen scheduling policy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Scheduling is classified as a **Module 1** core topic under "Introduction to Operating Systems & Process Management." Students must master both **preemptive** and **non-preemptive** scheduling, along with the standard algorithms: **FCFS, SJF, Priority, and Round Robin**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Runway Controller

Imagine a single runway at a busy international airport. Dozens of aircraft (processes) are queued in the holding pattern, all wanting to land on the same runway (CPU). The **Air Traffic Controller (Scheduler)** must decide:

- **Which aircraft lands next?** (Which process gets the CPU?)
- **Should a landing aircraft be aborted if an emergency plane arrives?** (Preemption)
- **How do we minimize total waiting time for all passengers?** (Average Waiting Time)

Some airlines have **priority status** (VIP cargo, medical flights) and are allowed to land first. In emergencies, a controller can instruct an aircraft to **abort landing and circle again** to let a critical flight use the runway — this is exactly what *preemptive scheduling* does with a running process.

## 1.3 Types of Schedulers in an OS

The KTU syllabus mandates understanding three distinct schedulers:

| Scheduler Type | Function | Frequency of Execution | Typical Decision |
|----------------|----------|------------------------|------------------|
| **Long-Term Scheduler (Job Scheduler)** | Decides which jobs enter the Ready Queue from the disk | Infrequent (seconds to minutes) | Multiprogramming degree |
| **Short-Term Scheduler (CPU Scheduler)** | Decides which ready process gets the CPU next | Very frequent (milliseconds) | Process dispatch |
| **Medium-Term Scheduler** | Temporarily swaps processes out of main memory to disk to reduce multiprogramming degree | Periodic | Swapping in/out |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Short-Term Scheduler** is the focus of "CPU Scheduling" in this module. It is invoked whenever the CPU becomes idle (e.g., process termination, I/O request, or preemption time-slice expiry).

## 1.4 When Does Scheduling Happen? (Scheduling Triggers)

The CPU scheduler is invoked at four critical decision points:

1. A running process **terminates** (Non-preemptive).
2. A running process **switches to I/O wait** state (Non-preemptive).
3. A process **transitions from I/O wait to ready** (Preemptive possible).
4. A process is **preempted** after its time quantum expires (Preemptive only).

Circumstances **1 and 2** involve no choice — only one process is ready. Circumstances **3 and 4** involve true scheduling decisions among multiple ready processes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Scheduling Criteria — The Five Performance Metrics

The KTU examiner expects students to define and apply these five core criteria for any scheduling algorithm evaluation:

1. **CPU Utilization** — Percentage of time the CPU is busy doing useful work. Keep it as high as possible (theoretical ideal: **100%**).
2. **Throughput** — Number of processes completed per unit time. Higher is better.
3. **Turnaround Time (TAT)** — Total time from process submission to completion.
4. **Waiting Time (WT)** — Total time a process spends in the Ready Queue waiting for the CPU.
5. **Response Time (RT)** — Time from submission to first response (important in interactive systems).

## 2.2 Preemptive vs. Non-Preemptive Scheduling

| Property | Non-Preemptive | Preemptive |
|----------|---------------|------------|
| **Definition** | Once CPU is allocated to a process, it runs to completion or until it voluntarily releases CPU (I/O) | CPU can be forcibly taken away from a running process |
| **Also Called** | Cooperative scheduling | Time-sharing scheduling |
| **Risk** | A buggy/hanging process can freeze the entire system | Higher overhead due to context switching |
| **Used By** | Windows 3.1, early UNIX, MS-DOS | Windows NT/10/11, Linux, modern UNIX, macOS |
| **KTU Examples** | FCFS, Non-Preemptive SJF, Non-Preemptive Priority | SRTF, Preemptive Priority, Round Robin |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Trap:** A common mistake is treating SJF as inherently non-preemptive. In fact, **SJF is a policy** (shortest job first); it can be implemented as either **non-preemptive SJF** or **preemptive SJF (SRTF — Shortest Remaining Time First)**. Always specify the variant in your answer.

## 2.3 The Four Core Algorithms (KTU Module 1 Mandate)

### Algorithm 1: First-Come, First-Served (FCFS)
- **Logic:** The process that arrives first gets the CPU first. Implemented using a simple FIFO queue.
- **Type:** Non-preemptive.
- **Pros:** Simplest algorithm; fair; no starvation.
- **Cons:** Suffers from the **"Convoy Effect"** — one slow process delays all subsequent processes.

### Algorithm 2: Shortest Job First (SJF)
- **Logic:** Select the process with the smallest next CPU burst length. If tie, apply FCFS.
- **Type:** Can be Non-preemptive (run-to-completion) or Preemptive (SRTF).
- **Pros:** **Provably optimal** for minimizing average waiting time.
- **Cons:** Requires knowing the next CPU burst length in advance (estimated using exponential averaging); can cause **starvation** of long processes.

### Algorithm 3: Priority Scheduling
- **Logic:** Each process has a priority number (integer). The CPU is allocated to the process with the highest priority (lowest integer value by convention).
- **Type:** Can be Non-preemptive or Preemptive.
- **Pros:** Flexible — allows OS to favor critical system tasks.
- **Cons:** **Starvation** of low-priority processes. Solution: **Aging** (gradually increase the priority of waiting processes over time).

### Algorithm 4: Round Robin (RR)
- **Logic:** Each process gets a small unit of CPU time called a **time quantum** (or time slice). After the quantum expires, the process is preempted and added to the end of the ready queue.
- **Type:** Preemptive (timer-based).
- **Pros:** Excellent for time-sharing systems; fair; no starvation.
- **Cons:** Performance heavily depends on quantum size. If quantum is too small → excessive context switching overhead. If too large → degenerates into FCFS.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **essential** for every scheduling problem in the KTU End Semester Examination. Memorize and apply them with precision.

| # | Metric | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|--------|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | **Completion Time** $C_i$ | $C_i = \text{time when process } i \text{ finishes}$ | Read from Gantt chart end | Seconds |
| 2 | **Turnaround Time** $T_i$ | $T_i = C_i - A_i$ | $A_i$ = Arrival time | Seconds |
| 3 | **Waiting Time** $W_i$ | $W_i = T_i - B_i$ | $B_i$ = CPU Burst time | Seconds |
| 4 | **Response Time** $R_i$ | $R_i = \text{First CPU Allocation} - A_i$ | First dispatch time minus arrival | Seconds |
| 5 | **Average Waiting Time** $\overline{W}$ | $\overline{W} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i$ | Mean waiting time | Seconds |
| 6 | **Average Turnaround Time** $\overline{T}$ | $\overline{T} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$ | Mean TAT | Seconds |
| 7 | **Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{n}{C_{\max} - C_{0,\min}}$ | Processes completed per unit time | Processes/sec |
| 8 | **CPU Utilization** | $\text{CPU Util} = \dfrac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100\%$ | Busy time divided by total | Percentage |
| 9 | **Burst Time Estimation (Exponential Averaging)** | $\tau_{n+1} = \alpha \cdot t_n + (1-\alpha) \cdot \tau_n$ | Predicts next CPU burst | Time units |

**Notes on the Formula Sheet:**
- In the equation $W_i = T_i - B_i$, the subtraction operator is a horizontal minus sign, not a vertical bar.
- For RR scheduling, waiting time must be computed as $W_i = T_i - B_i$ *after* accounting for all context switches; do **not** simply subtract burst from turnaround.
- The exponential averaging formula uses $t_n$ (actual burst of instance $n$) and $\tau_n$ (predicted burst), with $\alpha$ in $[0, 1]$ controlling weight on recent history.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

CPU scheduling theory underpins the design of:

- **Real-time embedded systems** (e.g., automotive ECUs, avionics flight control) where RMS and EDF schedulers guarantee hard deadlines.
- **Cloud data centers** (e.g., Google Borg, Kubernetes) where multi-level feedback queue variants schedule thousands of containers.
- **Mobile operating systems** (Android/Linux CFS, iOS) where UI responsiveness depends on latency-aware schedulers.
- **High-Performance Computing** clusters where fair-share and gang scheduling parallelize scientific workloads.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: FCFS Scheduling (Non-Preemptive)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
Consider the following set of processes with their arrival and burst times. Compute the Average Waiting Time (AWT) and Average Turnaround Time (ATT) using **FCFS** scheduling.

| Process | Arrival Time $A_i$ | Burst Time $B_i$ |
|---------|--------------------|------------------|
| P1 | 0 | 4 |
| P2 | 1 | 3 |
| P3 | 2 | 2 |
| P4 | 3 | 1 |

### Step 1: Order Processes by Arrival Time
Processes are queued in the order they arrive: P1, P2, P3, P4.

### Step 2: Construct the Gantt Chart
We allocate CPU time sequentially. Each process runs to completion (non-preemptive).

The Gantt chart representation is:

```
| P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 |
0    4    7    9    10
```

### Step 3: Tabulate Completion Times $C_i$

| Process | Arrival $A_i$ | Burst $B_i$ | Completion $C_i$ |
|---------|---------------|-------------|------------------|
| P1 | 0 | 4 | 4 |
| P2 | 1 | 3 | 7 |
| P3 | 2 | 2 | 9 |
| P4 | 3 | 1 | 10 |

### Step 4: Compute Turnaround Time $T_i = C_i - A_i$

For P1:

$$T_1 = C_1 - A_1 = 4 - 0 = 4$$

For P2:

$$T_2 = C_2 - A_2 = 7 - 1 = 6$$

For P3:

$$T_3 = C_3 - A_3 = 9 - 2 = 7$$

For P4:

$$T_4 = C_4 - A_4 = 10 - 3 = 7$$

### Step 5: Compute Waiting Time $W_i = T_i - B_i$

For P1:

$$W_1 = T_1 - B_1 = 4 - 4 = 0$$

For P2:

$$W_2 = T_2 - B_2 = 6 - 3 = 3$$

For P3:

$$W_3 = T_3 - B_3 = 7 - 2 = 5$$

For P4:

$$W_4 = T_4 - B_4 = 7 - 1 = 6$$

### Step 6: Compute Averages

$$\overline{T} = \frac{T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4}{4} = \frac{4 + 6 + 7 + 7}{4} = \frac{24}{4} = 6 \text{ units}$$

$$\overline{W} = \frac{W_1 + W_2 + W_3 + W_4}{4} = \frac{0 + 3 + 5 + 6}{4} = \frac{14}{4} = 3.5 \text{ units}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result:** For FCFS, AWT = 3.5 units, ATT = 6 units.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: SJF (Non-Preemptive) Scheduling

**Problem:** Using the same process set, apply **Non-Preemptive SJF**.

### Step 1: Identify Scheduling Order
At $t = 0$, only P1 is available (it has the only ready process), so P1 runs to completion. At $t = 4$, the ready queue contains P2, P3, P4. The shortest burst is P4 (1 unit), so P4 runs next. At $t = 5$, P3 (2 units) is shorter than P2 (3 units), so P3 runs, then P2.

### Step 2: Gantt Chart

```
| P1 | P4 | P3 | P2 |
0    4    5    7    10
```

### Step 3: Tabulation

| Process | $A_i$ | $B_i$ | $C_i$ | $T_i$ | $W_i$ |
|---------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| P1 | 0 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 0 |
| P2 | 1 | 3 | 10 | 9 | 6 |
| P3 | 2 | 2 | 7 | 5 | 3 |
| P4 | 3 | 1 | 5 | 2 | 1 |

### Step 4: Averages

$$\overline{T} = \frac{4 + 9 + 5 + 2}{4} = \frac{20}{4} = 5 \text{ units}$$

$$\overline{W} = \frac{0 + 6 + 3 + 1}{4} = \frac{10}{4} = 2.5 \text{ units}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Non-Preemptive SJF improved the AWT from 3.5 to 2.5 units. This empirically confirms SJF's **theoretical optimality** for minimizing average waiting time.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: SRTF (Preemptive SJF) Scheduling

**Problem:** Apply **SRTF (Shortest Remaining Time First)** to the same process set.

### Step 1: Continuously Re-evaluate the Ready Queue
- At $t = 0$: Only P1 ready. P1 starts. Remaining of P1 = 4.
- At $t = 1$: P2 arrives (burst 3). P1 remaining = 3. Since 3 < 3 (tie), conventionally P1 continues. P1 remaining = 3 → 2.
- At $t = 2$: P3 arrives (burst 2). P1 remaining = 2. Tie. P1 continues. P1 remaining = 2 → 1.
- At $t = 3$: P4 arrives (burst 1). P1 remaining = 1. Tie. P1 continues. P1 remaining = 1 → 0. **P1 finishes at $t = 4$.**
- At $t = 4$: Ready queue has P2 (remaining 3), P3 (remaining 2), P4 (remaining 1). Shortest is **P4**. P4 runs to completion at $t = 5$.
- At $t = 5$: Ready: P2 (3), P3 (2). Shortest is **P3**. P3 runs to $t = 7$.
- At $t = 7$: P2 runs to $t = 10$.

### Step 2: Gantt Chart

```
| P1 | P4 | P3 | P2 |
0    4    5    7    10
```

> [!NOTE]
> In this specific case, SRTF produces the same result as Non-Preemptive SJF. This happens because P1 was already the shortest job among all arrivals at the start. To see the real power of SRTF, we need a case where a shorter job arrives *while* a long one is running.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Round Robin (Quantum = 2)

**Problem:** Same process set, **Time Quantum $q = 2$**.

### Step 1: Maintain Ready Queue
- $t = 0$ to $t = 2$: P1 runs (remaining 2). Ready queue at $t = 2$: [P2, P3, P4, P1].
- $t = 2$ to $t = 4$: P2 runs (remaining 1). Ready queue at $t = 4$: [P3, P4, P1, P2].
- $t = 4$ to $t = 6$: P3 runs (remaining 0). **P3 finishes at $t = 6$.** Ready queue: [P4, P1, P2].
- $t = 6$ to $t = 7$: P4 runs for 1 unit (its remaining is 1, less than quantum). **P4 finishes at $t = 7$.** Ready queue: [P1, P2].
- $t = 7$ to $t = 8$: P1 runs (remaining 1). **P1 finishes at $t = 8$.** Ready queue: [P2].
- $t = 8$ to $t = 9$: P2 runs (remaining 0). **P2 finishes at $t = 9$.**

### Step 2: Gantt Chart

```
| P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 | P1 | P2 |
0    2    4    6    7    8    9
```

### Step 3: Tabulation

| Process | $A_i$ | $B_i$ | $C_i$ | $T_i$ | $W_i$ |
|---------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| P1 | 0 | 4 | 8 | 8 | 4 |
| P2 | 1 | 3 | 9 | 8 | 5 |
| P3 | 2 | 2 | 6 | 4 | 2 |
| P4 | 3 | 1 | 7 | 4 | 3 |

### Step 4: Averages

$$\overline{T} = \frac{8 + 8 + 4 + 4}{4} = \frac{24}{4} = 6 \text{ units}$$

$$\overline{W} = \frac{4 + 5 + 2 + 3}{4} = \frac{14}{4} = 3.5 \text{ units}$$

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive Comparative Analysis Matrix

This table consolidates results across all four algorithms for **the same process set** — a frequent KTU Part B (14-mark) question pattern.

| Algorithm | Preemptive? | AWT | ATT | Starvation Risk | Convoy Effect |
|-----------|-------------|-----|-----|-----------------|----------------|
| FCFS | No | 3.5 | 6 | No | Yes (high) |
| Non-Preemptive SJF | No | 2.5 | 5 | Yes (long jobs) | No |
| SRTF | Yes | 2.5 | 5 | Yes (long jobs) | No |
| Round Robin (q=2) | Yes | 3.5 | 6 | No | No |

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "compare and evaluate" scheduling algorithms, always present the comparison in a **single consolidated table** with metrics on the rows and algorithms on the columns. Examiners award full marks for clear tabular presentation.

## 3.6 Analytical Note on Optimality

**Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** *SJF is provably optimal with respect to minimizing average waiting time among all non-preemptive scheduling algorithms.*

**Proof Sketch:** The optimality of SJF can be shown by an **exchange argument**. Consider two adjacent processes P_i and P_j in a schedule, where $B_i > B_j$ but P_i runs before P_j. The combined waiting time of the pair is:

$$W_{i,j} = B_i + (B_i + B_j) = 2B_i + B_j$$

If we swap them, the new combined waiting time becomes:

$$W_{j,i} = B_j + (B_j + B_i) = 2B_j + B_i$$

The reduction in waiting time is:

$$\Delta W = W_{i,j} - W_{j,i} = (2B_i + B_j) - (2B_j + B_i) = B_i - B_j > 0$$

Since $B_i > B_j$, we have $\Delta W > 0$, meaning swapping reduces total waiting time. Thus any schedule that is not in non-decreasing order of burst times is suboptimal. Hence SJF (which orders by shortest burst) is optimal.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Hierarchy of OS Schedulers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Processes in Disk] --> B{Long-Term Scheduler}
    B -->|Admit| C[Ready Queue]
    C --> D{Short-Term Scheduler}
    D -->|Dispatch| E[CPU]
    E --> F{Burst Complete or I/O}
    F -->|I/O Request| G[Waiting Queue]
    F -->|Terminate| H[Exit]
    G -->|I/O Complete| C
    I[Medium-Term Scheduler] -->|Swap Out| J[Suspended Queue on Disk]
    C -->|High Memory Pressure| I
    J -->|Swap In| C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The Long-Term Scheduler gates admission from disk to the Ready Queue. The Short-Term Scheduler picks the next running process. The Medium-Term Scheduler is invoked under memory pressure to swap out entire processes.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Scheduling Algorithm Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Scheduling Algorithms] --> B[Non-Preemptive]
    A --> C[Preemptive]
    B --> D[FCFS]
    B --> E[Non-Preemptive SJF]
    B --> F[Non-Preemptive Priority]
    C --> G[Preemptive SJF aka SRTF]
    C --> H[Preemptive Priority]
    C --> I[Round Robin]
    C --> J[Multilevel Queue]
    C --> K[Multilevel Feedback Queue]
    D --> D1[FIFO queue, no preemption]
    E --> E1[Shortest burst first, no preemption]
    F --> F1[Highest priority first, no preemption]
    G --> G1[Shortest remaining time, preempt on arrival]
    H --> H1[Highest priority, preempt on arrival]
    I --> I1[Time quantum based cyclic dispatch]
    J --> J1[Multiple queues by process type]
    K --> K1[Processes can move between queues]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Decision Flow of the Short-Term Scheduler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Becomes Idle] --> B{Are there processes in the Ready Queue?}
    B -->|No| C[CPU Idle, wait for I/O completion]
    B -->|Yes| D{Is preemption enabled in the system?}
    D -->|No| E[Select first process in FCFS order]
    D -->|Yes| F{Is current process quantum expired or higher priority process arrived?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Preempt current process, push back to queue]
    F -->|No| H[Continue current process]
    G --> I[Apply selection policy SJF, Priority, or RR]
    E --> I
    I --> J[Dispatch selected process to CPU]
    H --> J
    J --> K[Process executes on CPU]
    K --> A
```
