goal = minimal | Reduces contention | | Busy Wait / Spinlock | $while$ loop polling | Wastes CPU; non-blocking primitives preferred | | Atomic Load–Store | Required assumption | Peterson's correctness depends on this | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Multicore CPU Programming:** Underlying principle for `pthread_mutex`, `std::mutex` (C++), `synchronized` blocks (Java). - **Real-Time OS (RTOS):** Critical sections guard peripheral access registers, DMA descriptors. - **Database Engines:** Lock managers and MVCC implement mutual exclusion at the row/table level. - **Linux Kernel:** Spinlocks (used in interrupt context) follow Peterson's two-flag idea, generalized to N CPUs. - **Embedded Firmware:** Bare-metal drivers on ARM Cortex-M use Peterson-style software flags when no hardware mutex is available. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Proofs & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — Peterson's Solution Execution Let $P_0$ and $P_1$ be two concurrent processes. The shared variables are: - $flag[0] = flag[1] = FALSE$ initially - $turn = 0$ initially ### 3.1.1 Case 1 — Both processes reach Entry Section simultaneously **Step 1:** $P_0$ executes `flag[0] = TRUE`. **Step 2:** $P_0$ executes `turn = 1`. *(If $P_0$ stops here, $turn = 1$.)* **Step 3:** $P_1$ executes `flag[1] = TRUE`. **Step 4:** $P_1$ executes `turn = 0`. *(Last write wins.)* **Step 5:** $P_1$ evaluates `while(flag[0] == TRUE && turn == 0)`. Now $flag[0] = TRUE$ and $turn = 0$, so condition is true → $P_1$ **spins**. **Step 6:** $P_0$ evaluates `while(flag[1] == TRUE && turn == 1)`. Now $flag[1] = TRUE$ but $turn = 0$ (not 1), so condition is false → $P_0$ **enters critical section**. ✓ **Step 7:** $P_0$ exits → `flag[0] = FALSE`. $P_1$ re-evaluates loop → `flag[0]` is now FALSE → $P_1$ enters. ✓ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** Despite both processes wanting in, exactly one enters. The `turn` assignment acts as a **last-resort tie-breaker**. ### 3.1.2 Case 2 — $P_0$ finishes CS, $P_1$ was waiting **Step 1:** $P_0$ is in CS; $P_1$ is spinning on `flag[0] == TRUE && turn == 0`. **Step 2:** $P_0$ executes `flag[0] = FALSE` (Exit Section). **Step 3:** $P_1$'s `while` condition becomes `flag[0] == FALSE && turn == 0` → evaluates to `false` → $P_1$ enters. ✓ **Step 4:** $P_1$ may later set `flag[1] = TRUE`, set `turn = 0`, etc. ## 3.2 Formal Proof of Mutual Exclusion > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theorem:** Peterson's Solution guarantees Mutual Exclusion between $P_0$ and $P_1$. ### Proof (By Contradiction): Assume, for contradiction, that **both $P_0$ and $P_1$ are in their critical sections simultaneously**. For $P_0$ to be in CS, its `while` condition must have been false: $$flag[1] == FALSE \quad \lor \quad turn \neq 1$$ For $P_1$ to be in CS, its `while` condition must have been false: $$flag[0] == FALSE \quad \lor \quad turn \neq 0$$ Now combine: - $P_0$ is in CS $\Rightarrow flag[0] = TRUE$ (set earlier in entry). - $P_1$ is in CS $\Rightarrow flag[1] = TRUE$ (set earlier in entry). Substituting $flag[0] = TRUE$ into $P_1$'s exit condition: $$TRUE == FALSE \quad \lor \quad turn \neq 0$$ $$\Rightarrow turn \neq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad turn = 1$$ Substituting $flag[1] = TRUE$ into $P_0$'s exit condition: $$TRUE == FALSE \quad \lor \quad turn \neq 1$$ $$\Rightarrow turn \neq 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad turn = 0$$ **Contradiction!** We derived $turn = 1$ and $turn = 0$ simultaneously. Therefore, both processes cannot be in CS at the same time. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.3 Formal Proof of Bounded Waiting > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theorem:** Once $P_i$ has set $flag[i] = TRUE$, $P_j$ can enter the critical section **at most once** before $P_i$ enters. ### Proof Sketch: - When $P_i$ sets `flag[i] = TRUE` and then `turn = j`, it deliberately yields. - If $P_j$ subsequently enters CS, it must have observed $turn = j$ as the *last* value (last-write-wins on `turn`). - $P_j$ on exit sets `flag[j] = FALSE`. - $P_i$, still spinning, re-evaluates: now `flag[j] = FALSE`, so the condition fails and $P_i$ enters next. - **At most one entry by $P_j$ between consecutive entries of $P_i$.** ✓ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Scenario Outcomes | Scenario | $flag[0]$ | $flag[1]$ | $turn$ | $P_0$ Status | $P_1$ Status | |----------|-----------|-----------|--------|--------------|--------------| | Initial | F | F | 0 | Remainder | Remainder | | $P_0$ wants in, $P_1$ idle | T | F | 0 or 1 | Enters | Spins if flag set | | $P_1$ wants in, $P_0$ idle | F | T | 0 or 1 | Spins if flag set | Enters | | Both want in, $P_0$ last writes `turn` | T | T | 0 | Enters | Waits | | Both want in, $P_1$ last writes `turn` | T | T | 1 | Waits | Enters | | $P_0$ in CS, $P_1$ arrives | T | T | 0 or 1 | In CS | Waits (turn != 1) | | $P_0$ exits, $P_1$ waiting | F | T | 0 or 1 | Remainder | Enters next | ## 3.5 Limitations of Peterson's Solution | # | Limitation | Engineering Implication | |---|------------|--------------------------| | 1 | Restricted to **2 processes** | Modern apps need N-process locking | | 2 | Requires **atomic load/store** | Modern CPUs reorder memory accesses; need **memory barriers (`mfence`, `dmb`)** | | 3 | **Busy-waiting** wastes CPU cycles | Spinlocks unsuitable for long CS; use blocking primitives (semaphores) | | 4 | Vulnerable to **compiler reordering** of flag/turn writes | Use `volatile` / atomic intrinsics in C/C++ | | 5 | Not suitable for **preemptive kernels** with interrupt-driven I/O | OS uses spinlocks with interrupt disable | ## 3.6 Memory Ordering Issue (Advanced Insight for KTU) Modern CPUs and compilers may **reorder** independent loads/stores. Without a memory fence: $$P_0: \text{flag[0]} = \text{TRUE}; \text{turn} = 1;$$ A compiler could reorder this to: $$P_0: \text{turn} = 1; \text{flag[0]} = \text{TRUE};$$ Now $P_1$ might read $flag[0] = FALSE$ *and* $turn = 1$, conclude that $P_0$ doesn't want in, and enter — **violating mutual exclusion**. > [!NOTE] > **Fix:** Use a **memory barrier** (e.g., `__sync_synchronize()` in GCC, `MemoryBarrier()` in Windows) between the two writes, or use C11 `_Atomic` with `seq_cst` ordering. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 General Process Structure with Critical Section ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start / Remainder Section] --> B{Entry Section
Request access} B --> C[Critical Section
Access shared data] C --> D[Exit Section
Release access] D --> E[Remainder Section] E --> F{Need CS again?} F -->|Yes| B F -->|No| G[Terminate] style A fill:#e3f2fd style B fill:#fff3e0 style C fill:#ffcdd2 style D fill:#c8e6c9 style E fill:#e3f2fd style F fill:#f5f5f5 style G fill:#eeeeee ``` ## 4.2 Race Condition Timeline (Lost Update Visualization) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant P0 participant P1 participant MEM as Shared Memory
count = 5 P0->>MEM: READ count MEM-->>P0: 5 P0->>P0: Compute 5 + 1 = 6 (reg) P1->>MEM: READ count MEM-->>P1: 5 P1->>P1: Compute 5 + 1 = 6 (reg) P0->>MEM: WRITE 6 Note over MEM: count = 6 P1->>MEM: WRITE 6 Note over MEM: count = 6 (Lost Update!) Note over P0,P1: Expected 7, Got 6 ``` ## 4.3 Peterson's Solution — State Machine for $P_0$ ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Remainder Remainder --> EntryWant: flag[0] = TRUE EntryWant --> YieldTurn: turn = 1 YieldTurn --> CheckCond{flag[1] == TRUE
AND
turn == 1 ?} CheckCond -->|Yes| YieldTurn: Spin / busy wait CheckCond -->|No| CriticalSection CriticalSection --> ExitRelease: flag[0] = FALSE ExitRelease --> Remainder Remainder --> [*] style CriticalSection fill:#ffcdd2 style Remainder fill:#c8e6c9 style YieldTurn fill:#fff59d ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Peterson's Coordination Engine ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SHARED[Shared Memory Region] F0[flag 0] F1[flag 1] TN[turn] end subgraph P0[Process P0] E0[Entry Section] CS0[Critical Section] X0[Exit Section] R0[Remainder] end subgraph P1[Process P1] E1[Entry Section] CS1[Critical Section] X1[Exit Section] R1[Remainder] end R0 --> E0 E0 -->|Set flag 0 TRUE| F0 E0 -->|Set turn 1| TN E0 -->|Test flag 1 AND turn 1| F1 F1 -->|Both true| E0 F1 -->|Otherwise| CS0 CS0 --> X0 X0 -->|Clear flag 0| F0 X0 --> R0 R1 --> E1 E1 -->|Set flag 1 TRUE| F1 E1 -->|Set turn 0| TN E1 -->|Test flag 0 AND turn 0| F0 F0 -->|Both true| E1 F0 -->|Otherwise| CS1 CS1 --> X1 X1 -->|Clear flag 1| F1 X1 --> R1 style CS0 fill:#ffcdd2 style CS1 fill:#ffcdd2 style F0 fill:#fff59d style F1 fill:#fff59d style TN fill:#fff59d ``` ## 4.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Synchronization Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{How many
processes?} Q1 -->|Exactly 2| Q2{Need OS
support?} Q1 -->|More than 2| Q3{Atomic HW
available?} Q2 -->|No| A1[Use Peterson's
Software Solution] Q2 -->|Yes| A2[Use Mutex / Semaphore] Q3 -->|Yes| A3[Use Hardware
TSL / CAS Spinlock] Q3 -->|No| A4[Use OS Mutex
or Message Passing] A1 --> Z[Tradeoff:
Educational, fragile] A2 --> Z A3 --> Z A4 --> Z style A1 fill:#bbdefb style A2 fill:#c8e6c9 style A3 fill:#fff9c4 style A4 fill:#f8bbd0 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Race Condition. Give a suitable example.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **race condition** is a situation in concurrent programming where the final outcome of a computation depends on the **non-deterministic order** in which multiple processes access and manipulate a shared resource. At least one of these accesses is a write, and the operations are **not synchronized**. **Example:** Consider two processes $P_1$ and $P_2$ both incrementing a shared integer variable `count` (initially 5) using the three-step sequence: `READ → INCREMENT → WRITE`. - $P_1$ reads `count = 5`, computes 6, but before writing, $P_2$ reads `count = 5`, computes 6, and writes 6. - Then $P_1$ writes 6. The final value of `count` is 6 instead of the expected 7. - This lost update is a race condition; the value "races" against the timing of execution. **Marking Tip (1 mark each):** Definition + Condition + Example. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] List and briefly explain the three requirements that a solution to the Critical Section Problem must satisfy.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **Mutual Exclusion (Safety):** If a process $P_i$ is executing in its critical section, then no other process $P_j$ can be executing in its critical section at the same time. This prevents inconsistent state. 2. **Progress (Liveness):** If no process is in the critical section and there exist some processes that wish to enter, then only those processes not in their remainder section get to participate in deciding which one will enter next, and this selection cannot be postponed indefinitely. 3. **Bounded Waiting (Fairness):** There exists a bound on the number of times other processes can enter their critical sections after a process has indicated its desire to enter and before that process itself enters. This guarantees no starvation. **Marking Tip:** 1 mark per requirement with a one-line technical justification. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. The student answers either **Question A** OR **Question B** (full 14 marks). ### ⭐ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** #### **Q.A.(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper]** **Explain Peterson's Solution for the Critical Section Problem. Provide the algorithm and verify that it satisfies Mutual Exclusion.** **Model Answer:** **1. Introduction (1 Mark):** Peterson's Solution, proposed by Gary L. Peterson in 1981, is a software algorithm that solves the Critical Section Problem for **two processes** $P_0$ and $P_1$ using two shared variables — a `flag[2]` array and a `turn` variable — without any hardware atomic instructions beyond basic load/store. **2. Shared Variables (1 Mark):** - `int turn;` — initially 0; indicates whose turn it is. - `boolean flag[2];` — initially `false`; `flag[i] = TRUE` means $P_i$ is ready to enter CS. **3. Algorithm for Process $P_i$ (2 Marks):** ``` do { flag[i] = TRUE; turn = j; while (flag[j] == TRUE && turn == j) ; // busy wait CRITICAL SECTION // ... shared data access ... flag[i] = FALSE; // exit section REMAINDER SECTION } while (TRUE); ``` where $j = 1 - i$ denotes the *other* process. **4. Working Explanation (1 Mark):** - $P_i$ announces intent via `flag[i] = TRUE`. - $P_i$ politely gives the turn to $P_j$ via `turn = j`. - $P_i$ spins **only if** $P_j$ also wants in *and* the turn belongs to $P_j$ (`turn == j`). - The last write to `turn` wins, ensuring that in case of contention, exactly one of the two processes proceeds. **5. Mutual Exclusion Proof (2 Marks):** Assume, for contradiction, both $P_0$ and $P_1$ are in CS simultaneously. Then: - $P_0$ in CS $\Rightarrow flag[1] == FALSE$ OR $turn \neq 1$. But $P_1$ is in CS $\Rightarrow flag[1] = TRUE$. So $turn \neq 1 \Rightarrow turn = 0$. - $P_1$ in CS $\Rightarrow flag[0] == FALSE$ OR $turn \neq 0$. But $P_0$ is in CS $\Rightarrow flag[0] = TRUE$. So $turn \neq 0 \Rightarrow turn = 1$. This gives $turn = 0$ **and** $turn = 1$ — a contradiction. Hence mutual exclusion holds. $\blacksquare$ --- #### **Q.A.(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **Differentiate between Peterson's Solution and a hardware-based solution (Test-and-Set). Discuss the limitations of Peterson's Solution in modern systems.** **Model Answer:** **1. Comparison Table (3 Marks):** | Parameter | Peterson's Solution | Test-and-Set (TSL) | |-----------|---------------------|---------------------| | Type | Software | Hardware-assisted | | Atomic primitive required | No (just load/store) | Yes (`TSL` instruction) | | Applicable to | **Only 2 processes** | N processes | | Memory barriers needed | Yes, to prevent reordering | Inherent atomicity | | Busy-waiting | Yes | Yes (spinlock) | | Portability | High (portable C code) | Architecture-specific | | Performance | Slower (multiple checks) | Faster (single atomic op) | | Starvation prevention | Yes (bounded waiting) | Depends on queueing discipline | **2. Limitations of Peterson's Solution in Modern Systems (3 Marks):** - **Two-Process Restriction:** Cannot be directly scaled to N processes without significant rework (Bakery's algorithm is one extension, but is more complex). - **Memory Reordering Hazard:** Modern CPUs and optimizing compilers may reorder the two writes (`flag[i] = TRUE` and `turn = j`), breaking correctness. Must use `volatile`, `atomic` qualifiers, or memory barriers. - **Cache Coherence Overhead:** On multicore systems, `flag` and `turn` are in different cache lines; each update triggers inter-core cache invalidation, reducing scalability. - **Busy-Waiting Wastes CPU:** In long CS, spin-waiting burns CPU cycles that could run other processes. Blocking primitives (semaphores) are preferred. - **Not Suitable for Interrupt Context:** Cannot atomically disable interrupts in user space; OS-level primitives are needed. - **Vulnerability to Spectre/Meltdown-Style Attacks:** Timing of memory accesses can leak information; modern kernels use `seqlock` or RCU instead. **3. Conclusion (1 Mark):** Peterson's solution is pedagogically invaluable for understanding mutual exclusion but has been superseded in production systems by hardware atomic primitives (CAS, TSL) wrapped in OS-level constructs (spinlocks, mutexes, semaphores, monitors). --- ### ⭐ **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### **Q.B.(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2021 / 2024 Model]** **What is a Race Condition? With a neat diagram, explain how it leads to inconsistent data. How does the Critical Section Problem address it?** **Model Answer:** **1. Definition of Race Condition (1 Mark):** A race condition is a flaw that occurs when the timing or interleaving of multiple concurrent processes leads to unexpected, non-deterministic outcomes while accessing shared data. **2. Diagram — Inconsistent Data Scenario (2 Marks):** ``` Shared Variable: balance = 500 Threads T1 and T2 both execute: balance = balance + 100 T1: READ balance → 500 T2: READ balance → 500 T1: ADD 100 → 600 (in T1 register) T2: ADD 100 → 600 (in T2 register) T1: WRITE 600 to memory T2: WRITE 600 to memory Final balance = 600 ❌ (Expected 700) ``` **3. How Race Conditions Cause Inconsistency (2 Marks):** - The READ–MODIFY–WRITE sequence is **not atomic**. - The lost update occurs because T1's WRITE happens *between* T2's READ and T2's WRITE, or T2's WRITE overwrites T1's update. - The shared variable's final state is unpredictable and depends on the relative speed of execution. **4. How Critical Section Problem Solves It (2 Marks):** - The Critical Section Problem requires that the entire `READ – MODIFY – WRITE` sequence be wrapped in a **mutual-exclusion-protected region** (the critical section). - Using flags/locks (Peterson's), hardware atomics, or OS-level mutexes, only **one process** can execute the CS at a time. - This makes the compound operation appear **atomic** to other processes, eliminating the race. - The CS solution also enforces **progress** (no deadlock) and **bounded waiting** (no starvation). --- #### **Q.B.(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – June 2022 / 2024 Model]** **Explain in detail the three requirements of the Critical Section Problem. State whether Peterson's Solution satisfies all three. Justify with reasoning.** **Model Answer:** **1. The Three Requirements (4 Marks):** **(i) Mutual Exclusion:** $$\forall i \neq j : \neg (P_i \in CS \land P_j \in CS)$$ At any instant, at most one process is allowed to be inside its critical section. This is a **safety property** — violation leads to data corruption. **(ii) Progress:** If no process is in the CS and some processes wish to enter, the selection of the next entrant must be made only by those processes that are not currently in their remainder section. This is a **liveness property** — prevents deadlock where processes wait forever even though the CS is free. **(iii) Bounded Waiting:** There must exist a finite upper bound $B$ on the number of times other processes can enter the CS after a process $P_i$ has declared its intention and before $P_i$ gets to enter. This is a **fairness property** — prevents indefinite starvation. **2. Does Peterson's Solution Satisfy All Three? (3 Marks):** | Requirement | Satisfied? | Justification | |-------------|------------|---------------| | **Mutual Exclusion** | ✅ Yes | Proven by contradiction (see Section 3.2) — both cannot be in CS at the same time as it would require `turn` to equal both 0 and 1. | | **Progress** | ✅ Yes | If $P_j$ is not interested, `flag[j] = FALSE`, so the `while` condition fails and $P_i$ proceeds without waiting. Selection is made by the *requesting* process itself. | | **Bounded Waiting** | ✅ Yes | When $P_i$ wants in, it sets `turn = j`. If $P_j$ enters first, $P_j$ on exit sets `flag[j] = FALSE`. $P_i$ re-evaluates and enters on its next check. So $P_j$ can enter **at most once** before $P_i$ enters. Bounded by 1 turn. | **Conclusion:** Peterson's Solution satisfies all three requirements of the Critical Section Problem, making it a **correct** two-process mutual-exclusion algorithm — albeit with practical limitations on modern hardware. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks** > > 1. **Skipping the `turn = j` line in Peterson's algorithm** (–2 marks): Students often write only `flag[i] = TRUE;` and forget the `turn` assignment. The `turn` is **essential** for bounded waiting — without it, mutual exclusion can fail when both processes set flags simultaneously. > 2. **Confusing "Progress" with "Bounded Waiting"** (–1 mark): These are *different* properties. Progress is about *no deadlock*; Bounded Waiting is about *no starvation*. Examiners test this distinction. > 3. **Writing code for N processes and calling it Peterson's** (–1 mark): Peterson's is *strictly* for 2 processes. For N processes, reference **Bakery's Algorithm** or **Lamport's Distributed Mutex**. > 4. **Forgetting memory ordering issues** (–1–2 marks): In modern questions, KTU may ask why Peterson's "doesn't work directly on modern CPUs" — answer must mention **memory reordering** and **memory barriers**. > 5. **No diagram in long answers** (–1 mark): A timeline / state diagram showing interleaving of $P_0$ and $P_1$ earns full visualization marks. > 6. **Incomplete proof of mutual exclusion** (–2 marks): A "proof" that just says "it works" is unacceptable. Use the formal contradiction argument. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density KTU Revision Bullets — Process Synchronization Module** - **Process Synchronization** = coordinating cooperating processes that share resources. - **Independent vs Cooperating Processes:** Sync matters only for cooperating ones. - **Race Condition** = outcome depends on interleaving; occurs with unsynchronized shared data access (≥1 write). - **Lost Update** is the *classic* race condition symptom (counter example). - **Critical Section** = code segment that accesses shared variables/resources. - **General Process Template:** Entry Section → CS → Exit Section → Remainder Section. - **Three CSP Requirements (in order):** Mutual Exclusion, Progress, Bounded Waiting. - **Mutual Exclusion** = safety (no overlap in CS). - **Progress** = liveness (no deadlock; CS not freezable). - **Bounded Waiting** = fairness (no starvation; bounded number of bypasses). - **Peterson's Solution** = software solution for **exactly 2 processes**. - **Two Shared Variables in Peterson's:** `flag[2]` (boolean) and `turn` (int 0 or 1). - **Algorithm steps for $P_i$:** set `flag[i]=TRUE`, set `turn=j`, wait while `flag[j] && turn==j`, CS, set `flag[i]=FALSE`. - **`turn = j` is the tie-breaker** — last writer wins; guarantees bounded waiting. - **Mutual Exclusion Proof** uses contradiction: derives $turn=0$ and $turn=1$ simultaneously. - **Bounded Waiting bound in Peterson's** = 1 (other process can enter at most once before you). - **Limitations:** 2-process limit, memory-reordering hazard, busy-wait waste, no interrupt atomicity, not scalable. - **Modern Fix for Reordering:** use `volatile`, `atomic`, memory fences (`mfence`, `dmb`). - **Hardware alternative:** `TSL` (Test-and-Set), `CAS` (Compare-And-Swap), `XCHG` — atomic and N-process capable. - **OS-level alternatives:** Mutex (binary semaphore), Counting Semaphore, Monitor, Condition Variables. - **Spinlock vs Blocking Lock:** Peterson's is a spinlock; semaphores block the process. - **Preemptive vs Non-Preemptive:** Peterson's works in both; assumes sequential memory consistency. - **Priority Inversion:** A higher-priority process waits for a lower-priority one — **not** solved by Peterson's; solved by **priority inheritance** in modern OS. - **Dekker's Algorithm** is the predecessor of Peterson's; both are 2-process software solutions. - **Bakery's Algorithm** is the N-process extension of Lamport — uses ticket-number ordering. - **Memory Model:** Peterson's requires **SC (Sequential Consistency)**; on x86 with TSO, you need `MFENCE` between writes. - **KTU Favourite Line:** "Peterson's solution is a **software-based** solution that satisfies **all three** requirements of CSP for **two processes** using **shared variables only**." - **Common exam trap:** "Peterson's solution works on all modern multiprocessor systems without modification" — **FALSE** (needs memory barriers). - **Exam trap #2:** "Progress means a process can always enter CS immediately" — **FALSE** (Progress only forbids idle processes blocking entry; a waiting process must still wait if another is in CS). - **Exam trap #3:** "Peterson's solution supports N processes" — **FALSE** (2-process only). - **Mnemonic for CSP requirements:** **M-P-B** → **M**utual e**X**clusion, **P**rogress, **B**ounded waiting. - **Mnemonic for Peterson's vars:** **F**lag for **F**eel (intent), **T**urn for **T**ie-breaker.

topics.title: Synchronization Hardware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Synchronization Hardware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Synchronization Hardware** refers to the set of **atomic machine-level instructions** and **memory-ordering primitives** provided by the processor architecture that allow the operating system to implement mutual exclusion and process coordination without relying purely on software-only solutions (such as Peterson's algorithm or Dekker's algorithm).

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus context, synchronization hardware is treated as the **first layer of defence** for the Critical Section Problem. It guarantees that a sequence of read-modify-write operations on a shared variable executes as a **single, indivisible (atomic)** unit. The key hardware primitives covered are:

- **Test-and-Set (TAS)**
- **Compare-and-Swap (CAS)**
- **Swap / Exchange**
- **Fetch-and-Add**
- **Load-Linked / Store-Conditional (LL/SC)**
- **Memory Barriers / Memory Fences**

Standard metrics associated with synchronization hardware in the syllabus include:

- **Atomics: 1 instruction = 1 logical step**
- **Memory Fence Latency: 10 – 200 CPU cycles** (architecturally dependent)
- **Bounded Waiting: O(n) attempts in worst case** for n contending processes

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Toilet Door Bolt" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a single-stall public restroom. The bolt on the door is a **physical Test-and-Set** mechanism. When you slide the bolt:
>
> 1. You **simultaneously check** if the door is unlocked (test).
> 2. You **simultaneously lock** it (set).
>
> No two people can ever see "unlocked" at the same time because the check-and-lock is a **single, inseparable atomic motion of the hand**. Software-only critical section solutions attempt to do this with two separate hand gestures ("check, then lock"), which the hardware interleaves — that is why software alone fails on modern multi-core CPUs. The hardware bolt guarantees atomicity the way your hand does.

### 1.3 Why Software Solutions Alone Are Insufficient

Peterson's algorithm and Dekker's algorithm were designed for **single-core processors** that assumed no instruction reordering. Modern multi-core CPUs perform:

- **Instruction-level parallelism**
- **Out-of-order execution**
- **Store buffering**

These optimizations break the *happens-before* guarantees that software algorithms rely on. Therefore, the **KTU 2024 Scheme** explicitly requires the study of **hardware-supported mutual exclusion** as a robust solution.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Fundamental Requirements (KTU Board Favourite)

Any solution — hardware or software — to the Critical Section Problem must satisfy:

1. **Mutual Exclusion** — At most one process is in the critical section at any instant.
2. **Progress** — If no process is in the CS and some processes *want* to enter, only those processes not in the remainder section decide who enters next, and this decision cannot be postponed indefinitely.
3. **Bounded Waiting** — There exists a bound on the number of times other processes are allowed to enter the CS after a process has requested entry and before its request is granted.

### 2.2 Operational Steps — Generic Atomic Instruction Pattern

A typical hardware atomic instruction follows the **RMW (Read-Modify-Write)** pattern:

- **Step 1: Read** the current value of the shared variable from memory into a CPU register.
- **Step 2: Modify** the value according to the operation (set, increment, swap).
- **Step 3: Write** the new value back to memory.
- **Step 4: Lock** the memory bus (or cache line) for the entire duration so that no other CPU core can interleave its own RMW.

Because the bus/cache-line is locked, the operation is **indivisible from the viewpoint of every other processor in the system**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises **every** hardware instruction, its semantics, and its typical use case. This is a **high-frequency board question zone**.

| # | Instruction | C-Style Signature | Atomic Operation | Typical Kernel Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Test-and-Set** | `boolean TestAndSet(boolean *target)` | `old = *target; *target = true; return old;` | Simple spinlock (mutex) |
| 2 | **Compare-and-Swap** | `T CompareAndSwap(T *ptr, T expected, T new)` | Atomically replace *ptr with *new if *ptr == expected, else no-op; always return the observed *ptr | Lock-free stacks/queues, bounded-wait mutex |
| 3 | **Swap / Exchange** | `void Swap(boolean *a, boolean *b)` | Atomically exchange *a and *b | Hardware-level spinlock construction |
| 4 | **Fetch-and-Add** | `int FetchAndAdd(int *ptr, int incr)` | `old = *ptr; *ptr = old + incr; return old;` | Ticket lock, fair scheduling |
| 5 | **Load-Linked** | `T LoadLinked(T *ptr)` | Read *ptr and mark cache line | Building block for LL/SC |
| 6 | **Store-Conditional** | `bool StoreConditional(T *ptr, T val)` | Store val to *ptr only if cache line is still marked; return success/fail | Wait-free algorithms (MIPS, ARM) |
| 7 | **Memory Barrier (Fence)** | `void mfence()` (x86) | Force all prior loads/stores to complete before any subsequent loads/stores | Ordering of lock releases / publishes |

**Logical Bounded-Waiting Bound (TAS-based, n processes):**

$$ \text{MaxWaiting} \le n - 1 $$

This means after a process Pi declares *waiting = true*, at most **n − 1** other processes can enter the critical section before Pi is granted access.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Synchronization hardware is the **backbone of every modern operating system kernel**:

- **Linux kernel**: `cmpxchg` (CAS), `xchg` (Swap), `lock` prefix, `smp_mb()` memory barriers.
- **Windows NT kernel**: Uses `InterlockedCompareExchange`, `InterlockedExchange` (Win32 APIs).
- **Java `AtomicInteger`**: Internally uses CAS via `sun.misc.Unsafe`.
- **Databases**: Lock-free hash maps (e.g., `ConcurrentHashMap`) rely entirely on CAS for scalability.
- **Real-time embedded systems**: Use hardware spinlocks in AUTOSAR OS to meet microsecond deadlines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Test-and-Set (TAS) Semantics

The hardware guarantees the following C-style pseudocode executes **atomically**:

```c
boolean TestAndSet(boolean *target) {
    boolean rv = *target;   // READ step
    *target = true;          // WRITE step
    return rv;               // return OLD value
}
```

**Transition Explanation:**

- **Line 1**: A snapshot of the current lock state is read into a temporary register `rv`.
- **Line 2**: The lock is unconditionally set to `true` (locked).
- **Line 3**: The **old** value of `*target` is returned. Returning the *old* value (not the new one) is the critical design decision — it tells the caller whether the lock was previously free.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "old value" matters:** If we returned the *new* value (`true` always), every caller would see the lock as taken, and no process could ever enter. Returning the *old* value is what makes TAS a valid **mutual-exclusion primitive**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Solving the Critical Section Problem with TAS (Software uses Hardware)

The KTU board repeatedly asks: *"Show how Test-and-Set solves the critical section problem."* Below is the full, step-by-step model answer.

**Shared Declaration (Global):**

```c
boolean lock = false;   // lock is initially FREE
```

**Process Pi (i = 0, 1, ..., n−1):**

```c
do {
    // --- ENTRY SECTION ---
    waiting[i] = true;          // Pi declares intent
    key = true;
    while (waiting[i] && key) {
        key = TestAndSet(&lock);
    }
    waiting[i] = false;

    // --- CRITICAL SECTION ---
    // Pi executes its critical work here.
    // ... shared data access ...

    // --- EXIT SECTION ---
    j = (i + 1) % n;
    while ((j != i) && !waiting[j]) {
        j = (j + 1) % n;
    }
    if (j == i) {
        lock = false;           // No one is waiting; release the lock.
    } else {
        waiting[j] = false;     // Hand the lock directly to process Pj.
    }

    // --- REMAINDER SECTION ---
    // Pi does non-critical work and eventually loops back.
} while (true);
```

#### 3.2.1 Logical Walkthrough — Why This Satisfies All Three Requirements

**Mutual Exclusion Proof:**

Assume two processes Pi and Pk both pass the `while` loop. For Pi to exit the loop, `key` must be `false`. `key` is set to `false` **only** by `TestAndSet` returning `false`, which means `lock` was `false` at the moment of the atomic TAS. For Pk to also exit, it must have read `lock == false` via its own TAS, but TAS is atomic, so this is impossible. Therefore, at most one process can hold the lock at any instant.

**Progress Proof:**

The exit section performs a **deterministic circular scan** (`j = (i+1) mod n, (i+2) mod n, ...`). The first waiting process found is granted the lock. This is non-blocking because `lock = false` is set only when no one is waiting.

**Bounded Waiting Proof:**

After Pi sets `waiting[i] = true` and exits, it scans circularly starting at `(i+1) mod n`. At most **n − 1** processes can be checked before the scan returns to i. Therefore, after Pi declares waiting, at most n − 1 other processes can enter before Pi.

### 3.3 Derivation: Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Semantics

```c
T CompareAndSwap(T *ptr, T expected, T new) {
    T actual = *ptr;          // READ
    if (actual == expected) {
        *ptr = new;           // conditional WRITE
    }
    return actual;            // always return observed value
}
```

**Transition Explanation:**

- **Line 1**: Read the current value at the memory location.
- **Line 2–4**: Only if the value matches `expected`, perform the swap. This check-and-swap is the indivisible unit.
- **Line 5**: Return the **actual** value seen, so the caller can decide whether the swap succeeded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAS does not always succeed.** That is its key feature — it tells the *caller* whether the swap happened. This makes it the foundation of **lock-free programming**.

### 3.4 Derivation: Bounded-Waiting Mutex with CAS

The KTU syllabus requires a CAS-based solution that achieves all three requirements:

**Shared Declarations:**

```c
int lock = 0;   // 0 = free, 1 = busy
```

**Process Pi:**

```c
do {
    // --- ENTRY SECTION ---
    waiting[i] = true;
    int key = 1;
    while (waiting[i] && key == 1) {
        key = CompareAndSwap(&lock, 0, 1);
    }
    waiting[i] = false;

    // --- CRITICAL SECTION ---
    // ... critical work ...

    // --- EXIT SECTION ---
    int j = (i + 1) % n;
    while ((j != i) && !waiting[j]) {
        j = (j + 1) % n;
    }
    if (j == i) {
        lock = 0;                       // CompareAndSwap(lock, 1, 0) is also valid
    } else {
        waiting[j] = false;             // Hand off to Pj
    }

    // --- REMAINDER SECTION ---
} while (true);
```

**Bounded Waiting Verification (algebraic):**

After Pi sets `waiting[i] = true`, the exit-section circular scan of the *previous* process traverses indices $(i, i+1, \ldots, n-1, 0, \ldots, i-1)$. The number of *distinct* indices visited is exactly $n$, but Pi is the *last* in the scan order, so at most $n - 1$ other processes can be granted the lock before Pi.

Formally, the worst-case starvation bound is:

$$ B_{\text{wait}}^{\max} = n - 1 $$

where $n$ is the total number of contending processes.

### 3.5 Derivation: Memory Barriers (Memory Fences)

Modern CPUs reorder loads and stores for performance. Without fences, a process can observe stale data even after acquiring a lock. The four canonical fence orders are summarised algebraically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Store Barrier (sfence)} & : \forall s \in \text{prior}, \forall t \in \text{subsequent stores}, s \prec t \\
\text{Load Barrier (lfence)} & : \forall s \in \text{prior loads}, \forall t \in \text{subsequent}, s \prec t \\
\text{Full Barrier (mfence)} & : \forall s, t \text{ of any type}, s \prec t \\
\text{Acquire-Release (ARM)} & : \text{rcpc/rcsc pairing across lock-unlock}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Transition Explanation:**

- The $\prec$ symbol means "happens before" in the C++ memory model.
- **Store barrier** ensures that all writes before the fence are visible globally before any write after the fence.
- **Load barrier** ensures that all reads before the fence complete before any read after.
- **Full barrier** combines both — strongest guarantee, used in `mutex_unlock()`.
- **Acquire-Release** is the lighter model used on ARM/POWER; `acquire` on lock, `release` on unlock.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises that **memory barriers are not optional**. Without them, even a perfectly correct TAS/CAS implementation can produce stale reads on weak-memory-order architectures (ARM, POWER).

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Hardware Primitives vs. Requirements

| Requirement | TAS (naive) | TAS + waiting[] | CAS (naive) | CAS + waiting[] | LL/SC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mutual Exclusion** | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| **Progress** | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| **Bounded Waiting** | ✘ | ✔ | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ |
| **Starvation-Free** | ✘ | ✔ | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ |
| **Fairness** | ✘ | FIFO-ish | ✘ | FIFO-ish | Implementation-defined |
| **Hardware Cost** | 1 atomic instr | 1 atomic instr | 1 atomic instr | 1 atomic instr | 2 atomic instrs |

The matrix above should be **memorised** — it is a guaranteed sub-part of a 14-mark KTU ESE question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Hardware-Synchronised Mutual Exclusion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Pi Starts] --> B[Set waiting i to true]
    B --> C[Call Hardware Atomic Instruction TAS or CAS]
    C --> D{Atomic Operation Succeeds}
    D -- Yes, key returned 0 / matches expected --> E[Enter Critical Section]
    E --> F[Execute Shared Resource Access]
    F --> G[Exit Section Begins]
    G --> H[Circular Scan for Next Waiting Process j]
    H --> I{Is there a waiting process j}
    I -- Yes --> J[Hand off waiting j to false]
    I -- No --> K[Release Lock lock equals 0 or false]
    J --> L[Remainder Section]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Loop back to Process Start]

    C -.->|No, key returned 1 / mismatch| N[Bounded Spin Wait]
    N --> C

    subgraph CPU_HW[CPU Hardware Atomic Unit]
        C
    end

    subgraph SHARED_MEM[Shared Memory]
        O[Boolean lock variable]
        P[Boolean array waiting n]
    end
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**

- The **Process Pi** block follows the standard KTU mutual-exclusion template.
- The **Hardware Atomic Unit** is the new component introduced by Synchronization Hardware — it guarantees the RMW (Read-Modify-Write) is indivisible.
- The **dashed return arrow** from `N (Bounded Spin Wait)` back to `C` shows that failure leads to *bounded* re-attempts, which is the key difference between naive spinlocks and starvation-free solutions.
- The **Shared Memory** subgraph highlights the two shared structures: a single lock variable and a boolean waiting array.

### 4.2 Memory Barrier Ordering Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Core1 as CPU Core 1 (Writer)
    participant Mem as Main Memory
    participant Core2 as CPU Core 2 (Reader)

    Note over Core1: Store data = 42
    Core1 ->> Mem: Store data = 42
    Core1 ->> Mem: Store ready = 1
    Note over Core1: MFENCE sfence issued here
    Mem -->> Core1: Both writes globally visible

    Note over Core2: Load ready
    Core2 ->> Mem: Load ready
    Note over Core2: LFENCE lfence issued here
    Mem -->> Core2: ready = 1
    Core2 ->> Mem: Load data
    Mem -->> Core2: data = 42 (guaranteed non-stale)
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**

- **Core1** performs a `sfence`/`mfence` between writing the *data* and the *ready* flag. This guarantees the flag is published only **after** the data is globally visible.
- **Core2** performs an `lfence`/`mfence` after reading the *ready* flag. This forces the subsequent *data* read to occur **after** the flag is observed, preventing stale reads.
- Without these fences, modern out-of-order CPUs could let Core2 see `ready = 1` while still returning a stale `data = 0`.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define the Test-and-Set instruction. Why is it considered an atomic operation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Test-and-Set is a hardware atomic instruction that tests and modifies the contents of a memory location in a single indivisible step. Its pseudocode is:

```c
boolean TestAndSet(boolean *target) {
    boolean rv = *target;   // read
    *target = true;          // set
    return rv;               // return old value
}
```

It is **atomic** because the CPU hardware locks the memory bus (or the cache line) for the entire duration of the read-modify-write, preventing any other processor from observing or performing an intermediate state. This atomicity is what allows it to be safely used for implementing mutual exclusion without race conditions.

*(Expected length: 3–4 lines + 1 pseudocode block; full marks for stating the indivisibility guarantee.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *What are memory barriers? Why are they required even when using atomic instructions like CAS?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Memory barriers** (also called memory fences) are hardware instructions that enforce a specific ordering of memory operations — loads and stores — across CPU cores. Common variants include `sfence` (store fence), `lfence` (load fence), and `mfence` (full fence).

They are required even with atomic CAS because **CAS only guarantees atomicity of a single memory location, not ordering of surrounding memory accesses**. On weak-memory-order architectures such as ARM and POWER, the CPU can reorder independent loads and stores for performance, which can cause a process to read stale data after acquiring a lock. Memory barriers prevent this reordering, thereby preserving the *happens-before* relationship essential for correct synchronisation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

> **Question A [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the Test-and-Set instruction and show how it can be used to solve the Critical Section Problem with **bounded waiting** for *n* processes. Provide the full algorithm and prove bounded waiting. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare Compare-and-Swap and Test-and-Set instructions. Show how Compare-and-Swap implements a bounded-waiting mutual exclusion lock. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Instruction Definition (1 mark):**

Test-and-Set is an atomic RMW instruction:

```c
boolean TestAndSet(boolean *target) {
    boolean rv = *target;
    *target = true;
    return rv;
}
```

**Step 2 — Algorithm (3 marks):**

Shared data: `boolean lock = false; boolean waiting[n];`

For process Pi:

```c
do {
    waiting[i] = true;
    key = true;
    while (waiting[i] && key) {
        key = TestAndSet(&lock);
    }
    waiting[i] = false;

    /* CRITICAL SECTION */

    j = (i + 1) % n;
    while ((j != i) && !waiting[j]) {
        j = (j + 1) % n;
    }
    if (j == i) {
        lock = false;
    } else {
        waiting[j] = false;
    }

    /* REMAINDER SECTION */
} while (true);
```

**Step 3 — Bounded-Waiting Proof (3 marks):**

After Pi sets `waiting[i] = true`, the exit section of the *current* lock-holder scans circularly starting from `(i+1) mod n`. The scan will find Pi (or some waiting process) in at most $n - 1$ iterations. Therefore, after Pi has declared its intent, at most $n - 1$ other processes can enter the critical section before Pi. This proves the bound $B_{\text{wait}} \le n - 1$, which is the **bounded-waiting** property required by the KTU syllabus.

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Property | Test-and-Set | Compare-and-Swap |
|---|---|---|
| Returns | Old boolean value | Always actual value (even on mismatch) |
| Conditional write? | No (always sets to true) | Yes (only if value matches expected) |
| Supports bounded wait? | Yes, with extra waiting[] array | Yes, with extra waiting[] array |
| Use in lock-free structures? | Limited (cannot detect failure easily) | Yes (CAS is the foundation of lock-free programming) |
| Hardware availability | Universal (x86, ARM, MIPS) | Universal |

**Step 2 — CAS-based Bounded-Waiting Mutex (3 marks):**

```c
do {
    waiting[i] = true;
    int key = 1;
    while (waiting[i] && key == 1) {
        key = CompareAndSwap(&lock, 0, 1);   // swap only if lock == 0
    }
    waiting[i] = false;

    /* CRITICAL SECTION */

    int j = (i + 1) % n;
    while ((j != i) && !waiting[j]) {
        j = (j + 1) % n;
    }
    if (j == i) {
        lock = 0;
    } else {
        waiting[j] = false;
    }
} while (true);
```

**Step 3 — Why CAS is preferred (1 mark):**

CAS returns the *actual* value seen, so the caller can implement **optimistic concurrency** — try, check, retry — which is essential for lock-free data structures in modern multicore systems.

---

> **Question B [KTU University Exam — May 2024] (Alternative Choice)**
>
> **(a)** Explain memory barriers in detail. Discuss the differences between `sfence`, `lfence`, and `mfence`. Why are they critical for modern multi-core processors? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the Load-Linked / Store-Conditional (LL/SC) instruction pair. How does it differ from Compare-and-Swap, and what are its advantages? Show an LL/SC-based implementation of a mutual exclusion lock. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Concept of Memory Barriers (2 marks):**

Modern multi-core CPUs reorder memory accesses for performance. Memory barriers (fences) are special instructions that **enforce a partial order** on memory operations — they prevent the CPU from moving certain loads/stores across the barrier. This is essential for correct synchronisation because atomic instructions like CAS guarantee atomicity of *one* location, not ordering of *all* memory operations around it.

**Step 2 — Types of Barriers (3 marks):**

- **`sfence` (Store Fence):** All store instructions before `sfence` are completed before any store after `sfence`. Used in producer code when publishing data.
- **`lfence` (Load Fence):** All load instructions before `lfence` are completed before any load after `lfence`. Used in consumer code when acquiring data.
- **`mfence` (Full Fence):** Combines both — all prior loads and stores complete before any subsequent loads and stores. This is the **strongest** barrier and is used in `mutex_unlock()` and `mutex_lock()`.

**Step 3 — Why Critical for Multi-Core (2 marks):**

On weak-memory-order architectures (ARM, POWER), a process can acquire a lock, modify shared data, release the lock, and another process can acquire the lock but still read the **old** data due to store-load reordering. Memory barriers prevent this by ensuring that the writes performed inside the critical section are **globally visible** before the lock release, and that the reads performed after lock acquisition observe the **most recent** values.

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) (Alternative) — Mutex with LL/SC **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — LL/SC Semantics (2 marks):**

```c
T LoadLinked(T *ptr) {
    /* Performs a normal load and marks the cache line as "linked" */
    return *ptr;
}

bool StoreConditional(T *ptr, T val) {
    /* Stores val to *ptr only if the cache line is still linked (unmodified) */
    if (cache_line_still_linked) {
        *ptr = val;
        return true;
    } else {
        return false;
    }
}
```

**Step 2 — Mutex Implementation with LL/SC (3 marks):**

```c
void mutex_lock(lock_t *lock) {
    while (true) {
        while (LoadLinked(&lock->flag) == 1) { /* spin */ }
        if (StoreConditional(&lock->flag, 1) == true) {
            return;   // lock acquired
        }
        /* if SC failed, another process intervened; retry */
    }
}

void mutex_unlock(lock_t *lock) {
    lock->flag = 0;
}
```

**Step 3 — Advantages over CAS (2 marks):**

- **Aborts on interference:** If any other core writes to the cache line between LL and SC, SC fails. This is **more robust** than CAS, which suffers from the ABA problem.
- **Livelock-free under contention:** LL/SC naturally supports bounded retry.
- **No false success on false sharing:** CAS can erroneously succeed if the value cycles back to the expected value due to memory reuse (the ABA problem); LL/SC detects interference at the cache-line level, avoiding this.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Returning the wrong value in TAS:** Many students write `return *target;` (the new value) instead of returning the *old* value stored in a temporary. This is a **2-mark deduction** and shows a fundamental misunderstanding of the primitive.
> 2. **Confusing atomicity with ordering:** Students often claim "CAS is enough for synchronisation" and ignore memory barriers. The KTU board **explicitly tests** the distinction between atomicity of a single location and ordering of all memory operations.
> 3. **Omitting the bounded-waiting proof:** Simply presenting the algorithm is worth only 3 of the 7 marks. The remaining 4 marks are awarded for the **circular-scan argument** and the explicit bound $B_{\text{wait}} \le n - 1$.
> 4. **Confusing `mfence` with `sfence`:** Full fences enforce **both** load and store ordering; partial fences enforce only one direction. Misstating this is a common 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Using `while(true)` without the bounded-waiting array:** This gives a correct mutual-exclusion solution but **violates the bounded-waiting requirement** — a 3-mark deduction in any 7-mark sub-part.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Synchronization Hardware** is the **first robust layer** for solving the Critical Section Problem on multi-core systems.
- **Test-and-Set (TAS)** = atomic read + unconditional set + return *old* value.
- **Compare-and-Swap (CAS)** = atomic read + *conditional* write + always return *actual* value; foundation of **lock-free programming**.
- **Swap / Exchange** = atomic exchange of two values; simplest of all primitives.
- **Fetch-and-Add** = atomic increment; basis of **ticket locks** and **fair scheduling**.
- **LL/SC** = `LoadLinked` reads; `StoreConditional` writes *only if* the cache line was not modified; used in **MIPS, ARM, POWER**.
- **Memory Barriers** = `sfence`, `lfence`, `mfence`; they enforce ordering, not atomicity.
- **Three correctness requirements** = **Mutual Exclusion, Progress, Bounded Waiting** ($B_{\text{wait}} \le n - 1$).
- **Hardware solutions are immune to instruction reordering** — software-only solutions (Peterson, Dekker) are not.
- **Naive TAS/CAS = NO bounded waiting.** Always pair with a `waiting[]` array and a **circular scan** exit section.
- **CAS drawback — ABA problem:** A→B→A transitions can fool CAS into a false success. LL/SC avoids this.
- **Real kernels** (Linux, Windows NT) combine **CAS + memory barriers + ticket locks + MCS locks** for scalability.
- **The KTU 2024 board tests:** definition, algorithm, bounded-waiting proof, comparison table, and the distinction between atomicity and ordering — master all four.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mutex Locks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Mutex Locks # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Mutex Lock** (short for **Mutual Exclusion Lock**) is a synchronization primitive provided by the operating system and supported directly in hardware, used to enforce mutual exclusion — ensuring that only **one thread at a time** can access a shared critical section or shared resource. It is a variable (typically of type `pthread_mutex_t` in POSIX systems) that can be in one of two states at any given moment: **locked (busy)** or **unlocked (free)**. When a thread invokes the `acquire()` routine on a mutex, it obtains a *lock* on the mutex. If the mutex is already locked by another thread, the calling thread is blocked until the lock is released. The `release()` routine frees the mutex, allowing exactly one of the waiting threads (typically scheduled by the OS) to acquire it. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 (Process Synchronization):** > "Mutex Locks — `acquire()` and `release()` semantics; hardware support for mutual exclusion; atomic instructions; comparison with binary semaphores." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a single-occupancy **public restroom** in a railway station. There is **one key** hanging on the door. 1. You want to enter. You check if the key is hanging on the hook (i.e., the mutex is *unlocked*). 2. If the key is there, you **take the key** and walk in (you *acquire* the lock). You lock the door from the inside. 3. Anyone else trying to enter must **wait outside** until you finish. 4. When you come out, you **place the key back on the hook** (you *release* the lock). 5. The next waiting person can now take the key and enter. In this analogy: - The **restroom** = the critical section (shared resource) - The **key** = the mutex lock - The **people in queue** = threads waiting to acquire the lock - **"Key on the hook"** = mutex state = **0 (unlocked)** - **"Key in someone's pocket"** = mutex state = **1 (locked)** > [!NOTE] > A mutex is a **locking mechanism**, not a data structure in itself. It guards the entry to a critical section. The thread that locks the mutex must be the **same thread** that unlocks it — this is a defining property distinguishing it from a binary semaphore. ## 1.3 Acquire and Release Semantics The two fundamental operations defined on a mutex are: | Operation | Pseudocode | Behavior | |---|---|---| | `acquire()` | `while (lock == busy) wait; lock = busy;` | Spin (busy-wait) or block until lock becomes free, then claim it. | | `release()` | `lock = free;` | Free the lock and (optionally) wake one waiting thread. | A thread wishing to enter a critical section must first call `acquire()` on the mutex. If `acquire()` returns successfully, the thread has the lock. When the thread exits the critical section, it must call `release()`. --- ## 1.4 Key Properties of a Mutex Lock 1. **Mutual Exclusion** — Only one thread holds the lock at a time. 2. **Ownership / Binding** — A mutex is *owned* by the thread that locks it. The same thread must release it. (Unlike semaphores.) 3. **Atomicity** — The `acquire()` and `release()` operations are themselves implemented as **atomic (indivisible) hardware instructions**. 4. **No implicit thread management in its simplest form** — The naïve version uses *busy waiting* (spinning). Advanced implementations use OS-supported blocking to avoid CPU wastage. > [!WARNING] > A common student error in KTU exams is conflating a mutex with a **binary semaphore**. A binary semaphore has **no ownership constraint** — Thread A can `wait()` on a semaphore, and Thread B can `signal()` (release) it. A mutex requires the **same thread** to perform both operations. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How a Mutex Works A mutex is, at its conceptual core, just a single shared integer variable (`lock`), conventionally interpreted as: $$\text{lock} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{mutex is FREE (unlocked)} \\ 1 & \text{mutex is HELD (locked)} \end{cases}$$ The simplest (but flawed) software implementation looks like: ``` acquire() { while (lock == 1) // busy wait (spin) ; // do nothing lock = 1; // claim the lock } release() { lock = 0; // free the lock } ``` ### Why this naïve version FAILS The two statements `while (lock == 1)` and `lock = 1` are **not atomic**. Suppose two threads $T_1$ and $T_2$ run concurrently: 1. $T_1$ reads `lock = 0` and exits the `while` loop. 2. **Context switch** — $T_2$ runs. 3. $T_2$ reads `lock = 0` (still 0, because $T_1$ hasn't written yet) and exits the loop. 4. $T_2$ writes `lock = 1`. 5. Context switches back to $T_1$, which writes `lock = 1`. Now both threads believe they hold the lock — **mutual exclusion is broken**. This is the **Race Condition** that mutex design must eliminate. ## 2.2 Why Atomicity is Mandatory To be correct, the test-and-set of the lock variable must be **indivisible**. This is achieved via **hardware support**: special CPU instructions that read and write a memory location in a single, uninterruptible bus cycle. The two most common atomic primitives are: 1. **`test_and_set()`** — Atomically reads the old value of a memory location and sets it to a new value, returning the old value. 2. **`compare_and_swap()`** — Atomically compares a memory location to an expected value; if equal, swaps it with a new value, returning the old value. ## 2.3 Correct Implementation Using `test_and_set` ``` acquire() { while (test_and_set(&lock) == 1) ; // busy wait } release() { lock = 0; } ``` The `test_and_set(&lock)` instruction is implemented in hardware such that no other instruction can execute between its read and write phases. Therefore, only **one thread at a time** can observe the old value `0` and transition the lock to `1`. ## 2.4 Correct Implementation Using `compare_and_swap` (CAS) ``` acquire() { do { // No-op (spins) } while (compare_and_swap(&lock, 0, 1) != 0); } release() { lock = 0; } ``` The CAS call returns the *previous* value of `lock`. If it was `0`, CAS succeeds (sets to 1) and the thread proceeds. If it was `1`, CAS fails (does nothing) and the thread spins. > [!IMPORTANT] > Modern x86 CPUs provide `CMPXCHG` (Compare and Exchange) and `XCHG` instructions. On x86, `lock` is acquired via a hardware memory bus lock, guaranteeing atomicity even on multi-core systems. ## 2.5 The Busy-Wait (Spinlock) Problem The implementations above are called **spinlocks** because the waiting thread continuously spins, consuming CPU cycles. This is acceptable when: - Locks are held for **very short durations**. - The system is **single-core** with preemption disabled (no other thread can release the lock while we spin). - The hardware provides efficient atomic operations. It is **not acceptable** for long critical sections or single-core systems where the holding thread cannot run while the spinning thread hogs the CPU. ## 2.6 Blocking Mutex (OS-Supported) To avoid busy waiting, modern OS kernels combine atomic instructions with a **wait queue** mechanism: ``` acquire() { while (test_and_set(&lock, 1) == 1) { add this thread to lock's wait queue; block this thread (sleep); } } release() { lock = 0; wake up one thread from the wait queue (if any); } ``` This is the model used by **POSIX `pthread_mutex_t`**, Windows CRITICAL_SECTION, and Linux futexes. The blocking operation deschedules the thread, freeing the CPU for other work. ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Symbol / Construct | Definition / Rule | |---|---|---| | Mutex state | $\text{lock} \in \{0, 1\}$ | $0$ = free, $1$ = held | | Acquire precondition | $\text{lock} == 0$ | Must be true to proceed | | Release postcondition | $\text{lock} \leftarrow 0$ | Atomic write | | Atomicity requirement | `test_and_set` or `compare_and_swap` | Must be hardware-supported | | Critical section size | $T_{CS}$ | Time spent inside the lock | | Spinlock efficiency | $E = \dfrac{T_{CS}}{T_{CS} + T_{OS}}$ | Ratio of useful work to total wait time | | Threads in wait queue | $N_{waiting}$ | Threads blocked on this mutex | | Mutex ownership | Thread-bound | Same thread acquires and releases | | Binary semaphore | Not thread-bound | Any thread can signal | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world engineering utility:** Mutex locks are the workhorse of multi-threaded servers (e.g., database engines like MySQL InnoDB), real-time embedded systems, and OS kernel internals. They protect linked-list operations, hash table updates, and file descriptor tables — any shared mutable data structure that would otherwise be corrupted by concurrent access. --- ## 2.8 Comparison Table — Mutex vs Binary Semaphore vs Spinlock | Property | Mutex | Binary Semaphore | Spinlock | |---|---|---|---| | States | 0 or 1 | 0 or 1 | 0 or 1 | | Ownership | Yes (same thread) | No | Yes | | Waiting mechanism | Blocking (sleep) | Blocking (sleep) | Busy-wait (spin) | | Use case | Long critical sections | Signaling between threads | Very short CS / interrupt context | | POSIX type | `pthread_mutex_t` | `sem_t` | (manual via `atomic_flag`) | | Priority inheritance | Often supported | Not applicable | Not applicable | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **ownership property** of a mutex is what makes it suitable for protecting resources in object-oriented designs (e.g., protecting a member function's data). A semaphore is suitable only for *signaling* — e.g., "Producer signals Consumer that data is ready." # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Proof — Why `test_and_set` Guarantees Mutual Exclusion We need to prove: **At any instant, at most one thread can be inside the critical section guarded by the mutex.** ### 3.1.1 Define the atomicity contract Let the hardware-implemented `test_and_set(addr)` instruction have the following properties: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Let } old &= \text{value at } addr \text{ before the instruction} \\ \text{After execution:} \quad value \text{ at } addr &= new \\ \text{Return value of the instruction} &= old \end{aligned} $$ **Property A (Atomicity):** Between the read of `old` and the write of `new`, no other instruction (on any processor core) can access the memory address `addr`. **Property B (Sequential consistency):** Other processors observe either the state *before* or *after* the atomic instruction — never an intermediate state. ### 3.1.2 Mutex `acquire()` algorithm $$ \begin{aligned} \text{acquire}() &= \text{while } \text{test\_and\_set}(\&lock, 1) == 1: \\ & \quad \text{spin (or block)} \\ \text{Postcondition:} \quad lock &= 1 \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.3 Proof by contradiction Assume two threads $T_1$ and $T_2$ are both inside the critical section simultaneously. For $T_1$ to enter, it must have executed `test_and_set(&lock, 1)` and received a return value of $0$. This means that *just before* $T_1$'s atomic instruction, the value of `lock` was $0$, and the atomic instruction set it to $1$. For $T_2$ to also enter, it must have executed `test_and_set(&lock, 1)` and also received a return value of $0$. This requires that *just before* $T_2$'s atomic instruction, the value of `lock` was $0$. But by Property A, between the moment $T_1$ set `lock = 1` and any subsequent read, no other instruction can interleave. Therefore, when $T_2$ executes its `test_and_set`, it must observe `lock = 1` and return $1$, causing $T_2$ to spin. **Contradiction.** $\blacksquare$ > [!NOTE] > This proof relies on the atomicity guarantee of `test_and_set`. If this guarantee is broken (e.g., a buggy CPU or compiler reordering), mutual exclusion fails — which is why memory barriers (`mfence`, `dmb`) are required on weakly-ordered architectures like ARM. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Lifecycle of a Mutex Acquire/Release Pair | Step | Thread State | Mutex State (`lock`) | Action | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Ready | 0 (free) | Thread $T_1$ calls `acquire()` | | 2 | Running | 0 → 1 | $T_1$ executes `test_and_set`, gets 0, sets to 1 | | 3 | Running (in CS) | 1 (held) | $T_1$ performs critical section work | | 4 | Ready | 1 | $T_2$ calls `acquire()` | | 5 | Blocked / Spinning | 1 | $T_2$'s `test_and_set` returns 1, $T_2$ waits | | 6 | Running | 1 → 0 | $T_1$ finishes CS, calls `release()`, sets lock to 0 | | 7 | Ready → Running | 0 → 1 | $T_2$'s next `test_and_set` succeeds, $T_2$ enters CS | ## 3.3 Busy-Wait Cost Analysis Suppose a lock is held for $T_{CS}$ time units (the critical section duration), and the overhead of one context switch is $T_{OS}$. For a spinlock on a single-core system, while the holding thread is delayed (because the spinning thread is using the CPU), the lock is never released — this is the **priority inversion / starvation** scenario. For a spinlock on a **multi-core** system, the spinning thread wastes exactly one CPU core. The throughput efficiency is: $$ \begin{aligned} E &= \frac{T_{CS}}{T_{CS} + T_{spin}} \\ \end{aligned} $$ where $T_{spin}$ is the time spent spinning. For a system with $n$ threads contending for one lock, the average wait time is: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{wait, avg} &= (n - 1) \cdot T_{CS} \\ \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU Module 3 numerical problems sometimes ask: *"When should a spinlock be preferred over a blocking mutex?"* The answer is: when $T_{CS} < T_{context\_switch}$ — i.e., when the lock is held for less time than it takes to perform a context switch. In that case, spinning is more efficient than blocking. ## 3.4 Worked Example — Tracing a Mutex Interaction **Problem:** Two threads $T_A$ and $T_B$ contend for a mutex `M` that protects a shared variable `counter`. Initially, `M = 0` (unlocked) and `counter = 5`. The following sequence of events occurs: 1. $T_A$ calls `acquire(M)`. 2. $T_A$ reads `counter`, computes `counter + 1 = 6`. 3. $T_B$ calls `acquire(M)` — what happens? 4. $T_A$ writes `counter = 6`. 5. $T_A$ calls `release(M)`. **Solution:** - **After step 1:** $T_A$ succeeds; `M = 1` (locked). $T_A$ enters the critical section. - **After step 2:** $T_A$ holds the lock and computes locally. `counter` is still `5` in shared memory. - **After step 3:** $T_B$ calls `acquire(M)`. Since `M = 1`, $T_B$ blocks (or spins). It cannot read or write `counter` yet. - **After step 4:** $T_A$ writes `counter = 6` safely — no other thread can interfere. - **After step 5:** `M = 0`. $T_B$ is unblocked, acquires `M = 1`, and may now read `counter = 6`. **Final state:** `counter = 6`, `M = 1` (held by $T_B$). Mutual exclusion preserved. $\blacksquare$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mutex Lock State Machine The following Mermaid state diagram captures the lifecycle of a mutex from the perspective of the operating system kernel and the threads interacting with it. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Free : mutex initialized Free --> Held : threadA acquire()
test_and_set returns 0
lock = 1 Held --> Held : threadB acquire()
test_and_set returns 1
add threadB to wait queue
block threadB Held --> Free : threadA release()
lock = 0
wake one waiter Free --> [*] : mutex destroyed ``` ## 4.2 Thread-Mutex Interaction Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Thread T1 calls acquire] --> B{test_and_set
returns 0?} B -- Yes --> C[Set lock = 1
Enter Critical Section] B -- No --> D[Add T1 to wait queue] D --> E[Block T1 / Sleep] E --> F[Wait for wake-up signal] F --> B C --> G[Execute critical section work] G --> H[Thread T1 calls release] H --> I[Set lock = 0] I --> J{Wait queue
non-empty?} J -- Yes --> K[Wake one thread from queue] J -- No --> L[Lock remains free] K --> M[Unblocked thread retries acquire] L --> M ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Mutex Implementation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HW["Hardware Layer"] CPU[CPU Core 1] CPU2[CPU Core 2] ATOMIC[Atomic Instruction Unit
test_and_set / compare_and_swap] end subgraph OS["OS Kernel Layer"] MUTEXVAR[Mutex Variable: lock] WAITQ[Wait Queue: list of blocked threads] SCHED[Scheduler / Dispatcher] end subgraph APP["Application Layer"] T1[Thread T1] T2[Thread T2] T3[Thread T3] end T1 -->|acquire| MUTEXVAR T2 -->|acquire| MUTEXVAR T3 -->|acquire| MUTEXVAR MUTEXVAR --> ATOMIC ATOMIC --> CPU ATOMIC --> CPU2 MUTEXVAR -->|if locked| WAITQ WAITQ --> SCHED SCHED -->|wake up| T1 SCHED -->|wake up| T2 T1 -->|release| MUTEXVAR T2 -->|release| MUTEXVAR ``` ## 4.4 Sequence Diagram — Acquire/Release in Multi-Threaded Context ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant T1 as Thread T1 participant Mutex as Mutex Lock participant T2 as Thread T2 participant Mem as Shared Memory T1->>Mutex: acquire() activate Mutex Mutex->>Mutex: test_and_set = 0 Mutex-->>T1: Lock acquired (lock = 1) deactivate Mutex T1->>Mem: read counter T2->>Mutex: acquire() activate Mutex Mutex->>Mutex: test_and_set = 1 (already locked) Mutex-->>T2: Blocked (added to wait queue) deactivate Mutex T1->>Mem: write counter + 1 T1->>Mutex: release() activate Mutex Mutex->>Mutex: lock = 0 Mutex->>T2: Wake up T2 deactivate Mutex T2->>Mutex: acquire() (retry) activate Mutex Mutex->>Mutex: test_and_set = 0 Mutex-->>T2: Lock acquired deactivate Mutex T2->>Mem: read counter T2->>Mutex: release() activate Mutex Mutex->>Mutex: lock = 0 deactivate Mutex ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a mutex lock. Explain the role of `acquire()` and `release()` operations in ensuring mutual exclusion.** **Model Answer:** A **mutex lock** is a synchronization variable used to enforce mutual exclusion on a shared resource, ensuring that only one thread can execute the protected critical section at a time. It has two states: **locked (1)** and **unlocked (0)**. - The `acquire()` operation is invoked by a thread before entering the critical section. It checks the lock state; if the lock is unlocked, it atomically sets it to locked and grants the thread access. If the lock is already held, the calling thread waits (busy-spins or blocks) until the holder releases it. - The `release()` operation is invoked by a thread upon exiting the critical section. It atomically sets the lock back to unlocked and, in blocking implementations, wakes up one waiting thread from the mutex's wait queue. Together, these two operations — implemented using atomic hardware instructions like `test_and_set` or `compare_and_swap` — guarantee that only one thread at a time can be inside the critical section, thereby satisfying the **mutual exclusion** property of process synchronization. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]` **Distinguish between a mutex lock and a binary semaphore.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Mutex Lock | Binary Semaphore | |---|---|---| | **Ownership** | Thread-bound — the same thread that acquires must release | No ownership — any thread can signal/release | | **Initial value** | Always 0 (unlocked initially in dynamic use) | Can be 0 or 1 depending on initial state | | **Purpose** | Enforce mutual exclusion on a shared resource | Used for both mutual exclusion and signaling between threads | | **POSIX type** | `pthread_mutex_t` | `sem_t` | | **Priority inheritance** | Often supported to prevent priority inversion | Not generally supported | In essence, **every mutex is conceptually similar to a binary semaphore**, but the ownership constraint makes mutexes suitable for protecting resources within object-oriented designs, whereas semaphores are preferred for producer-consumer signaling patterns. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Question:** **(a)** Explain the **`test_and_set()`** instruction. Using it, write the `acquire()` and `release()` routines for a mutex lock. Show that mutual exclusion is guaranteed. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **busy-wait (spinlock) problem** of a naïve mutex implementation. How does the operating system overcome this problem using wait queues? **(7 marks)** --- #### (a) Model Solution The `test_and_set()` instruction is a hardware-supported atomic instruction with the following semantics: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{boolean test\_and\_set}(bool \ast target) \\ \text{old} &\leftarrow \ast target \\ \ast target &\leftarrow \text{true} \\ \text{return } &old \end{aligned} $$ The hardware guarantees that the read of `*target` and the write of `true` to `*target` occur **atomically** — no other instruction on any processor core can interleave between them. **`acquire()` routine:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{acquire}(\text{mutex} \ast L) \{ \\ & \quad \text{while (test\_and\_set}(\&L \to \text{lock}) == \text{true}) \\ & \quad\quad ; \quad \text{// busy wait} \\ \} \end{aligned} $$ **`release()` routine:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{release}(\text{mutex} \ast L) \{ \\ & \quad L \to \text{lock} \leftarrow \text{false}; \\ \} \end{aligned} $$ **Proof of mutual exclusion:** Assume two threads $T_1$ and $T_2$ both succeed in `acquire()`. For $T_1$ to succeed, `test_and_set` must have returned `false`, meaning the lock was `false` immediately before $T_1$'s atomic instruction, and was then set to `true`. By the atomicity property, $T_2$'s subsequent `test_and_set` will read `true` and return `true`, causing $T_2$ to spin. Thus, both cannot be inside the critical section. $\blacksquare$ #### (b) Model Solution In the naïve spinlock implementation, a thread that fails to acquire the lock enters a **busy-wait loop** — it continuously executes the `test_and_set` instruction, consuming CPU cycles without doing useful work. This causes: 1. **Wasted CPU time** — On a single-core system, if the spinning thread preempts the thread that holds the lock, the lock will never be released (since the holder cannot run). This is a serious liveness issue. 2. **Poor scalability** — On multi-core systems, every spinning thread wastes one CPU core. 3. **Power inefficiency** — Continuous spinning consumes power, which is critical in mobile and embedded devices. **OS-supported solution using wait queues:** The OS augments the spinlock with a **wait queue** (a linked list of blocked threads) and blocking primitives: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{acquire}(\text{mutex} \ast L) \{ \\ & \quad \text{while (test\_and\_set}(\&L \to \text{lock}) == \text{true}) \{ \\ & \quad\quad \text{add this thread to } L \to \text{wait\_queue}; \\ & \quad\quad \text{block this thread} \quad \text{// deschedule, free CPU} \\ & \quad \} \\ \} \\ \\ \text{release}(\text{mutex} \ast L) \{ \\ & \quad L \to \text{lock} \leftarrow \text{false}; \\ & \quad \text{wake\_up}(\&L \to \text{wait\_queue}); \quad \text{// wake one waiting thread} \\ \} \end{aligned} $$ The `block` operation removes the thread from the ready queue and puts it to sleep. The `release` operation wakes up one waiting thread, which then retries `acquire`. This is the basis of POSIX `pthread_mutex_t` and Windows CRITICAL_SECTION. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - June 2022]` **Question:** **(a)** What is a mutex lock? Describe the **`compare_and_swap()`** atomic instruction and use it to implement a correct mutex lock. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What are the limitations of mutex locks? Explain how **priority inversion** can occur and how **priority inheritance protocol** solves it. **(7 marks)** --- #### (a) Model Solution A **mutex lock** is a synchronization primitive that enforces mutual exclusion, ensuring that only one thread at a time can execute a critical section. The `compare_and_swap()` instruction atomically performs the following: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{int compare\_and\_swap}(int \ast value, int \text{ expected}, int \text{ new}) \{ \\ & \quad \text{int actual} \leftarrow \ast value; \\ & \quad \text{if (actual} == \text{expected}) \\ & \quad\quad \ast value \leftarrow \text{new}; \\ & \quad \text{return actual;} \\ \} \end{aligned} $$ This entire sequence is **atomic** — guaranteed by hardware. **Mutex implementation using CAS:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{acquire}(\text{mutex} \ast L) \{ \\ & \quad \text{do} \{ \\ & \quad\quad \text{// spin, no-op} \\ & \quad \} \text{ while (compare\_and\_swap}(\&L \to \text{lock}, 0, 1) != 0); \\ \} \\ \\ \text{release}(\text{mutex} \ast L) \{ \\ & \quad L \to \text{lock} \leftarrow 0; \\ \} \end{aligned} $$ The CAS returns the **previous** value of the lock. If it was `0`, the swap succeeded and the thread now holds the lock (`lock = 1`). If it was `1`, CAS failed (no write) and the thread retries. This is the standard implementation used in many modern lock-free data structures. #### (b) Model Solution **Limitations of mutex locks:** 1. **Deadlock** — If two threads each hold a lock that the other needs, both will wait forever. 2. **Priority inversion** — A high-priority thread can be blocked indefinitely waiting for a low-priority thread to release a lock. 3. **Convoy effect** — When a lock holder is descheduled, all waiting threads stall. 4. **Not composable** — Functions that internally lock a mutex cannot be called safely from contexts where the same mutex is already held (non-reentrancy, unless `pthread_mutexattr_settype(..., PTHREAD_MUTEX_RECURSIVE)` is used). 5. **Scalability** — On highly contended locks, performance degrades due to cache line bouncing across cores. **Priority Inversion:** Consider three threads: - $T_H$ (high priority) — needs mutex $M$. - $T_M$ (medium priority) — does not need $M$. - $T_L$ (low priority) — currently holds $M$. Without priority inheritance: 1. $T_L$ acquires $M$ and starts the critical section. 2. $T_H$ becomes ready, preempts $T_L$, and tries to acquire $M$ — **blocks**. 3. $T_M$ becomes ready, preempts $T_L$ (since $T_L$ is still runnable, just doesn't have CPU). 4. $T_M$ runs for a long time. $T_L$ never gets CPU, so $M$ is never released. 5. $T_H$ waits indefinitely — **unbounded priority inversion**. **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) solution:** When a high-priority thread $T_H$ blocks on a mutex held by a low-priority thread $T_L$, the kernel **temporarily boosts** $T_L$'s priority to match $T_H$'s. Now: - $T_L$ runs at $T_H$'s priority, completing the critical section quickly. - $T_M$ cannot preempt $T_L$ (since $T_M$'s priority is lower than the boosted $T_L$). - After $T_L$ releases $M$, its priority reverts to normal. - $T_H$ acquires $M$ and proceeds. This bounds the inversion to a single critical section's duration. POSIX mutexes with `PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT` attribute enable this protocol. $\blacksquare$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing ownership:** Students often write that a semaphore and a mutex are identical.
