Marks are deducted for not mentioning the **ownership** property of mutexes. > 2. **Skipping the atomicity proof:** When asked to "show that mutual exclusion is guaranteed," simply writing the code is not enough — you must invoke the **atomicity property** of `test_and_set` or `compare_and_swap` explicitly. > 3. **Omitting the hardware support:** A complete answer must mention that the atomic instructions are implemented in hardware (CPU bus lock or cache coherence protocol) and not via software tricks. > 4. **Missing the deadlock / priority inversion angle:** When asked about "limitations," students list only deadlocks and forget priority inversion, which is a high-weight Module 3 topic. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - A **mutex lock** is a thread-bound synchronization primitive that enforces mutual exclusion on a shared resource. - A mutex has two states: **locked (1)** and **unlocked (0)**. - The two operations are `acquire()` and `release()` — both must be **atomic**. - Atomicity is achieved through hardware instructions: **`test_and_set`** or **`compare_and_swap`** (CAS). - The **same thread** that acquires the mutex must release it — this is the ownership property. - A **spinlock** is a mutex implemented via busy-waiting; suitable only for very short critical sections. - A **blocking mutex** uses an OS wait queue to deschedule waiting threads, freeing the CPU. - **Mutex vs Binary Semaphore:** Mutex has ownership, semaphore does not. - **Priority inheritance** prevents unbounded priority inversion in real-time systems. - **Use spinlocks** when $T_{CS} < T_{context\_switch}$; use blocking mutexes otherwise. - Common API: POSIX `pthread_mutex_lock()`, `pthread_mutex_unlock()`. - Mutex locks can suffer from **deadlock**, **priority inversion**, and **convoy effect** — design carefully. - Atomic instructions require **memory barriers** on weakly-ordered architectures (ARM, POWER) to prevent compiler/CPU reordering from breaking mutual exclusion.

topics.title: Semaphores
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Semaphores

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Semaphore** is a synchronization tool (an integer variable) proposed by **Edsger W. Dijkstra (1965)** that uses two standard atomic operations, `wait()` (also denoted as `P()` or `down()`) and `signal()` (also denoted as `V()` or `up()`), to control access to shared resources and solve the Critical Section Problem in concurrent processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> "A semaphore S is an integer variable that, apart from initialization, is accessed only through two standard atomic operations: **wait** and **signal**."
> The word *atomic* means indivisible — no two processes can execute wait/signal on the same semaphore at the same instant.

Formally, a semaphore can be defined as the structure:

$$
S = \{ \text{value} \in \mathbb{Z}, \ \text{queue of waiting processes} \}
$$

The two atomic operations are defined as:

$$
\text{wait}(S): \quad \text{while} \ S \le 0 \ \text{do} \ \text{wait}; \quad S := S - 1
$$

$$
\text{signal}(S): \quad S := S + 1; \quad \text{wakeup a process from queue (if any)}
$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🚻

Imagine a public restroom in a railway station with **only 1 key** hanging on a board at the entrance:

1. When a person (process) needs to use the restroom (shared resource), they **remove the key** from the board → this is `wait(S)`. The semaphore value drops (S = 0).
2. The next person arrives, sees **no key on the board**, and must **wait** in a queue until the key returns → this is the *blocked state*.
3. When the person finishes, they **return the key to the board** → this is `signal(S)`. The semaphore value rises (S = 1) and the first waiting person is unblocked.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The *key* is the semaphore. The act of *taking* the key is `wait()`. The act of *returning* the key is `signal()`. The number of keys = the integer value of the semaphore.

## 1.3 Types of Semaphores (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Type | Value Range | Common Use | Also Known As |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Binary Semaphore** | $0$ or $1$ | Mutual Exclusion (Mutex) | Mutex Lock |
| **Counting Semaphore** | Any non-negative integer $\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$ | Resource allocation with N instances | General Semaphore |

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Distinction:** A **binary semaphore** differs from a **mutex lock** in one important way — a mutex has a concept of *ownership* (only the locking process can unlock), whereas a binary semaphore has *no ownership* (any process may signal). KTU frequently tests this difference.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Atomic Operations — The Heart of Semaphores

The correctness of a semaphore depends entirely on its operations being **atomic** (executed without interruption). Dijkstra originally named them using Dutch mnemonics:

- **P(S)** — *Proberen* means "to test" → `wait(S)` 
- **V(S)** — *Verhogen* means "to increment" → `signal(S)`

### 2.1.1 The `wait()` Operation — Step-by-Step Semantics

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Algorithm: } \text{wait}(S) \\
&1. \quad S := S - 1 \quad \text{(decrement the counter)} \\
&2. \quad \text{if } S < 0 \text{ then} \\
&3. \qquad \text{block this process} \\
&4. \qquad \text{place the process in } S.\text{waiting\_queue} \\
&5. \quad \text{end if}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why does `S < 0` mean "block"?** Because if the initial value was 1 and one process has taken the key, the value becomes 0 (allowed). The next process decrements to -1 (not allowed — must wait). So *negative* values essentially count how many processes are waiting.

### 2.1.2 The `signal()` Operation — Step-by-Step Semantics

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Algorithm: } \text{signal}(S) \\
&1. \quad S := S + 1 \quad \text{(increment the counter)} \\
&2. \quad \text{if } S \le 0 \text{ then} \\
&3. \qquad \text{remove a process } P_i \text{ from } S.\text{waiting\_queue} \\
&4. \qquad \text{wakeup}(P_i) \quad \text{(move } P_i \text{ from blocked to ready)} \\
&5. \quad \text{end if}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why `S ≤ 0` check in signal?** If the semaphore is still negative after incrementing, it means processes are waiting. We must wake exactly one. If S is positive after incrementing, no one is waiting — no wakeup needed.

## 2.2 Strong vs Weak Semaphores

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strong Semaphore:** Uses **FIFO (First-In-First-Out)** ordering when waking up processes. This *guarantees freedom from starvation* because every process gets a fair turn.
>
> **Weak Semaphore:** Has **no specified wakeup order**. A process could be starved indefinitely if unlucky. (KTU examiner's favorite contrast question!)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Cheat Table

| Concept | Formula / Condition | Interpretation | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Semaphore Invariant (Binary)** | $S \in \{0, 1\}$ | Only one process in critical section at a time | Mutual Exclusion |
| **Semaphore Invariant (Counting)** | $S \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$ | Tracks available resource instances | Resource Management |
| **Wait Blocking Condition** | $S_{\text{after decrement}} < 0$ | Process is blocked (added to queue) | `wait()` |
| **Signal Wakeup Condition** | $S_{\text{after increment}} \le 0$ | At least one process in queue | `signal()` |
| **Process Waiting Count** | $\vert S \vert$ when $S < 0$ | Number of processes currently blocked | Synchronization |
| **P() Operation (Dijkstra)** | $S := S - 1$; test | Proberen (test) | Original Notation |
| **V() Operation (Dijkstra)** | $S := S + 1$; wakeup | Verhogen (increment) | Original Notation |
| **n-Process Critical Section** | Mutex $S$ initialized to **1** | One process at a time in CS | Synchronization |
| **Resource Allocation (N units)** | $S$ initialized to **N** | N concurrent users allowed | Counting Semaphore |
| **Order of Operations** | `wait(mutex)` before CS, `signal(mutex)` after | Entry & Exit Protocol | Standard Pattern |
| **Number of wakeups in signal()** | Exactly **1** (if queue non-empty) | Prevents lost wakeups | Atomicity Guarantee |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the absolute value is not in the table above:** In markdown tables, the pipe symbol breaks rendering. In the next section (derivations), the proper LaTeX absolute-value notation $\vert S \vert$ is used inside math blocks where it is safe.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why Do We Need Semaphores?

In real engineering systems, semaphores are used for:

1. **Operating System Kernels** — Linux `sem()` system calls, Windows `CreateSemaphore()`.
2. **Embedded Real-Time Systems** — Synchronizing sensor reading tasks with actuator control tasks.
3. **Database Transaction Managers** — Controlling concurrent access to database rows.
4. **Network Protocol Stacks** — Coordinating sender/receiver buffer access in TCP/IP.
5. **Automotive ECU Systems (AUTOSAR)** — Protecting shared CAN bus message buffers.

> The semaphore is the **most fundamental building block** of all higher-level synchronization primitives (monitors, condition variables, message queues).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why `S < 0` in `wait()` Indicates Blocking

We start with the *invariant* that the semaphore's value must reflect the actual system state. Let:

- $S$ = current value of the semaphore.
- $n_{\text{available}}$ = number of currently available resource instances.
- $n_{\text{waiting}}$ = number of processes blocked in the queue.

**Step 1:** When a process calls `wait(S)`, it attempts to *consume* one resource.

$$
S_{\text{new}} = S - 1
$$

**Step 2:** After decrement, the value of $S$ is the new available count. If this count is non-negative, a resource is available.

$$
\text{if } S_{\text{new}} \ge 0 \implies \text{resource available} \implies \text{do not block}
$$

$$
\text{if } S_{\text{new}} < 0 \implies \text{no resource available} \implies \text{must block}
$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, the blocking condition is precisely:

$$
\boxed{\,S - 1 < 0 \iff S < 1\,}
$$

When initially $S = 1$ (binary semaphore with free resource), the first `wait()` makes $S = 0$ (still $\ge 0$, so process enters CS). The second `wait()` makes $S = -1 < 0$, so this second process must block. **This is the proof that binary semaphores enforce mutual exclusion.**

## 3.2 Derivation: Why `S ≤ 0` in `signal()` Indicates Wakeup Needed

**Step 1:** When a process calls `signal(S)`, it returns one resource.

$$
S_{\text{new}} = S + 1
$$

**Step 2:** If after incrementing, the value is positive, then there is a free resource *and* no one is waiting (because if someone were waiting, $S$ would have been negative — the wait would have decremented past zero).

$$
\text{if } S_{\text{new}} > 0 \implies \text{no waiters} \implies \text{no wakeup}
$$

**Step 3:** If $S_{\text{new}}$ is still $\le 0$, it means that even after returning one resource, the count is not positive, which can only happen if at least one process is still waiting.

$$
\boxed{\,S + 1 \le 0 \iff S \le -1 \iff \text{at least one waiter exists}\,}
$$

Therefore we must wake exactly one process from the queue. This derivation is **examiner-rewarded** in KTU answers.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Two Processes Entering Critical Section

**Problem:** Two processes $P_1$ and $P_2$ share a critical section protected by a binary semaphore `mutex` initialized to **1**. Show the state of `mutex` and the queue as they execute.

**Solution Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Initial State:} \quad \text{mutex} = 1, \quad \text{Queue} = \emptyset \\
&\\
&\text{Step 1: } P_1 \text{ executes } \text{wait(mutex)} \\
&\quad \text{mutex} = 1 - 1 = 0 \ge 0 \implies P_1 \text{ enters CS} \\
&\\
&\text{Step 2: } P_2 \text{ executes } \text{wait(mutex)} \\
&\quad \text{mutex} = 0 - 1 = -1 < 0 \implies P_2 \text{ blocked, joins queue} \\
&\quad \text{Queue} = \{P_2\} \\
&\\
&\text{Step 3: } P_1 \text{ executes } \text{signal(mutex)} \\
&\quad \text{mutex} = -1 + 1 = 0 \le 0 \implies \text{wakeup } P_2 \\
&\quad \text{Queue} = \emptyset, \quad P_2 \text{ moves to Ready queue} \\
&\\
&\text{Step 4: } P_2 \text{ resumes CS} \\
&\quad \text{Step 5: } P_2 \text{ executes } \text{signal(mutex)} \\
&\quad \text{mutex} = 0 + 1 = 1 > 0 \implies \text{no wakeup needed}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Note in Step 3, even after `signal()`, `mutex` is **0** (not 1). This is because $P_2$ has already been "promised" the resource. The semaphore value of 0 means *"the resource is in use, but no one is currently waiting"*. This subtle behavior is a common KTU 14-mark question.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Semaphore vs Other Synchronization Tools

| Property | Semaphore | Mutex Lock | Monitor | Message Passing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | OS-level primitive | Locking primitive | Language construct | IPC mechanism |
| **Ownership** | No | Yes | Implicit (in language) | No |
| **Blocking** | Yes (on wait) | Yes (on lock) | Yes (on condition) | Yes (on receive) |
| **Starvation-Free** | Only strong semaphore | Depends on impl. | Yes (in theory) | Yes (in theory) |
| **Binary Form** | Yes | Yes | N/A | N/A |
| **Counting Form** | Yes | No | No | N/A |
| **Busy Wait** | No (proper impl.) | No (proper impl.) | No | No |
| **Kernel Support** | Required | Required | Required | Required |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ |

## 3.5 Producer-Consumer Problem — Full Semaphore Solution

**Problem Setup:** A producer process produces items and puts them into a bounded buffer of size $N$. A consumer process removes items. Both share the buffer.

**Semaphores Used:**

| Semaphore | Initial Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `mutex` | **1** | Mutual exclusion on buffer access |
| `empty` | **N** | Counts empty slots |
| `full` | **0** | Counts filled slots |

**Complete Pseudocode (Standard KTU Format):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Producer Process:} \\
&\quad \text{while (true) \{ } \\
&\quad\qquad \text{// produce an item in next\_produced} \\
&\quad\qquad \text{wait(empty);} \\
&\quad\qquad \text{wait(mutex);} \\
&\quad\qquad \quad \text{// add next\_produced to buffer} \\
&\quad\qquad \text{signal(mutex);} \\
&\quad\qquad \text{signal(full);} \\
&\quad \text{\}} \\
&\\
&\textbf{Consumer Process:} \\
&\quad \text{while (true) \{ } \\
&\quad\qquad \text{wait(full);} \\
&\quad\qquad \text{wait(mutex);} \\
&\quad\qquad \quad \text{// remove an item from buffer to next\_consumed} \\
&\quad\qquad \text{signal(mutex);} \\
&\quad\qquad \text{signal(empty);} \\
&\quad\qquad \text{// consume the item in next\_consumed} \\
&\quad \text{\}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of wait() calls matters!** In the producer, we call `wait(empty)` *before* `wait(mutex)`. If we reversed them, we could cause **deadlock** when the buffer is full. This is a high-frequency KTU pitfall.

**Proof of Correctness — Invariant Analysis:**

- $\text{empty} + \text{full} = N$ at all times ✓ (conservation of slots)
- `mutex` $\in \{0, 1\}$ at all times ✓ (binary semaphore)
- No process accesses buffer without holding `mutex` ✓ (mutual exclusion)
- Consumer never reads from empty buffer (`full` $\ge 0$ guaranteed) ✓
- Producer never writes to full buffer (`empty` $\ge 0$ guaranteed) ✓

    C -- No --> D([Process Pi enters Critical Section])

    E --> F([Process Pi moved to Blocked state])
    D --> G([Process Pi executes Critical Section code])
    G --> H([Process Pi calls signalS])
    H --> I[S value = S value plus 1]
    I --> J{S value less than or equal 0?}
    J -- No --> K([No wakeup needed, return])
    J -- Yes --> L[Remove process Pj from S waiting queue]
    L --> M([Wakeup Pj, move to Ready state])
    M --> N([P j may later re enter Critical Section])
    K --> N

# 🎓 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a semaphore. Explain the two atomic operations performed on a semaphore.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **semaphore** is a synchronization integer variable used to control access to shared resources in a concurrent system. It is accessed only through two indivisible (atomic) operations:

1. **wait(S)** — Also called `P(S)` or `down(S)`. It decrements the semaphore value by 1. If the resulting value becomes negative, the calling process is blocked and added to the semaphore's waiting queue.

2. **signal(S)** — Also called `V(S)` or `up(S)`. It increments the semaphore value by 1. If any processes are waiting on this semaphore, exactly one is removed from the queue and moved to the ready state.

These operations are **atomic**, meaning they execute without interruption, which guarantees that no race condition occurs on the semaphore itself. *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two operations with behavior.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between binary semaphore and counting semaphore.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Binary Semaphore | Counting Semaphore |
|---|---|---|
| **Value Range** | Only $0$ or $1$ | Any non-negative integer $\ge 0$ |
| **Use Case** | Mutual exclusion (mutex) | Managing $N$ instances of a resource |
| **Initialization** | Usually **1** | Usually **N** (the number of resources) |
| **Example** | Single printer, single lock | Pool of 5 database connections |

A binary semaphore restricts access to **one** process at a time, while a counting semaphore allows up to **N** processes to access N identical resources concurrently. *(1 mark per correct differentiation row × 3.)*

---

## 📚 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** Module 3 questions come in pairs. Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks, split as Part (a) = 7 marks and Part (b) = 7 marks.

---

### ✏️ Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of semaphores. With a neat diagram, describe the implementation of `wait()` and `signal()` operations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (2 marks):** A semaphore is an integer synchronization variable introduced by Dijkstra in 1965. It is used to enforce mutual exclusion and coordinate process execution in concurrent systems. Only two atomic operations — `wait()` (P operation) and `signal()` (V operation) — can modify its value.

**Implementation of `wait(S)` (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{wait}(S)\ \{\\
&\qquad S = S - 1;\\
&\qquad \text{if } (S < 0)\ \{\\
&\qquad\qquad \text{block this process};\\
&\qquad\qquad \text{place process in S.L (waiting list)};\\
&\qquad \}\\
&\}
\end{aligned}
$$

The process is blocked (added to the queue $S.L$) whenever the decremented value goes below zero. The negative magnitude $\vert S \vert$ represents the number of processes waiting.

**Implementation of `signal(S)` (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{signal}(S)\ \{\\
&\qquad S = S + 1;\\
&\qquad \text{if } (S \le 0)\ \{\\
&\qquad\qquad \text{remove a process } P \text{ from S.L};\\
&\qquad\qquad \text{wakeup}(P);\\
&\qquad \}\\
&\}
\end{aligned}
$$

If after increment the value is still non-positive, at least one process is waiting; one is removed and woken up.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the state-machine diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes. Show the test on $S < 0$ and $S \le 0$ branches with arrows to Block and Wakeup operations.

---

**(b)** Write the semaphore-based solution to the **Producer-Consumer problem** using a bounded buffer of size $N$. Explain why `wait()` ordering matters. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Semaphores Used (1 mark):**

- `mutex` initialized to **1** → for buffer access mutual exclusion
- `empty` initialized to **N** → counts empty slots
- `full` initialized to **0** → counts filled slots

**Producer Code (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Producer:}\\
&\text{do \{ }\\
&\quad \text{// produce an item in nextP}\\
&\quad \text{wait}(\text{empty});\\
&\quad \text{wait}(\text{mutex});\\
&\quad \quad \text{// add nextP to buffer}\\
&\quad \text{signal}(\text{mutex});\\
&\quad \text{signal}(\text{full});\\
&\text{\} while (true);}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Consumer Code (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Consumer:}\\
&\text{do \{ }\\
&\quad \text{wait}(\text{full});\\
&\quad \text{wait}(\text{mutex});\\
&\quad \quad \text{// remove item from buffer to nextC}\\
&\quad \text{signal}(\text{mutex});\\
&\quad \text{signal}(\text{empty});\\
&\quad \text{// consume the item in nextC}\\
&\text{\} while (true);}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why Ordering Matters (1 mark):**

In the producer, `wait(empty)` is called *before* `wait(mutex)`. If reversed, when the buffer is full:
1. Producer acquires `mutex` first (S = 0).
2. Then calls `wait(empty)` — but `empty` is 0, so producer blocks *while holding mutex*.
3. Consumer tries to call `wait(mutex)` to remove an item — but mutex is held — **DEADLOCK**.

By calling `wait(empty)` first, the producer blocks *before* taking the mutex, leaving the consumer free to enter and consume.

---

### ✏️ Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 3 Choice]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** What is a semaphore? Explain the **Readers-Writers problem** and provide a semaphore-based solution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Semaphore (1 mark):** As defined earlier, a semaphore is a non-negative integer variable supporting only `wait()` and `signal()` atomic operations.

**Readers-Writers Problem (2 marks):** A shared database is accessed by two kinds of processes:
- **Reader** — only reads the data (non-destructive).
- **Writer** — modifies the data (destructive).

The constraint is: *any number of readers can read simultaneously, but a writer must have exclusive access — no readers and no other writers can be present when a writer is active.*

**Semaphores & Variables (1 mark):**

- `mutex` = **1** → protects the shared `readcount` variable
- `wrt` = **1** → ensures only one writer
- `readcount` → integer counting active readers, initialized to **0**

**Code (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Writer Process:}\\
&\text{wait}(\text{wrt});\\
&\quad \text{// writing is performed}\\
&\text{signal}(\text{wrt});\\
&\\
&\text{Reader Process:}\\
&\text{wait}(\text{mutex});\\
&\quad \text{readcount} = \text{readcount} + 1;\\
&\text{signal}(\text{mutex});\\
&\text{if } (\text{readcount} == 1)\ \text{wait}(\text{wrt});\\
&\quad \text{// reading is performed}\\
&\text{wait}(\text{mutex});\\
&\quad \text{readcount} = \text{readcount} - 1;\\
&\text{signal}(\text{mutex});\\
&\text{if } (\text{readcount} == 0)\ \text{signal}(\text{wrt});
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The *first* reader locks `wrt` (blocking writers). All subsequent readers just increment `readcount`. The *last* reader to finish unlocks `wrt`, allowing a waiting writer to proceed.

---

**(b)** Compare **strong** and **weak** semaphores. Can a weak semaphore lead to starvation? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Strong Semaphore (3 marks):**
- A semaphore that uses a **FIFO (First-In-First-Out)** policy for the waiting queue.
- When `signal()` is executed, the process that has been waiting the *longest* is selected and woken up.
- **Guarantee:** *No starvation.* Every process that calls `wait()` will eventually be woken up, because wakeup is strictly in queue-arrival order.

**Weak Semaphore (2 marks):**
- A semaphore with **no specified ordering** for the waiting queue.
- The implementation may wake up *any* process from the queue.
- **No starvation guarantee.** A process can be stuck in the queue indefinitely if other waiting processes keep getting selected.

**Can a weak semaphore cause starvation? (2 marks):**
**Yes.** Justification: Consider three processes $P_1, P_2, P_3$ all waiting on semaphore $S$. If a `signal()` operation arbitrarily wakes up $P_3$ first, then later $P_2$, $P_1$ may be perpetually bypassed. If a *new* process keeps calling `wait()` and an *unlucky* existing waiter keeps getting deprioritized, that process starves. This violates the *bounded waiting* requirement of the Critical Section Problem. Therefore, KTU examiners expect the answer: **"Yes, a weak semaphore can lead to starvation; only a strong semaphore (FIFO) is starvation-free."**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Read Carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting atomicity** — If a student writes `wait()` and `signal()` as plain C statements without mentioning *atomicity*, they lose 1–2 marks. Always state: *"These operations are atomic, executed without interruption."*
>
> 2. **Wrong ordering of wait() in Producer-Consumer** — The most classic KTU trap. Reversing the order of `wait(empty)` and `wait(mutex)` causes deadlock. The examiner will deduct 2 marks if the student writes `wait(mutex); wait(empty);` in the producer.
>
> 3. **Confusing Binary Semaphore with Mutex Lock** — A binary semaphore has *no ownership*; any process can `signal()`. A mutex has ownership — only the locking process can unlock. If the question says "mutex", do not call it a binary semaphore. Many students lose 1 mark here.
>
> 4. **Drawing the value of S incorrectly after signal()** — After a `signal()` that wakes a process, the value of S becomes **0**, not **1**. Many students write S = 1 directly. This is wrong because the woken process has already been "promised" the slot. (See Section 3.3 derivation above.)
>
> 5. **Not specifying P/V or wait/signal notation consistency** — Don't mix `P(S)` and `wait(S)` in the same answer. Pick one notation and stick to it. The standard KTU convention is **`wait()` and `signal()`** (or equivalently `P()` and `V()`).
>
> 6. **Missing the "wakeup exactly one process" clause in signal()** — Always say: "If $S \le 0$ after increment, *exactly one* process is removed and woken up." The word *exactly* is important.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Semaphores**
>
> - ✅ A **semaphore** is a non-negative integer variable accessed only via atomic `wait()` and `signal()` operations.
> - ✅ `wait(S)` is also called **`P(S)`** (Proberen = test) or `down(S)`.
> - ✅ `signal(S)` is also called **`V(S)`** (Verhogen = increment) or `up(S)`.
> - ✅ The **atomicity** of wait/signal is what makes semaphores correct — no interrupts during these operations.
> - ✅ **Binary semaphore** takes values $\{0, 1\}$; used for mutual exclusion.
> - ✅ **Counting semaphore** takes values $\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$; used for managing N identical resources.
> - ✅ In `wait()`: if $S < 0$ after decrement, block the process. Magnitude $\vert S \vert$ = number of waiters.
> - ✅ In `signal()`: if $S \le 0$ after increment, wake *exactly one* waiter.
> - ✅ **Strong semaphores** (FIFO wakeup) prevent starvation; **weak semaphores** (no order) may starve.
> - ✅ **Producer-Consumer** uses 3 semaphores: `mutex = 1`, `empty = N`, `full = 0`. The order of `wait(empty)` before `wait(mutex)` in the producer is **critical** to avoid deadlock.
> - ✅ **Readers-Writers** problem: `mutex = 1` (protects readcount), `wrt = 1` (writer lock). First reader locks `wrt`; last reader unlocks it.
> - ✅ Binary semaphore $\ne$ Mutex — ownership is the key difference.
> - ✅ After `signal()` that wakes a process, the semaphore value is **0** (not 1) because the woken process already holds a "virtual" slot.
> - ✅ Original proposer: **Edsger W. Dijkstra, 1965**.
> - ✅ Semaphores are the **foundation** of higher-level constructs like monitors and condition variables.

---

> **End of Module 3 — Semaphores Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, PCERT601 — Operating Systems, Module 3 (Process Synchronization).*

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Producer–Consumer Problem** (also known as the **Bounded–Buffer Problem**) is a classical synchronization problem in operating systems that models two cooperating processes — a *producer* that generates data items and a *consumer* that removes them — which share a common, fixed-size buffer. The fundamental challenge is to ensure that:

1. The producer must **wait** when the buffer is **full** (cannot overwrite unread data).
2. The consumer must **wait** when the buffer is **empty** (cannot read data that does not exist).
3. Access to the shared buffer must be **mutually exclusive** to prevent race conditions.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is solved using **semaphores** — specifically a combination of *binary semaphores* (for mutual exclusion) and *counting semaphores* (for resource counting).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The Producer–Consumer problem is one of the *three* canonical synchronization problems (along with **Readers–Writers** and **Dining Philosophers**) and is a high-frequency topic in KTU ESE Part B questions (14-mark problems).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Buffet Counter

Imagine a **restaurant buffet line**:
- The **Chef (Producer)** continuously cooks dishes and places them on a serving counter (the **bounded buffer**) that can hold a maximum of, say, **5 plates**.
- The **Customer (Consumer)** walks up and picks a plate to eat.
- If the counter is already full of 5 plates, the chef **must stop cooking and wait** until a customer removes at least one plate.
- If the counter is empty, the customer **must wait** until the chef places at least one dish.
- Only **one person at a time** can place or remove a plate from the counter (no two hands grabbing the same plate).

This is **exactly** the producer–consumer problem. The counter = bounded buffer, the chef = producer process, the customer = consumer process, and the rule "one person at a time" = mutual exclusion enforced by a semaphore.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?** In real operating systems, this pattern is used in: **pipe operations**, **print spoolers**, **kernel logging buffers**, **I/O buffering**, **producer–consumer thread pools**, and **inter-process communication (IPC)** via message queues.

## 1.3 The Three Semaphores

The standard KTU-mandated solution uses **three semaphores**:

| Semaphore | Type | Initial Value | Purpose |
|-----------|------|---------------|---------|
| `mutex`   | Binary (0/1) | **1** | Ensures mutual exclusion on buffer access |
| `empty`   | Counting     | **N** | Counts the number of **empty** buffer slots |
| `full`    | Counting     | **0** | Counts the number of **full** (filled) buffer slots |

Where **N** is the size of the bounded buffer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

### The Shared Data Structure
A circular (or linear) buffer of fixed size **N** slots, where each slot can hold exactly **one** data item. The buffer maintains two indices:
- `in`  — the next position where the producer will write.
- `out` — the next position from which the consumer will read.

Both indices wrap around modulo **N** (`in = (in + 1) mod N`, `out = (out + 1) mod N`).

### Why Synchronization is Required (The "Why")

Without synchronization, two failure modes occur:
1. **Race Condition on Buffer Index:** Both producer and consumer may simultaneously update `in` or `out`, corrupting the buffer state.
2. **Out-of-Bounds Access:** The producer may write to a slot that the consumer is concurrently reading, leading to inconsistent data; or the consumer may read from a slot that hasn't been written yet.

### How the Three Semaphores Solve the Problem (The "How")

- **`empty`** is initialized to **N**. Every `wait(empty)` decrements it; every `signal(empty)` increments it. If `empty` becomes 0, the producer blocks — this is how we enforce "wait when full."
- **`full`** is initialized to **0**. Every `wait(full)` decrements it; every `signal(full)` increments it. If `full` becomes 0, the consumer blocks — this is how we enforce "wait when empty."
- **`mutex`** is initialized to **1**. It guarantees that only one process executes the **critical section** (the actual buffer manipulation) at a time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation:** The order of `wait` calls matters! The producer must call `wait(empty)` **before** `wait(mutex)`, and the consumer must call `wait(full)` **before** `wait(mutex)`. Reversing the order leads to **deadlock** — a classic KTU exam pitfall (see Section 5).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Invariant | Explanation |
|---------|---------------------|-------------|
| Buffer invariant | $\text{empty} + \text{full} = N$ | Always holds; sum of empty and filled slots equals total buffer size |
| Total slots | $N$ | Fixed buffer capacity |
| Initial semaphore values | $\text{mutex}=1,\ \text{empty}=N,\ \text{full}=0$ | Standard initialization for bounded buffer |
| Producer's wait count | $\text{wait}(\text{empty}) \Rightarrow \text{empty} = \text{empty} - 1$ | Blocks producer when empty reaches 0 |
| Consumer's wait count | $\text{wait}(\text{full}) \Rightarrow \text{full} = \text{full} - 1$ | Blocks consumer when full reaches 0 |
| Buffer index update (Producer) | $\text{in} = (\text{in} + 1) \mod N$ | Circular wrap-around of write pointer |
| Buffer index update (Consumer) | $\text{out} = (\text{out} + 1) \mod N$ | Circular wrap-around of read pointer |
| Condition for producer to block | $\text{empty} = 0$ | Buffer completely full |
| Condition for consumer to block | $\text{full} = 0$ | Buffer completely empty |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Producer–Consumer Pattern |
|--------------------|----------------------------------|
| **OS Kernel Logs** | Kernel `printk` ring buffer (e.g., Linux `dmesg` uses a bounded circular buffer) |
| **Pipe / FIFO IPC** | Unix pipes internally use bounded buffers between writer and reader |
| **Thread Pools** | Task queue fed by producer threads, drained by worker (consumer) threads |
| **Stream Processing** | Kafka, RabbitMQ — producers enqueue messages, consumers dequeue them |
| **Print Spoolers** | Print jobs queued in a bounded directory/spool, printed by spooler process |
| **Audio/Video Pipelines** | Decoded frames placed in buffer, rendered by display thread |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Complete Algorithm (Pseudocode)

### Shared Declarations
The data structure and semaphores are shared between the producer and consumer:

```
#define N 100                // Buffer size

typedef int semaphore;
semaphore mutex = 1;         // Mutual exclusion
semaphore empty = N;         // Counts empty slots
semaphore full  = 0;         // Counts full slots

item buffer[N];              // The bounded buffer
int   in   = 0;              // Producer's next write index
int   out  = 0;              // Consumer's next read index
```

### Producer Process
```
void producer(void) {
    item nextProduced;
    while (TRUE) {
        nextProduced = produce_item();        // Generate next item
        wait(empty);                          // Decrement empty count
        wait(mutex);                          // Enter critical section
        buffer[in] = nextProduced;            // Place item in buffer
        in = (in + 1) % N;                    // Advance write index
        signal(mutex);                        // Leave critical section
        signal(full);                         // Increment full count
    }
}
```

### Consumer Process
```
void consumer(void) {
    item nextConsumed;
    while (TRUE) {
        wait(full);                           // Decrement full count
        wait(mutex);                          // Enter critical section
        nextConsumed = buffer[out];           // Remove item from buffer
        out = (out + 1) % N;                  // Advance read index
        signal(mutex);                        // Leave critical section
        signal(empty);                        // Increment empty count
        consume_item(nextConsumed);           // Use the item
    }
}
```

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Analysis

### Step 1: Initialization Verification
At time $t = 0$, the system state is:

$$\text{empty} = N,\quad \text{full} = 0,\quad \text{mutex} = 1,\quad \text{in} = 0,\quad \text{out} = 0$$

**Logic check:** Initially, all $N$ slots are empty, so $\text{empty} = N$ is correct. No slots are full, so $\text{full} = 0$ is correct. The buffer is unlocked, so $\text{mutex} = 1$ is correct. The invariant $\text{empty} + \text{full} = N$ evaluates to $N + 0 = N$ ✓.

### Step 2: Producer's First Iteration

The producer executes `produce_item()`, generating some data item. Then:

- `wait(empty)`: decrements `empty` from $N$ to $N-1$. Since $N \geq 1$ (assume valid buffer), this does **not** block.
- `wait(mutex)`: decrements `mutex` from $1$ to $0$. The producer now holds the lock.
- **Critical section**: writes the item at `buffer[in] = buffer[0]`, then sets `in = (0+1) \mod N = 1$.
- `signal(mutex)`: increments `mutex` from $0$ to $1$. Lock released.
- `signal(full)`: increments `full` from $0$ to $1$. One slot is now reported as full.

After Step 2: $\text{empty} = N-1$, $\text{full} = 1$, $\text{in} = 1$. Invariant: $(N-1) + 1 = N$ ✓.

### Step 3: Consumer's First Iteration

- `wait(full)`: decrements `full` from $1$ to $0$. Does not block (since $full \geq 1$).
- `wait(mutex)`: decrements `mutex` from $1$ to $0$. Consumer holds the lock.
- **Critical section**: reads `buffer[out] = buffer[0]`, sets `out = (0+1) \mod N = 1`.
- `signal(mutex)`: increments `mutex` from $0$ to $1$. Lock released.
- `signal(empty)`: increments `empty` from $N-1$ to $N$. One slot freed.
- `consume_item()`: process the data.

After Step 3: $\text{empty} = N$, $\text{full} = 0$, $\text{out} = 1$, `in = 1`. Invariant: $N + 0 = N$ ✓. System returned to initial state — as expected after one produce + one consume cycle.

### Step 4: Deadlock / Starvation Analysis

**Can the producer block forever?** Only if `empty` remains 0 indefinitely. But the consumer, on every iteration, calls `signal(empty)`, so `empty` strictly increases. Since the consumer is not blocked (it must have been woken by the producer's `signal(full)`), the producer will eventually be unblocked. ✓

**Can the consumer block forever?** Symmetric argument: `full` is incremented by the producer's `signal(full)`. Since the producer is not blocked (it must have been unblocked by the consumer's `signal(empty)`), the consumer will eventually proceed. ✓

**Can `mutex` be permanently locked?** No. The critical section is bounded (just a few assignments); thus `signal(mutex)` always follows `wait(mutex)` in finite time. ✓

**Conclusion:** The algorithm is **deadlock-free** and **starvation-free** under fair semaphore implementation.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Buffer Size N = 3)

Let $N = 3$. Initial state: $\text{empty} = 3$, $\text{full} = 0$, $\text{mutex} = 1$.

| Step | Action | empty | full | mutex | in | out | Note |
|------|--------|-------|------|-------|----|----|------|
| 0 | Init | 3 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | All slots empty |
| 1 | P produces | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | Buffer[0] filled |
| 2 | P produces | 1 | 2 | 1 | 2 | 0 | Buffer[1] filled |
| 3 | P produces | 0 | 3 | 1 | 0 | 0 | Buffer[2] filled (wrap) |
| 4 | P tries to produce | — | — | — | — | — | **Blocks** (empty = 0) |
| 5 | C consumes | 1 | 2 | 1 | 0 | 1 | Buffer[0] freed → unblocks P |
| 6 | P produces | 0 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 1 | Buffer[1] filled |
| 7 | C consumes | 1 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 2 | Buffer[1] freed |

At every step, $\text{empty} + \text{full} = 3$ ✓. Producer blocks at step 4 exactly when buffer is full; consumer would block at any point where it tries to consume when `full = 0`.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **architectural flow** between the producer, the bounded buffer, and the consumer, including the role of each semaphore.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROC["PRODUCER PROCESS"]
        P1["produce_item"]
        P2["wait empty"]
        P3["wait mutex"]
        P4["buffer in = item"]
        P5["in = in + 1 mod N"]
        P6["signal mutex"]
        P7["signal full"]
    end

    subgraph BUF["BOUNDED BUFFER N SLOTS"]
        S0["Slot 0"]
        S1["Slot 1"]
        S2["Slot 2"]
        SN["Slot N-1"]
    end

    subgraph SEM["SEMAPHORES"]
        M["mutex = 1"]
        E["empty = N"]
        F["full = 0"]
    end

    subgraph CONS["CONSUMER PROCESS"]
        C1["wait full"]
        C2["wait mutex"]
        C3["item = buffer out"]
        C4["out = out + 1 mod N"]
        C5["signal mutex"]
        C6["signal empty"]
        C7["consume_item"]
    end

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7
    P7 -.signals.-> F
    P2 -.waits on.-> E
    P3 -.waits on.-> M

    P4 --> S0
    P5 --> S1
    P6 --> S2

    C1 -.waits on.-> F
    C2 -.waits on.-> M
    C3 --> C4 --> C5 --> C6 --> C7
    C6 -.signals.-> E

    S0 --> C3
    SN --> C3
```

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram of the Producer

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Producing
    Producing --> CheckEmpty: produce_item
    CheckEmpty --> Blocked: empty equals 0
    CheckEmpty --> AcquireMutex: empty greater than 0
    Blocked --> AcquireMutex: empty signaled
    AcquireMutex --> WriteBuffer: mutex acquired
    WriteBuffer --> UpdateIndex: in equals next slot
    UpdateIndex --> ReleaseMutex: in equals in plus 1 mod N
    ReleaseMutex --> SignalFull: mutex released
    SignalFull --> Producing: full incremented
```

## 4.3 Circular Buffer Visualization (N = 5)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CIRC["CIRCULAR BUFFER N EQUALS 5"]
        D0["Slot 0 FILLED"]
        D1["Slot 1 FILLED"]
        D2["Slot 2 EMPTY"]
        D3["Slot 3 EMPTY"]
        D4["Slot 4 EMPTY"]
    end

    IN["IN POINTER at Slot 2"]
    OUT["OUT POINTER at Slot 0"]

    D2 -.next write.-> IN
    D0 -.next read.-> OUT

    IN -.wraps to.-> D0
    OUT -.advances to.-> D1
```

## 4.4 Mutual Exclusion & Flow Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Producer Thread"] -->|wait empty| B{empty greater than 0}
    B -- No --> BB["Producer BLOCKED in queue"]
    B -- Yes --> C["wait mutex"]
    C --> D{mutex equals 1}
    D -- No --> DD["Producer WAITS for mutex"]
    D -- Yes --> E["CRITICAL SECTION write to buffer"]
    E --> F["signal mutex"]
    F --> G["signal full"]
    G --> A

    H["Consumer Thread"] -->|wait full| I{full greater than 0}
    I -- No --> II["Consumer BLOCKED in queue"]
    I -- Yes --> J["wait mutex"]
    J --> K{mutex equals 1}
    K -- No --> KK["Consumer WAITS for mutex"]
    K -- Yes --> L["CRITICAL SECTION read from buffer"]
    L --> M["signal mutex"]
    M --> N["signal empty"]
    N --> O["consume_item"]
    O --> H

    G -.increments.-> I
    N -.increments.-> B
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between counting semaphores and binary semaphores. Which type is used for `mutex` and which for `empty`/`full` in the bounded-buffer (producer–consumer) problem?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **binary semaphore** can take only two values: 0 or 1. It is used to implement **mutual exclusion** — i.e., ensuring that only one process enters the critical section at a time. In the producer–consumer problem, `mutex` is a binary semaphore initialized to 1.

A **counting semaphore** can take any non-negative integer value. It is used to control access to a resource that has multiple instances. In the producer–consumer problem, `empty` (initialized to **N**, the number of empty buffer slots) and `full` (initialized to **0**, the number of filled slots) are counting semaphores.

| Semaphore | Type | Initial Value | Purpose |
|-----------|------|---------------|---------|
| `mutex`   | Binary    | 1 | Mutual exclusion on buffer |
| `empty`   | Counting  | N | Counts available empty slots |
| `full`    | Counting  | 0 | Counts filled slots |

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What happens in the producer–consumer problem if the order of `wait` operations is reversed (i.e., producer does `wait(mutex)` before `wait(empty)`)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

If the order is reversed, **deadlock** occurs. Consider the scenario where the buffer is full (`empty = 0`) and the consumer is the only one that can make `empty` non-zero. The producer executes `wait(mutex)` first and acquires the lock (since `mutex = 1`). It then executes `wait(empty)`, but `empty = 0`, so the producer **blocks** — and crucially, it still holds the `mutex` lock.

The consumer now wants to consume (to unblock the producer), but to enter its critical section it must call `wait(mutex)`, which **blocks** because the producer holds the lock. Now both processes are blocked indefinitely, each waiting for the other — this is a **classic deadlock**. The remedy is to always perform `wait(empty)` / `wait(full)` **before** `wait(mutex)`.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **Producer–Consumer (Bounded Buffer) problem** in detail. State the role of each semaphore used in the solution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the **complete pseudocode** for the producer and consumer processes using semaphores. Explain why `wait(empty)` must be called before `wait(mutex)`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Definition (2 marks):** The Producer–Consumer (Bounded Buffer) problem involves two types of cooperating processes that share a common, fixed-size buffer. The *producer* generates data items and places them into the buffer; the *consumer* removes items and processes them. Synchronization is required to ensure: (i) the producer does not overwrite unread data when the buffer is full, (ii) the consumer does not read empty slots when the buffer is empty, and (iii) buffer access is mutually exclusive.

**Three Semaphores (3 marks):**

1. **`mutex` (binary semaphore, initial = 1):** Provides mutual exclusion on the critical section — the actual manipulation of the buffer (`buffer[in] = item` and `in = (in+1) % N` for producer; `item = buffer[out]` and `out = (out+1) % N` for consumer). Without `mutex`, race conditions on the indices and buffer contents would corrupt the data.

2. **`empty` (counting semaphore, initial = N):** Maintains the count of empty (available) buffer slots. The producer performs `wait(empty)` before each write, so when `empty = 0` (buffer full), the producer blocks. After each consume, the consumer performs `signal(empty)` to indicate a freed slot.

3. **`full` (counting semaphore, initial = 0):** Maintains the count of filled (occupied) buffer slots. The consumer performs `wait(full)` before each read, so when `full = 0` (buffer empty), the consumer blocks. After each produce, the producer performs `signal(full)` to indicate a newly available item.

**Invariant (2 marks):** The fundamental invariant $\text{empty} + \text{full} = N$ must always hold. This guarantees that the producer and consumer never operate on a slot that is simultaneously considered "empty" and "full," eliminating race conditions on the data.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Pseudocode for Producer (3 marks):**
```
do {
    wait(empty);                     // Check for empty slot
    wait(mutex);                     // Enter critical section
    buffer[in] = nextProduced;       // Place item
    in = (in + 1) % N;               // Advance write pointer
    signal(mutex);                   // Leave critical section
    signal(full);                    // Signal that buffer has a new item
} while (TRUE);
```

**Pseudocode for Consumer (3 marks):**
```
do {
    wait(full);                      // Check for filled slot
    wait(mutex);                     // Enter critical section
    nextConsumed = buffer[out];      // Remove item
    out = (out + 1) % N;             // Advance read pointer
    signal(mutex);                   // Leave critical section
    signal(empty);                   // Signal that a slot is freed
    consume_item(nextConsumed);      // Process the item
} while (TRUE);
```

**Explanation of ordering (1 mark):** `wait(empty)` must be called **before** `wait(mutex)` in the producer (and similarly `wait(full)` before `wait(mutex)` in the consumer) to prevent deadlock. If the buffer is full, `empty = 0`. If `wait(mutex)` were called first, the producer would acquire the lock, then block on `wait(empty)` — and since the lock is held, the consumer cannot acquire it to consume and signal `empty`. Both processes would block forever. By checking `empty` first, the producer blocks **before** acquiring the lock, allowing the consumer to enter the critical section, consume an item, and signal `empty` to unblock the producer.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** Describe the **circular buffer** implementation of the producer–consumer problem. How are the indices `in` and `out` updated? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a bounded buffer of size **N = 5**. Initially, the producer inserts 3 items and the consumer removes 1 item. Tabulate the values of `empty`, `full`, `in`, and `out` after each significant operation. Verify the invariant $\text{empty} + \text{full} = 5$ at every step. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Circular Buffer Concept (3 marks):** In the circular buffer implementation, the buffer of size **N** is treated as a ring — the slot indices wrap around from $N-1$ back to $0$. This allows continuous use of the buffer without needing to shift items, and it makes efficient use of memory. The buffer is shared between the producer and consumer via two pointers:

- **`in`**: The next index where the producer will write. Initialized to **0**.
- **`out`**: The next index from which the consumer will read. Initialized to **0**.

**Index Update Mechanism (3 marks):**

After the producer writes an item at position `in`:
$$\text{in} = (\text{in} + 1) \mod N$$

After the consumer reads an item from position `out`:
$$\text{out} = (\text{out} + 1) \mod N$$

The modulo operation ensures wrap-around. For example, with $N = 5$, if `in = 4`, then after `in = (4+1) mod 5 = 0`, the next write goes to slot 0.

**Distinguishing Full from Empty (1 mark):** When `in == out`, the buffer could be either full or empty. This ambiguity is resolved by the **semaphores** (`empty` and `full`) which explicitly track the count. As long as $\text{empty} + \text{full} = N$, the state is consistent.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Initial state (1 mark):** $\text{empty} = 5$, $\text{full} = 0$, $\text{in} = 0$, $\text{out} = 0$. Invariant: $5 + 0 = 5$ ✓.

**Tabulation (5 marks):**

| Step | Operation | empty | full | in | out | Check (empty + full) |
|------|-----------|-------|------|----|----|----------------------|
| 0    | Initialize | 5 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $5 + 0 = 5$ ✓ |
| 1    | Producer writes item 1 at buffer[0] | 4 | 1 | 1 | 0 | $4 + 1 = 5$ ✓ |
| 2    | Producer writes item 2 at buffer[1] | 3 | 2 | 2 | 0 | $3 + 2 = 5$ ✓ |
| 3    | Producer writes item 3 at buffer[2] | 2 | 3 | 3 | 0 | $2 + 3 = 5$ ✓ |
| 4    | Consumer reads item 1 from buffer[0] | 3 | 2 | 3 | 1 | $3 + 2 = 5$ ✓ |

**Verification (1 mark):** At every step, $\text{empty} + \text{full} = 5 = N$, confirming the invariant holds. The buffer has 2 items remaining (at slots 1 and 2), `in` points to slot 3 (next write position), and `out` points to slot 1 (next read position). The system is in a valid state with no race conditions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Scheme Exams:**
>
> 1. **Swapping `empty` and `full` initial values** (writing `empty = 0, full = N`) — costs 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Reversing `wait` order** in pseudocode (`wait(mutex)` before `wait(empty)`) — KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for this and mark the deadlock justification as incorrect.
> 3. **Forgetting to perform `signal(mutex)`** after the critical section — this leaks the lock and causes deadlock; examiners deduct **1 mark** per missing signal.
> 4. **Not specifying semaphore types** (binary vs counting) in definitions — KTU 2024 Scheme rubric awards marks for explicit type declaration; omitting it costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing `in` and `out` updates** — writing `out = (out + 1) % N` for the producer or vice versa — costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Not stating the invariant** $\text{empty} + \text{full} = N$ in the answer — KTU examiners expect this for full marks in part (a).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Problem name:** Producer–Consumer / Bounded–Buffer. (KTU Module 3, high-weightage topic.)
- **Three semaphores:** `mutex = 1` (binary), `empty = N` (counting), `full = 0` (counting).
- **Producer order:** `wait(empty)` → `wait(mutex)` → critical section → `signal(mutex)` → `signal(full)`.
- **Consumer order:** `wait(full)` → `wait(mutex)` → critical section → `signal(mutex)` → `signal(empty)`.
- **Critical invariant:** $\text{empty} + \text{full} = N$ — always true at every instant.
- **Circular buffer:** $\text{in} = (\text{in} + 1) \mod N$ and $\text{out} = (\text{out} + 1) \mod N$.
- **Block conditions:** Producer blocks when `empty = 0`; Consumer blocks when `full = 0`.
- **Why ordering matters:** Reversing `wait` order causes **deadlock**, not just incorrect behavior.
- **Mutex is binary**, not counting — it only takes values 0 or 1.
- **Real-world uses:** Pipes, print spoolers, kernel log buffers, thread pools, message queues.
- **KTU 2024 marks pattern:** Part A (2 × 3 = 6 marks) often tests semaphore types or deadlock reasoning; Part B (14 marks) typically asks for full pseudocode + explanation of one of the three semaphores + invariant verification.
- **Mnemonic for semaphore order in Producer:** "**E**mpty before **M**utex" — **E < M** alphabetically and temporally.
- **Mnemonic for semaphore order in Consumer:** "**F**ull before **M**utex" — **F < M** alphabetically and temporally.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dining Philosophers and Readers-Writers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Dining Philosophers and Readers-Writers ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The Dining Philosophers Problem > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > The Dining Philosophers Problem is a classic concurrent programming synchronization problem formulated by **Edsger Dijkstra in 1965**. It illustrates the challenges of avoiding **deadlock**, **starvation**, and ensuring **mutual exclusion** when multiple processes compete for a finite set of shared resources. Five philosophers sit around a circular table, alternating between **thinking** and **eating**, requiring two shared chopsticks (resources) to eat. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Imagine **5 friends** sitting around a round dining table. Between each pair of friends, there is **exactly one chopstick**. To eat their food (say, noodles), each friend needs **two chopsticks** — the one on their left and the one on their right. Each friend thinks for a while, then gets hungry, picks up the left chopstick, then the right chopstick, eats, and puts them back. The challenge? If everyone picks up their left chopstick at the same time, **no one can pick up the right chopstick** because it's already taken — and everyone starves forever. This is a **deadlock**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Syllabus Highlight:** The Dining Philosophers Problem is the **canonical example** used to teach: > - **Deadlock detection and avoidance** > - **Resource contention** among processes > - **Starvation-free synchronization** > - **Mutual exclusion** without busy waiting --- ### 1.2 The Readers-Writers Problem > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > The Readers-Writers Problem is a classic synchronization problem concerning access to a **shared database or data structure**. Multiple concurrent **reader threads** may access the data simultaneously (shared read access is safe), but a **writer thread** requires **exclusive access** (no readers or other writers may access the data during a write). The challenge is to coordinate access so that data integrity is preserved while maximizing concurrency. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of a **shared Google Document**. Many people can **view (read)** the document at the same time — that causes no problem. However, if one person is **editing (writing)** the document, no one else should be reading or writing simultaneously, otherwise conflicting edits would corrupt the data. The problem is: how do we let as many readers as possible read concurrently, while ensuring that a writer gets exclusive access when needed — and no one starves? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Syllabus Highlight:** The Readers-Writers Problem demonstrates: > - **Shared vs. exclusive resource access** > - **Reader-preference** vs. **Writer-preference** scheduling > - **Semaphore-based mutual exclusion** with counters > - **Starvation avoidance** in concurrent access control --- ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants in Synchronization > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU-Exam Constants to Memorize:** > - **N = 5** philosophers in the classical Dining Philosophers setup > - **Number of resources (chopsticks) = 5** (one between each pair) > - **Resources required per process = 2** (left and right chopstick) > - **Total resources in system = 5** > - **Maximum simultaneous resources held** in deadlock = **5** (one per philosopher) --- ### 1.4 Why These Problems Matter in Real Engineering | Problem | Real-World Engineering Application | |---|---| | Dining Philosophers | **Database connection pool management**, **transaction locking**, **multi-threaded resource allocators** in OS kernels | | Readers-Writers | **File systems**, **caching layers** (Redis), **in-memory databases**, **configuration registries** | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Dining Philosophers — Problem Setup The problem consists of: - **5 philosophers** (processes/threads) labeled $P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$ - **5 chopsticks** (resources/semaphores) labeled $C_0, C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4$ - Chopstick $C_i$ is positioned **between** philosopher $P_i$ and $P_{(i+1) \bmod 5}$ - Philosopher $P_i$ needs chopsticks $C_i$ (left) and $C_{(i-1) \bmod 5}$ (right) to eat **States of a Philosopher:** - **THINKING** — not holding any chopstick - **HUNGRY** — waiting for chopsticks - **EATING** — holding both chopsticks ### 2.2 Naive (Flawed) Solution Each philosopher executes the following pseudocode: ``` do { wait(chopstick[i]); // pick up left wait(chopstick[(i+1) % 5]); // pick up right /* EAT */ signal(chopstick[i]); // put down left signal(chopstick[(i+1) % 5]); // put down right /* THINK */ } while (true); ``` **Why this fails — The Deadlock Scenario:** 1. All 5 philosophers get hungry **simultaneously** 2. Each picks up their **left chopstick** (all 5 chopsticks now held) 3. Each tries to pick up their **right chopstick** — but it's held by the neighbor 4. **Circular wait** forms: $P_0 \rightarrow P_1 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow P_3 \rightarrow P_4 \rightarrow P_0$ 5. **Deadlock!** All philosophers starve forever. > [!WARNING] > **All four Coffman conditions for deadlock are satisfied:** > 1. **Mutual Exclusion:** Only one philosopher holds a chopstick > 2. **Hold and Wait:** Each holds left, waits for right > 3. **No Preemption:** Chopsticks cannot be forcibly taken > 4. **Circular Wait:** $P_0 \rightarrow P_1 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow P_4 \rightarrow P_0$ --- ### 2.3 Solution 1 — Resource Hierarchy (Dijkstra's Ordered Acquisition) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Strategy:** Break the circular wait by **ordering the resources** and requiring philosophers to pick them up in **ascending order** (lower-numbered first). **Modified Pseudocode:** ``` do { if (i % 2 == 0) { wait(chopstick[i]); // even: pick up lower first wait(chopstick[(i+1) % 5]); } else { wait(chopstick[(i+1) % 5]); // odd: pick up lower first wait(chopstick[i]); } /* EAT */ signal(chopstick[i]); signal(chopstick[(i+1) % 5]); /* THINK */ } while (true); ``` **Logic:** Every philosopher picks the **lower-numbered chopstick first**. Since chopsticks are numbered $0$ to $4$, this breaks the circular wait because the last philosopher in the chain will see that the higher-numbered chopstick they need is available. **Drawback:** Still does not guarantee **starvation-freedom** for all N — only deadlock-freedom. --- ### 2.4 Solution 2 — Arbitrator / Dining Hall Monitor Use a **central mutex** `mutex` and a **state array** `state[5]`: **Data Structures:** - `state[i] ∈ {THINKING, HUNGRY, EATING}` — initially `THINKING` - `self[i]` — semaphore for each philosopher (initially 0) - `mutex` — binary semaphore (initially 1) **Philosopher `i` process:** ``` do { think(); pickup(i); eat(); putdown(i); } while (true); ``` **`pickup(i)` procedure:** ``` wait(mutex); state[i] = HUNGRY; test(i); signal(mutex); wait(self[i]); ``` **`putdown(i)` procedure:** ``` wait(mutex); state[i] = THINKING; test(LEFT); // test left neighbor test(RIGHT); // test right neighbor signal(mutex); ``` **`test(i)` procedure:** ``` if (state[i] == HUNGRY && state[LEFT] != EATING && state[RIGHT] != EATING) { state[i] = EATING; signal(self[i]); } ``` Where: $$\text{LEFT}(i) = (i + 4) \bmod 5$$ $$\text{RIGHT}(i) = (i + 1) \bmod 5$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this works:** The `mutex` ensures atomic updates to the `state` array. When a philosopher puts down chopsticks, they wake up neighbors who were HUNGRY and can now EAT. **No deadlock, no starvation (under FIFO scheduling).** --- ### 2.5 Readers-Writers Problem — The Two Classical Variants #### Variant 1: First Readers-Writers Problem (Readers Preference) > [!NOTE] > **Rule:** Once a reader begins reading, all subsequent readers may enter even if a writer is waiting. A writer can only enter when **no readers are active**. **Data Structures:** - `rw_mutex` = 1 (binary semaphore, protects `readcount`) - `mutex` = 1 (binary semaphore, exclusive database access) - `readcount` = 0 (integer, number of active readers) **Reader Process:** ``` wait(rw_mutex); readcount++; if (readcount == 1) wait(mutex); // first reader locks database signal(rw_mutex); /* READ DATABASE */ wait(rw_mutex); readcount--; if (readcount == 0) signal(mutex); // last reader releases database signal(rw_mutex); ``` **Writer Process:** ``` wait(mutex); /* WRITE DATABASE */ signal(mutex); ``` **Problem:** **Writer starvation** is possible. If readers keep arriving, `readcount` never reaches 0, and the writer waits forever. #### Variant 2: Second Readers-Writers Problem (Writers Preference) **Additional Semaphore:** `turnstile = 1` to block readers when a writer is waiting. **Reader Process:** ``` wait(turnstile); wait(rw_mutex); readcount++; if (readcount == 1) wait(mutex); signal(rw_mutex); signal(turnstile); /* READ DATABASE */ wait(rw_mutex); readcount--; if (readcount == 0) signal(mutex); signal(rw_mutex); ``` **Writer Process:** ``` wait(turnstile); wait(mutex); /* WRITE DATABASE */ signal(mutex); signal(turnstile); ``` **Problem:** **Reader starvation** is possible. If writers keep arriving, readers wait forever. #### Variant 3: Fair Readers-Writers Solution Use a **FIFO queue** mechanism or an additional `queue` semaphore to ensure neither readers nor writers starve. --- ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes | |---|---|---| | Right neighbor of $P_i$ | $(i+1) \bmod N$ | For N=5: $P_4$'s right is $P_0$ | | Left neighbor of $P_i$ | $(i-1+N) \bmod N$ | Equivalent to $(i+4) \bmod 5$ | | Total resources (Dining P) | $N$ | $N=5$ in classical problem | | Resources per process | $2$ | Left + Right chopstick | | Deadlock condition | All $N$ processes hold 1 resource | Circular wait formed | | Readcount increment | `readcount = readcount + 1` | On reader entry | | Readcount decrement | `readcount = readcount - 1` | On reader exit | | First reader lock | `readcount == 1 \Rightarrow \text{wait}(mutex)$ | Triggers database lock | | Last reader release | `readcount == 0 \Rightarrow \text{signal}(mutex)$ | Triggers database unlock | | Mutex semaphore | Binary $\{0, 1\}$ | Initial value: **1** | | rw_mutex semaphore | Binary $\{0, 1\}$ | Initial value: **1** | | turnstile semaphore | Binary $\{0, 1\}$ | Initial value: **1** (writers preference) | | readcount variable | Non-negative integer | Initial value: **0** | --- ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility **Dining Philosophers Application — Connection Pool:** A web server has a **fixed pool of 5 database connections**. Each query handler (philosopher) needs **2 connections** to perform a join operation. The dining philosophers' solution (monitor-based) ensures no deadlock when 5 queries execute concurrently. **Readers-Writers Application — Cache Invalidation:** In a **memcached/Redis** system, multiple application servers (readers) read cached values, while one server (writer) updates the cache. The readers-writers solution ensures updates are atomic without blocking all reads. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Deadlock Verification in Naive Dining Philosophers Solution **Step 1:** Assume all 5 philosophers become hungry at time $t_0$. **Step 2:** Each $P_i$ executes `wait(chopstick[i])` simultaneously. Since all chopsticks are free, all 5 `wait` operations succeed. **Step 3:** At time $t_0 + \epsilon$, each $P_i$ attempts `wait(chopstick[(i+1) \bmod 5])`. **Step 4:** For $P_0$: needs $C_1$, but $P_1$ holds $C_1$. **Blocked.** For $P_1$: needs $C_2$, but $P_2$ holds $C_2$. **Blocked.** For $P_2$: needs $C_3$, but $P_3$ holds $C_3$. **Blocked.** For $P_3$: needs $C_4$, but $P_4$ holds $C_4$. **Blocked.** For $P_4$: needs $C_0$, but $P_0$ holds $C_0$. **Blocked.** **Step 5:** Resource allocation graph contains a cycle: $$P_0 \rightarrow C_0 \rightarrow P_4 \rightarrow C_4 \rightarrow P_3 \rightarrow C_3 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow C_2 \rightarrow P_1 \rightarrow C_1 \rightarrow P_0$$ **Step 6:** The cycle has length $2N = 10$ edges (alternating process-resource-process). **Conclusion:** Deadlock is guaranteed whenever all philosophers transition THINKING $\rightarrow$ HUNGRY simultaneously and acquire left chopsticks in the same order. --- ### 3.2 Proof of Deadlock-Freedom in Resource Hierarchy Solution **Claim:** If every philosopher picks up the **lower-numbered chopstick first**, the system is deadlock-free. **Proof by Contradiction:** Assume deadlock occurs. Then every $P_i$ holds exactly one chopstick and is waiting for another. **Step 1:** Philosopher $P_i$ holds one chopstick and waits for another. Without loss of generality, suppose $P_i$ holds $C_a$ and waits for $C_b$. **Step 2:** By the resource hierarchy rule, $P_i$ acquired the **lower** of $\{C_a, C_b\}$ first. So $a < b$. **Step 3:** For $P_i$ to be waiting on $C_b$, the holder of $C_b$ must be $P_{i+1}$ (or $P_{i-1}$, depending on orientation). In the circular layout, $P_{i+1}$ is waiting for the chopstick held by $P_{i+2}$, and so on. **Step 4:** Following the wait chain: $$P_i \text{ holds } C_a, \text{ waits } C_b$$ $$P_{i+1} \text{ holds } C_b, \text{ waits } C_c \text{ (where } b < c\text{)}$$ $$P_{i+2} \text{ holds } C_c, \text{ waits } C_d \text{ (where } c < d\text{)}$$ $$\vdots$$ $$P_{i+N-1} \text{ holds } C_z, \text{ waits } C_a \text{ (where } z < a\text{)}$$ **Step 5:** Combining the inequalities: $$a < b < c < d < \dots < z < a$$ **Step 6:** This is a contradiction. $\Rightarrow$ No deadlock can exist. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.3 Readers-Writers — Trace of readcount Transitions **Scenario:** Three readers $R_1, R_2, R_3$ and one writer $W_1$ arrive in order $R_1, W_1, R_2, R_3$. **Variant 1 (Readers Preference) Trace:** | Step | Action | rw_mutex | readcount | mutex | Database State | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $R_1$ `wait(rw_mutex)` | 0 | 0 | 1 | idle | | 2 | $R_1$: readcount=1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | idle | | 3 | $R_1$: readcount==1, `wait(mutex)` | 0 | 1 | 0 | **R-locked** | | 4 | $R_1$ `signal(rw_mutex)` | 1 | 1 | 0 | R-locked | | 5 | $R_1$ **READS** | 1 | 1 | 0 | R-active | | 6 | $W_1$ `wait(mutex)` | 1 | 1 | 0 | **W-blocked** | | 7 | $R_2$ `wait(rw_mutex)` | 0 | 1 | 0 | R-active | | 8 | $R_2$: readcount=2 | 0 | 2 | 0 | R-active | | 9 | $R_2$ `signal(rw_mutex)` | 1 | 2 | 0 | R-active | | 10 | $R_2$ **READS** | 1 | 2 | 0 | R-active | | 11 | $R_3$ `wait(rw_mutex)` | 0 | 2 | 0 | R-active | | 12 | $R_3$: readcount=3 | 0 | 3 | 0 | R-active | | 13 | $R_3$ `signal(rw_mutex)` | 1 | 3 | 0 | R-active | | 14 | $R_3$ **READS** | 1 | 3 | 0 | R-active | | 15 | $R_1$ finishes, `wait(rw_mutex)` | 0 | 3 | 0 | R-active | | 16 | $R_1$: readcount=2 | 0 | 2 | 0 | R-active | | 17 | $R_1$ `signal(rw_mutex)` | 1 | 2 | 0 | R-active | | 18 | $R_2$ finishes, readcount=1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | R-active | | 19 | $R_3$ finishes, readcount=0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | R-locked | | 20 | $R_3$: readcount==0, `signal(mutex)` | 1 | 0 | 1 | **W-unblocked** | | 21 | $W_1$ `wait(mutex)` succeeds | 1 | 0 | 0 | **W-locked** | | 22 | $W_1$ **WRITES** | 1 | 0 | 0 | W-active | **Observation:** $W_1$ waited through **3 readers** because of readers preference. This is **writer starvation potential**. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | Naive DP | Resource Hierarchy | Monitor Solution | First RW | Second RW | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Deadlock-free?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | **Starvation-free?** | ❌ No | ❌ Possible | ✅ Yes (FIFO) | ❌ Writer starvation | ❌ Reader starvation | | **Mutual Exclusion?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | **No Busy Waiting?** | ❌ Busy wait | ❌ Busy wait | ✅ Yes | ❌ Busy wait | ❌ Busy wait | | **Concurrent Reads?** | N/A | N/A | N/A | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | **Semaphores Used** | 5 | 5 | 1 mutex + 5 self | 2 | 3 | | **Complexity** | Low | Low | Medium | Low | Medium | --- ### 3.5 Derivation of State Transition Function for Monitor Solution Define the **enabled** predicate for philosopher $P_i$: $$\text{enabled}(i) \equiv \big( \text{state}[i] = \text{HUNGRY} \big) \wedge \big( \text{state}[\text{LEFT}(i)] \neq \text{EATING} \big) \wedge \big( \text{state}[\text{RIGHT}(i)] \neq \text{EATING} \big)$$ If `enabled(i)` is true, then `test(i)` sets `state[i] = EATING` and `signal(self[i])`. **Transition Function $T$:** $$T(\text{state}) = \begin{cases} \text{state}[i] \leftarrow \text{EATING} & \text{if } \text{enabled}(i) \\ \text{state}[j] \leftarrow \text{THINKING} & \text{if } j \text{ is current eater} \end{cases}$$ **Safety Property:** The system never transitions to a state where two adjacent philosophers are simultaneously EATING. **Proof:** $\text{state}[i] = \text{EATING} \Rightarrow \text{state}[\text{LEFT}(i)] \neq \text{EATING} \wedge \text{state}[\text{RIGHT}(i)] \neq \text{EATING}$. Since LEFT and RIGHT of $P_i$ are $P_i$'s immediate neighbors, no two adjacent philosophers eat. $\blacksquare$ **Liveness Property:** If philosopher $P_i$ is HUNGRY, they will eventually EAT (assuming no deadlock and FIFO scheduling of the `mutex`). --- ### 3.6 Step-by-Step Pseudocode for Monitor Solution (Full Trace) Consider the scenario: $P_0$ is EATING, then $P_0$ finishes, then $P_2$ and $P_4$ become HUNGRY. ``` Initial: state = [THINKING, THINKING, THINKING, THINKING, THINKING] T1: P_0 calls pickup(0) wait(mutex) → mutex = 0 state[0] = HUNGRY test(0): state[LEFT(0)] = state[4] = THINKING ≠ EATING ✓ state[RIGHT(0)] = state[1] = THINKING ≠ EATING ✓ state[0] = EATING signal(self[0]) → self[0] = 1 signal(mutex) → mutex = 1 wait(self[0]) → self[0] = 0 (already signaled) P_0 EATING T2: P_0 finishes, calls putdown(0) wait(mutex) → mutex = 0 state[0] = THINKING test(LEFT) = test(4): state[4] = THINKING, skip test(RIGHT) = test(1): state[1] = THINKING, skip signal(mutex) → mutex = 1 T3: P_2 calls pickup(2) wait(mutex) → mutex = 0 state[2] = HUNGRY test(2): state[1] = THINKING ✓ state[3] = THINKING ✓ state[2] = EATING signal(self[2]) signal(mutex) P_2 EATING T4: P_2 finishes, putdown(2) test(1) and test(3) — both THINKING, no change T5: P_4 calls pickup(4) state[4] = HUNGRY test(4): state[3] = THINKING ✓ state[0] = THINKING ✓ state[4] = EATING signal(self[4]) P_4 EATING ``` This demonstrates **non-adjacent philosophers can EAT concurrently** ($P_2$ and $P_4$ could eat simultaneously since they are separated by $P_3$). ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **State the Dining Philosophers Problem. Explain why a naive solution leads to deadlock.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Dining Philosophers Problem** (Dijkstra, 1965) describes 5 philosophers sitting around a circular table with 5 chopsticks placed between them. Each philosopher alternates between thinking and eating, and requires **2 chopsticks** (the one on their left and the one on their right) to eat. In the **naive solution**, each philosopher picks up the left chopstick first using `wait(chopstick[i])`, then the right chopstick using `wait(chopstick[(i+1) mod 5])`. If all 5 philosophers become hungry simultaneously, each picks up their left chopstick successfully. Now, each philosopher attempts to pick up the right chopstick, but it is already held by the neighbor. This creates a **circular wait**: $P_0 \rightarrow P_1 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow P_3 \rightarrow P_4 \rightarrow P_0$. All four Coffman deadlock conditions (mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait) are satisfied, and the system deadlocks — every philosopher starves forever. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Differentiate between the First and Second Readers-Writers Problems.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | First Readers-Writers | Second Readers-Writers | |---|---|---| | **Preference** | Readers preference | Writers preference | | **Writer Starvation** | Possible (writer may wait indefinitely) | Prevented | | **Reader Starvation** | Prevented | Possible (readers may wait indefinitely) | | **Semaphores Used** | `mutex`, `rw_mutex` | `mutex`, `rw_mutex`, `turnstile` | | **Mechanism** | Once readers start, no writer can enter | Once writer is waiting, new readers are blocked | | **Use Case** | Read-heavy workloads (e.g., analytics dashboards) | Write-heavy workloads (e.g., log aggregators) | The **First Problem** uses a `readcount` counter and a `mutex` on the database. The **Second Problem** adds a `turnstile` semaphore that forces arriving readers to wait when a writer is queued. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Q3 (a)** Explain the Dining Philosophers Problem in detail. Discuss the conditions that lead to deadlock in the naive solution. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition (2 marks):** The Dining Philosophers Problem is a classical synchronization problem formulated by Edsger Dijkstra. It involves **N philosophers** (typically N=5) sitting around a circular table with **N chopsticks** placed between adjacent philosophers. Each philosopher alternates between three states: **THINKING**, **HUNGRY**, and **EATING**. To transition from HUNGRY to EATING, a philosopher must acquire **two chopsticks** — the one on their left and the one on their right. **Naive Solution (2 marks):** Each philosopher $P_i$ executes: ``` wait(chopstick[i]); // pick up left wait(chopstick[(i+1) mod 5]); // pick up right // EAT signal(chopstick[i]); // put down left signal(chopstick[(i+1) mod 5]); // put down right // THINK ``` **Deadlock Analysis (3 marks):** When all 5 philosophers become hungry simultaneously: 1. **Mutual Exclusion:** Each chopstick can be held by only one philosopher at a time. 2. **Hold and Wait:** Each philosopher holds the left chopstick and waits for the right chopstick. 3. **No Preemption:** A chopstick cannot be forcibly taken from a philosopher. 4. **Circular Wait:** $P_0 \rightarrow P_1 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow P_3 \rightarrow P_4 \rightarrow P_0$ forms a cycle. Since all four Coffman conditions are satisfied, **deadlock occurs**. The resource allocation graph contains a cycle of length 10 (5 processes + 5 resources). **(7 marks total)** --- **Q3 (b)** With suitable pseudocode, explain the **Monitor-based solution** to the Dining Philosophers Problem. Show that it ensures **no deadlock and no starvation**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Data Structures (2 marks):** - `state[5]` array: Each entry is `THINKING`, `HUNGRY`, or `EATING` (initially all `THINKING`) - `self[5]`: Array of semaphores, one per philosopher (initially 0) - `mutex`: Binary semaphore (initially 1) protecting the `state` array **Procedures (3 marks):** `pickup(i)`: ``` wait(mutex); state[i] = HUNGRY; test(i); signal(mutex); wait(self[i]); ``` `putdown(i)`: ``` wait(mutex); state[i] = THINKING; test(LEFT); test(RIGHT); signal(mutex); ``` `test(i)`: ``` if (state[i] == HUNGRY && state[LEFT] != EATING && state[RIGHT] != EATING) { state[i] = EATING; signal(self[i]); } ``` **Correctness Proof (2 marks):** - **No Deadlock:** The `mutex` ensures atomic state transitions. A philosopher enters EATING only when both neighbors are NOT EATING. The system can never reach a state where all 5 are HUNGRY and blocked because `putdown` always tests both neighbors. - **No Starvation:** Under FIFO scheduling of `mutex`, every HUNGRY philosopher will eventually be tested by a neighboring putdown and granted `self[i]`. Hence, every philosopher eventually eats. **(7 marks total)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]** **Q4 (a)** Describe the **Readers-Writers Problem**. Write the pseudocode for the **First Readers-Writers Problem** and explain how mutual exclusion is achieved. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Problem Description (2 marks):** The Readers-Writers Problem coordinates concurrent access to a shared database. Multiple readers can access the data simultaneously (since reads do not modify the data). However, a writer requires **exclusive access** — no readers or other writers may access the data during a write.
