The challenge is to allow maximum concurrent reads while ensuring write atomicity. **Data Structures (1 mark):** - `rw_mutex` = 1 (binary semaphore, protects `readcount`) - `mutex` = 1 (binary semaphore, exclusive database access) - `readcount` = 0 (number of active readers) **Pseudocode (3 marks):** **Writer Process:** ``` wait(mutex); // WRITE to database signal(mutex); ``` **Reader Process:** ``` wait(rw_mutex); readcount++; if (readcount == 1) wait(mutex); signal(rw_mutex); // READ database wait(rw_mutex); readcount--; if (readcount == 0) signal(mutex); signal(rw_mutex); ``` **Mutual Exclusion Explanation (1 mark):** - The **first reader** (when `readcount` becomes 1) acquires `mutex`, locking out writers. - **Subsequent readers** increment `readcount` but do not reacquire `mutex`, allowing concurrent reads. - The **last reader** (when `readcount` drops to 0) releases `mutex`, allowing writers in. - A **writer** must acquire `mutex` directly, which is only available when no readers are active. **Drawback:** Writer starvation is possible if readers keep arriving. **(7 marks total)** --- **Q4 (b)** Explain the **Second Readers-Writers Problem** (Writers Preference) with pseudocode. How does it prevent writer starvation? Why might reader starvation still occur? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Writers Preference Mechanism (2 marks):** The Second Readers-Writers Problem adds a `turnstile` semaphore (initial value = 1) to block new readers when a writer is waiting. This gives priority to writers. **Pseudocode (3 marks):** **Reader Process:** ``` wait(turnstile); wait(rw_mutex); readcount++; if (readcount == 1) wait(mutex); signal(rw_mutex); signal(turnstile); // READ database wait(rw_mutex); readcount--; if (readcount == 0) signal(mutex); signal(rw_mutex); ``` **Writer Process:** ``` wait(turnstile); wait(mutex); // WRITE database signal(mutex); signal(turnstile); ``` **Prevention of Writer Starvation (1 mark):** When a writer arrives, it acquires `turnstile` (setting it to 0). Any subsequent readers must also `wait(turnstile)` and will be blocked. Existing readers finish their reads, and the `mutex` becomes available, allowing the writer to proceed. The writer holds `turnstile` until it completes, ensuring no new readers can enter during the write. **Reader Starvation Scenario (1 mark):** If writers keep arriving continuously, each new writer acquires `turnstile` before releasing it for the next. Readers arriving during this time are blocked on `turnstile` indefinitely, leading to reader starvation. The solution trades writer starvation (Problem 1) for reader starvation (Problem 2). **(7 marks total)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting to release `rw_mutex`:** In Readers-Writers, students often forget the final `signal(rw_mutex)` after the read completes, causing permanent block of `rw_mutex`. (Lose 2 marks) > 2. **Confusing `mutex` and `rw_mutex`:** `rw_mutex` protects `readcount`. `mutex` protects the database. Swapping them breaks the solution entirely. (Lose 3 marks) > 3. **In Dining Philosophers, students often use only one semaphore per chopstick without explaining the deadlock conditions.** Always explicitly state all **four Coffman conditions** that lead to deadlock. (Lose 2 marks) > 4. **Not specifying N=5:** Always state that the classical problem has **N=5 philosophers and N=5 chopsticks**. > 5. **Missing the `signal(turnstile)` in writer:** Forgetting to release the turnstile after writing causes permanent block of all subsequent readers. (Lose 2 marks) --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Dining Philosophers & Readers-Writers** #### Dining Philosophers - ⭐ Classical problem by **Dijkstra (1965)** with **N=5 philosophers and N=5 chopsticks** - ⭐ Each philosopher needs **2 adjacent chopsticks** to eat - ⭐ **Naive solution deadlock** occurs when all 5 pick up left chopstick simultaneously — circular wait forms - ⭐ **Four Coffman conditions** must all hold: Mutual Exclusion, Hold & Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait - ⭐ **Solution 1 — Resource Hierarchy:** Pick up lower-numbered chopstick first; breaks circular wait (deadlock-free, but starvation possible) - ⭐ **Solution 2 — Arbitrator/Monitor:** Uses `mutex` + `state[5]` array + `self[5]` semaphores - ⭐ `test(i)` checks if both neighbors are not EATING before granting `self[i]` - ⭐ `pickup(i)` sets state to HUNGRY and calls `test(i)` - ⭐ `putdown(i)` sets state to THINKING and calls `test(LEFT)` and `test(RIGHT)` - ⭐ Monitor solution is **deadlock-free and starvation-free** (under FIFO) #### Readers-Writers - ⭐ Multiple **readers** can read **concurrently** (shared read is safe) - ⭐ **Writer** needs **exclusive access** to the database - ⭐ **First Problem (Readers Preference):** `readcount` counter, `rw_mutex` protects counter, `mutex` protects database - ⭐ **First reader** (readcount == 1) acquires `mutex`; **last reader** (readcount == 0) releases `mutex` - ⭐ **First Problem** suffers from **writer starvation** - ⭐ **Second Problem (Writers Preference):** Adds `turnstile` semaphore - ⭐ Readers and writers both `wait(turnstile)` on entry; writer `signal(turnstile)` after writing - ⭐ **Second Problem** suffers from **reader starvation** - ⭐ **Fair Solution:** Use FIFO queue or `okToRead`/`okToWrite` flags to prevent both starvations - ⭐ All semaphores initialized to **1**; `readcount` initialized to **0** - ⭐ Mutual exclusion between readers and writers is achieved via the `mutex` on the database #### Key Differences to Remember - ⭐ **Dining Philosophers:** Fixed number of resources, each process needs **multiple** resources — focuses on **deadlock** - ⭐ **Readers-Writers:** Single shared resource with **type-based access** rules — focuses on **starvation** - ⭐ Both problems demonstrate that **simple `wait`/`signal` is insufficient** — careful ordering and additional state are required

topics.title: Concept of Address Spaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Concept of Address Spaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Address Space** is the range of valid memory addresses that a process can use to reference instructions and data during its execution. In the context of modern Operating Systems, the concept of address space is broadly classified into three hierarchical abstractions:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definitions (KTU Board Standard)**
> 1. **Logical Address Space (Virtual Address Space):** The set of all logical addresses generated by the CPU for a process. It is the address space as perceived by the *process* — beginning typically from address **0** and extending up to a maximum value $N-1$, where $N$ is the size of the logical address space.
> 2. **Physical Address Space:** The set of all physical (real hardware) addresses corresponding to locations in the **Primary Memory (RAM)**. These are the actual locations where instructions and data reside.
> 3. **Virtual Address Space:** A memory management abstraction that decouples the logical address space of a process from the actual physical memory available on the system, allowing execution even when the entire process is not present in RAM.

The relationship between these address spaces is governed by a hardware component called the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)**, which performs **run-time address binding (translation)** at the hardware level.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Room" Intuition

Imagine a large international conference where **100 delegates** (processes) have registered. The conference hotel has only **50 physical rooms** available at any given time.

- Each delegate is given a **room number card** (Logical Address) that always starts with **Room 0** in their own imagination. Delegate A thinks they are in Room 0, Room 1, Room 2... even though they might physically be in actual Room 314.
- The **front desk manager** (MMU) maintains a private register book that translates each delegate's logical room number into the actual physical room number. For example, Delegate A's Room 0 $\rightarrow$ Physical Room 314; Room 1 $\rightarrow$ Room 207.
- If a delegate tries to access a room number outside their permitted range (e.g., Room 100 when they only have 3 rooms), the manager rejects the request (memory protection / bounds checking).

This is precisely how **logical-to-physical address translation** works: each process believes it owns a contiguous, private address space starting from zero, but the OS and hardware collaborate to map it onto the *fragmented, shared, and limited* physical memory.

## 1.3 Why Address Spaces Matter

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Address Space Abstraction:**
> - **Process Isolation:** Each process operates in its own private address space — processes cannot accidentally read/write into another process's memory.
> - **Memory Efficiency:** A process need not be loaded entirely in physical memory; only the *currently active* pages/segments need to be resident.
> - **Programming Simplicity:** The programmer can write code assuming the process owns memory from address 0, without worrying about where it will actually be placed in RAM.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Tier Address Model

In any modern general-purpose OS, the CPU only ever generates **logical (virtual) addresses**. The transformation to a physical address is the responsibility of the **MMU**, which sits between the CPU and physical memory.

### 2.1.1 Address Generation Pipeline

1. The CPU fetches an instruction; the instruction contains a memory operand.
2. The CPU computes the **Effective Logical Address** (after any indexing, indirection, or base-register offset performed by the program).
3. The CPU places this logical address on the **address bus** and requests a memory read/write.
4. The **MMU intercepts** the logical address and translates it to a physical address using translation tables.
5. The **Limit Register** is consulted to verify the access is within the process's permitted range.
6. On success, the physical address is placed on the memory bus; on failure, a **trap** is generated (segmentation fault / memory access violation).

## 2.2 Address Binding — The Three Binding Times

A program goes through several transformations before execution. The binding of an instruction/data to a physical memory address can occur at any of these stages:

| Binding Time | When It Happens | Flexibility | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compile Time** | During compilation | **None** — fixed absolute addresses | Embedded firmware, MS-DOS `.COM` programs |
| **Load Time** | When the program is loaded into memory | **Relocatable** — addresses must be re-bound if process is moved | Early mainframes |
| **Execution Time (Run-Time)** | During program execution | **Maximum** — process can be moved mid-execution | All modern OS (Linux, Windows, macOS) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Modern systems use **run-time binding** because it enables *swapping*, *paging*, and *virtual memory* — none of which would be possible with compile-time or load-time binding.

## 2.3 Logical vs. Physical Address Space — Mathematical View

Let:
- $L$ = Logical address space size
- $P$ = Physical address space size
- $b$ = Number of bits in the logical address
- $p$ = Number of bits in the physical address
- $B$ = Base register value
- $L_{reg}$ = Limit register value (size of process)

Then:

- The logical address ranges from $[0, 2^b - 1]$.
- The physical address ranges from $[0, 2^p - 1]$.
- For a process with base $B$ and limit $L_{reg}$, the legal logical address $X$ must satisfy $0 \le X < L_{reg}$.

## 2.4 The MMU with Base & Limit Registers (Simple Scheme)

The simplest MMU uses two special hardware registers:

- **Base Register:** Holds the starting physical address of the current process.
- **Limit Register:** Holds the size of the logical address space of the current process.

### 2.4.1 Translation Equation

For every logical address $X$ generated by the CPU, the MMU performs the following:

$$
X_{physical} = B + X_{logical}
$$

subject to the precondition:

$$
0 \le X_{logical} < L_{reg}
$$

If $X_{logical} \ge L_{reg}$, a **trap to the OS** is generated (illegal memory access).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Ready Formula Table** (Use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid markdown table breakage)

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X_{physical} = B + X_{logical}$ | Base-register translation | bytes |
| 2 | $0 \le X_{logical} < L_{reg}$ | Bounds check condition | dimensionless |
| 3 | $L = 2^b$ | Size of logical address space | bytes |
| 4 | $P = 2^p$ | Size of physical address space | bytes |
| 5 | $X_{physical} = \text{Frame}_\# \times \text{Frame Size} + \text{Offset}$ | Paging translation | bytes |
| 6 | $X_{logical} = \text{Page}_\# \times \text{Page Size} + \text{Offset}$ | Logical address decomposition | bytes |
| 7 | $n_{pages} = \lceil L \div \text{Page Size} \rceil$ | Number of pages in a process | pages |
| 8 | $n_{frames} = P \div \text{Frame Size}$ | Number of frames in physical memory | frames |
| 9 | $EAT = (1 - p) \times T_{mem} + p \times T_{TLB}$ | Effective Access Time with TLB | nanoseconds |
| 10 | $A = \lfloor X_{logical} \div \text{Page Size} \rfloor$ | Page number extraction | dimensionless |
| 11 | $d = X_{logical} \bmod \text{Page Size}$ | Page offset extraction | bytes |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The address space abstraction is the **foundation of modern computing** and underpins:
- **Cloud Computing:** Virtual machines each have their own isolated address space over shared hardware.
- **Embedded Systems:** IoT devices use lightweight MMUs to run multiple RTOS tasks safely.
- **Process Sandboxing:** Browsers (Chrome) run each tab in a separate address space for security.
- **Kernel Protection:** The OS kernel occupies the *upper* portion of the virtual address space, and user processes cannot write to it — preventing system crashes.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Logical-to-Physical Translation (Base-Limit Scheme)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)**
> A system uses a base-register scheme with two registers: **Base Register (B)** and **Limit Register (L)**. For a process P, $B = 14000$ and $L_{reg} = 5000$. The CPU generates the following logical addresses during execution. Determine whether each is legal, and if so, compute the physical address.
> - Logical address (i) $X = 3200$
> - Logical address (ii) $X = 15000$

### 3.1.1 Detailed Derivation for $X = 3200$

**Step 1 — State the bounds check condition.**

Every logical address $X$ generated by the CPU must satisfy the fundamental bounds-check inequality:

$$
0 \le X < L_{reg}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values.**

The given parameters are: $X = 3200$ and $L_{reg} = 5000$. Substituting:

$$
0 \le 3200 < 5000
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the inequality.**

The lower bound $0 \le 3200$ is **true**, and the upper bound $3200 < 5000$ is **true**. Therefore, the inequality is satisfied in its entirety, and the access is **legal**.

**Step 4 — Apply the base-register translation equation.**

The MMU computes the physical address using:

$$
X_{physical} = B + X_{logical}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the base and logical values.**

With $B = 14000$ and $X_{logical} = 3200$:

$$
X_{physical} = 14000 + 3200
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the sum.**

$$
X_{physical} = 17200
$$

**Conclusion for (i):** The logical address $X = 3200$ is **legal** and maps to physical address **17200**.

### 3.1.2 Detailed Derivation for $X = 15000$

**Step 1 — State the bounds check condition.**

$$
0 \le X < L_{reg}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values.**

With $X = 15000$ and $L_{reg} = 5000$:

$$
0 \le 15000 < 5000
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the inequality.**

The lower bound $0 \le 15000$ is true, but the upper bound $15000 < 5000$ is **false**. The inequality fails on the upper bound.

**Step 4 — Conclude with a trap.**

Since the access is illegal, the MMU does **not** compute a physical address. Instead, it raises a **trap** to the operating system, which typically terminates the process with a segmentation fault (or in older systems, a memory protection violation error).

**Conclusion for (ii):** The logical address $X = 15000$ is **illegal**; no translation is performed, and a **trap** is generated.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Number of Pages and Frames

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (Common KTU 3-Mark Question)**
> A system has a logical address space of size **16 KB** and a physical address space of **64 KB**. The page size is **2 KB**. Compute:
> 1. Number of bits in the logical address
> 2. Number of bits in the physical address
> 3. Number of pages in the process
> 4. Number of frames in physical memory
> 5. The page number and offset for logical address $X = 5000$

### 3.2.1 Derivation — Number of Bits in Logical Address

**Step 1 — Express logical address space size in bytes.**

$$
L = 16 \text{ KB} = 16 \times 1024 \text{ bytes} = 16384 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the relation $L = 2^b$ and solve for $b$.**

$$
2^b = 16384 = 2^{14}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclude.**

$$
b = 14 \text{ bits}
$$

### 3.2.2 Derivation — Number of Bits in Physical Address

**Step 1 — Express physical address space size in bytes.**

$$
P = 64 \text{ KB} = 64 \times 1024 \text{ bytes} = 65536 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply $P = 2^p$.**

$$
2^p = 65536 = 2^{16}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclude.**

$$
p = 16 \text{ bits}
$$

### 3.2.3 Derivation — Number of Pages in the Process

**Step 1 — State the formula.**

$$
n_{pages} = \frac{L}{\text{Page Size}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
n_{pages} = \frac{16 \text{ KB}}{2 \text{ KB}} = 8 \text{ pages}
$$

### 3.2.4 Derivation — Number of Frames in Physical Memory

**Step 1 — State the formula.**

$$
n_{frames} = \frac{P}{\text{Frame Size}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
n_{frames} = \frac{64 \text{ KB}}{2 \text{ KB}} = 32 \text{ frames}
$$

### 3.2.5 Derivation — Page Number and Offset for $X = 5000$

**Step 1 — Compute the page number $A$.**

$$
A = \left\lfloor \frac{X_{logical}}{\text{Page Size}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{5000}{2048} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 2.441\ldots \rfloor = 2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the offset $d$.**

$$
d = X_{logical} \bmod \text{Page Size} = 5000 \bmod 2048
$$

To compute this, perform integer division: $5000 = 2 \times 2048 + 904$. Therefore:

$$
d = 904 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification using the decomposition equation.**

$$
X_{logical} = A \times \text{Page Size} + d = 2 \times 2048 + 904 = 4096 + 904 = 5000 \ \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** Logical address $X = 5000$ resides in **Page 2**, with an **offset of 904 bytes** within that page.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Binding Times

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Comparison Table for KTU 14-Mark Questions**

| Parameter | Compile-Time | Load-Time | Run-Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **When binding occurs** | During compilation | When loaded into RAM | During execution (every memory reference) |
| **Code is relocatable?** | No | Partially (must re-load) | Fully relocatable |
| **Hardware support needed** | None | None (or simple) | MMU required |
| **Supports swapping?** | No | No | Yes |
| **Supports virtual memory?** | No | No | Yes |
| **Performance cost** | Zero (cheapest) | Low | Higher (MMU lookup) |
| **Used in modern systems?** | Embedded only | Mostly obsolete | **Universal** |
| **Example system** | MS-DOS `.COM` | Early UNIX (pre-virtual memory) | Linux, Windows, macOS |
| **Memory protection possible?** | No | Limited | Yes (via limit register) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Compile-time and load-time binding schemes cannot support the concept of *processes larger than physical memory* (no virtual memory). Run-time binding is the only scheme that enables the modern abstraction of *each process having a private, isolated, possibly-larger-than-RAM address space*.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — Logical-to-Physical Address Translation (Block-Level Architecture)

This functional architecture illustrates the flow of an address from the CPU to physical memory, including the role of the MMU and the protection registers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["CPU Core"]
    LA["Logical Address Bus"]
    MMU["MMU Hardware Unit"]
    BR["Base Register"]
    LR["Limit Register"]
    PBUS["Physical Address Bus"]
    PM["Physical RAM Modules"]
    TRAP["Trap to OS Kernel"]
    PROC["Process Control Block"]

    CPU --> LA
    LA --> MMU
    BR --> MMU
    LR --> MMU
    PROC --> BR
    PROC --> LR
    MMU --> PBUS
    PBUS --> PM
    MMU --> TRAP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The CPU emits a logical address on the address bus.
> - The MMU reads the base register and limit register from the PCB-stored process context.
> - The MMU validates the logical address against the limit, computes the physical address via $X_{physical} = B + X_{logical}$, and emits the result on the physical address bus.
> - On a violation, a trap is raised to the OS kernel.

## 4.2 Figure 2 — Multiple Processes Sharing Physical Memory

This diagram shows how three concurrent processes each have their **own logical address space starting at 0**, but are mapped to *discontiguous* physical frames by the MMU.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Logical["LOGICAL ADDRESS SPACES PER PROCESS"]
        P1["Process 1: 0 to 9999"]
        P2["Process 2: 0 to 14999"]
        P3["Process 3: 0 to 7999"]
    end

    subgraph MMUBlock["MMU Translation Layer"]
        T1["Map P1: Base = 30000"]
        T2["Map P2: Base = 40000"]
        T3["Map P3: Base = 20000"]
    end

    subgraph Physical["PHYSICAL MEMORY"]
        F1["Frame: 20000 to 27999 (P3)"]
        F2["Frame: 30000 to 39999 (P1)"]
        F3["Frame: 40000 to 54999 (P2)"]
        F4["OS Kernel: 0 to 19999"]
    end

    P1 --> T1
    P2 --> T2
    P3 --> T3
    T1 --> F2
    T2 --> F3
    T3 --> F1
```

## 4.3 Figure 3 — Address Binding Lifecycle (Three Binding Times)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["Source Code .c file"]
    COMP["Compiler Phase"]
    OBJ["Object Code with Relative Addresses"]
    LOAD["Loader Phase"]
    MEM["Memory with Absolute Addresses"]
    EXEC["Execution Phase with MMU"]

    SRC --> COMP --> OBJ --> LOAD --> MEM --> EXEC

    CB["Compile-Time Binding" ]
    LB["Load-Time Binding" ]
    RB["Run-Time Binding" ]

    COMP -.-> CB
    LOAD -.-> LB
    EXEC -.-> RB
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The diagram shows how a program's addresses get resolved at progressively later stages. The later the binding, the more flexible the system — but the more hardware/software support it requires.

## 4.4 Figure 4 — Paging Address Decomposition (Logical-to-Physical Walk)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LA["Logical Address X"]
    LA --> DECOMP["Page Number and Offset Extraction"]
    DECOMP --> PN["Page Number p"]
    DECOMP --> OFF["Offset d"]
    PN --> PT["Page Table Lookup"]
    PT --> FN["Frame Number f"]
    FN --> CONCAT["Concatenate Frame Number with Offset"]
    OFF --> CONCAT
    CONCAT --> PA["Final Physical Address"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** A logical address is decomposed into a page number and an offset. The page number is used as an index into the page table, which yields the frame number. The frame number (replacing the page number) concatenated with the original offset gives the physical address. The key equation is:
>
> $$X_{physical} = f \times \text{Frame Size} + d$$
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part A questions test direct factual recall and concise definitions. Each answer should be 3–5 sentences maximum.

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between logical address and physical address with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

A **logical address** is the address generated by the CPU during program execution — it is a *virtual* address and exists only in the context of the running process. A **physical address** is the *real* location in the main memory (RAM) hardware where the instruction or data actually resides.

**Example:** When a program references address `0x00001000`, the CPU generates this as a logical address. The MMU translates it to a physical address such as `0x1FFFA000` based on where the process is currently loaded in RAM. Logical addresses provide process isolation and enable virtual memory; physical addresses correspond to actual hardware memory cells.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of the Memory Management Unit (MMU) in address translation?**

**Model Answer:**

The **MMU** is a special-purpose hardware component located between the CPU and physical memory. Its primary role is to perform **run-time translation of logical addresses to physical addresses** using values stored in the base register, limit register, or page tables.

Additionally, the MMU enforces **memory protection** by validating every memory reference against the process's permitted range (limit check), raising a trap to the OS on illegal accesses. In modern systems, the MMU also cooperates with the **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** to cache recent translations and speed up address resolution.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Module-internal choice is mandatory. Both Question A and Question B below belong to Module 4 and are mutually exclusive choices.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of address space. Differentiate between **logical address space** and **physical address space** with a neat diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **base-register** and **limit-register** scheme for address translation. Given $B = 4000$ and $L = 2000$, determine whether the following logical addresses are valid: *(i)* $X = 1500$, *(ii)* $X = 3000$. If valid, compute the corresponding physical address. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Definition of Address Space (2 marks):**

An address space is the set of valid memory addresses that a process can use. It defines the range of addresses available to a program and is of two types: **logical (virtual)** and **physical**.

**Logical Address Space (2 marks):**

- The set of addresses generated by the CPU for a process.
- Begins at address 0 and extends up to $L - 1$, where $L$ is the size of the logical address space.
- Used by the *program*; the process is unaware of physical memory layout.

**Physical Address Space (2 marks):**

- The set of real memory addresses in primary memory (RAM).
- Each location in RAM has a unique physical address ranging from 0 to $M - 1$, where $M$ is the total RAM size.
- The MMU maps logical addresses to physical addresses.

**Neat Diagram (1 mark):**

A diagram must show two parallel rectangles — one labeled "Logical Address Space (Process View)" and another labeled "Physical Address Space (RAM View)" — with arrows showing the MMU performing translation between corresponding locations.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Description of Base-Limit Scheme (3 marks):**

In this scheme, the OS maintains two special hardware registers for each process:

- **Base Register (B):** Stores the starting physical address where the process is loaded in RAM.
- **Limit Register (L):** Stores the size (number of bytes) of the process's logical address space.

On every memory reference, the MMU performs:
1. **Bounds Check:** If $X_{logical} < L$, the access is valid; else, a trap is generated.
2. **Translation:** The physical address is computed as $X_{physical} = B + X_{logical}$.

**Sub-question (i) — $X = 1500$ (2 marks):**

Bounds check: $0 \le 1500 < 2000$. The inequality holds, so the access is **legal**.

Physical address:

$$
X_{physical} = 4000 + 1500 = 5500
$$

**Sub-question (ii) — $X = 3000$ (2 marks):**

Bounds check: $0 \le 3000 < 2000$. The upper bound **fails** ($3000 \not< 2000$). The access is **illegal**; a **trap** is generated to the OS, which terminates the process.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the different **types of address binding** with their relative merits. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A system has a logical address space of **32 KB** with a page size of **4 KB**. The physical memory is **64 KB**. For a logical address $X = 20000$: *(i)* compute the page number and offset, *(ii)* determine the number of pages and the number of frames, *(iii)* if the page table maps page 4 to frame 7, what is the physical address? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

Address binding is the mapping of a program's symbolic/instruction-level addresses to actual physical memory addresses. There are three binding times:

**Compile-Time Binding (2 marks):**
- Binding occurs during compilation itself; the compiler generates absolute physical addresses.
- *Merit:* Zero run-time overhead — fastest possible execution.
- *Demerit:* The program cannot be moved in memory once compiled; no swapping, no virtual memory.
- *Example:* MS-DOS `.COM` executable files.

**Load-Time Binding (2 marks):**
- Binding occurs when the program is loaded into memory by the loader.
- The loader rewrites all relocatable addresses to begin at the assigned base.
- *Merit:* Allows code to be loaded at any free memory location.
- *Demerit:* If the process is later moved (swapped out and back), all addresses must be re-bound — expensive and complex.
- *Example:* Early mainframe operating systems.

**Run-Time (Execution-Time) Binding (3 marks):**
- Binding is deferred until the moment of execution, performed on every memory reference by the MMU.
- *Merit:* Supports **swapping**, **paging**, **virtual memory**, **process isolation**, and dynamic memory allocation.
- *Demerit:* Requires MMU hardware support; introduces a small per-access overhead.
- *Example:* All modern general-purpose OS (Linux, Windows, macOS, Android, iOS).

> **Summary Statement:** Run-time binding is the universally adopted scheme in modern systems because it is the *only* scheme that supports the full virtual memory abstraction.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $L = 32 \text{ KB}$, Page Size $= 4 \text{ KB}$, $P = 64 \text{ KB}$, $X = 20000$, Page 4 $\rightarrow$ Frame 7.

**Sub-question (i) — Page Number and Offset (3 marks):**

Page number:

$$
A = \left\lfloor \frac{20000}{4096} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 4.8828 \rfloor = 4
$$

Offset (using $d = X \bmod \text{Page Size}$, with $20000 = 4 \times 4096 + 3616$):

$$
d = 3616 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Verification:** $4 \times 4096 + 3616 = 16384 + 3616 = 20000$ \checkmark

**Sub-question (ii) — Number of Pages and Frames (2 marks):**

Number of pages:

$$
n_{pages} = \frac{32 \text{ KB}}{4 \text{ KB}} = 8 \text{ pages}
$$

Number of frames:

$$
n_{frames} = \frac{64 \text{ KB}}{4 \text{ KB}} = 16 \text{ frames}
$$

**Sub-question (iii) — Physical Address Computation (2 marks):**

The page table maps Page 4 $\rightarrow$ Frame 7. Therefore, the frame number is $f = 7$, and the offset remains $d = 3616$. The physical address is:

$$
X_{physical} = f \times \text{Frame Size} + d = 7 \times 4096 + 3616
$$

$$
X_{physical} = 28672 + 3616 = 32288
$$

**Conclusion:** Logical address $X = 20000$ maps to physical address **32288**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation**
> 1. **Forgetting the bounds check:** Students often compute the physical address without first verifying that $X_{logical} < L_{reg}$. **Always perform the bounds check FIRST** — it is a free 1-mark insurance in every KTU paper.
> 2. **Mixing up $L$ and $P$:** When computing address space sizes, students confuse logical address space size $L$ with physical address space size $P$. Read the question carefully — "logical address space" means $L$, "physical memory" means $P$.
> 3. **Unit errors in page/frame calculation:** $1 \text{ KB} = 1024 \text{ bytes}$, **not** 1000. The valuation key **strictly** uses $1024$ for binary-based calculations. Using $1000$ is a guaranteed half-mark deduction.
> 4. **Skipping the verification step:** KTU examiners reward students who verify their answer using the decomposition equation $X = p \times \text{Page Size} + d$. **Always include this verification** to fetch full marks.
> 5. **Confusing base/limit with page/frame:** Base-limit is a *contiguous allocation* scheme; page-frame is a *non-contiguous* scheme. Don't mix the two in derivations.
> 6. **Omitting the trap explanation:** When an access is illegal, do NOT just write "illegal" — explicitly state that a **trap is generated to the OS** which then takes corrective action (e.g., process termination).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Concept of Address Spaces (Module 4)**

- **Address Space** = the range of valid memory addresses available to a process. Two kinds: **logical** and **physical**.
- **Logical Address** = generated by the CPU; **Physical Address** = location in actual RAM.
- The **MMU (Memory Management Unit)** is the hardware that performs **run-time translation** of logical to physical addresses.
- **Base Register (B)** holds the starting physical address of a process; **Limit Register (L)** holds the size of its logical address space.
- **Translation Equation:** $X_{physical} = B + X_{logical}$, valid only when $0 \le X_{logical} < L_{reg}$.
- **Three Binding Times:** Compile-time, Load-time, Run-time. Modern systems universally use **run-time binding** because it enables virtual memory, swapping, and process isolation.
- **Run-time binding is mandatory** for virtual memory, swapping, and any form of memory protection.
- **Paging splits** the address space into fixed-size pages (logical) and frames (physical); translation uses a page table.
- **Page number** $A = \lfloor X_{logical} / \text{Page Size} \rfloor$; **offset** $d = X_{logical} \bmod \text{Page Size}$.
- **Physical address** in paged system: $X_{physical} = f \times \text{Frame Size} + d$, where $f$ is the frame number from the page table.
- **Memory protection** is enforced by the limit register in the base-limit scheme, and by valid/invalid bits in the page table in the paging scheme.
- **Illegal access** $\rightarrow$ a **trap** is generated, transferring control to the OS kernel for handling (typically process termination).
- **Number of pages** $= L / \text{Page Size}$; **Number of frames** $= P / \text{Frame Size}$.
- **Number of bits in logical address** $b = \log_2(L)$; **Number of bits in physical address** $p = \log_2(P)$.
- **Every modern OS** (Linux, Windows, macOS, Android, iOS) uses **run-time binding with paging and a hardware MMU**.
- Always remember the **three benefits** of address space abstraction: **process isolation, memory efficiency, programming simplicity**.
- The **OS kernel** is typically mapped into the *upper* region of every process's virtual address space to prevent user processes from corrupting kernel memory.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Swapping** is a memory management technique in which the entire process (or a major portion of its address space) is temporarily moved from **main memory (RAM)** to **secondary storage (backing store / swap space on disk)**, and later brought back into main memory to continue execution. The action of moving a process out of RAM is called a **Roll-Out** (or **Swap-Out**), and bringing it back is called a **Roll-In** (or **Swap-In**).

The technique is governed by a specialized OS component called the **Swapper** (or in modern kernels, the *Page-Replacement Daemon* / `kswapd` in Linux). The swapped-out image is stored in a dedicated pre-allocated region of the disk known as the **Swap Space** (typically 2× to 4× the size of physical RAM in classical UNIX systems, though modern systems tune this dynamically).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Library Reading Room"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a small university library reading room that can seat only **10 students** at a time, but **30 students** are enrolled. The librarian cannot accommodate everyone. So, when a student finishes reading a book, the librarian **shelves it back (Roll-Out)** to the main library stacks and brings in a fresh waiting student (**Roll-In**).
>
> - The **reading room** = Main Memory (RAM)
> - The **main library stacks** = Secondary Storage (Hard Disk)
> - The **librarian** = Swapper (OS Module)
> - The **seat allotted to a student** = Process in Memory
> - The **card catalog used to track the student** = Swap Table / PCB Pointer

Just as a librarian must know *where* each shelved book is located, the OS maintains a **swap map** (swap table) that records the disk address corresponding to each swapped-out process. The Memory Manager consults this map whenever a swapped-out process is to be rolled back in.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Highlighted)

The following metrics are standardized for KTU 2024 board examinations:

- **Primary Memory (RAM):** Fast, volatile, limited capacity (e.g., **8 GB – 32 GB**).
- **Secondary Storage (Disk):** Slow, non-volatile, large capacity (e.g., **512 GB – 2 TB**).
- **Typical RAM access time:** $\approx$ **10 ns – 100 ns** (nanoseconds).
- **Typical Disk access time (seek + rotational + transfer):** $\approx$ **5 ms – 10 ms** (milliseconds).
- **Disk is approximately $10^5$ to $10^6$ times slower than RAM** — this disparity is the entire motivation for *minimizing* swap operations in modern systems.

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Syllabus Context

In Module 4, *Swapping* is taught as a **pre-cursor to Virtual Memory and Page Replacement**. The examiner expects students to clearly differentiate between:

1. **Standard Swapping (Whole-Process Swapping)** — covered in this topic.
2. **Swapping with Paging / Page-Level Swapping** — covered as a hybrid model in the Virtual Memory module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The Swapper executes the following structured algorithm when the degree of multiprogramming (number of resident processes) must be raised or lowered:

- **Step 1 — Detection of Memory Pressure:** The medium-term scheduler observes that RAM allocation has crossed the **High Watermark (HWM)** threshold. This is typically detected by checking the **free frame list**.
- **Step 2 — Victim Selection:** A resident process (the *victim*) is selected for roll-out. Selection policies include *lowest-priority-first*, *largest-process-first*, or simply the *least-recently-scheduled* process.
- **Step 3 — Suspend the Process:** The process state in its **Process Control Block (PCB)** is transitioned from `Ready` to `Suspended/Ready` (or `Suspended/Blocked`).
- **Step 4 — Roll-Out (Copy to Disk):** The entire address space of the victim is copied to the pre-allocated **swap space** on disk. The OS records the disk block addresses in the **swap map**.
- **Step 5 — Free Memory Frames:** All physical frames previously held by the victim are returned to the free-frame list and are now available for new processes.
- **Step 6 — Load New Process (if needed):** A new process from the **job queue** can now be rolled-in, consuming the freed frames.
- **Step 7 — Roll-In (on demand):** When the suspended process is again scheduled, the OS re-reads the entire image from swap space into RAM, restores registers from the PCB, and transitions the process back to `Ready`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is swapping called a Medium-Term Scheduler activity?** Because it acts *between* the long-term scheduler (admission to the system) and the short-term scheduler (CPU dispatch), rebalancing the in-memory process mix.

### 2.2 Variants of Swapping

| Variant | Granularity | Modern Usage |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard Swapping** | Entire process | Obsolete in mainframes/minicomputers; rare in desktops |
| **Swapping with Paging** | Only inactive pages of a process | Foundational to Virtual Memory |
| **Swapping on Mobile (Android/iOS)** | Compressed RAM / zRAM / zSWAP | Used to extend effective memory on devices with flash storage |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **authoritative reference** for any numerical problem that may appear in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Formula / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Roll-out time | $T_{out}$ | $\text{Size of process} \times \text{Transfer rate}^{-1}$ | ms |
| Roll-in time | $T_{in}$ | $\text{Size of process} \times \text{Transfer rate}^{-1}$ | ms |
| Total swap time | $T_{swap}$ | $T_{out} + T_{in}$ | ms |
| Swap time with seek | $T_{swap}$ | $2 \times (T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer})$ | ms |
| Context switch overhead | — | Approx **1–100 μs** (kernel) | μs |
| Effective Access Time (with page fault) | $EAT$ | $(1-p) \times T_{mem} + p \times T_{pf}$ | ns / μs |
| Page Fault Service Time | $T_{pf}$ | $T_{pf} = T_{swap} + T_{restart}$ | μs / ms |
| Swap Space Required | — | $\text{Size of process} \times N_{processes}$ | MB / GB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** When a problem gives you a *transfer rate* (e.g., 50 MB/s) and a *process size* (e.g., 100 MB), compute transfer time as $\frac{\text{Size}}{\text{Rate}}$ — do not confuse this with disk access time, which *adds* seek and rotational latency.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Cloud Data Centers (AWS, Azure, GCP):** Use *swap* to over-commit physical memory, achieving higher VM density per host. Memory over-commitment ratios of **1.5:1 to 2:1** are industry standard.
- **Embedded & IoT Systems:** Linux-based routers and gateways rely on zRAM/zSWAP to avoid wearing out flash storage via excessive swap writes.
- **High-Performance Computing (HPC):** Aggressive swapping is generally *disabled* on HPC nodes because the swap latency would dominate the runtime of scientific workloads.
- **Mobile Operating Systems (Android):** Uses *LMK (Low Memory Killer)* combined with zRAM swap to reclaim memory before invoking the OOM (Out-Of-Memory) killer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Time to Swap a Process

Let us derive the *total swap time* required by the medium-term scheduler to roll-out a process and roll-in a new one of equivalent size.

**Given Assumptions (Standard KTU Problem Setup):**
- Process size $= S$ bytes.
- Disk transfer rate $= R$ bytes/second.
- Average seek time $= T_{seek}$.
- Average rotational latency $= T_{rot}$.
- Transfer time per byte (i.e., the inverse of throughput) $= T_{tx} = \frac{1}{R}$ seconds/byte.

**Step 1 — Compute the time to copy the process to disk (Roll-Out).**

$$
T_{out} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + (S \times T_{tx})
$$

*Explanation:* The disk arm first seeks to the swap-space cylinder ($T_{seek}$), waits for the sector to rotate under the head ($T_{rot}$), and then transfers $S$ bytes sequentially.

**Step 2 — Compute the time to copy the process back to RAM (Roll-In).**

$$
T_{in} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + (S \times T_{tx})
$$

*Explanation:* The second disk access is also a complete I/O — seek, rotate, transfer — because the OS may not write and read from the same contiguous cylinder every time.

**Step 3 — Total swap time.**

$$
T_{swap} = T_{out} + T_{in}
$$

Substituting,

$$
T_{swap} = 2 \times \left( T_{seek} + T_{rot} + (S \times T_{tx}) \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of transfer rate $R$.**

$$
T_{swap} = 2 \times \left( T_{seek} + T_{rot} + \frac{S}{R} \right)
$$

*Final compact form for board answers:*

$$
\boxed{T_{swap} = 2 T_{seek} + 2 T_{rot} + \frac{2S}{R}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A process of size **100 MB** is to be swapped. The disk has an average seek time of **5 ms**, rotational latency of **4 ms**, and a transfer rate of **50 MB/s**. Compute the total swap time.

**Solution — Exhaustive Steps:**

$$
T_{seek} = 5 \text{ ms} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{rot} = 4 \text{ ms} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}
$$

$$
\frac{S}{R} = \frac{100 \text{ MB}}{50 \text{ MB/s}} = 2 \text{ s} = 2000 \text{ ms}
$$

Now substitute into the boxed formula:

$$
T_{swap} = 2 T_{seek} + 2 T_{rot} + \frac{2S}{R}
$$

$$
T_{swap} = 2(5) + 2(4) + 2(2000)
$$

$$
T_{swap} = 10 + 8 + 4000
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{swap} = 4018 \text{ ms} \approx 4.018 \text{ seconds}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Notice that the *transfer time* (4000 ms) dominates the *seek* and *rotational* components (18 ms). For KTU 2024, you **must explicitly state** this dominance in your final paragraph for full marks.

### 3.3 Derivation: Effective Access Time (EAT) with Page Fault

In a paging + swapping hybrid system, the Effective Access Time is computed as:

**Given:**
- Memory access time $= T_{mem} = 100$ ns (typical).
- Page fault rate $= p$ (a probability, $0 \le p \le 1$).
- Page fault service time $= T_{pf} = 10$ ms (typical).

**Step 1 — Probability of no page fault.** This is $(1 - p)$.

**Step 2 — Expected access time equation.**

$$
EAT = (1 - p) \times T_{mem} + p \times T_{pf}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values** (assume $p = 0.001$, i.e., 1 fault in 1000 accesses):

$$
EAT = (0.999)(100 \text{ ns}) + (0.001)(10 \text{ ms})
$$

$$
EAT = 99.9 \text{ ns} + 10{,}000 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
\boxed{EAT = 10{,}099.9 \text{ ns} \approx 10.1 \mu s}
$$

*Note:* Even a page fault rate as small as **0.1%** degrades EAT by a factor of **~100×** — this is the central KTU message of Module 4.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Strategy | Swap Granularity | Hardware Cost | Latency | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| No Swapping | N/A | High (full RAM needed) | None | Real-time systems |
| Standard Swapping | Whole process | Low | Very High ($4+$ s) | Mainframes (historical) |
| Swapping + Paging | Pages (4 KB) | Moderate | Moderate | Modern OS (Linux, Windows) |
| No-Swap Linux (`swapoff`) | None | N/A | N/A | HPC clusters, DB servers |
| zRAM / Compressed Swap | Compressed page in RAM | CPU cycles | Low | Android, ChromeOS |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Swapper

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[CPU Dispatcher] -->|Requests new process| node2[Short-Term Scheduler]
    node2 -->|Free frame available?| node3{Memory Manager Decision}
    node3 -->|Yes| node4[Load from Job Queue]
    node3 -->|No| node5[Medium-Term Scheduler Activated]
    node5 -->|Select victim process| node6[Swap-Out Module]
    node6 -->|Copy address space| node7[Swap Space on Disk]
    node7 -->|Update swap map| node8[Swap Table in Kernel]
    node5 -->|Roll-In waiting process| node9[Swap-In Module]
    node9 -->|Read from disk| node7
    node9 -->|Restore PCB| node10[Process Control Block]
    node10 -->|Add to Ready Queue| node2
    node6 -->|Free physical frames| node11[Free Frame List]
    node11 -->|Allocate to new process| node4
```

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Roll-Out and Roll-In Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU Scheduler
    participant MMU as Memory Manager
    participant SWP as Swapper Module
    participant DISK as Swap Space (Disk)
    participant PCB as Process Control Block

    Note over CPU,PCB: Phase 1: Memory Pressure Detected
    CPU->>MMU: Request more frames
    MMU->>SWP: Free frames needed (High Watermark crossed)
    SWP->>PCB: Select victim process P1
    PCB->>SWP: Suspend P1, set state = Suspended/Ready
    SWP->>DISK: Roll-Out P1 (copy to swap space)
    DISK-->>SWP: Acknowledge, return disk block address
    SWP->>PCB: Store disk address in PCB
    SWP-->>MMU: Frames released to free list

    Note over CPU,PCB: Phase 2: Roll-In of Waiting Process
    CPU->>MMU: Schedule P2 from job queue
    MMU->>SWP: Need to load P2
    SWP->>DISK: Roll-In P2 (read from swap space)
    DISK-->>SWP: Page frames returned
    SWP->>PCB: Restore P2 state = Ready
    SWP-->>MMU: P2 now resident in RAM
    MMU-->>CPU: P2 dispatched to CPU
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Address Space Transformation During Swapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RAM["MAIN MEMORY (RAM)"]
        A[Process A in frames 0-9]
        B[Process B in frames 10-19]
        C[Process C in frames 20-29]
    end

    subgraph DISK["SWAP SPACE (DISK)"]
        D[Reserved swap partition]
        E[Suspended process images]
    end

    subgraph FREE["FREE FRAME LIST"]
        F[Available frames for new processes]
    end

    RAM -->|Roll-Out Process C| DISK
    DISK -->|Roll-In Process D| RAM
    RAM -->|Released frames| FREE
    FREE -->|Allocate to new process| RAM
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams are deliberately **block-level** rather than physical layout drawings, to keep Mermaid syntax 100% safe and renderable on all KTU e-learning platforms.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Define swapping. Differentiate between roll-in and roll-out operations.*

**Model Answer:**

Swapping is a memory management technique in which the OS moves entire processes in and out of main memory to a dedicated region of secondary storage called *swap space*, in order to manage the degree of multiprogramming dynamically.

| Aspect | Roll-Out (Swap-Out) | Roll-In (Swap-In) |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | RAM $\rightarrow$ Disk | Disk $\rightarrow$ RAM |
| Trigger | Memory pressure / free-frame shortage | Process rescheduled after suspension |
| Effect | Frees physical frames | Occupies physical frames |
| Process state | Transitions to *Suspended/Ready* | Transitions to *Ready* |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for direction + 1 for state transition)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Why is swap space size typically kept larger than the total physical RAM in classical UNIX systems? What happens if swap space is exhausted?*

**Model Answer:**

Classical UNIX systems kept swap space **2× to 4× the size of physical RAM** to allow:
1. **Over-commitment of memory** — multiple processes can reserve more virtual memory than physically available.
2. **Accommodation of peak workloads** — memory bursts above the resident set can be temporarily offloaded to disk.
3. **Hibernation support** — the full RAM image must fit into swap when the system suspends to disk.

If swap space is **exhausted**, the kernel invokes the **OOM (Out-Of-Memory) Killer**, which terminates one or more processes (typically the highest-`oom_score` process) to reclaim memory and prevent a kernel panic. *(3 marks: 1 for the 2×–4× rule + 1 for over-commitment + 1 for OOM behaviour)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Instructions for students:** KTU 2024 ESE allows an internal choice between two questions on the same module. Both questions below are complete and independent — answer *either* one in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, Set A]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the role of the **medium-term scheduler** in swapping. Describe the data structures the OS must maintain to support standard (whole-process) swapping.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A process of size **250 MB** is rolled out and another process of the same size is rolled in. The disk parameters are: average seek time = **8 ms**, rotational latency = **3 ms**, transfer rate = **100 MB/s**. Calculate the total swap time and state one optimization that modern systems use to reduce this overhead.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The **medium-term scheduler** sits between the long-term scheduler (admission control) and the short-term scheduler (CPU dispatch). Its primary role in swapping is to **temporarily remove a process from main memory** to reduce the degree of multiprogramming when memory is scarce, and to **bring it back later** when memory becomes available.

The OS maintains the following data structures to support whole-process swapping:

1. **Swap Map / Swap Table:** A kernel-resident table that maps each process PID to its disk-block address in the swap partition. Without this map, the OS cannot locate the suspended process image on roll-in.
2. **Process Control Block (PCB) — Extended Fields:** The PCB must record the *suspension point*, *disk address of the swap image*, *original memory size*, and the *state* (`Suspended/Ready` or `Suspended/Blocked`).
3. **Free Frame List:** Maintained by the memory manager, this bitmap or linked-list tracks all unallocated physical frames — essential for finding contiguous frames during roll-in.
4. **Swap Space Allocation Map:** A bitmap of the swap partition itself, indicating which disk blocks are free and which are occupied by suspended processes.

*(7 marks: 1 for medium-term role + 6 distributed across the four data structures with one-line purposes)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$$
S = 250 \text{ MB}, \quad T_{seek} = 8 \text{ ms}, \quad T_{rot} = 3 \text{ ms}, \quad R = 100 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute transfer time per process.**

$$
\frac{S}{R} = \frac{250 \text{ MB}}{100 \text{ MB/s}} = 2.5 \text{ s} = 2500 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the swap-time formula.**

$$
T_{swap} = 2 T_{seek} + 2 T_{rot} + \frac{2S}{R}
$$

$$
T_{swap} = 2(8) + 2(3) + 2(2500)
$$

$$
T_{swap} = 16 + 6 + 5000
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{swap} = 5022 \text{ ms} \approx 5.022 \text{ seconds}}
$$

**Optimization used in modern systems:** Modern kernels use **demand paging** combined with a **page replacement algorithm (e.g., LRU, Clock)**, which swaps *only the inactive pages* (typically 4 KB) rather than the entire process. This reduces the I/O volume by **3 to 4 orders of magnitude** and is the foundation of the Virtual Memory subsystem.

*(7 marks: 1 for parameter identification + 1 for transfer time + 2 for formula application + 1 for arithmetic + 1 for units + 1 for optimization)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, Set B]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the standard swapping mechanism. How does it differ from swapping in a paging-based system?

**(b)** [7 Marks] In a paging system, the memory access time is **200 ns**, and the page fault service time is **10 ms**. If the page fault rate is **0.0001**, calculate the Effective Access Time (EAT). Comment on the result.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

*(Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

In **standard swapping**, the unit of transfer between RAM and disk is the *entire process image*. The medium-term scheduler:

1. Detects memory pressure (free-frame list low).
2. Selects a victim process from the ready queue.
3. Copies the *entire* process address space from RAM to the swap partition (Roll-Out).
4. Updates the PCB and swap table.
5. Later, when the process is re-scheduled, copies the *entire* image back from disk to RAM (Roll-In).
6. Resumes execution from the saved PC value in the PCB.

**Difference from Paging-Based Swapping:**

| Feature | Standard Swapping | Swapping with Paging |
|---|---|---|
| Unit of transfer | Entire process | One page (typically 4 KB) |
| I/O volume | Very high | Low |
| Speed | Slow (seconds) | Fast (milliseconds) |
| Granularity of decision | Process-level | Page-level |
| Overhead on roll-in | Entire process context reloaded | Only faulted page is reloaded |
| Modern relevance | Obsolete | Universal in Linux/Windows |

*(7 marks: 1 for diagram + 4 for steps + 2 for comparison table)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Given data.**

$$
T_{mem} = 200 \text{ ns}, \quad T_{pf} = 10 \text{ ms} = 10 \times 10^{6} \text{ ns}, \quad p = 0.0001
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the EAT formula.**

$$
EAT = (1 - p) \times T_{mem} + p \times T_{pf}
$$

$$
EAT = (1 - 0.0001)(200) + (0.0001)(10 \times 10^{6})
$$

$$
EAT = (0.9999)(200) + (0.0001)(10{,}000{,}000)
$$

$$
EAT = 199.98 \text{ ns} + 1000 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
\boxed{EAT = 1199.98 \text{ ns} \approx 1.2 \mu s}
$$

**Step 3 — Comment.**

Without page faults, the access time would be 200 ns. With a fault rate of just **0.01%**, the EAT has increased to **1.2 μs — a 6× degradation**. This shows that even an *extremely low* page fault rate has a disproportionate effect on performance, which is why efficient page replacement algorithms are critical to OS design.

*(7 marks: 1 for unit conversion + 2 for substitution + 1 for arithmetic + 1 for boxed answer + 2 for the comment/discussion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Disaster:** When computing $T_{swap}$ or $EAT$, students often mix $ms$ and $ns$ units. *Always convert everything to the same base unit (preferably ns) before substituting.*
> 2. **Forgetting the Factor of 2:** Standard swapping involves *two* disk accesses (roll-out and roll-in). Forgetting this gives you half the actual answer.
> 3. **Confusing Swap Space with Virtual Memory:** Swap space is the *physical disk region*; Virtual Memory is the *abstraction* the process sees. Examiners deduct marks for using these terms interchangeably.
> 4. **Page Fault Service Time = Swap Time + Restart Time:** When $T_{pf}$ is *not* given directly, you must compute it. Many students omit the restart overhead (~100–1000 ns).
> 5. **Writing "Swap" for "Roll-Out":** Use the correct terminology. *Roll-Out* is moving out, *Roll-In* is moving in.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam Day:**

- ☐ Swapping = movement of *whole process* between RAM and disk (swap space).
- ☐ Roll-Out = RAM $\rightarrow$ Disk; Roll-In = Disk $\rightarrow$ RAM.
- ☐ Swapper = medium-term scheduler subsystem.
- ☐ Swap Space = reserved disk partition (typically 2×–4× RAM in classical UNIX).
- ☐ Data structures required: **Swap Table / Swap Map**, **Extended PCB**, **Free Frame List**, **Swap Space Allocation Bitmap**.
- ☐ Master formula: $T_{swap} = 2T_{seek} + 2T_{rot} + \frac{2S}{R}$.
- ☐ Effective Access Time: $EAT = (1-p)T_{mem} + p \cdot T_{pf}$.
- ☐ Page Fault Service Time = $T_{pf} = T_{swap} + T_{restart}$.
- ☐ Even $p = 0.0001$ (0.01%) degrades EAT by **5–6×** — emphasize this in any essay answer.
- ☐ Modern OS = Swapping + Paging hybrid (Virtual Memory).
- ☐ Mobile OS = zRAM / zSWAP (compressed in-RAM swap) to avoid flash wear.
- ☐ HPC clusters = swap is **disabled** (latency dominates).
- ☐ OOM Killer = invoked when swap space + RAM both exhausted.
- ☐ Always write the **unit** (ms, μs, ns) in the final boxed answer for full marks.
- ☐ Distinguish: *Swapping* (whole process, obsolete) vs. *Paging* (4 KB pages, modern).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Contiguous Memory Allocation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Contiguous Memory Allocation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Contiguous Memory Allocation** is a classical memory management scheme in which every process is allocated a single, continuous block (chunk) of physical memory addresses. In this scheme, the entire logical address space of a process must occupy a consecutive sequence of physical memory locations from a base address `B` to a limit `B + \text{Size} - 1`, with no gaps and no interleaving from other processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Contiguous allocation is the foundational technique upon which early mainframe systems (IBM/360, early UNIX) and fixed-partition multiprogramming were built. The KTU 2024 module expects students to compare *fixed* vs *variable* partitioning strategies, the three classical fit strategies, and the resulting fragmentation problems.

The CPU-generated logical address of the form

$$\text{Logical Address} = \text{Base Register} + \text{Offset}$$

is translated at runtime by adding the contents of a per-process **relocation register (base register)** and comparing the result against a **limit register** to ensure that the process never accesses memory outside its authorized block.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hotel Corridor

Imagine a long hotel corridor with 100 rooms in a row, numbered **0 to 99**. When a guest (process) checks in and requests 6 rooms, the manager (OS) walks down the corridor and books **6 adjacent rooms** (say, 12–17) and hands the guest a key card that only opens those 6 doors.

- The **base register** = the first room number (12).
- The **limit register** = the number of rooms (6).
- If the guest tries to open room 25, the OS detects an out-of-bounds access and triggers a **trap** (a "memory violation" interrupt).

This is exactly how contiguous allocation works — the OS hands each process a *base + limit* pair, and the MMU hardware enforces the boundary on every memory reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Boundary Rule:** A process of size $S$ bytes starting at physical address $B$ is allowed to access addresses in the closed interval $[B,\ B+S-1]$. Any access outside this range causes a **memory protection violation** (trap to OS).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

- **Internal Fragmentation** = allocated memory − requested memory (wasted *inside* the partition).
- **External Fragmentation** = total free memory − largest single free block (wasted *between* allocated blocks).
- **Memory Utilization** = $\frac{\text{Total memory in use}}{\text{Total physical memory}} \times 100\%$
- **50% Rule (Knuth's bound)** for First-Fit with uniformly distributed requests: approximately **1/3 of memory** is expected to be unusable due to external fragmentation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Major Strategies

Contiguous allocation is implemented in two flavours:

### 2.1.1 Fixed (Static) Partitioning
- Memory is divided into a fixed number of partitions **at system boot**.
- Each partition holds **exactly one** process at a time.
- Drawback: The partition size must be chosen before execution; if it is too big, **internal fragmentation** occurs; if too small, the process will not fit.
- **Degree of Multiprogramming (DoM)** = number of partitions = $N$.

### 2.1.2 Variable (Dynamic) Partitioning
- Partitions are created **on demand**, exactly sized to the process.
- No internal fragmentation in theory, but causes **external fragmentation** as processes enter and leave.
- Requires an **allocation strategy**: First-Fit, Best-Fit, Worst-Fit (Next-Fit is a First-Fit variant that resumes search from the last allocation point).

## 2.2 The Three Classical Fit Strategies

Let a request be of size $R$ and free blocks in memory be $\{F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_n\}$ where each $F_i$ is the size of the $i^{\text{th}}$ free hole.

| Strategy | Selection Rule | Behaviour | Tends To Produce |
|---|---|---|---|
| **First-Fit** | Pick the *first* $F_i \ge R$ | Fastest; scans from beginning | Smaller holes near the start |
| **Best-Fit** | Pick the *smallest* $F_i \ge R$ | Minimises leftover per allocation | Many tiny, unusable holes |
| **Worst-Fit** | Pick the *largest* $F_i$ | Leaves the largest possible remainder | Large holes that survive longer |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** Best-Fit sounds optimal, but KTU questions frequently show that it produces the *worst* external fragmentation because it deliberately creates the smallest possible remainder — which is usually too small to be reused. First-Fit is generally the fastest *and* the most space-efficient in practice (per Knuth's analysis).

## 2.3 Fragmentation — The Two Villains

**Internal Fragmentation** $I$: Occurs only in fixed partitioning when the partition is larger than the process.

$$I = \text{Partition Size} - \text{Process Size}$$

**External Fragmentation** $E$: Occurs in variable partitioning when free memory exists but is not contiguous.

$$E = \text{Total Free Memory} - \text{Largest Single Free Block}$$

**Compaction (Defragmentation):** A technique that physically shifts all allocated blocks to one end of memory, coalescing all free space into a single large hole. It is *expensive* (CPU + I/O intensive) and only possible with *dynamic relocation* hardware.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Logical Address | $\text{LA} = \text{Base} + \text{Offset}$ | Offset $< \text{Limit}$ |
| 2 | Physical Address | $\text{PA} = \text{Base Register} + \text{Offset}$ | Computed by MMU |
| 3 | Protection Check | $0 \le \text{Offset} < \text{Limit}$ | Else $\rightarrow$ trap |
| 4 | Internal Fragmentation | $I_i = P_i - A_i$ | $P_i$ = partition size, $A_i$ = allocated process size (bytes) |
| 5 | Total Internal Fragmentation | $I_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (P_i - A_i)$ | Across $N$ partitions |
| 6 | External Fragmentation | $E = F_{\text{total}} - F_{\max}$ | $F_{\max}$ = largest free hole (bytes) |
| 7 | Memory Utilization | $U = \dfrac{\sum A_i}{M} \times 100\%$ | $M$ = total physical memory |
| 8 | 50% Rule (Knuth) | $E_{\text{expected}} \approx \frac{1}{3} M$ | For First-Fit under uniform random requests |
| 9 | Number of Partitions | $N = \left\lfloor \dfrac{M}{P} \right\rfloor$ | For equal fixed partitions of size $P$ |

> **Real-World Utility:** Contiguous allocation is still the underlying model in many embedded RTOSes, in MS-DOS, and in the early boot stages of modern kernels. The base + limit registers are the direct ancestors of the more advanced segmentation and paging mechanisms discussed in Module 5 of the KTU syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical KTU Worked Example

> **Problem Statement:** Memory is of size $\mathbf{M = 100\ KB}$ and uses **variable (dynamic) contiguous allocation**. The sequence of process requests and releases is given below. For each allocation, show the memory layout using **First-Fit**, and compute the **external fragmentation** at the end.

| Step | Action | Request |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Allocate | P1 = 20 KB |
| 2 | Allocate | P2 = 30 KB |
| 3 | Allocate | P3 = 15 KB |
| 4 | Terminate | P1 |
| 5 | Allocate | P4 = 18 KB |
| 6 | Terminate | P2 |
| 7 | Allocate | P5 = 25 KB |

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Solution (First-Fit)

**Step 1 — Allocate P1 (20 KB):**
Memory is entirely free (hole = 100 KB). First-Fit places P1 at the very start.

$$\text{Base}_{P1} = 0,\quad \text{Limit}_{P1} = 20\ \text{KB}$$

Remaining free hole: addresses 20 to 99 → **80 KB**.

**Step 2 — Allocate P2 (30 KB):**
First-Fit scans from base 0, finds the hole at 20 KB of size 80 KB $\ge$ 30 KB ✓. Allocates P2 there.

$$\text{Base}_{P2} = 20,\quad \text{Limit}_{P2} = 30\ \text{KB}$$

New free hole: 50 → 99 = **50 KB**.

**Step 3 — Allocate P3 (15 KB):**
Scans from base 0. P1 (0–19) and P2 (20–49) are taken. Hole starts at 50, size = 50 KB $\ge$ 15 ✓.

$$\text{Base}_{P3} = 50,\quad \text{Limit}_{P3} = 15\ \text{KB}$$

New free hole: 65 → 99 = **35 KB**.

**Step 4 — Terminate P1:**
Hole 0–19 (size 20 KB) is added back. The free list now contains: hole at 0 (20 KB) and hole at 65 (35 KB).

**Step 5 — Allocate P4 (18 KB):**
First-Fit scans from address 0. The very first hole is at 0 of size 20 KB $\ge$ 18 ✓. Allocates P4 at 0.

$$\text{Base}_{P4} = 0,\quad \text{Limit}_{P4} = 18\ \text{KB}$$

The hole at 0 now becomes 18 → 19 = **2 KB**. The hole at 65 is still **35 KB**.

**Step 6 — Terminate P2:**
Hole at 20 → 49 of size 30 KB is added. Free holes:
- 18 → 19 (2 KB)
- 20 → 49 (30 KB)
- 65 → 99 (35 KB)

Note: the 2 KB hole at 18 is **adjacent** to the 30 KB hole at 20, so they **coalesce** into one hole of 32 KB at 18 → 49.

Free holes after coalescing: **32 KB** and **35 KB**.

**Step 7 — Allocate P5 (25 KB):**
First-Fit scans from address 0. Occupied regions: P4 (0–17), P3 (50–64). First hole encountered is at 18, size 32 KB $\ge$ 25 ✓.

$$\text{Base}_{P5} = 18,\quad \text{Limit}_{P5} = 25\ \text{KB}$$

Final free hole: 43 → 49 (7 KB) and 65 → 99 (35 KB).

### 3.1.2 External Fragmentation at the End

Total free memory = $7\ \text{KB} + 35\ \text{KB} = 42\ \text{KB}$.

Largest single free block = $35\ \text{KB}$.

$$E = F_{\text{total}} - F_{\max} = 42\ \text{KB} - 35\ \text{KB} = 7\ \text{KB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although 42 KB is free, a request for **36 KB** would be rejected — the system has *enough* total free memory but not *enough contiguous* free memory. This is the essence of **external fragmentation**.

## 3.2 Comparative Matrix: All Three Strategies on the Same Trace

Let us re-run Steps 1–7 using **Best-Fit** and **Worst-Fit** to produce a side-by-side comparison.

After Step 6, the free holes (after coalescing in Best/Worst-Fit is typically done but let us keep them separate to faithfully model the algorithm) are:

$$\text{Holes} = \{2\ \text{KB at }18,\ 30\ \text{KB at }20,\ 35\ \text{KB at }65\}$$

Request: $P5 = 25\ \text{KB}$.

| Strategy | Selected Hole | Base of P5 | Reasoning | Free Holes After |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **First-Fit** | 32 KB (18→49, coalesced) | 18 | First hole $\ge$ 25 from start | 7 KB (43→49), 35 KB (65→99) |
| **Best-Fit** | 30 KB (20→49) | 20 | Smallest hole $\ge$ 25 | 2 KB (18→19), 5 KB (50→54), 35 KB (65→99) |
| **Worst-Fit** | 35 KB (65→99) | 65 | Largest hole overall | 2 KB (18→19), 30 KB (20→49), 10 KB (90→99) |

External fragmentation at the end of Step 7:

$$E_{\text{FF}} = (7+35) - 35 = 7\ \text{KB}$$
$$E_{\text{BF}} = (2+5+35) - 35 = 7\ \text{KB}$$
$$E_{\text{WF}} = (2+30+10) - 30 = 12\ \text{KB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In this particular trace, **First-Fit and Best-Fit tie** at 7 KB of external fragmentation, while **Worst-Fit** is the worst with 12 KB. KTU exam questions often illustrate this exact pattern to demonstrate the counter-intuitive result that *Best-Fit is not always best*.

## 3.3 Internal Fragmentation in Fixed Partitioning — Derivation

Suppose a system has $M = 100\ \text{KB}$ total memory divided into **4 equal fixed partitions** of size $P = 25\ \text{KB}$ each. Three processes request: A = 18 KB, B = 22 KB, C = 12 KB.

The number of partitions and the partition size are computed as:

$$N = \frac{M}{P} = \frac{100}{25} = 4,\quad P = 25\ \text{KB}$$

Internal fragmentation for each allocated partition:

$$I_A = 25 - 18 = 7\ \text{KB}$$
$$I_B = 25 - 22 = 3\ \text{KB}$$
$$I_C = 25 - 12 = 13\ \text{KB}$$

Total internal fragmentation:

$$I_{\text{total}} = 7 + 3 + 13 = 23\ \text{KB}\ \text{(with one partition idle)}$$

Memory utilization (only the third partition is empty):

$$U = \frac{18+22+12}{100} \times 100\% = 52\%$$

> **47% of memory is wasted** when the partitions are not perfectly sized to the process mix — a classic KTU illustration of why fixed partitioning scales poorly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Address Translation Pipeline

The following flowchart depicts how the **MMU (Memory Management Unit)** translates a logical address into a physical address using the base and limit registers, the cornerstone hardware support for contiguous allocation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CPU Generates Logical Address (Offset)"] --> B{"Offset less than Limit register"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Physical Address equals Base plus Offset"]
    B -- No --> D["Trap to Operating System: Memory Violation"]
    C --> E["Memory Bus Reads / Writes at Physical Address"]
    D --> F["OS Kills Process or Raises SIGSEGV Signal"]
    E --> G["Execution Continues Normally"]
    F --> G
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Allocation Strategy Decision Tree

This diagram contrasts the three classical placement strategies by following the same request of size 25 KB against the three available free holes $\{2, 30, 35\}$ KB.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Request: 25 KB"] --> A{"Allocation Strategy"}
    A -- First Fit --> B["Scan from start of free list"]
    B --> C["First hole bigger than or equal to 25 KB"]
    C --> D["Select 32 KB hole at offset 18"]
    A -- Best Fit --> E["Examine all holes"]
    E --> F["Choose smallest hole that fits"]
    F --> G["Select 30 KB hole at offset 20"]
    A -- Worst Fit --> H["Examine all holes"]
    H --> I["Choose largest hole"]
    I --> J["Select 35 KB hole at offset 65"]
    D --> K["Final Memory State"]
    G --> K
    J --> K
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Memory Map After Worked Example

The following horizontal block diagram shows the final memory map of the 100 KB physical memory after Step 7 of the worked example (using First-Fit).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M["Physical Memory 100 KB"] --> A1["P4: 18 KB at offset 0"]
    A1 --> A2["P5: 25 KB at offset 18"]
    A2 --> A3["Hole 7 KB at offset 43"]
    A3 --> A4["P3: 15 KB at offset 50"]
    A4 --> A5["Hole 35 KB at offset 65"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fragmentation Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F["Fragmentation in Contiguous Memory Allocation"] --> IF["Internal Fragmentation"]
    F --> EF["External Fragmentation"]
    IF --> IF1["Cause: Fixed partition larger than process"]
    IF --> IF2["Wasted memory lies inside the allocated block"]
    IF --> IF3["Solution: Use variable sized partitions"]
    EF --> EF1["Cause: Variable partitions, processes come and go"]
    EF --> EF2["Wasted memory exists as small holes between blocks"]
    EF --> EF3["Solutions: Compaction, Paging, Segmentation"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**

**Q:** Differentiate between **internal fragmentation** and **external fragmentation** in the context of contiguous memory allocation. State one technique to overcome each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Internal Fragmentation | External Fragmentation |
|---|---|---|
| **Where wasted memory lies** | Inside the allocated block | Outside allocated blocks (in free holes) |
| **When it occurs** | Fixed / static partitioning where the partition is larger than the process | Variable partitioning when free memory is broken into non-contiguous holes |
| **Example** | 25 KB partition holding a 18 KB process wastes 7 KB inside | 35 KB free hole + 7 KB free hole, but a 36 KB request fails |
| **Solution** | Variable-size partitioning (no internal fragmentation) | **Compaction** (shifting allocated blocks) or **paging** |

*(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for solution — 3 marks total)*

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Q:** Explain the role of the **base register** and **limit register** in contiguous memory allocation. Why are both needed together?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The **base register** holds the starting physical address of the process's allocated block. It is added to every logical address offset to obtain the physical address. *(1 mark)*
- The **limit register** holds the size of the allocated block. The MMU checks that the CPU-generated offset is strictly less than the limit before allowing the address translation; otherwise a **trap** is raised. *(1 mark)*
- **Why both?** Base alone tells *where* the block starts, but without limit a process could access memory belonging to other processes or the OS. Limit alone tells *how big* the block is, but not where it begins. Together they provide both **relocation** and **protection**. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Consider a system with **100 KB** of physical memory that uses **variable contiguous allocation**. Processes arrive in this order: **P1 (20 KB), P2 (30 KB), P3 (15 KB)**. After this, P1 terminates, then P4 (18 KB) arrives, P2 terminates, and finally P5 (25 KB) arrives. Show the memory layout after each step using **First-Fit** allocation, and compute the **external fragmentation** at the end. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Repeat the above trace using **Best-Fit**. Show the final memory layout and the external fragmentation. Compare your results and state which strategy produced less fragmentation. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — First-Fit (7 marks)**

Following the exact derivation from Section 3.1.1:

| Step | Action | Base | Limit | Free Holes (size) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Allocate P1 (20 KB) | 0 | 20 | 80 KB (20–99) |
| 2 | Allocate P2 (30 KB) | 20 | 30 | 50 KB (50–99) |
| 3 | Allocate P3 (15 KB) | 50 | 15 | 35 KB (65–99) |
| 4 | Terminate P1 | — | — | 20 KB (0–19), 35 KB (65–99) |
| 5 | Allocate P4 (18 KB) | 0 | 18 | 2 KB (18–19), 35 KB (65–99) |
| 6 | Terminate P2 | — | — | Coalesced 32 KB (18–49), 35 KB (65–99) |
| 7 | Allocate P5 (25 KB) | 18 | 25 | 7 KB (43–49), 35 KB (65–99) |

**Final External Fragmentation:**

$$E_{\text{FF}} = (7 + 35) - 35 = 7\ \text{KB}$$

*(1 mark for each step's correct memory map ≈ 4 marks, 1 mark for coalescing in Step 6, 2 marks for the final E calculation. Total 7.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — Best-Fit (7 marks)**

Re-running the trace with Best-Fit:

| Step | Action | Selected Hole | Base | Free Holes After |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Allocate P1 (20 KB) | 100 KB (only hole) | 0 | 80 KB (20–99) |
| 2 | Allocate P2 (30 KB) | 80 KB | 20 | 50 KB (50–99) |
| 3 | Allocate P3 (15 KB) | 50 KB | 50 | 35 KB (65–99) |
| 4 | Terminate P1 | — | — | 20 KB (0–19), 35 KB (65–99) |
| 5 | Allocate P4 (18 KB) | 20 KB (best of {20, 35} ≥ 18) | 0 | 2 KB (18–19), 35 KB (65–99) |
| 6 | Terminate P2 | — | — | 2 KB (18–19), 30 KB (20–49), 35 KB (65–99) |
| 7 | Allocate P5 (25 KB) | 30 KB (best of {2, 30, 35} ≥ 25) | 20 | 2 KB (18–19), 5 KB (50–54), 35 KB (65–99) |

**Final External Fragmentation:**

$$E_{\text{BF}} = (2 + 5 + 35) - 35 = 7\ \text{KB}$$

**Comparison:** In this trace, **both First-Fit and Best-Fit yield 7 KB** of external fragmentation. However, Best-Fit leaves **more numerous** (three vs. two) but **smaller** holes. Worst-Fit (which the question does not ask) would yield 12 KB. *(2 marks for comparison and conclusion.)*

---

### **Question B (Alternative, 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**
