**(a)** Explain the **three classical fit strategies** (First-Fit, Best-Fit, Worst-Fit) for variable contiguous memory allocation. Include in your answer a discussion of which strategy is fastest and which minimises external fragmentation in the long run. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A system has 4 fixed partitions of sizes **60 KB, 100 KB, 200 KB, and 400 KB** (non-equal). Four processes arrive: **P1 = 70 KB, P2 = 150 KB, P3 = 220 KB, P4 = 50 KB**. Apply the *best-fit* fixed-partition algorithm (each process picks the smallest partition that fits it) and compute the **total internal fragmentation**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — Strategies (7 marks)**

*(2 marks for naming/definition of each strategy.)*

- **First-Fit:** Allocates the *first* free block large enough. **Fastest** because it stops at the first match — no need to scan the whole free list. *(1 mark)*
- **Best-Fit:** Allocates the *smallest* free block large enough. **Leaves the smallest possible remainder** per allocation, but over time these remainders are too tiny to be reused → degrades into a long list of unusable holes. *(1 mark)*
- **Worst-Fit:** Allocates the *largest* free block. Leaves the largest possible remainder, hoping it remains useful. Generally the **worst** for external fragmentation in practice. *(1 mark)*

**Long-run verdict (1 mark):** Empirical studies and Knuth's analysis show that **First-Fit** is generally **faster and produces less external fragmentation (≈ 1/3 of memory wasted)** in the long run. Best-Fit, despite its name, tends to be the worst because it deliberately creates the smallest remainders.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — Fixed Partition Best-Fit (7 marks)**

| Process | Size | Best-Fit Partition (smallest that fits) | Internal Fragmentation |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 70 KB | 100 KB (since 60 KB < 70 KB fails) | $100 - 70 = 30$ KB |
| P2 | 150 KB | 200 KB (100 KB < 150 KB fails) | $200 - 150 = 50$ KB |
| P3 | 220 KB | 400 KB (200 KB < 220 KB fails) | $400 - 220 = 180$ KB |
| P4 | 50 KB | 60 KB (smallest partition) | $60 - 50 = 10$ KB |

**Total Internal Fragmentation:**

$$I_{\text{total}} = 30 + 50 + 180 + 10 = 270\ \text{KB}$$

Memory in use = $70 + 150 + 220 + 50 = 490$ KB out of 760 KB total ⇒ **Utilization ≈ 64.5%** and **waste ≈ 35.5%** (all internal). *(1 mark for utilization, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting coalescing (adjacent-hole merging):** After a process terminates, holes that are physically adjacent MUST be merged before the next allocation. Skipping coalescing gives a different (and incorrect) memory state. *(Lose ~1 mark per affected step)*
> 2. **Confusing "remaining free memory" with "external fragmentation":** External fragmentation is not the total free memory; it is the *difference* between total free memory and the *largest single contiguous hole*. Writing "$E = 42$ KB" when $F_{\max} = 35$ KB loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Mislabelling First-Fit and Next-Fit:** First-Fit always restarts the scan from address 0; Next-Fit restarts from the **last allocation point**. KTU questions often test this subtlety.
> 4. **Forgetting the protection check:** When asked about base+limit registers, students often describe the translation but forget to mention the **limit comparison** that produces a trap on violation. *(Lose 1 mark)*
> 5. **Mixing up internal and external fragmentation causes:** A common error is to say "internal fragmentation occurs in variable partitioning" — this is wrong. Internal fragmentation is a fixed-partition phenomenon.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Contiguous allocation** = each process occupies a single, physically adjacent block of memory described by a **base** and a **limit** register. *(Module 4 anchor concept)*
- **Fixed partitioning** suffers from **internal fragmentation**; **variable partitioning** suffers from **external fragmentation**.
- **First-Fit** is generally the fastest and empirically the best; **Best-Fit** is theoretically appealing but practically poor; **Worst-Fit** is rarely useful.
- **External fragmentation** = total free memory − largest free hole (not the total free memory itself).
- **Internal fragmentation** = partition size − process size (summed across all partitions).
- **Compaction** eliminates external fragmentation but is expensive and requires dynamic relocation hardware.
- **Base register** provides *relocation*; **limit register** provides *protection*; **both are mandatory** for safe contiguous allocation.
- **Coalescing of adjacent holes** must be performed on every deallocation to prevent unnecessary fragmentation.
- The **50% Rule (Knuth)**: under uniform random requests, First-Fit wastes about 1/3 of memory to external fragmentation.
- **Degree of Multiprogramming** is bounded by the number of partitions in fixed schemes and by available contiguous memory in variable schemes.
- Real-world descendants: **segmentation with base+limit** in early x86 protected mode, and the **boot-time allocator** in modern kernels.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed and Variable Partitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fixed and Variable Partitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Contiguous Memory Allocation** is a memory management scheme in which each process is allocated a single contiguous block of physical memory. Within this scheme, the OS divides the main memory into partitions to hold the active processes. The two classical strategies for creating these partitions are:

- **Fixed (Static) Partitions:** The main memory is divided into a fixed number of partitions (partitions of equal or unequal fixed size) at system boot/load time. These partitions **do not change** during the lifetime of the system.
- **Variable (Dynamic) Partitions:** Partitions are created dynamically as per the requirement of the incoming process. The size of each partition is exactly equal to the size of the process it holds, eliminating the boundary-mismatch of fixed partitions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** *Module 4 – Memory Management Strategies: Contiguous memory allocation – Fixed and Variable Partitions.* This topic carries direct weightage in Part A (3 marks) and frequently appears as a 14-mark sub-part question in Part B.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The Co-operative Hostel Room System:**

Imagine a college hostel with rooms of two different fixed sizes (Single rooms = 100 sq.ft, Double rooms = 200 sq.ft). When a single student arrives but only a Double room is free, the student gets the entire Double room, but the extra 100 sq.ft remains **wasted inside the room** (you cannot give it to anyone else). This is **Fixed Partitioning** with **Internal Fragmentation**.

Now imagine a *smart* hostel that converts a warehouse into rooms of *exact* dimensions whenever a student arrives — the moment the student leaves, the walls are demolished, and the free space is merged back into one large continuous block. This is **Variable Partitioning**. The downside: after many students have come and gone, the warehouse is broken into many small scattered gaps that are too small for any new arrival, even though the *total* free area is large. This is **External Fragmentation**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Definitions)

| Term | Standard KTU Definition | Unit |
|------|------------------------|------|
| **Internal Fragmentation** | Wasted space **inside** an allocated partition because the process is smaller than the partition. | Bytes / KB / MB |
| **External Fragmentation** | Wasted space **outside** all allocated partitions — free memory exists but is not contiguous. | Bytes / KB / MB |
| **Compaction** | OS technique of shifting all allocated processes to one end to merge free holes into one large block. | Logical address adjustment |
| **Degree of Multiprogramming** | Maximum number of processes simultaneously resident in memory. | Integer (count) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used by KTU Examiners:** Memory sizes are always expressed in **KB** (or sometimes MB). The **partition overhead** (OS resident portion) is typically **5 % to 10 %** of total RAM in numerical problems — keep this constant in mind.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fixed (Static) Partitions — Operational Logic

**Step-by-step working model:**

1. At system startup, the OS designer pre-decides partition sizes (e.g., partitions of 100 KB, 200 KB, 300 KB, 400 KB).
2. A **partition queue / multiple queues** are maintained — one for each partition size.
3. When a process arrives, the OS picks the *smallest* partition that can still hold the process (a heuristic called the *“best-fit-to-partition”* rule).
4. If no partition is free, the process is kept in a **ready/waiting queue**.
5. As soon as a partition is freed by a terminated process, the next waiting process in that queue is loaded.

**The "Why" behind the design:**
- The hardware/MMU does not need complex address translation — only a simple *base register + limit register* per partition.
- Implementation is **trivial**: just a partition table.

**The "How" of the cost:**
- **Internal fragmentation** is the silent killer. If 12 partitions of 100 KB are created and the average process needs only 38 KB, ~62 KB is wasted **per partition** ≈ **62 % waste**.

### 2.2 Variable (Dynamic) Partitions — Operational Logic

**Step-by-step working model:**

1. The entire available memory is treated as a single large free hole.
2. When a process of size *S* arrives, the OS allocates *exactly S* bytes, leaving a smaller free hole behind.
3. When a process terminates, its block is added back to the free list.
4. **Adjacent free holes are coalesced (merged)** by the allocator to form larger holes.
5. If the next arriving process is bigger than any free hole, the OS must either (a) wait, (b) compact, or (c) use a non-contiguous scheme.

**Allocation Strategies (the famous "Three Fits"):**

| Strategy | Selection Rule | KTU Marker |
|----------|---------------|------------|
| **First-Fit** | Allocate the *first* hole big enough scanning from the top of memory. | Fastest; moderate external fragmentation. |
| **Best-Fit** | Allocate the *smallest* hole that is big enough. | Slowest; produces the smallest leftover holes (worst external frag by leftover). |
| **Worst-Fit** | Allocate the *largest* hole. | Leaves the largest possible remainder. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Variables |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Number of equal-sized partitions | $N \;=\; \left\lfloor \dfrac{M_{total}}{P_{size}} \right\rfloor$ | $M_{total}$ = total user memory, $P_{size}$ = partition size |
| 2 | Internal Fragmentation (per partition) | $IF_i \;=\; P_{size} - S_i$ | $S_i$ = actual process size |
| 3 | Total Internal Fragmentation | $IF_{total} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} (P_{size,i} - S_i)$ | Only for occupied partitions |
| 4 | External Fragmentation (informal) | $EF \;=\; F_{total} - F_{largest\_usable}$ | $F_{largest\_usable} = $ size of largest free hole |
| 5 | Total Free Memory | $F_{total} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{H} H_j$ | $H_j$ = size of $j$-th free hole, $H$ = number of holes |
| 6 | Degree of Multiprogramming (Fixed) | $DOP \;=\; \text{number of partitions}$ | Static in fixed partitioning |
| 7 | Memory Utilization (%) | $U \;=\; \dfrac{\sum S_i}{M_{total}} \times 100$ | A higher value is better |
| 8 | Compaction Time Cost | $T_{compaction} \;\approx\; C \cdot M_{total}$ | $C$ = constant; OS-level cost |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** Vertical bars inside table cells (for absolute value) are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking the markdown table syntax. This is mandated in the KTU-Premier-Engine V10 protocol.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fixed partitioning** survives in **embedded real-time systems** (automotive ECUs, avionics) where workloads are known in advance and predictability outweighs efficiency.
- **Variable partitioning** was the **IBM OS/360 MFT (Multiprogramming with a Fixed number of Tasks)** and **MVT (Multiprogramming with a Variable number of Tasks)** — historically important in the evolution of paging.
- Modern OS kernels (Linux, Windows) use **paging** instead, but the *fit-strategies* (first-fit, best-fit) are still used inside the **buddy-system slab allocators**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Fixed Partition Analysis (KTU Standard 7-mark sub-part)

> **Problem:** A system has **512 KB** of user memory, divided into **4 equal fixed partitions** of **128 KB each**. Five processes arrive in the order: **P1 = 60 KB, P2 = 100 KB, P3 = 35 KB, P4 = 128 KB, P5 = 110 KB**. Use the *smallest-partition-that-fits* rule. Compute: (a) Number of partitions, (b) Total internal fragmentation, (c) Number of processes that must wait, (d) Final memory utilization.

**Solution:**

**(a) Number of partitions:**
We divide total memory by partition size.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \left\lfloor \frac{M_{total}}{P_{size}} \right\rfloor \\[6pt]
  &= \left\lfloor \frac{512 \text{ KB}}{128 \text{ KB}} \right\rfloor \\[6pt]
  &= 4 \text{ partitions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Allocation Table (smallest-partition-that-fits rule):**

| Process | Size (KB) | Allocated Partition (KB) | Internal Fragmentation (KB) |
|---------|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| P1 | 60 | 128 | $128 - 60 = 68$ |
| P2 | 100 | 128 | $128 - 100 = 28$ |
| P3 | 35 | 128 | $128 - 35 = 93$ |
| P4 | 128 | 128 | $128 - 128 = 0$ |
| P5 | 110 | — (no free 128 KB) | **Waiting** |

**Total Internal Fragmentation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IF_{total} &= 68 + 28 + 93 + 0 \text{ KB} \\[4pt]
           &= 189 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Number of waiting processes:** **1** (P5).

**(d) Memory Utilization (only occupied memory):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{\sum S_i}{M_{total}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{60 + 100 + 35 + 128}{512} \times 100 \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{323}{512} \times 100 \\[6pt]
  &= 63.09\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verification:** Used = 323 KB, Internal Fragmentation = 189 KB, Total = 512 KB ✓

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Variable Partition with First-Fit

> **Problem:** Free memory holes in order (top to bottom): **A = 200 KB, B = 400 KB, C = 150 KB, D = 300 KB, E = 250 KB**. Process arrival order: **Q1 = 220 KB, Q2 = 130 KB, Q3 = 280 KB, Q4 = 90 KB**. Apply **First-Fit** and report the final hole list and external fragmentation.

**Solution:**

| Step | Request | Scanning Order | Hole Allocated | Remaining Holes (Top → Bottom) |
|------|---------|----------------|----------------|--------------------------------|
| Start | — | — | — | 200, 400, 150, 300, 250 |
| 1 | Q1 = 220 | 200 ✗, 400 ✓ | **400 → 180** | 200, 180, 150, 300, 250 |
| 2 | Q2 = 130 | 200 ✓ | **200 → 70** | 70, 180, 150, 300, 250 |
| 3 | Q3 = 280 | 70 ✗, 180 ✗, 150 ✗, 300 ✓ | **300 → 20** | 70, 180, 150, 20, 250 |
| 4 | Q4 = 90 | 70 ✗, 180 ✓ | **180 → 90** | 70, 90, 150, 20, 250 |

**Final Holes:** 70, 90, 150, 20, 250 (in KB) — total free = 580 KB.

**External Fragmentation (KTU definition):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{total} &= 70 + 90 + 150 + 20 + 250 = 580 \text{ KB} \\[4pt]
F_{largest\_usable} &= 250 \text{ KB (largest single hole)} \\[4pt]
EF &= F_{total} - F_{largest\_usable} \\[4pt]
   &= 580 - 250 = 330 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note for KTU valuation:** Many students compute *all* 580 KB as external fragmentation, but technically only the *unusable remainder* is counted (the 250 KB is still usable for a future request ≤ 250 KB).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Fixed vs Variable Partitions (Board Comparison Table)

| Parameter | Fixed Partitioning | Variable Partitioning |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Partition creation time | System generation | Process arrival |
| Partition size | Static, pre-decided | Dynamic, equals process size |
| Internal fragmentation | **Yes** (major drawback) | **No** (theoretically zero) |
| External fragmentation | **No** (initially) | **Yes** (major drawback) |
| Degree of multiprogramming | Fixed (= number of partitions) | Variable, bounded by total memory |
| Hardware support needed | Base + Limit registers | Base + Limit registers + compaction logic |
| Implementation complexity | Very simple | Moderate to high |
| Compaction required? | No | Yes (periodically) |
| Memory utilization (typical) | 40 – 60 % | 70 – 90 % |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Memory Layout — Fixed Partitions (Equal Size)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph RAM[Main Memory 512 KB]
        OS["OS Resident 64 KB"]
        P1["Partition 1 : 100 KB"]
        P2["Partition 2 : 100 KB"]
        P3["Partition 3 : 100 KB"]
        P4["Partition 4 : 100 KB"]
        P5["Partition 5 : 100 KB"]
    end

    A1["Process A : 60 KB"] -.allocated.-> P1
    A2["Process B : 88 KB"] -.allocated.-> P2
    A3["Process C : 40 KB"] -.allocated.-> P3
    A4["Process D : 100 KB"] -.allocated.-> P4
    A5["Process E : 75 KB WAITING"] -.blocked.-> P5

    style OS fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style P2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style P3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style P4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style P5 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style A5 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The pink-colored `Process E` cannot be loaded even though `Partition 5` is free, because **75 KB < 100 KB is technically true**, but in this particular case it is *loaded*. The diagram is illustrative — in real KTU problems the *smallest-partition-that-fits* rule decides the placement.

### 4.2 Memory Layout — Variable Partitions (First-Fit Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA[Initial State : One large hole 1300 KB] --> stepB[Process Q1 220 KB arrives]
    stepB --> stepC[Q1 occupies top 220 KB Hole becomes 1080 KB]
    stepC --> stepD[Process Q2 130 KB arrives]
    stepD --> stepE[Q2 occupies next 130 KB Hole becomes 950 KB]
    stepE --> stepF[Process Q3 terminates 220 KB freed]
    stepF --> stepG[Adjacent free holes merged into one 220 KB hole]
    style stepA fill:#E0FFFF
    style stepG fill:#98FB98
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Memory Allocator

```mermaid
graph TB
    HW[CPU Request for Process Load] --> MMU[MMU Base and Limit Registers]
    MMU --> ALG{Allocation Strategy}
    ALG --> ALG1[First Fit]
    ALG --> ALG2[Best Fit]
    ALG --> ALG3[Worst Fit]
    ALG1 --> FL[Free Hole List]
    ALG2 --> FL
    ALG3 --> FL
    FL --> CHK{Hole Found}
    CHK -->|Yes| ALLOC[Allocate exact process size]
    CHK -->|No| COMP[Trigger Compaction]
    COMP --> FL
    ALLOC --> UPD[Update Hole List and Base Registers]
    style HW fill:#FFA500
    style ALG fill:#FFD700
    style CHK fill:#FF69B4
    style COMP fill:#87CEFA
    style UPD fill:#90EE90
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. Differentiate between internal fragmentation and external fragmentation. In which type of partitioning does each occur?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Internal Fragmentation | External Fragmentation |
|--------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Definition | Wasted memory **inside** an allocated partition | Wasted memory **outside** allocations — free but non-contiguous |
| Occurs in | **Fixed (static) partitions** | **Variable (dynamic) partitions** |
| Cause | Process size < partition size | Process termination leaves scattered small holes |
| Solution | Use variable partitioning, paging | Compaction, paging, segmentation |

*(Full marks awarded for tabulated comparison + stating which scheme produces which type.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Compare First-Fit, Best-Fit, and Worst-Fit memory allocation strategies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **First-Fit:** Scans free list and allocates the *first* hole that is big enough. **Fastest** allocation, but tends to fragment the front of memory.
- **Best-Fit:** Allocates the *smallest* hole that is big enough. Slowest because the entire list must be searched; produces the smallest remainder (often unusable).
- **Worst-Fit:** Allocates the *largest* hole. Leaves the largest possible remainder, theoretically most resistant to small leftovers, but in practice often the worst performer (Sleator's result, KTU note).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **fixed partitioning** memory management scheme with a neat diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a system with **800 KB** of user memory divided into **5 equal partitions of 160 KB each**. Processes arrive in the order: **P1 = 75 KB, P2 = 140 KB, P3 = 180 KB, P4 = 60 KB, P5 = 160 KB, P6 = 95 KB**. Apply the *smallest-partition-that-fits* strategy and compute: (i) internal fragmentation, (ii) waiting processes, (iii) memory utilization. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Fixed Partitioning (also called Static Partitioning):** The OS designer pre-divides main memory into a fixed number of partitions at system generation time. Each partition can hold **exactly one** process. A partition is either *free* or *occupied* — partial occupancy is not allowed.

**Diagram (textual representation, to be drawn in exam):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Memory} &= \underbrace{OS}_{64 \text{ KB}} \mid \underbrace{P_1}_{160 \text{ KB}} \mid \underbrace{P_2}_{160 \text{ KB}} \mid \underbrace{P_3}_{160 \text{ KB}} \mid \underbrace{P_4}_{160 \text{ KB}} \mid \underbrace{P_5}_{160 \text{ KB}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Advantages (write any 3):**
1. Simple to implement — only a partition table is required.
2. Minimal hardware support (base + limit register).
3. Predictable performance, suitable for real-time systems.
4. No external fragmentation.

**Limitations (write any 3):**
1. **Internal fragmentation** — significant wastage of memory.
2. **Fixed degree of multiprogramming** — cannot exceed the number of partitions.
3. **Sub-optimal use** if process sizes vary widely.
4. A large process may not fit into any single partition.

---

**Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Allocation Table (smallest-partition rule):**

| Process | Size (KB) | Allocated Partition (KB) | Internal Fragmentation (KB) |
|---------|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| P1 | 75 | 160 | $160 - 75 = 85$ |
| P2 | 140 | 160 | $160 - 140 = 20$ |
| P3 | 180 | — (all 160 KB partitions occupied) | **Waiting** |
| P4 | 60 | 160 | $160 - 60 = 100$ |
| P5 | 160 | 160 | $160 - 160 = 0$ |
| P6 | 95 | — (no free 160 KB) | **Waiting** |

**(i) Total Internal Fragmentation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IF_{total} &= 85 + 20 + 100 + 0 \text{ KB} \\[4pt]
           &= 205 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Waiting processes:** **2** (P3 and P6).

**(iii) Memory Utilization:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{75 + 140 + 60 + 160}{800} \times 100 \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{435}{800} \times 100 \\[4pt]
  &= 54.375\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain **variable (dynamic) partitioning** with a diagram. Discuss the role of **compaction** in eliminating external fragmentation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system has free holes in the order (top to bottom): **H1 = 300 KB, H2 = 150 KB, H3 = 500 KB, H4 = 200 KB, H5 = 100 KB**. Process requests arrive in the order: **R1 = 220 KB, R2 = 90 KB, R3 = 400 KB, R4 = 150 KB**. Using **Best-Fit**, show the allocation step-by-step and calculate the final external fragmentation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Variable Partitioning:** Memory is divided into partitions whose size is *exactly* the size of the process. Initially, the whole user memory is one large free hole. As processes arrive, holes shrink; as processes leave, holes grow and adjacent holes are merged.

**Diagram (memory snapshot after 3 processes loaded):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Memory} &= \underbrace{P_1}_{120 \text{ KB}} \mid \underbrace{\text{Hole}}_{80 \text{ KB}} \mid \underbrace{P_2}_{200 \text{ KB}} \mid \underbrace{\text{Hole}}_{30 \text{ KB}} \mid \underbrace{P_3}_{350 \text{ KB}} \mid \underbrace{\text{Hole}}_{220 \text{ KB}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compaction:**
- It is the OS-level technique of **shifting all allocated processes to one end** (typically lower addresses) to merge all free holes into a single large contiguous block.
- Implemented by adjusting the **base registers** of each process; the logical address space is unchanged.
- **Drawback:** Compaction is **CPU-intensive** and causes I/O delays because processes being shifted cannot perform I/O during relocation (KTU 2024 favourite follow-up question).

---

**Solution to Q.B (b) — Best-Fit Strategy:**

| Step | Request | All Holes (Top → Bottom) | Selected Hole (Smallest ≥ Req) | After Allocation |
|------|---------|--------------------------|--------------------------------|------------------|
| Start | — | 300, 150, 500, 200, 100 | — | — |
| 1 | R1 = 220 | 300, 150, 500, 200, 100 | 300 (smallest ≥ 220) | 80, 150, 500, 200, 100 |
| 2 | R2 = 90 | 80, 150, 500, 200, 100 | 100 (smallest ≥ 90) | 80, 150, 500, 200, 10 |
| 3 | R3 = 400 | 80, 150, 500, 200, 10 | 500 (only hole ≥ 400) | 80, 150, 100, 200, 10 |
| 4 | R4 = 150 | 80, 150, 100, 200, 10 | 150 (only hole ≥ 150 except 200; smallest is 150) | 80, 0, 100, 200, 10 |

**Final Holes:** 80, 100, 200, 10 (in KB) → Total free = **390 KB**

**External Fragmentation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{total} &= 390 \text{ KB} \\[4pt]
F_{largest} &= 200 \text{ KB} \\[4pt]
EF &= 390 - 200 = 190 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse internal and external fragmentation.** In variable partitioning, internal fragmentation is **zero** by design — so if your solution shows both, you will lose 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Always show the step-by-step hole list** in tabular form. Examiners give 1 mark for the table itself.
> 3. **Best-Fit vs First-Fit confusion:** Best-Fit searches the *entire* list for the *smallest sufficient* hole — not the *first* sufficient one.
> 4. **Compaction answer trap:** Many students write "compaction is done by swapping" — wrong. It is done by *relocation* via base-register update.
> 5. **Formula for external fragmentation** is not always "sum of all holes". KTU accepts the *largest-remaining* definition (total − largest single hole). State your assumption clearly.
> 6. **In fixed-partition problems**, if a process is *larger* than every partition, it **cannot be allocated at all** — do not say "use best-fit", it does not apply.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Fixed partitioning = Internal fragmentation = Wasted space inside the partition.**
- **Variable partitioning = External fragmentation = Wasted space between partitions.**
- **Partition table** is the data structure used in fixed partitioning.
- **Free-hole list (linked list)** is the data structure used in variable partitioning.
- **Three fits:** First-Fit (fastest), Best-Fit (slowest, smallest leftover), Worst-Fit (largest leftover).
- **Coalescing** is the merging of adjacent free holes — must be done whenever a process terminates.
- **Compaction** relocates processes to merge all holes; it does **not** happen automatically — it is a costly OS-level decision.
- **Degree of multiprogramming** is **fixed** in static partitions and **variable** in dynamic partitions.
- **Base + Limit registers** are the minimum hardware required for both schemes.
- **KTU favourite traps:** (i) Mixing up internal and external fragmentation, (ii) Forgetting to subtract OS memory, (iii) Computing utilization only over *user* memory and not *total* memory.
- **Modern relevance:** Buddy-system and slab allocators in Linux kernel are descendants of variable partitioning with first/best-fit.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Segmentation** is a non-contiguous memory management scheme supported by the operating system (in cooperation with the hardware Memory Management Unit, MMU) that divides a program's logical address space into variable-sized blocks called **segments**, where each segment represents a logically meaningful unit of the program such as the main code, the global/static data, the heap, the stack, or a user-defined logical data structure (like an array, a symbol table, or a linked list).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition Snapshot**
> A segment is a logical unit of a program (e.g., code, data, stack). A logical address in segmentation is a two-dimensional tuple `<segment-number, offset>`. The OS maintains a **Segment Table** per process, with each entry containing the segment's physical base address, its limit (size), and protection/validity bits. The hardware MMU translates the logical address to a physical address dynamically at every memory reference.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large **library**:
- The library contains thousands of books scattered across the shelves.
- Instead of dumping the books in one huge row, the librarian arranges the books into **chapters or subject-wise sections** (Fiction, Science, History, Magazines).
- Each section is a **segment**. Sections have different sizes because History has more books than Magazines.
- A reader wanting a specific book (e.g., *Operating System Concepts* under "Computer Science") only needs to know the **section name (segment number)** and the **book's position inside that section (offset)**.

This is exactly how segmentation works for a program:
- The program is not a flat array of bytes; it is a collection of *meaningful* pieces.
- A variable `x` does not live at "address 5000"; it lives at "**offset 4 inside the data segment**" — and the OS figures out the rest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Paging**
> In **paging**, memory is chopped into *fixed-size* frames for the OS's convenience. In **segmentation**, memory is chopped into *variable-size* pieces for the **programmer's logical convenience**. The programmer is aware of segments; the programmer is typically not aware of pages.

### 1.3 Why Segmentation? (The Motivation)

- Provides a **logical view of memory** that mirrors the structure of the program.
- Enables **protection** by assigning access rights (read/write/execute) per segment.
- Enables **sharing of code and data** between processes (e.g., shared library segment).
- Supports **dynamic memory growth** of logical units (e.g., the stack/heap can grow independently).
- Allows programmers to use **symbolic names** for memory regions instead of raw numeric addresses.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Logical address size** in a typical segmented system: **2 × s** bits, where `s` is the segment number width.
- **Offset (d) width**: determines the maximum size of a single segment (`2^d` bytes).
- **Segment Table Entry (STE) size**: typically **3 words** (base, limit, protection), e.g., **24–32 bytes** in older x86 architectures.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logical Address Structure

In segmentation, a logical (virtual) address is **two-dimensional**:

$$
\text{Logical Address} = \langle s, \ d \rangle
$$

where:
- **s** = segment number (segment selector)
- **d** = offset (displacement) within the segment

This is fundamentally different from the one-dimensional address used in paging or contiguous allocation.

### 2.2 Segment Table: The Heart of Segmentation

Each process has its own **Segment Table** kept in main memory. A pointer to it is stored in a special hardware register (e.g., the **Segment Table Base Register, STBR**, in x86) or the PCB.

**Typical Segment Table Entry (STE):**

| Field | Meaning | Size (typical) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Base (B)** | Starting physical address of the segment in RAM | 24 bits |
| **Limit (L)** | Length of the segment (used for bounds checking) | 16–20 bits |
| **Protection bits** | Read / Write / Execute permissions | 3–4 bits |
| **Valid bit** | Whether the segment is currently in memory | 1 bit |
| **Other flags** | Privilege level, dirty, accessed, etc. | varies |

> [!NOTE]
> Some KTU textbooks model the protection field as simply **R/W/X** bits; others merge protection with valid-bit into a single "Access Rights" word. The mathematical translation logic is identical.

### 2.3 Address Translation (The Core Algorithm)

For every memory reference of the form `<s, d>` issued by the CPU:

**Step 1:** Extract segment number `s` and offset `d` from the logical address.

**Step 2:** Use `s` as an index into the Segment Table to fetch the corresponding STE.

**Step 3:** Compare the offset `d` against the **limit** `L` stored in the STE.

> If $0 \leq d < L$, the address is legal; the CPU proceeds to Step 4.
>
> If $d \geq L$, the MMU raises a **trap** to the OS — a **segmentation fault** (yes, this is where the modern Linux/macOS "Segmentation fault (core dumped)" message literally originates!).

**Step 4:** Compute the physical address by adding the offset to the base:

$$
\text{Physical Address} \;=\; \text{Base} + d
$$

**Step 5:** Use the physical address to access main memory.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Unit / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Logical address format | $\langle s, \ d \rangle$ | Tuple, 2-D |
| Address validation | $0 \leq d < L$ | Bounds check |
| Physical address | $\text{PA} = B + d$ | bytes |
| Max segment count | $2^{\vert s \vert}$ | segments |
| Max segment size | $2^{\vert d \vert}$ | bytes |
| Total logical space | $2^{\vert s \vert} \times 2^{\vert d \vert}$ | bytes |
| Segment table size | $\text{num\_segments} \times \text{STE\_size}$ | bytes |
| STBR / STLR | Segment-Table Base / Limit Registers | special CPU regs |

> Use `\vert` for absolute value bars inside table cells to avoid breaking markdown pipe syntax.

### 2.5 Protection & Sharing (Engineering Utility)

Because each STE carries its own access-rights bits, the MMU can enforce **per-segment protection**:
- The **code segment** can be marked `Read + Execute` (no self-modifying code).
- The **stack segment** can be marked `Read + Write + Execute`.
- The **shared library segment** of two different processes can be mapped to the **same physical frames**, saving RAM — this is the foundation of dynamic linking (e.g., the `.so` files on Linux).

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Domain | Use Case of Segmentation |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Compilers** | Emit per-symbol type/segment metadata for code generation |
| **Embedded Systems** | Isolated memory regions for safety-critical code (e.g., AUTOSAR) |
| **Databases** | Logical segments map to separate tablespaces/tables |
| **OS Kernels** | x86 still uses segmentation in 64-bit long mode for kernel/user separation |
| **Networking** | Segmentation Offload (TSO/GSO) in NICs — same word, different context |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Physical Address from a Logical Address

We start with the formal statement:

> **Given:** a logical address $\langle s, d \rangle$ and a segment table entry $(B, L)$ for segment $s$.
> **Find:** the physical address $\text{PA}$ (or conclude a fault).

We will derive the formula and then show a complete numerical worked example.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Logical address} \;&=\; \langle s, \ d \rangle \\
\text{Segment Table}[s] \;&=\; (B_s, \ L_s, \ \text{protection}_s) \\
\text{Validity check:} \;&\; 0 \leq d < L_s \\
\text{If valid, physical address:} \;&\; \text{PA} \;=\; B_s + d \\
\text{If invalid, raise trap:} \;&\; \text{TRAP} \rightarrow \text{OS} \rightarrow \text{Segmentation Fault}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The form $\text{PA} = B_s + d$ is the fundamental translation equation. Every other variant (segmented paging, multi-level segment tables, etc.) is a refinement of this single line.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Pure Segmentation (KTU Standard)

A system uses pure segmentation. The Segment Table of process P1 is given below. Compute the physical addresses for the following logical addresses, or report a trap.

| Segment | Base (B) | Limit (L) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | 1400 | 1000 |
| 1 | 6300 | 400 |
| 2 | 4300 | 1100 |
| 3 | 3200 | 990 |
| 4 | 6700 | 2200 |

**Logical addresses to translate:** (a) $\langle 0, 500 \rangle$  (b) $\langle 1, 411 \rangle$  (c) $\langle 2, 1050 \rangle$  (d) $\langle 3, 980 \rangle$  (e) $\langle 4, 2200 \rangle$  (f) $\langle 1, 0 \rangle$

#### Solution 3.2 (a): $\langle 0, 500 \rangle$

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= 0, \quad d = 500 \\
\text{STE}[0] &: \ (B, L) = (1400, 1000) \\
\text{Validity:} \;&\; 0 \leq 500 < 1000 \quad \text{(true)} \\
\text{Physical Address:} \;&\; \text{PA} = 1400 + 500 = 1900
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** PA = **1900** ✅

#### Solution 3.2 (b): $\langle 1, 411 \rangle$

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= 1, \quad d = 411 \\
\text{STE}[1] &: \ (B, L) = (6300, 400) \\
\text{Validity:} \;&\; 0 \leq 411 < 400 \quad \text{(false — 411 is not less than 400)} \\
\text{Result:} \;&\; \text{TRAP — Segmentation Fault}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** **Invalid reference — trap to OS** ❌

#### Solution 3.2 (c): $\langle 2, 1050 \rangle$

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= 2, \quad d = 1050 \\
\text{STE}[2] &: \ (B, L) = (4300, 1100) \\
\text{Validity:} \;&\; 0 \leq 1050 < 1100 \quad \text{(true)} \\
\text{PA} \;&\; = 4300 + 1050 = 5350
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** PA = **5350** ✅

#### Solution 3.2 (d): $\langle 3, 980 \rangle$

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= 3, \quad d = 980 \\
\text{STE}[3] &: \ (B, L) = (3200, 990) \\
\text{Validity:} \;&\; 0 \leq 980 < 990 \quad \text{(true)} \\
\text{PA} \;&\; = 3200 + 980 = 4180
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** PA = **4180** ✅

#### Solution 3.2 (e): $\langle 4, 2200 \rangle$

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= 4, \quad d = 2200 \\
\text{STE}[4] &: \ (B, L) = (6700, 2200) \\
\text{Validity:} \;&\; 0 \leq 2200 < 2200 \quad \text{(false — offset must be strictly less than limit)} \\
\text{Result:} \;&\; \text{TRAP — Segmentation Fault}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** **Invalid reference — trap to OS** ❌

#### Solution 3.2 (f): $\langle 1, 0 \rangle$

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= 1, \quad d = 0 \\
\text{STE}[1] &: \ (B, L) = (6300, 400) \\
\text{Validity:} \;&\; 0 \leq 0 < 400 \quad \text{(true)} \\
\text{PA} \;&\; = 6300 + 0 = 6300
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** PA = **6300** ✅

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Logical-to-Physical Address Range Mapping

For each valid segment above, find the range of physical addresses that the segment occupies.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Segment 0: } & [1400, \ 1400 + 1000 - 1] = [1400, \ 2399] \\
\text{Segment 1: } & [6300, \ 6300 + 400 - 1] = [6300, \ 6699] \\
\text{Segment 2: } & [4300, \ 4300 + 1100 - 1] = [4300, \ 5399] \\
\text{Segment 3: } & [3200, \ 3200 + 990 - 1] = [3200, \ 4189] \\
\text{Segment 4: } & [6700, \ 6700 + 2200 - 1] = [6700, \ 8899]
\end{aligned}
$$

> These non-overlapping (or possibly overlapping if sharing is enabled) ranges show why **external fragmentation** is a problem in pure segmentation: each segment is a variable-size hole in memory, and after allocation/deallocation cycles, free holes become scattered and small.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Causes of Memory Access Failures in Segmentation

| Symptom | Probable Cause | KTU-Trap |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $d \geq L$ | Offset exceeds segment limit | **Bounds-check trap** (segfault) |
| Valid bit = 0 | Segment is swapped out to disk | **Page/Segment fault** |
| Write to R-only segment | Permission violation | **Protection fault** |
| Stack overflow | Stack segment limit exceeded | **Stack-overflow trap** |
| `s` out of STLR | Illegal segment number | **General-protection fault** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Logical Address to Physical Address Translation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU issues logical address s comma d] --> B[Extract segment number s]
    B --> C[Index into Segment Table using s]
    C --> D[Fetch STE with Base B and Limit L]
    D --> E{Is 0 less than or equal to d AND d less than L}
    E -->|No| F[Raise segmentation fault trap to OS]
    E -->|Yes| G[Check protection bits R W X]
    G --> H{Access permitted}
    H -->|No| I[Raise protection fault trap to OS]
    H -->|Yes| J[Compute Physical Address equals B plus d]
    J --> K[Access physical memory at address B plus d]
    F --> L[OS kills process or handles fault]
    I --> L
    K --> M[Return data to CPU]
```

### 4.2 Segment Table Architecture (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[CPU Logical Address s comma d] --> MMU[MMU Address Translation Unit]
    MMU --> STBR[STBR Segment Table Base Register]
    STBR --> STE0[Segment Table Entry 0 Base Limit Protection]
    STE0 --> STE1[Segment Table Entry 1 Base Limit Protection]
    STE1 --> STE2[Segment Table Entry 2 Base Limit Protection]
    STE2 --> STEn[Segment Table Entry n Base Limit Protection]
    STEn --> PA[Physical Memory]
    PA --> RAM1[Segment 0 mapped to physical frames]
    PA --> RAM2[Segment 1 mapped to physical frames]
    PA --> RAM3[Segment 2 mapped to physical frames]
    PA --> RAMn[Segment n mapped to physical frames]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of Pure Segmentation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LogicalSpace[Per Process Logical Address Space]
        LS0[Segment 0 Code]
        LS1[Segment 1 Data]
        LS2[Segment 2 Stack]
        LS3[Segment 3 Heap]
        LS4[Segment 4 User Defined]
    end
    subgraph SegmentTable[Segment Table per Process]
        ST0[Base 1400 Limit 1000]
        ST1[Base 6300 Limit 400]
        ST2[Base 4300 Limit 1100]
        ST3[Base 3200 Limit 990]
        ST4[Base 6700 Limit 2200]
    end
    subgraph PhysicalMemory[Physical RAM]
        PM0[Frame Block 1400 to 2399]
        PM1[Frame Block 6300 to 6699]
        PM2[Frame Block 4300 to 5399]
        PM3[Frame Block 3200 to 4189]
        PM4[Frame Block 6700 to 8899]
    end
    LS0 --> ST0
    LS1 --> ST1
    LS2 --> ST2
    LS3 --> ST3
    LS4 --> ST4
    ST0 --> PM0
    ST1 --> PM1
    ST2 --> PM2
    ST3 --> PM3
    ST4 --> PM4
```

### 4.4 Sharing Mechanism via Segmentation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Process P1 Segment Table] --> S1[Shared Code Segment STE points to physical 5000 to 7999]
    P2[Process P2 Segment Table] --> S1
    P1 --> P1Priv[P1 Private Data Segment physical 8000 to 9500]
    P2 --> P2Priv[P2 Private Data Segment physical 10000 to 11500]
    S1 --> SharedRAM[Shared Physical RAM Region 5000 to 7999]
    P1Priv --> Priv1[Private RAM for P1]
    P2Priv --> Priv2[Private RAM for P2]
```

> Note: All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `P1`, `ST0`, `PM0`) to comply with Mermaid parser rules; labels use clean uppercase text without bold/italic/HTML tags.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define segmentation. List any two advantages of segmentation over contiguous memory allocation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Segmentation is a memory management technique in which a program is divided into variable-sized logical units called *segments*, each representing a meaningful entity such as code, data, stack, or heap. A logical address is a 2-tuple `<segment-number, offset>`.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Logical view:** The address space mirrors the program's structure, making it easier to reason about, protect, and share.
2. **Protection and sharing:** Each segment can carry its own access-rights bits; the same physical segment (e.g., a shared library) can be mapped into the segment tables of multiple processes.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two advantages — full 3 marks.)*

#### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a segment table? Explain the role of the *base* and *limit* fields in a segment table entry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A segment table is a per-process data structure maintained by the OS that holds one entry per logical segment of the process. Each Segment Table Entry (STE) typically contains:
- **Base (B):** the starting physical address in RAM where the segment is loaded. It is added to the offset by the MMU to compute the physical address: $\text{PA} = B + d$.
- **Limit (L):** the length of the segment. The MMU performs a bounds check: if $d \geq L$, a segmentation fault trap is raised.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for base role, 1 mark for limit role — full 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B offers an **internal choice**. A student answers **either Question A or Question B**. Each carries **14 marks** and is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks** each.

---

#### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the address translation mechanism in a pure segmentation system. Show how a logical address `<s, d>` is converted to a physical address. What happens if `d ≥ L`?

**(b) [7 marks]** A system uses pure segmentation. The segment table of process P is:

| Segment | Base | Limit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | 2000 | 800 |
| 1 | 4500 | 600 |
| 2 | 1100 | 1200 |
| 3 | 7000 | 1500 |
| 4 | 9000 | 700 |

Translate the following logical addresses and report a trap if invalid: (i) `<0, 750>`, (ii) `<1, 600>`, (iii) `<2, 1199>`, (iv) `<3, 1500>`, (v) `<4, 700>`.

##### Model Solution (a) — Address Translation

1. The CPU generates a logical address in the form `<s, d>`, where `s` is the segment number and `d` is the offset within the segment.
2. The MMU uses a hardware register — the **Segment-Table Base Register (STBR)** — to locate the segment table of the current process in physical memory.
3. The segment number `s` is used as an **index** into the segment table to fetch the corresponding Segment Table Entry (STE).
4. The STE contains: the **base (B)** of the segment in physical memory, the **limit (L)** of the segment, and **protection bits**.
5. The MMU performs a **bounds check**: it verifies that $0 \leq d < L$. If this condition is **false**, the MMU raises a **trap** to the OS, which typically terminates the offending process (this is the "Segmentation Fault").
6. If the bounds check passes, the MMU checks the **protection bits** to confirm that the requested access (read/write/execute) is permitted for this segment.
7. If permitted, the **physical address** is computed as: $\text{PA} = B + d$.
8. The physical address is sent to the memory bus to read/write the actual byte.

**Diagram:** (Refer to the Mermaid architecture diagram in SECTION 4.1 of these notes.)

*(Mark split for 7 marks: 1 mark for diagram, 2 marks for steps 1–4, 2 marks for bounds check + trap, 1 mark for protection, 1 mark for PA formula + access.)*

##### Model Solution (b) — Numerical Translation

**(i) `<0, 750>`**
- STE[0]: Base = 2000, Limit = 800.
- Check: $0 \leq 750 < 800$ → True.
- $\text{PA} = 2000 + 750 = \mathbf{2750}$ ✅

**(ii) `<1, 600>`**
- STE[1]: Base = 4500, Limit = 600.
- Check: $0 \leq 600 < 600$ → **False** (600 is **not** strictly less than 600).
- **TRAP — Segmentation Fault** ❌

**(iii) `<2, 1199>`**
- STE[2]: Base = 1100, Limit = 1200.
- Check: $0 \leq 1199 < 1200$ → True.
- $\text{PA} = 1100 + 1199 = \mathbf{2299}$ ✅

**(iv) `<3, 1500>`**
- STE[3]: Base = 7000, Limit = 1500.
- Check: $0 \leq 1500 < 1500$ → **False**.
- **TRAP — Segmentation Fault** ❌

**(v) `<4, 700>`**
- STE[4]: Base = 9000, Limit = 700.
- Check: $0 \leq 700 < 700$ → **False**.
- **TRAP — Segmentation Fault** ❌

*(Mark split for 7 marks: 1.4 marks per sub-part — 1 mark for the bounds check and 0.4 for the final PA or trap result.)*

---

#### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Differentiate between **paging** and **segmentation**. Which scheme suffers from **external fragmentation** and why? Explain how segmentation can be combined with paging to overcome the disadvantages of both.

**(b) [7 marks]** In a segmented-paging system, a logical address consists of `<segment-number, page-number, offset>`. The segment table has 4 entries, each pointing to a page table. Each page table has a maximum of 8 pages, and each page is 1 KB. Given a logical address with segment = 1, page = 5, offset = 100:
- (i) Compute the size of the logical address space and physical address space (assume physical memory = 16 MB).
- (ii) Describe the full two-level translation.
- (iii) State the number of page table entries and the number of frames required.

##### Model Solution (a) — Comparison and Hybrid Scheme

| Feature | Paging | Segmentation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Division unit | Fixed-size *pages* | Variable-size *segments* |
| Programmer aware? | No (transparent) | Yes (logical units) |
| Logical address | 1-D (page #, offset) | 2-D (segment #, offset) |
| Fragmentation | Internal only | External only |
| Protection | Per page | Per segment |
| Sharing | At page granularity | At whole-segment granularity |
| Hardware | Page table + TLB | Segment table |

- **External fragmentation** is the problem of **segmentation**: because segments are variable-sized, after many allocations and deallocations, free memory is broken into small holes too small to satisfy a new request.
- **Internal fragmentation** is the problem of **paging**: the last page of a process is usually not fully used, wasting on average half a page per process.
- **Segmented Paging** (e.g., x86, MULTICS, IBM/370):
  - The user sees a segmented logical address (clean programming model, protection, sharing per segment).
  - Each segment is broken internally into fixed-size pages.
  - The OS now only needs to allocate **whole pages** for a segment, eliminating external fragmentation.
  - Each segment is described by `(base-page-table-address, page-table-length)` in the segment table.

*(Mark split: 2 marks for the table, 2 marks for fragmentation analysis, 3 marks for segmented-paging explanation + diagram.)*

##### Model Solution (b) — Segmented-Paging Translation

**Given parameters:**
- Segments = $2^2 = 4$ (segment number width = 2 bits)
- Pages per segment = $2^3 = 8$ (page number width = 3 bits)
- Page size = 1 KB = $2^{10}$ bytes (offset width = 10 bits)
- Physical memory = 16 MB = $2^{24}$ bytes (frame number width = 24 − 10 = 14 bits)

**(i) Address space sizes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Logical address space} &= 2^{(2+3+10)} = 2^{15} = 32 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Physical address space} &= 2^{24} = 16 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Two-level translation for `<s=1, p=5, d=100>`:**

1. Use `s = 1` to index the **Segment Table** of the process → fetch the page-table base address of segment 1.
2. Use `p = 5` to index the **Page Table of segment 1** → fetch the frame number `f` corresponding to page 5.
3. The physical address is formed as: $\text{PA} = f \times 2^{10} + d = f \cdot 1024 + 100$.
4. The MMU accesses physical memory at PA.

**(iii) Frame/Entry counts:**

- Each segment can have up to 8 pages → each page table has **8 entries**.
- Logical address space = 32 KB, page size = 1 KB → total pages needed = 32 KB / 1 KB = **32 pages** total across all segments.
- Number of frames = 16 MB / 1 KB = $2^{14} = $ **16,384 frames** in physical memory.

*(Mark split for 7 marks: 2 marks for (i), 3 marks for (ii) step-by-step, 2 marks for (iii) numerical answers.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks in Segmentation Questions**
> 1. **The "off-by-one" trap:** Students write the bounds check as `0 ≤ d ≤ L`. The correct inequality is **strict**: $0 \leq d < L$. Offset equal to the limit is **outside** the segment. Expect to lose 1 full mark for this.
> 2. **Forgetting to add offset to base:** Students sometimes write PA = B alone. The correct equation is $\text{PA} = B + d$. Always add the offset.
> 3. **Confusing "segment number" with "segment number of bits":** The segment number is an **integer index** (e.g., 0, 1, 2); the **number of bits** in the segment field determines the *maximum* number of segments ($2^{\vert s \vert}$). Examiners test this difference.
> 4. **Confusing "Segmentation Fault" with "Page Fault":** A segmentation fault = offset out of bounds (or illegal access). A page fault = the page is valid but currently on disk. This distinction is a 2-mark question by itself.
> 5. **Missing protection check:** KTU specifically tests whether students mention that the MMU also checks R/W/X permission bits, not just the bounds.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (must-memorize):** Segmentation divides a program into **variable-size logical units** (segments). A logical address is the 2-tuple `<segment-number, offset>`.
- **Translation equation (must-memorize):** $\text{PA} = \text{Base} + d$, after the bounds check $0 \leq d < L$ passes.
- **Segment Table fields:** Base, Limit, Protection (R/W/X), Valid bit.
- **Hardware registers:** STBR (Segment-Table Base Register), STLR (Segment-Table Limit Register).
- **Fragmentation:** Pure segmentation suffers from **external fragmentation** (variable sizes). Pure paging suffers from **internal fragmentation** (last page partially used).
- **Protection:** Per-segment access control is a major advantage — code segment can be marked non-writable, etc.
- **Sharing:** Entire segments can be shared between processes by mapping two segment tables to the same physical region. This is the basis of shared libraries.
- **Segmentation fault:** Triggered when $d \geq L$ or when protection bits deny the access.
- **Hybrid (Segmented Paging):** First look up segment table → then page table → then frame. Eliminates both external fragmentation (from paging) and gives logical-view benefits (from segmentation). Used in x86 and MULTICS.
- **Comparison traps to remember for KTU:**
  - Segmentation is **programmer-visible**; paging is **programmer-invisible**.
  - Segmentation address is **2-D**; paging address is **1-D**.
  - Segmentation uses **variable size**; paging uses **fixed size**.
- **Common KTU numerical traps:**
  - Always check $d < L$ (strictly less than).
  - If invalid, write the word **"TRAP"** or **"Segmentation Fault"** — do not invent a physical address.
  - When asked for "address range," remember $\text{range} = [B, \ B + L - 1]$, not $[B, B+L]$.
- **Real-world connection:** Every time you see `Segmentation fault (core dumped)` on a Linux terminal, you are seeing the modern descendant of the MMU trap described in this module.
- **Exam tip:** Draw the Mermaid-style flowchart (CPU → STBR → STE → Bounds Check → PA) **before** writing the answer. Examiners award easy partial credit for a clear diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Paging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Paging # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Paging** is a **non-contiguous memory allocation scheme** (a memory management technique) that eliminates the need for allocating physically contiguous memory to a process. It works by dividing the process's **logical address space** into fixed-size blocks called **Pages**, and dividing the **physical memory (RAM)** into fixed-size blocks called **Frames** (or Page Frames) of identical size. A data structure called the **Page Table** (maintained by the Operating System, typically per-process) is used to maintain the mapping between every logical page number and its corresponding physical frame number in main memory. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, paging is a **demand-driven, hardware-assisted logical-to-physical address translation mechanism** that allows a process to be loaded and executed even when its pages are scattered across non-contiguous physical frames, thereby removing the problem of **external fragmentation** that plagued contiguous allocation schemes. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Library Book Sticker" Model** > > Imagine a giant library with **1,000,000 shelves** (physical memory / RAM). A student wants to study a **2,000-page textbook** (a process) but the librarian refuses to give her a single block of 2,000 contiguous shelves. Instead, the librarian tears the textbook into 200-page chapters (these are the **pages**) and the 1,000,000 shelves into 5,000 racks of 200 shelves each (these are the **frames**). > > The student is given a **Master Index Card** (the **Page Table**) that says: "Chapter 1 is on Rack 47, Chapter 2 is on Rack 1, Chapter 3 is on Rack 9,004..." etc. Whenever the student wants to read a specific page in a chapter, she looks up the rack number on the index card, walks to that rack, and reads. The chapter number is the **logical address (page number)**; the rack number is the **physical address (frame number)**. The page *within* a chapter is the **offset**. This is exactly what paging does: it decouples a process's *logical* view of memory (a clean, contiguous, linear block) from its *physical* reality (scattered frames), using a page table as the index card. ## 1.3 Why Paging? — The Motivation - **Eliminates External Fragmentation:** Free memory can be allocated in scattered frames; no large contiguous hole is required. - **Simplifies Allocation:** The OS only needs to find free frames (anywhere), not free contiguous blocks. - **Enables Virtual Memory:** Paging is the *foundation* of virtual memory, where pages can be temporarily stored on disk (swap space) and brought in on demand. - **Provides Process Isolation & Protection:** Each process has its own page table, preventing one process from accessing another's memory. > [!WARNING] > **Important Distinction for KTU 2024 Exams:** Paging *eliminates external fragmentation entirely* but suffers from a small amount of **internal fragmentation** in the last page of a process (since the process size is rarely an exact multiple of the page size). ## 1.4 The Two Address Spaces | Concept | Symbol | Description | |---|---|---| | **Logical Address Space** | $2^n$ bytes | The address range a process *thinks* it has (generated by CPU) | | **Physical Address Space** | $2^m$ bytes | The actual RAM installed in the system | | **Logical Address** | `(p, d)` | Page number `p` + Page offset `d` | | **Physical Address** | `(f, d)` | Frame number `f` + Same page offset `d` | > Offset `d` is **unchanged** during translation. Only the page number `p` is replaced by the frame number `f`. This is the heart of the paging mechanism. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Architecture — How Paging Works (Step-by-Step) The paging mechanism executes the following sequence every time the CPU generates a memory address: 1. **CPU generates a logical address** consisting of a *page number (p)* and a *page offset (d)*. The page number `p` occupies the **higher-order bits** of the logical address; the offset `d` occupies the **lower-order bits**. 2. The **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** — a hardware component — intercepts the logical address. 3. The MMU uses the page number `p` as an **index into the Process's Page Table** (held in main memory; base address stored in the **Page Table Base Register, PTBR**). 4. The Page Table entry at index `p` contains the corresponding **Frame Number (f)** in physical RAM. 5. The MMU combines the frame number `f` (higher-order bits) with the original offset `d` (lower-order bits) to form the **Physical Address**. 6. This physical address is sent to the memory bus, and the data is fetched. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Page Table Entry (PTE):** Each entry typically contains the *Frame Number*, a *Valid/Invalid bit* (V/I), a *Protection bit* (Read/Write/Execute), a *Dirty bit* (modified), and a *Reference bit* (used for page replacement algorithms like LRU and Clock). ## 2.2 Address Structure — The Bit-Partitioning Math Let the **logical address space** have size $2^n$ bytes, and the **page size** be $2^k$ bytes. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Number of bits for offset } (d) &= k \\ \text{Number of bits for page number } (p) &= n - k \\ \text{Number of logical pages} &= 2^{n-k} \end{aligned} $$ Similarly, if physical address space has size $2^m$ bytes: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Number of physical frames} &= 2^{m-k} \\ \text{Frame number bits} &= m - k \end{aligned} $$ > The **offset `d` requires the same number of bits in both logical and physical addresses** because the offset is an intra-page displacement, and pages/frames have identical size. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | Page Size = Frame Size | $P = F$ | Always identical (typically **4 KB** in modern systems) | | Number of Pages in Process | $N_{pages} = \lceil \text{Process Size} / P \rceil$ | Pages (integer) | | Number of Frames in RAM | $N_{frames} = \text{RAM Size} / P$ | Frames (integer) | | Page Table Size | $N_{pages} \times \text{Size of one PTE}$ | Bytes (per process) | | Offset Bits | $k = \log_2(P)$ | Bits | | Page Number Bits (logical) | $n - k$ | Bits | | Frame Number Bits (physical) | $m - k$ | Bits | | Internal Fragmentation (worst) | $P - 1$ bytes | Last page only | | Internal Fragmentation (average) | $P / 2$ bytes | Statistical average | | EAT with TLB (basic) | $T = h \cdot (T_{tlb} + T_{m}) + (1 - h) \cdot (T_{tlb} + 2 \cdot T_{m})$ | nanoseconds | | EAT (when TLB hit is followed by cache miss) | $T = h \cdot (T_{tlb} + T_{m}) + (1 - h) \cdot (T_{tlb} + T_{m} + T_{m})$ | Same as above, expanded | | Multi-level Paging depth | Generally 2-level or 4-level for 64-bit | | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL KTU EXAM RULE:** In every formula table, the vertical pipe character `|` is rendered as `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown syntax. For example, "h \vert T" is correct; "h | T" would crash the table parser. ## 2.4 The 'Why' Behind the Design - **Why fixed-size pages?** Fixed sizes allow the offset to be reused as-is during translation. If pages were variable, the offset would need to be looked up separately, slowing every memory access. - **Why is the offset unchanged?** Because the CPU's offset specifies a *byte position within a page*. Since frames and pages are the *same size*, the byte-position mapping is one-to-one. - **Why a per-process page table?** Provides protection (a process cannot access frames outside its table), supports virtual memory (each page can be marked V/I), and allows independent logical views. - **Why a TLB?** Because consulting the page table in main memory **doubles the memory access time** (one access to read PTE, one to access data). The **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** — a small, fast, associative cache of recent page-to-frame translations — reduces this to near-original speed. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - Every modern general-purpose OS (Linux, Windows, macOS, Android) uses paging as its primary memory management scheme. - Modern x86-64 CPUs use **4-level paging** (PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT) with 4 KB pages. - ARM processors (used in mobile phones) use paging for virtual memory. - Database systems (Oracle, PostgreSQL) exploit page-level I/O for **buffer pool management**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Address Partitioning (KTU Classic Problem) **Problem:** A system has a **logical address space of 16 KB** and a **page size of 1 KB**. A process generates the logical address **0x3A4D** (hexadecimal). Find: (a) the page number, (b) the page offset, (c) the number of pages, and (d) construct the binary breakdown. ### Step 1 — Convert Sizes into Powers of 2 $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Logical address space} &= 16 \text{ KB} = 16 \times 2^{10} \text{ bytes} = 2^{14} \text{ bytes} \\ \text{Hence } n &= 14 \text{ bits (total logical address width)} \\ \text{Page size} &= 1 \text{ KB} = 2^{10} \text{ bytes} \\ \text{Hence offset bits } k &= 10 \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* We express everything as powers of 2 because that is how the hardware's bit-fields are partitioned. A 16 KB logical space requires 14 bits to address uniquely ($2^{14} = 16,384$). ### Step 2 — Page Number Bits $$ \begin{aligned} p\text{-bits} &= n - k = 14 - 10 = 4 \text{ bits} \\ \text{Number of pages} &= 2^4 = 16 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Convert Logical Address to Binary $$ \begin{aligned} 0\text{x}3\text{A}4\text{D} &= 0011\,1010\,0100\,1101 \text{ in binary (16-bit representation)} \\ &= 0011\,1010\,0100\,1101 \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* Each hex digit = 4 bits. `3`=`0011`, `A`=`1010`, `4`=`0100`, `D`=`1101`. The 16-bit representation is the concatenation. ### Step 4 — Split into Page Number and Offset The 16-bit logical address is split with the **upper 4 bits as page number** and the **lower 10 bits as offset**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Logical Address} &= \underbrace{0011}_{p}\,\underbrace{1010010011}_{d} \\ p_{\text{binary}} &= 0011 = 3_{10} \\ d_{\text{binary}} &= 1010010011 = 0\text{x}293 = 659_{10} \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* The 4 most-significant bits form `p`; the 10 least-significant bits form `d`. The offset (659) tells us *which byte within page 3*. ### Step 5 — Physical Address Reconstruction (assuming Frame Number = 5) If the page table maps page 3 to frame 5, then $f = 5 = 0101_2$. The physical address becomes: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Physical Address} &= \underbrace{0101}_{f}\,\underbrace{1010010011}_{d} \\ &= 0101\,1010\,0100\,1101 \\ &= 0\text{x}5\text{A}4\text{D} \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* The frame number replaces the page number in the high-order bits; the offset is **reused verbatim** (since pages and frames are the same size). --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Effective Access Time (EAT) with TLB **Problem:** A paging system has a TLB access time of **20 ns**, a memory access time of **100 ns**, and a TLB hit ratio of **80%**. Compute the **Effective Access Time (EAT)**. ### Step 1 — Identify the Two Scenarios There are exactly two outcomes per memory reference: - **TLB Hit (probability = h = 0.80):** TLB lookup succeeds → 1 TLB access + 1 memory access (for data). - **TLB Miss (probability = 1 - h = 0.20):** TLB lookup fails → 1 TLB access + 1 memory access (to fetch PTE) + 1 memory access (to fetch data). ### Step 2 — Compute Time for Each Scenario $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{hit}} &= T_{tlb} + T_{m} = 20 + 100 = 120 \text{ ns} \\ T_{\text{miss}} &= T_{tlb} + T_{m} + T_{m} = 20 + 100 + 100 = 220 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* On a miss, the MMU must consult the page table in main memory (one full memory access) *and then* access the desired data (another full memory access). ### Step 3 — Compute the Weighted EAT $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EAT} &= h \cdot T_{\text{hit}} + (1 - h) \cdot T_{\text{miss}} \\ &= (0.80 \times 120) + (0.20 \times 220) \\ &= 96 + 44 \\ &= 140 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* EAT is a *probability-weighted average* over all memory accesses. The result (140 ns) is only 40% slower than a system without paging (100 ns) — a 2× slowdown without TLB would have given 200 ns. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Page Table Size Calculation **Problem:** A process is **20 MB** in size. The page size is **4 KB**. Each Page Table Entry (PTE) is **4 bytes**. Compute: (a) the number of pages, (b) the page table size, and (c) the worst-case internal fragmentation. ### Step 1 — Number of Pages $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{pages}} &= \lceil \text{Process Size} / \text{Page Size} \rceil \\ &= \lceil 20 \times 2^{20} \text{ bytes} / 4 \times 2^{10} \text{ bytes} \rceil \\ &= \lceil 20 \times 2^{10} \rceil \\ &= 20480 \text{ pages} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Page Table Size $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PT Size} &= N_{\text{pages}} \times \text{PTE size} \\ &= 20480 \times 4 \text{ bytes} \\ &= 81920 \text{ bytes} = 80 \text{ KB} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Internal Fragmentation The last page contains $20 \text{ MB} \mod 4 \text{ KB}$ useful bytes, wasting the rest: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Used in last page} &= (20 \times 2^{20}) \mod (4 \times 2^{10}) \\ &= 0 \text{ bytes (since } 20 \text{ MB is exactly } 5120 \times 4 \text{ KB)} \\ \text{Internal Fragmentation} &= 0 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* Because 20 MB is an exact multiple of 4 KB, there is *zero* internal fragmentation in this case. In general, the worst case is `Page Size - 1` bytes wasted in the last page. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Page Table Variants | Variant | Structure | Memory Overhead | Speed | Used In | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Single-level Page Table** | 1 array, indexed by `p` | $O(2^{n-k})$ entries | Fast lookup (1 memory access) | Simple, small address spaces | | **Two-level Page Table** | Page Directory + Page Tables | Allocates only used ranges; saves space | 3 memory accesses for translation | x86 (32-bit), some embedded | | **Inverted Page Table** | One global table indexed by frame | $O(2^{m-k})$ entries (fixed) | Slower; needs hash table | Used when physical memory is small relative to logical | | **Hashed Page Table** | Hash of `(pid, p)` → entry | Variable | Very fast lookups | Some modern Unix systems | | **TLB (cache)** | Fully-associative hardware cache | A few KB of silicon | ~1 ns | All modern CPUs | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes **basic paging, TLB, and Effective Access Time (EAT)** calculations. Two-level and inverted page tables are conceptual highlights but EAT numerics on a single-level table with TLB are the most exam-frequent. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Paging Address Translation Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB CPU["CPU generates
Logical Address p d"] MMU["Memory Management Unit MMU
Hardware Component"] PTBR["Page Table Base Register PTBR
Stores Page Table Address"] PTE["Page Table Entry PTE
Contains Frame Number f and V I bit"] PT["Process Page Table
Stored in Main Memory"] RAM["Physical Memory RAM
Frames F0 F1 F2 F3 F4 F5 F6 F7"] TLB["Translation Lookaside Buffer TLB
Small Associative Cache"] CPU -->|"Logical Address p d"| MMU MMU -->|"Check cache"| TLB TLB -->|"TLB HIT returns f directly"| MMU TLB -->|"TLB MISS consult PTE"| PTBR PTBR -->|"Index by p"| PT PT -->|"PTE with f"| MMU MMU -->|"Physical Address f d"| RAM RAM -->|"Data word"| CPU ``` *Explanation:* The CPU issues a logical address. The MMU first checks the TLB (a fast associative cache). On a hit, the frame number is returned in ~1 cycle. On a miss, the MMU walks the page table in main memory using the base address from the PTBR. ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Logical-to-Physical Address Mapping (Block View) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LAS["LOGICAL ADDRESS SPACE OF PROCESS"] P0["Page 0"] P1["Page 1"] P2["Page 2"] P3["Page 3"] P4["Page 4"] end subgraph PT["PROCESS PAGE TABLE"] PT0["PTE 0 equals F2"] PT1["PTE 1 equals F0"] PT2["PTE 2 equals F5"] PT3["PTE 3 equals F1"] PT4["PTE 4 equals F4"] end subgraph PAS["PHYSICAL MEMORY FRAMES"] F0["Frame 0"] F1["Frame 1"] F2["Frame 2"] F3["Frame 3 FREE"] F4["Frame 4"] F5["Frame 5"] F6["Frame 6 FREE"] F7["Frame 7 FREE"] end P0 -.->|"mapped via PT0"| PT0 P1 -.->|"mapped via PT1"| PT1 P2 -.->|"mapped via PT2"| PT2 P3 -.->|"mapped via PT3"| PT3 P4 -.->|"mapped via PT4"| PT4 PT0 -.->|"f equals 2"| F2 PT1 -.->|"f equals 0"| F0 PT2 -.->|"f equals 5"| F5 PT3 -.->|"f equals 1"| F1 PT4 -.->|"f equals 4"| F4 ``` *Explanation:* Logical pages 0,1,2,3,4 of the process are scattered across physical frames 2,0,5,1,4 respectively. The page table acts as the index, and the OS can allocate any free frame (Frames 3, 6, 7 are free in this snapshot) without needing contiguity. ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — TLB Lookup Flow with EAT Outcomes ```mermaid flowchart TD START(["Memory Reference Issued"]) --> TLBCHECK{"TLB Lookup
Hit?"} TLBCHECK -->|"YES probability h"| HITA["Access Time equals T tlb plus T m"] TLBCHECK -->|"NO probability 1 minus h"| MISSA["Access Time equals T tlb plus 2 times T m
Read PTE from RAM plus read DATA from RAM"] HITA --> COMBINE1["Total time equals T tlb plus T m"] MISSA --> COMBINE2["Total time equals T tlb plus 2 times T m"] COMBINE1 --> RESULT["Effective Access Time
EAT equals h times T tlb plus T m plus 1 minus h times T tlb plus 2 times T m"] COMBINE2 --> RESULT RESULT --> END(["Return data to CPU"]) ``` *Explanation:* This is the canonical flow for KTU EAT questions. Memorize the two branch times and the weighted-average formula. ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Multi-Level Paging (Two-Level) Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB LA["Logical Address
Outer Page p1 plus Inner Page p2 plus Offset d"] PD["Outer Page Directory
Indexed by p1"] PT2["Inner Page Table
Indexed by p2"] PTE["PTE gives Frame Number f"] PA["Physical Address
f plus d"] LA -->|"Extract p1"| PD PD -->|"Pointer to inner page table"| PT2 PT2 -->|"Extract p2 yields PTE"| PTE PTE -->|"Get f"| PA ``` *Explanation:* For large address spaces (e.g., 32-bit with 4 KB pages → 1 million pages per process), a single-level page table would consume ~4 MB per process. Two-level paging uses an outer page directory that points to inner page tables; only inner tables for *valid* address ranges are allocated, dramatically reducing overhead for sparse processes. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023] **Define paging. Why is it called a non-contiguous memory allocation scheme?** **Model Answer:** Paging is a memory management technique that divides a process's logical address space into fixed-size blocks called *pages* and divides physical RAM into fixed-size blocks called *frames* of the same size. A *page table* maps each logical page to a physical frame, allowing the process's pages to reside in any free frames in RAM — not necessarily adjacent to one another. Because the OS can place pages of a process in *scattered* frames, paging is called a **non-contiguous allocation scheme**. It eliminates external fragmentation but may cause a small amount of internal fragmentation in the last page. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **What is a Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)? How does it improve paging performance?** **Model Answer:** The **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** is a small, fast, fully-associative hardware cache inside the CPU's Memory Management Unit (MMU) that stores the most-recently-used **page number → frame number** translations. Without a TLB, every memory reference requires *two* physical memory accesses (one to read the PTE from the page table, one to read the actual data), doubling the access time. With a TLB, when the CPU generates a logical address, the MMU first checks the TLB. If the page number is present (**TLB hit**), the frame number is obtained in ~1 cycle without consulting the page table, and only *one* memory access is needed for the data. This reduces the Effective Access Time (EAT) close to the raw memory access time, depending on the **hit ratio `h`**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the basic concept of paging with a neat diagram. Show how a logical address is translated to a physical address using the page table. **Model Answer:** **Concept of Paging:** Paging is a memory management scheme that: 1. Divides the logical address space of a process into equal-sized **pages** (typical size: 4 KB). 2. Divides the physical memory (RAM) into equal-sized **frames** of the same size as pages. 3. Maintains a **page table** for each process that stores the mapping `page number → frame number`. **Address Translation Steps:** - The CPU generates a logical address `(p, d)` where `p` = page number, `d` = offset. - The MMU uses `p` as an index into the page table. - The corresponding PTE contains the frame number `f`. - The physical address is constructed as `(f, d)`. - The data is fetched from physical memory at address `(f, d)`. **Diagram:** ``` +-------------------------+ +-------------------------+ | LOGICAL ADDRESS | | PHYSICAL ADDRESS | | +-------+-----------+ | | +-------+-----------+ | | | p | d | | | | f | d | | | | (n-k) | (k) | | | | (m-k) | (k) | | | +-------+-----------+ | | +-------+-----------+ | +-------------------------+ +-------------------------+ | ^ v | +-------------------------+ +-------------------------+ | PAGE TABLE | | MAIN MEMORY | | PTE 0 ---> Frame 2 | | Frame 0 | Frame 1 | | PTE 1 ---> Frame 0 | | Frame 2 | Frame 3 | | PTE 2 ---> Frame 5 | | Frame 4 | Frame 5 | | PTE 3 ---> Frame 1 | | Frame 6 | Frame 7 | | PTE 4 ---> Frame 4 | | | +-------------------------+ +-------------------------+ ``` *Explanation:* The CPU's logical address has `p` in the high bits and `d` in the low bits. The MMU uses `p` to index the page table, obtains `f`, replaces `p` with `f`, and keeps `d` unchanged. **Q. (b) [7 Marks]** A system has a logical address space of **32 KB**, page size of **1 KB**, and physical memory of **64 KB**. For the logical address **0x5A3F**, determine: (i) the page number, (ii) the offset, and (iii) the number of pages and frames.
