If the page table maps page 5 to frame 9, find the physical address. **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Compute Bits:** - Logical address space = 32 KB = $2^{15}$ bytes → $n = 15$ bits. - Page size = 1 KB = $2^{10}$ bytes → $k = 10$ offset bits. - Page number bits = $n - k = 15 - 10 = 5$ bits. **Step 2 — Convert Logical Address to Binary (15 bits):** - $0\text{x}5\text{A}3\text{F} = 0101\,1010\,0011\,1111$ (16 bits; we take the lower 15 = `101 1010 0011 1111`). **Step 3 — Partition:** - $p = $ upper 5 bits = `10110` = 22 in decimal. - $d = $ lower 10 bits = `1000111111` = 0x23F = 575 in decimal. **Step 4 — Number of Pages and Frames:** - Number of pages = $2^5 = 32$ pages. - Number of frames = $64 \text{ KB} / 1 \text{ KB} = 64$ frames. - Physical address size = $2^{16}$ bytes → frame bits = $16 - 10 = 6$ bits. **Step 5 — Map Page 22 to Frame 9:** - Frame 9 in 6 bits = `001001`. - Physical address = `001001 1000111111` = `0010 0110 0011 1111` = $0\text{x}263F$. **Answer:** Page = 22, Offset = 575, Pages = 32, Frames = 64, Physical Address = **0x263F**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024] *(Internal Choice Alternative)* **Q. (a) [7 Marks]** What is the **Effective Access Time (EAT)** in a paging system with TLB? Derive the formula and compute EAT given: TLB access time = **10 ns**, memory access time = **100 ns**, TLB hit ratio = **90%**. **Model Answer:** **Derivation:** In a paging system with TLB, every memory reference goes through one of two paths: - **Path 1 — TLB Hit (probability = h):** 1. TLB lookup: $T_{tlb}$ 2. Memory access for data: $T_m$ - Total: $T_{tlb} + T_m$ - **Path 2 — TLB Miss (probability = $1 - h$):** 1. TLB lookup: $T_{tlb}$ 2. Memory access for PTE: $T_m$ 3. Memory access for data: $T_m$ - Total: $T_{tlb} + 2T_m$ **Effective Access Time (weighted average):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EAT} &= h \cdot (T_{tlb} + T_m) + (1 - h) \cdot (T_{tlb} + 2T_m) \\ &= h \cdot T_{tlb} + h \cdot T_m + (1 - h) \cdot T_{tlb} + (1 - h) \cdot 2T_m \\ &= T_{tlb} + h \cdot T_m + (1 - h) \cdot 2T_m \\ &= T_{tlb} + (2 - h) \cdot T_m \end{aligned} $$ **Computation (with h = 0.90, $T_{tlb}$ = 10 ns, $T_m$ = 100 ns):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EAT} &= 10 + (2 - 0.90) \times 100 \\ &= 10 + 1.10 \times 100 \\ &= 10 + 110 \\ &= 120 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ **Answer:** The Effective Access Time is **120 ns** (only 20% slower than a non-paging system — a major win thanks to the high TLB hit ratio). **Q. (b) [7 Marks]** Explain **two-level paging**. Why is it needed? Given a 32-bit logical address with page size = 4 KB, determine the number of bits for outer page, inner page, and offset. **Model Answer:** **Need for Two-Level Paging:** In a single-level paging scheme, a 32-bit address space with 4 KB pages yields $2^{20}$ = **1,048,576 pages**. If each PTE is 4 bytes, the page table consumes **4 MB per process**. With 100 processes, this consumes 400 MB of physical memory — impractical. **Two-Level Paging Concept:** Two-level paging introduces an **outer page table** (called a *page directory*) that does not contain frame numbers; instead, each entry points to an *inner page table*. The inner page tables are allocated **only for the address ranges actually used**, eliminating the large unused slabs of a single-level table. **Address Breakdown (32-bit, 4 KB page):** - Page size = $4 \text{ KB} = 2^{12}$ bytes → $k = 12$ offset bits. - Total logical bits = 32. - Remaining bits for page-number = $32 - 12 = 20$ bits. - These 20 bits are split equally (typical design): **10 bits for outer page, 10 bits for inner page**. **Diagram (conceptual):** ``` +---------------------------+ | Logical Address (32 bits) | +-----+---------+-----------+ | p1 | p2 | d | | 10 | 10 | 12 | +-----+---------+-----------+ | | | v v v [Page ] [Page ] [Offset within [Directory] [Table] page] | | +--> points to ``` **Advantages:** - Allocates inner page tables only for valid address ranges → **saves memory**. - Scales to 64-bit systems with 4-level paging (PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT in x86-64). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** A 4 KB page size is $4 \times 1024 = 4096$ bytes = $2^{12}$ bytes — *not* 4000. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this error. > 2. **Reusing the offset incorrectly:** Some students *recalculate* the offset as `(d × frame_size / page_size)`. This is wrong — the offset is **byte-for-byte identical** in logical and physical addresses. > 3. **EAT formula with TLB miscount:** In a TLB miss, the total memory accesses are **2** (PTE + data), *not* 1. Using $T_{tlb} + T_m$ for the miss case is the most common mistake; it under-estimates EAT. > 4. **Page Table Size confusion:** The PT size is $N_{pages} \times \text{PTE size}$. A student often forgets to multiply by PTE size, calculating only the number of pages and stopping there. > 5. **Internal vs External Fragmentation:** Students often claim paging eliminates *all* fragmentation. It eliminates *external* fragmentation but causes a small *internal* fragmentation in the last page. Marks are deducted for this imprecision. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Summary — Paging** - [x] Paging divides logical address space into **pages** and physical memory into **frames** of the *same size*. - [x] The **page table** maps page number `p` to frame number `f`; offset `d` is preserved verbatim. - [x] Logical address `(p, d)`: `p` occupies **high-order bits**, `d` occupies **low-order bits**. - [x] Offset bits $k = \log_2(\text{page size})$; page number bits $= n - k$. - [x] Paging **eliminates external fragmentation** but causes a small **internal fragmentation** (worst case: $P - 1$ bytes in the last page). - [x] **MMU (Memory Management Unit)** is the hardware that performs translation; **PTBR** holds the page table's base address. - [x] **Page Table Entry (PTE)** typically contains: frame number, valid/invalid bit, protection bits, dirty bit, reference bit. - [x] **TLB** is a fast, fully-associative cache of recent translations. A **TLB hit** requires 1 memory access; a **TLB miss** requires 2. - [x] **EAT Formula:** $EAT = h \cdot (T_{tlb} + T_m) + (1 - h) \cdot (T_{tlb} + 2T_m)$ = $T_{tlb} + (2 - h) \cdot T_m$. - [x] **Two-level paging** is used to reduce the memory overhead of single-level page tables for large address spaces. - [x] **Inverted page table** uses one global table indexed by *frame number* (entry holds `(process_id, page_number)`). - [x] Typical page size in modern systems = **4 KB**; can also be 2 MB or 1 GB (huge pages) for performance. - [x] Paging is the **foundation of virtual memory** — pages can be swapped to disk when RAM is full. - [x] KTU 2024 module focus: **address translation arithmetic**, **page table size**, **EAT computation with TLB**, and **two-level paging overview**.

topics.title: Virtual Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Virtual Memory ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Virtual Memory** is a memory management technique that provides an **illusion of a large, contiguous main memory** to the user process, even when the actual physical memory (RAM) is much smaller. It achieves this by using a combination of **hardware (MMU)** and **software (OS)** to map virtual addresses used by a process to physical addresses in main memory, while transparently spilling unused portions to **secondary storage (disk)** acting as a backing store or swap space. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, Virtual Memory enables: - **Process Isolation**: Each process operates in its own address space. - **Efficient Memory Utilization**: Only the actively used pages are kept in RAM. - **Logical Address Space Expansion**: Programs larger than physical memory can execute. ### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Mandatory Vocabulary) | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Virtual Address** | Logical address generated by the CPU during program execution. | | **Physical Address** | Actual address in main memory (RAM). | | **MMU (Memory Management Unit)** | Hardware device that performs runtime virtual-to-physical address translation. | | **Page** | Fixed-size block of virtual memory (typically **4 KB**). | | **Frame** | Fixed-size block of physical memory (same size as a page). | | **Swap Space** | Reserved area on the disk used to hold pages not currently in RAM. | | **Page Fault** | Hardware trap raised when a referenced page is not in main memory. | | **Demand Paging** | Strategy where pages are loaded into memory *only when demanded* by the process. | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Library Desk Analogy** > > Imagine you are studying for an exam. You have a **small desk** (Physical RAM = **8 GB**) and a **massive bookshelf in another room** (Hard Disk = **1 TB**). You cannot carry all books to the desk at once. > > - **Virtual Memory** acts as your brain's **"illusion of having every book on the desk"**. You mentally know the entire library is accessible. > - When you need a specific chapter that is *not on the desk*, you go fetch it (**Page Fault** → Disk I/O). > - To make room, you return an old book to the shelf (**Page Replacement**). > - The desk librarian (the **MMU + OS**) keeps track of what is on the desk using a **register/chit** (the **Page Table**). This is exactly how Virtual Memory works — it decouples the **logical view** (a huge, flat address space) from the **physical reality** (a small, limited RAM). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Virtual Memory? — The "Why" Behind the Concept Virtual Memory was introduced to solve three critical problems in early multiprogramming systems: 1. **Size Limitation**: Programs were restricted by physical RAM size. A program needing 4 GB could not run on a 2 GB machine. 2. **Fragmentation**: Contiguous allocation suffered from external fragmentation; pure paging still suffered from internal fragmentation. 3. **Process Protection**: Without separate address spaces, one process could overwrite another's memory. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Insight**: Virtual Memory achieves **logical address space independence**, allowing the programmer to write code as if the entire memory belongs to a single process, while the OS+MMU handle the messy reality of limited RAM. ### 2.2 Demand Paging — The Heart of Virtual Memory **Demand Paging** is the most common implementation of virtual memory. It loads pages into RAM **only on demand (lazy loading)**. - The program starts with **no pages in memory**. - A `valid-invalid bit` (or `present bit`) in the page table entry indicates whether a page is in RAM. - `1` = Page is in memory (valid). - `0` = Page is NOT in memory (invalid → causes Page Fault). - The first instruction that references an unloaded page triggers a **Page Fault Trap** to the OS. #### Steps in Handling a Page Fault (KTU 14-Mark Favourite): 1. The hardware traps to the kernel, saving the program counter (PC) and registers. 2. The OS checks if the reference is **legal** (valid memory access). If illegal → kill the process. 3. If legal but the page is **not in RAM**, the OS finds a free frame in physical memory. 4. If no free frame exists, the OS invokes a **Page Replacement Algorithm** to evict a victim page. 5. The OS issues **disk I/O** to read the required page from swap space into the chosen frame. 6. While I/O is in progress, the OS may **schedule another process** (CPU is not idle). 7. When I/O completes, the OS updates the **page table** (sets the frame number and valid bit = 1). 8. The process is restarted at the **saved PC** — the instruction is re-executed. ### 2.3 Effective Access Time (EAT) — KTU Numerical Favourite > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Effective Access Time (EAT)** is the expected (average) memory access time, accounting for the possibility of a page fault. Let: - $p$ = **Page Fault Rate** (probability of a page fault, $0 \leq p \leq 1$). - $ma$ = **Memory Access Time** (typically **100 ns** for a hit). - $t_{pf}$ = **Page Fault Service Time** (typically **25 ms** or **25,000,000 ns**). $$ EAT = (1 - p) \cdot ma \;+\; p \cdot t_{pf} $$ If we include the **restart overhead** (re-executing the instruction adds one extra memory reference): $$ EAT = (1 - p) \cdot ma \;+\; p \cdot (t_{pf} \;+\; ma) $$ **Rule of Thumb for KTU**: To keep performance degradation under **10%**, $p$ must be extremely small (less than $1/250,000$). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | Effective Access Time (basic) | $EAT = (1-p) \cdot ma + p \cdot t_{pf}$ | nanoseconds (ns) | | EAT (with restart) | $EAT = (1-p) \cdot ma + p \cdot (t_{pf} + ma)$ | nanoseconds (ns) | | Page Fault Rate | $p = \dfrac{\text{Number of Page Faults}}{\text{Total Memory References}}$ | dimensionless | | Page Size | Typically **4 KB** = $2^{12}$ bytes | bytes | | Number of Pages | $n_{pages} = \lceil \dfrac{\text{Process Size}}{\text{Page Size}} \rceil$ | dimensionless | | Number of Frames | $n_{frames} = \dfrac{\text{Physical RAM Size}}{\text{Frame Size}}$ | dimensionless | | Address Structure (32-bit) | Logical Address = Page Number ($p$) $\vert$ Page Offset ($d$) | bits | | Page Table Size | $PT_{size} = n_{pages} \times PTE_{size}$ | bytes | | Memory Access with TLB | $EAT = (1-h) \cdot (ma + t_{TLB}) + h \cdot t_{TLB} + ma$ | ns (h = TLB hit ratio) | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cloud Computing & Virtualization**: Virtual memory is the conceptual ancestor of **virtual machines** in cloud servers (AWS EC2, Azure VMs). - **Mobile OS (Android / iOS)**: Implements demand paging with aggressive swapping to zRAM or flash storage. - **Database Systems**: Oracle and PostgreSQL use huge pages to reduce TLB misses and improve virtual memory performance. - **Embedded Systems**: Limited-RAM IoT devices rely on virtual memory via flash-backed swap. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Access Time With and Without Page Faults > [!NOTE] > The following derivation establishes the **Effective Access Time (EAT)** formula, which is a **guaranteed KTU numerical question** (3 to 14 marks). #### Step 1: Define the Two Possible Outcomes For any memory reference made by the CPU, exactly one of two cases occurs: - **Case 1 (Page Hit)**: The required page is already in physical memory. Access time = $ma$ (memory access time). - **Case 2 (Page Fault)**: The required page is NOT in memory. Access time = $t_{pf}$ (page fault service time). #### Step 2: Assign Probabilities - Probability of Case 1 (page hit) = $(1 - p)$. - Probability of Case 2 (page fault) = $p$. #### Step 3: Compute the Weighted Average The expected (effective) access time is the probability-weighted sum of both cases. $$ EAT = (1 - p) \cdot ma \;+\; p \cdot t_{pf} $$ #### Step 4: Account for the Restart Overhead When a page fault occurs, the OS must restart the faulting instruction. This causes **one additional normal memory reference** ($ma$) after the page is brought into memory. $$ t_{pf,\text{total}} = t_{pf} + ma $$ #### Step 5: Substitute the Revised Fault Time $$ EAT = (1 - p) \cdot ma \;+\; p \cdot (t_{pf} + ma) $$ #### Step 6: Expand the Expression $$ EAT = ma - p \cdot ma \;+\; p \cdot t_{pf} + p \cdot ma $$ The $-p \cdot ma$ and $+p \cdot ma$ cancel: $$ EAT = ma \;+\; p \cdot t_{pf} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight**: A single page fault costs **8 orders of magnitude** more than a normal access (100 ns vs 25,000,000 ns). Even a tiny $p$ severely degrades performance. This is why the OS aggressively tries to **minimize $p$** through good page replacement algorithms. #### Step 7: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem**: Given $ma = 200$ ns, page fault service time $= 10$ ms $= 10 \times 10^6$ ns, and page fault rate $p = 0.001$ (1 in 1000). Compute EAT with restart overhead. **Solution**: $$ EAT = (1 - 0.001) \cdot 200 \;+\; 0.001 \cdot (10 \times 10^6 + 200) $$ $$ EAT = 0.999 \cdot 200 \;+\; 0.001 \cdot (10{,}000{,}200) $$ $$ EAT = 199.8 \;+\; 10{,}000.2 $$ $$ \boxed{EAT \approx 10{,}200 \; \text{ns}} $$ This represents a **51× slowdown** compared to the no-fault case — clearly demonstrating why page faults must be rare. ### 3.2 Derivation: Page Table Address Translation > [!NOTE] > The following shows how a **logical (virtual) address** is split into page number and offset, and how the page number is translated to a frame number. #### Step 1: Address Decomposition For a **32-bit logical address** with page size = $4 \text{ KB} = 2^{12}$ bytes: - **Offset (d)** = lower 12 bits. - **Page Number (p)** = upper 20 bits. $$ \text{Logical Address} = \underbrace{p}_{\text{Page Number (20 bits)}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{d}_{\text{Offset (12 bits)}} $$ #### Step 2: Index into Page Table The page number $p$ is used as an index into the page table to fetch the corresponding **Page Table Entry (PTE)**. The PTE contains the **Frame Number (f)** in physical memory. $$ f = \text{PageTable}[p] $$ #### Step 3: Construct the Physical Address The frame number $f$ is concatenated with the offset $d$ to form the physical address. $$ \text{Physical Address} = \underbrace{f}_{\text{Frame Number}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{d}_{\text{Offset (12 bits)}} $$ #### Step 4: Worked Example **Given**: - Logical address = `0x00003A2C` (hex). - Page size = 4 KB. **Step A** — Convert hex to binary (16 bits shown for offset): $$ 0\text{x}00003A2C = 0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0011\;1010\;0010\;1100 $$ **Step B** — Split into page number (20 bits) and offset (12 bits): $$ p = 0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0011 = 3 \quad (\text{decimal}) $$ $$ d = 1010\;0010\;1100 = 0\text{x}A2C \quad (\text{decimal } 2604) $$ **Step C** — Suppose Page Table entry for page 3 → Frame 7. **Step D** — Form physical address: $$ \text{Physical Address} = 7 \;\vert\; 0\text{x}A2C $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Physical Address} = 0\text{x}00007A2C} $$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Page Replacement Algorithms | Algorithm | Strategy | Belady's Anomaly? | Hardware Support | Cost | |---|---|---|---|---| | **FIFO** | Replace the oldest page in memory. | **Yes** (suffers) | Minimal (just a queue). | **Low** | | **Optimal (OPT)** | Replace the page that will not be used for the longest time in the future. | **No** (theoretical best). | Requires future knowledge. | **High (Impossible)** | | **LRU (Least Recently Used)** | Replace the page that has not been used for the longest time in the past. | **No**. | Counter per page, or stack. | **Moderate** | | **MFU (Most Frequently Used)** | Replace the page with the largest count (least likely to be needed). | No. | Counter per page. | **Moderate** | | **LFU (Least Frequently Used)** | Replace the page with the smallest count. | No. | Counter per page. | **Moderate** | | **Clock (Second Chance)** | Approximates LRU using a reference bit and circular pointer. | No. | One ref bit per page. | **Low** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall**: Students often confuse **LRU** with **Optimal**. Remember: LRU uses *past* information; Optimal uses *future* information. Optimal is a **theoretical benchmark**, not a practical algorithm. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Address Translation Flow (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR CPU[CPU generates
Logical Address] MMU1[MMU splits address into
Page Number and Offset] PT[Page Table Lookup
in Main Memory] PTE[Page Table Entry contains
Frame Number and Valid Bit] MISS{Valid Bit
equals 0?} PF[Hardware Page Fault Trap
to Operating System] REPLACE[OS invokes Page Replacement
Algorithm to select victim] DISK[Disk I/O to load
required page from swap] PHYS[Physical Address constructed
Frame Number plus Offset] RAM[Access Physical Memory
at the constructed address] CPU --> MMU1 MMU1 --> PT PT --> PTE PTE --> MISS MISS -- Yes --> PF PF --> REPLACE REPLACE --> DISK DISK --> PT MISS -- No --> PHYS PT --> PHYS PHYS --> RAM ``` ### 4.2 Page Fault Service Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Instruction references
virtual page P] --> B{Is page P
in memory?} B -- Yes Hit --> C[MMU translates address
normal memory access] B -- No Fault --> D[Hardware raises
Page Fault Trap to OS] D --> E[OS saves process state
program counter and registers] E --> F{Is the reference
legally valid?} F -- No --> G[Kill process
segmentation fault] F -- Yes --> H{Free frame
available in RAM?} H -- Yes --> I[Select the free frame
for the new page] H -- No --> J[Run Page Replacement
Algorithm to choose victim] J --> K[Write victim page to disk
if dirty, update its PTE] I --> L[Issue Disk I/O
to read page P into frame] K --> L L --> M[Update Page Table entry
for page P, set valid bit to 1] M --> N[OS schedules another process
CPU remains utilized] N --> O[Restart the faulting process
at saved program counter] O --> A C --> P[Continue normal execution] ``` ### 4.3 Working Set Model Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Time[Time Axis] T0[Time t0] --> T1[Time t1] --> T2[Time t2] --> T3[Time t3] --> T4[Time t4] end subgraph WS[Working Set at time t2] P1[Page 1] P3[Page 3] P5[Page 5] P7[Page 7] end subgraph Delta[Delta Window size equals W] WIN[Recent Delta references
defines the working set] end T2 --> WS WS --> RAM[These pages must reside
in physical memory] RAM --> THRASH{Working Set exceeds
available frames} THRASH -- Yes --> SWAP[Thrashing occurs
excessive paging] THRASH -- No --> EFF[System runs efficiently] ``` ### 4.4 Page Replacement Strategies (Comparative Block) ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Page Fault Occurs
No free frame available] --> ALG{Which Replacement
Algorithm?} ALG -- FIFO --> F1[Oldest page in memory
is evicted first] ALG -- OPTIMAL --> O1[Page not used for longest
future time is evicted] ALG -- LRU --> L1[Page unused for longest
past time is evicted] ALG -- CLOCK --> C1[Circular scan with
reference bit checking] F1 --> RESULT[Update Page Table
Load new page] O1 --> RESULT L1 --> RESULT C1 --> RESULT RESULT --> CONTINUE[Process Execution
Resumes] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** > > **Q1.** What is **virtual memory**? How does it differ from physical memory? > > **Model Answer**: > Virtual memory is a memory management technique that provides processes with the illusion of a large, contiguous logical address space, even when the actual physical RAM is smaller. It uses a combination of hardware (MMU) and OS software to map virtual addresses to physical addresses, and spills unused portions of a process onto secondary storage (disk/swap space). > > **Key differences from physical memory**: > - Virtual memory is **logical** (what the process sees); physical memory is **actual RAM hardware**. > - Virtual memory size is **limited by address width** (e.g., 64-bit = 2⁶⁴ bytes); physical memory is **limited by installed RAM chips** (e.g., 8 GB, 16 GB). > - Virtual memory uses **demand paging**; physical memory is **directly accessed**. > - Virtual memory enables **process isolation and protection**; physical memory is shared. > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]** > > **Q2.** Define **page fault**. Explain the role of the **valid–invalid bit** in demand paging. > > **Model Answer**: > A **page fault** is a hardware trap (exception) raised by the memory management unit (MMU) when a process attempts to access a page that is not currently present in physical memory (RAM). > > The **valid–invalid bit** (also called the **present bit**) is a single bit stored in each **Page Table Entry (PTE)**: > - **1 (valid)**: The page is currently in physical memory; access proceeds normally. > - **0 (invalid)**: The page is either not in memory (causing a page fault) or is not a legal address for the process. > > On every memory reference, the MMU checks the valid–invalid bit. If it is 0, the MMU generates a page fault trap to the OS, which then services the fault by loading the required page from disk. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- > ### **Question A** (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Regular]** > > **(a)** Explain the **concept of virtual memory** with a neat diagram. Discuss its **advantages and disadvantages**. > **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Consider the following page reference string: > `7, 0, 1, 2, 0, 3, 0, 4, 2, 3, 0, 3, 2, 1, 2, 0, 1, 7, 0, 1` > > Assuming **3 page frames**, calculate the number of page faults using: > (i) **FIFO** page replacement algorithm > (ii) **LRU** page replacement algorithm > Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Q-A(a): **Concept**: Virtual memory is a memory management scheme that allows execution of processes that may not be completely in main memory. It separates the **logical memory** view of the user from the **physical memory**, using **demand paging** and **page replacement**. **Diagram Description** (refer to Section 4.1): CPU → MMU → Page Table → Frame in RAM, with disk swap for invalid entries. **Advantages**: 1. Allows programs **larger than physical RAM** to execute. 2. **More processes** can be kept in memory simultaneously → higher CPU utilization → better **multiprogramming**. 3. Reduces **I/O** needed for loading entire programs (only used pages are loaded). 4. Provides **process isolation and memory protection**. 5. Allows **shared memory** between processes (e.g., shared libraries, re-entrant code). **Disadvantages**: 1. **Thrashing**: Excessive paging activity degrades system performance. 2. **Page table overhead**: Large page tables consume significant memory. 3. **Address translation overhead**: Requires MMU and TLB hardware for fast translation. 4. **Complexity**: Implementation requires sophisticated OS algorithms. 5. **Disk wear**: Heavy swapping increases SSD/HDD wear (especially in mobile/embedded systems). #### Model Solution to Q-A(b): **Reference String**: `7, 0, 1, 2, 0, 3, 0, 4, 2, 3, 0, 3, 2, 1, 2, 0, 1, 7, 0, 1` (20 references) **Frames Available**: 3 **(i) FIFO Page Replacement**: The FIFO algorithm evicts the page that has been in memory the **longest** (oldest arrival). | Step | Reference | Frame 1 | Frame 2 | Frame 3 | Page Fault? | |------|-----------|---------|---------|---------|-------------| | 1 | 7 | 7 | — | — | **Yes** | | 2 | 0 | 7 | 0 | — | **Yes** | | 3 | 1 | 7 | 0 | 1 | **Yes** | | 4 | 2 | 2 | 0 | 1 | **Yes** (evict 7, oldest) | | 5 | 0 | 2 | 0 | 1 | No (hit) | | 6 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 1 | **Yes** (evict 0, oldest) | | 7 | 0 | 2 | 3 | 0 | **Yes** (evict 1, oldest) | | 8 | 4 | 4 | 3 | 0 | **Yes** (evict 2, oldest) | | 9 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 0 | **Yes** (evict 3, oldest) | | 10 | 3 | 4 | 2 | 3 | **Yes** (evict 0, oldest) | | 11 | 0 | 0 | 2 | 3 | **Yes** (evict 4, oldest) | | 12 | 3 | 0 | 2 | 3 | No (hit) | | 13 | 2 | 0 | 2 | 3 | No (hit) | | 14 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 3 | **Yes** (evict 2, oldest) | | 15 | 2 | 0 | 1 | 2 | **Yes** (evict 3, oldest) | | 16 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 2 | No (hit) | | 17 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 2 | No (hit) | | 18 | 7 | 7 | 1 | 2 | **Yes** (evict 0, oldest) | | 19 | 0 | 7 | 0 | 2 | **Yes** (evict 1, oldest) | | 20 | 1 | 7 | 0 | 1 | **Yes** (evict 2, oldest) | **Total Page Faults (FIFO) = 15** **(ii) LRU Page Replacement**: The LRU algorithm evicts the page that has **not been used for the longest time** in the past. | Step | Reference | Frame 1 | Frame 2 | Frame 3 | LRU Eviction | Page Fault? | |------|-----------|---------|---------|---------|--------------|-------------| | 1 | 7 | 7 | — | — | — | **Yes** | | 2 | 0 | 7 | 0 | — | — | **Yes** | | 3 | 1 | 7 | 0 | 1 | — | **Yes** | | 4 | 2 | 2 | 0 | 1 | 7 (LRU) | **Yes** | | 5 | 0 | 2 | 0 | 1 | — | No (hit) | | 6 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 1 | 0 (LRU) | **Yes** | | 7 | 0 | 2 | 3 | 0 | 1 (LRU) | **Yes** | | 8 | 4 | 4 | 3 | 0 | 2 (LRU) | **Yes** | | 9 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 0 | 3 (LRU) | **Yes** | | 10 | 3 | 4 | 2 | 3 | 0 (LRU) | **Yes** | | 11 | 0 | 4 | 2 | 0 | 3 (LRU) | **Yes** | | 12 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 0 | 4 (LRU) | **Yes** | | 13 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 0 | — | No (hit) | | 14 | 1 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 (LRU) | **Yes** | | 15 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 1 | — | No (hit) | | 16 | 0 | 3 | 2 | 0 | 1 (LRU) | **Yes** | | 17 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 0 | 3 (LRU) | **Yes** | | 18 | 7 | 1 | 2 | 7 | 0 (LRU) | **Yes** | | 19 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 7 | 2 (LRU) | **Yes** | | 20 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 7 | — | No (hit) | **Total Page Faults (LRU) = 16** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Page Replacement Numerical**: > 1. **Do NOT count the first loading of unique pages differently** from replacement faults. All first-time loads are also page faults. > 2. **Mark the eviction column clearly** — examiners look for justification of *which* page was evicted and *why*. > 3. For **LRU**, update the "recently used" timestamp on **every hit**, not just on faults. This is the most common error. > 4. For **Optimal**, you must look *ahead* in the reference string — students often confuse this with LRU. > 5. **Belady's Anomaly**: FIFO can perform *worse* with *more* frames. Mentioning this in viva earns bonus marks. --- > ### **Question B** (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]** > > **(a)** Explain the **demand paging** mechanism in detail. Describe the steps involved in handling a **page fault** with a flowchart. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Discuss the concept of **Thrashing**. Explain the **Working Set Model** to handle thrashing. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Q-B(a): **Demand Paging Concept**: Demand paging is a virtual memory implementation strategy where pages are loaded into physical memory **only when they are actually referenced (demanded)** by the process, not in advance. The program is started with no pages loaded, and pages are fetched lazily as needed. **Page Table Support**: Each PTE contains a **valid–invalid (present) bit**: - **1** = Page is in physical memory. - **0** = Page is not in memory (will cause page fault on access). **Page Fault Handling Steps** (refer to Section 4.2 for the flowchart): 1. The process references a page whose valid bit = 0. 2. The **MMU traps** to the OS, generating a **page fault exception**. 3. The OS **saves the process state** (registers, program counter) on the kernel stack. 4. The OS **verifies the reference is legal** (no segmentation violation). If illegal → terminates the process. 5. The OS locates the referenced page on the **swap device (backing store)**. 6. The OS searches for a **free frame** in physical memory: - If free frame exists → use it. - If not → invoke a **page replacement algorithm** to select a victim page. 7. The OS **schedules disk I/O** to read the required page into the chosen frame. The CPU may switch to another runnable process during this time. 8. Once I/O completes, the OS **updates the page table** (sets frame number, valid bit = 1). 9. The OS **restores the saved process state** and restarts execution at the saved PC. The faulting instruction is re-executed, this time succeeding. **Advantages of Demand Paging**: - Less I/O is needed. - Less memory is required. - Faster response time. - Allows more users/processes to be supported. **Disadvantages**: - Page fault overhead can severely impact performance. - The first few instructions of any program always cause page faults (no pages preloaded). #### Model Solution to Q-B(b): **Thrashing — Definition**: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Thrashing** occurs when a system spends **more time swapping pages** in and out of memory than **executing useful instructions**. The CPU utilization drops dramatically because the OS is constantly servicing page faults. **Why Thrashing Happens**: - A process is given **fewer frames** than it needs (less than its working set size). - The process constantly pages-faults, evicting pages it will soon need → re-faults. - The OS incorrectly assumes it needs more multiprogramming → admits more processes → worsens the problem. - This creates a **deadlock-like loop** where CPU utilization → 0. **Graphical Behavior**: - As the degree of multiprogramming increases initially, CPU utilization rises. - Beyond a point, adding more processes causes **CPU utilization to plummet** because there are not enough frames to go around. **Working Set Model — Solution to Thrashing**: > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Working Set Model** (Denning, 1968) states that a process executes **locally** — at any time, it accesses only a small subset of its total pages. This subset is called the **Working Set**. **Definitions**: - **Working Set (WSSᵢ)**: The set of pages referenced by process $P_i$ in the most recent $\Delta$ (delta) memory references. - **$\Delta$ (Window Size)**: A parameter controlling how far back we look in the reference history. - **Total Frames Required**: $$ D = \sum_{i=1}^{n} WSS_i $$ where $n$ is the number of processes. If $D > m$ (total physical frames), thrashing occurs. **Strategy**: 1. Track the working set of each process. 2. If a process is thrashing (working set ∉ memory), **suspend the process** until enough frames are free. 3. If total demand $D > m$, **reduce the degree of multiprogramming** (suspend or swap out a process). 4. If $D < m$, the OS can **admit more processes**. **Alternative: Page Fault Frequency (PFF) Strategy**: - Monitor the page fault rate of each process. - If the rate exceeds an upper threshold, **allocate more frames** to the process. - If the rate falls below a lower threshold, **take frames away** (process has excess memory). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Thrashing Question**: > 1. **Do NOT** confuse thrashing with **swapping**. Swapping is a controlled OS operation to free memory; thrashing is an **uncontrolled, pathological condition**. > 2. The **Working Set** changes dynamically as the process executes — examiners expect you to mention that WSS *evolves over time*. > 3. Drawing the **CPU utilization vs. degree of multiprogramming** graph is worth 2 marks by itself — do not skip it. > 4. Always state the **inequality condition** $D > m$ that causes thrashing. > 5. For the PFF strategy, mention **both thresholds** (upper and lower) — students often miss the lower one. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Virtual Memory** **Core Concepts**: - Virtual memory = illusion of large memory via paging + disk. - Demand paging = lazy loading of pages on first reference. - Page fault = hardware trap when a referenced page is absent. - Page replacement = swapping out a victim page to make room. - Thrashing = pathological condition of excessive paging. - Working Set = pages actively used by a process in a recent window. **Key Formulas to Memorize**: - $EAT = (1-p) \cdot ma + p \cdot t_{pf}$ (basic). - $EAT = ma + p \cdot t_{pf}$ (with restart overhead, after simplification). - Page Number bits = $\log_2(\text{Number of virtual pages})$. - Offset bits = $\log_2(\text{Page size in bytes})$. - $D = \sum WSS_i$ must be $\leq m$ (total frames) to avoid thrashing. **Algorithm Comparison Points**: - FIFO → simple but suffers from **Belady's Anomaly**. - Optimal → theoretical best, not implementable. - LRU → practical, no Belady's Anomaly, requires hardware support. - Clock / Second Chance → efficient LRU approximation. **Must-Draw Diagrams for Exams**: - Address translation flow (CPU → MMU → Page Table → RAM). - Page fault service sequence flowchart. - Working set model time-window diagram. - CPU utilization vs. multiprogramming degree (with thrashing region). **Examiner Pet Peeves to Avoid**: - Confusing virtual address with physical address. - Skipping the **valid–invalid bit check** in page fault flow. - Writing "we evict a page" without specifying **which algorithm** is used. - Missing the $D > m$ condition in thrashing explanation. - Forgetting the **effect of restart overhead** in EAT calculations. **Common Viva Questions**: - *Q: Why is Optimal not used in real systems?* — Requires future knowledge of references. - *Q: How does TLB improve virtual memory performance?* — Caches recent translations, avoids page table lookups. - *Q: What is the difference between internal and external fragmentation in paging?* — Internal = wasted space in last page; External = eliminated by paging. - *Q: Can a system have 100% CPU utilization with virtual memory?* — Not if thrashing occurs; CPU utilization drops sharply during thrashing. **One-Liner Definitions for Quick Recall**: - *Demand Paging*: "Pages in, just in time." - *Thrashing*: "Paging in circles." - *Working Set*: "The pages you are using right now." - *Page Fault*: "Memory says: I don't have this page."

topics.title: Demand Paging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Demand Paging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4: DEMAND PAGING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Demand Paging** is a memory management scheme in which pages of a process are loaded into the main memory **only when they are demanded (referenced) during execution**, rather than bringing the entire process into memory at once. A page is brought into RAM *on-demand* when a reference to that page causes a **page fault**.

The mechanism relies on the **valid/invalid bit** (also called the *present-absent bit*) associated with each entry in the **page table**:
- **Valid (`v`):** The page is present in a valid frame of physical memory.
- **Invalid (`i`):** The page is either not in memory or is in a logically invalid state (e.g., outside the process's virtual address space).

When the CPU generates a logical address, the system first checks this bit. If it is `i`, a **page fault trap** is raised, transferring control to the OS, which fetches the page from secondary storage (swap device) into a free frame.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bookshelf Analogy"

> [!TIP]
> Imagine you are a student writing a thesis. You do **not** carry the entire library of 10,000 books to your desk. Instead, you keep an **index card** for every book. When you need a chapter, you check the index card:
> - ✅ **Card says "On Desk"** → You read instantly.
> - ❌ **Card says "In Library"** → You walk to the library, fetch *only that specific book* (or just the relevant chapter), and place it on your desk.
>
> This is exactly how Demand Paging works. The **desk = RAM**, the **library = Disk (Swap device)**, the **index cards = Page Table**, and the act of fetching a book = **Page-In operation**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Glossary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Page Fault** | A hardware trap raised when a referenced page is not in memory (`i` bit set). |
| **Page Fault Rate (`p`)** | The probability, in `0 ≤ p ≤ 1`, that a memory reference causes a page fault. |
| **Pure Demand Paging** | A variant in which a process starts with *zero pages* in memory; the first instruction itself faults. |
| **Locality of Reference** | The empirical property that processes tend to cluster their memory references in time and space, making demand paging viable. |
| **Swap Space (Swap Device)** | A pre-reserved area of secondary storage used to hold pages that have been evicted from RAM. |
| **Effective Access Time (EAT)** | The *actual* average time to access a memory location, factoring in page fault service cost. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Demand Paging is the foundational concept before studying *Page Replacement Algorithms* and *Thrashing* (Module 4). The **valid/invalid bit semantics** and the **step-by-step page fault service sequence** are the most frequently tested items.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — "How a Page Fault is Served"

When a process references a page that is **not** in memory, the OS executes a precise, ordered sequence known as the **Page Fault Service Routine**. The KTU board examiners test the *exact ordering* of these steps:

1. **Trap to the OS** — The hardware raises a page-fault exception, saving the faulting instruction's Program Counter and the process state (registers, PSW).
2. **OS checks the reference** — The OS verifies that the memory reference is *legal* (process-owned) and not an *invalid* memory access (protection violation). If illegal → **segmentation fault / process termination**.
3. **Locate a free frame** — The OS scans its **free-frame list**. If one exists, use it; if not, invoke a **page-replacement algorithm** to evict a victim page.
4. **Schedule a disk I/O** — Issue a request to the disk controller to read the desired page from its **swap location** into the newly chosen frame.
5. **While I/O is in progress** — The CPU is free; the OS may **dispatch another runnable process** (this is called *overlap of I/O and CPU* and is critical for performance).
6. **Disk interrupt** — When I/O completes, the disk controller raises an interrupt; the OS updates the **page table** of the process (sets the `v` bit and records the frame number).
7. **Restart the faulting instruction** — Restore the saved process state and re-execute the instruction that caused the fault. The second attempt will find the page present (`v`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is "Restart" mandatory?** The faulting instruction was *interrupted mid-execution*. Its operand access was incomplete. Re-executing it from scratch produces the correct effect.

## 2.2 Hardware Support Required

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Page Table (with valid/invalid bit)** | Tells the MMU whether a page is resident. |
| **Secondary Storage (Swap space)** | Backing store for evicted pages. |
| **Disk Controller / DMA** | Performs asynchronous page-in/page-out. |
| **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** | Speeds up valid page translations; TLB-miss + page-fault is a *double fault* scenario. |
| **Free-Frame List / Bitmap** | Tracks which physical frames are unoccupied. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantitative problems in this module reduce to the **Effective Access Time (EAT)** equation below. Memorize it, then specialize it for each variant.

### 2.3.1 General EAT Equation (Single-Level Memory)

$$
\text{EAT} \;=\; (1 - p) \cdot \text{ma} \;+\; p \cdot \text{page fault service time}
$$

Where:
- $p$ = page fault rate, $0 \le p \le 1$
- $\text{ma}$ = normal memory access time (no fault)
- page fault service time includes all overheads (trap, I/O, restart)

### 2.3.2 Expanded Form (Detailed)

$$
\text{EAT} \;=\; (1 - p) \cdot \text{ma} \;+\; p \cdot \Big( T_{\text{trap}} + T_{\text{swap-out}} + T_{\text{swap-in}} + T_{\text{restart}} \Big)
$$

### 2.3.3 Special Case — No Page Replacement (Free Frame Available)

$$
\text{EAT} \;=\; (1 - p) \cdot \text{ma} \;+\; p \cdot \text{page fault service time}
$$

### 2.3.4 Worst-Case Lower Bound on Performance

> [!TIP]
> To keep slowdown $\le 10\%$, we need: $p \cdot \text{page fault service} \le 0.1 \cdot \text{ma}$
>
> Substituting typical values ($\text{ma} = 100$ ns, page-fault service $= 25$ ms $= 25{,}000{,}000$ ns):
>
> $p \le 0.1 \cdot 100 \, / \, 25{,}000{,}000 \;=\; 4 \times 10^{-7}$

### 2.3.5 Summary Table of Symbols

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{ma}$ | Memory access time | **100 ns** |
| $T_{\text{trap}}$ | Trap to OS handler | **1 – 100 µs** |
| $T_{\text{read}}$ | Disk read of page | **1 – 25 ms** |
| $T_{\text{restart}}$ | Resume faulting instruction | **1 – 100 µs** |
| $p$ | Page fault rate | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-8}$ |
| $\text{EAT}$ | Effective access time | µs–ms range |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Virtual Memory Systems:** Every modern OS (Windows, Linux, macOS, Android) uses demand paging to give each process an illusion of a vast, contiguous address space.
- **Memory-Constrained Devices:** Smartphones use demand paging to run more apps than the physical RAM would otherwise allow.
- **Cloud & Server Workloads:** The "swap behavior" of Linux KVM, VMware ESXi, and Docker containers is fundamentally demand-paging of anonymous memory.
- **Databases:** Oracle's SGA, MySQL's InnoDB buffer pool, and PostgreSQL's shared buffers exploit demand-paging-like mechanisms to keep hot pages resident.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does demand paging actually work in practice?** The answer lies in the **Principle of Locality** — *temporal locality* (recently accessed items are likely to be accessed again) and *spatial locality* (addresses near a referenced one are likely to be referenced soon). Without locality, demand paging would be catastrophically slow.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Access Time (EAT) for the Base Case

**Problem Setup (Standard KTU Variant):**
- Memory access time, $\text{ma} = 200$ ns
- Page fault service time $= 8$ ms $= 8 \times 10^{6}$ ns
- Page fault rate $= p = 0.001$ (i.e., 1 in 1000 references faults)

**Derivation:**

$$
\text{EAT} \;=\; (1 - p) \cdot \text{ma} \;+\; p \cdot \text{page fault service time}
$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$
\text{EAT} \;=\; (1 - 0.001) \cdot 200 \;+\; 0.001 \cdot 8 \times 10^{6}
$$

Compute each term separately:

$$
(1 - 0.001) \cdot 200 \;=\; 0.999 \cdot 200 \;=\; 199.8 \;\text{ns}
$$

$$
0.001 \cdot 8 \times 10^{6} \;=\; 8{,}000 \;\text{ns}
$$

Add the two terms:

$$
\text{EAT} \;=\; 199.8 + 8{,}000 \;=\; 8{,}199.8 \;\text{ns}
$$

Convert to a more readable unit:

$$
8{,}199.8 \;\text{ns} \;\approx\; 8.2 \;\mu\text{s}
$$

**Interpretation:** A mere **0.1 %** page-fault rate inflates access time from 200 ns to ≈ 8.2 µs — a **41× slowdown**. The system is *extremely* sensitive to the fault rate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — EAT with the "No Page Replacement" Assumption

**Problem Setup (Modified KTU Variant):**
- $\text{ma} = 100$ ns
- Page fault overhead = **no eviction needed** (a free frame always exists)
- Page fault service time = 1 ms $= 10^6$ ns
- $p = 0.0005$

**Derivation:**

$$
\text{EAT} \;=\; (1 - p) \cdot \text{ma} \;+\; p \cdot T_{\text{page fault}}
$$

Substitute:

$$
\text{EAT} \;=\; (1 - 0.0005) \cdot 100 \;+\; 0.0005 \cdot 10^{6}
$$

$$
(1 - 0.0005) \cdot 100 \;=\; 0.9995 \cdot 100 \;=\; 99.95 \;\text{ns}
$$

$$
0.0005 \cdot 10^{6} \;=\; 500 \;\text{ns}
$$

$$
\text{EAT} \;=\; 99.95 + 500 \;=\; 599.95 \;\text{ns}
$$

**Ratio vs. no-fault:**

$$
\frac{\text{EAT}}{\text{ma}} \;=\; \frac{599.95}{100} \;\approx\; 6\times
$$

Even with negligible fault rate, a single page fault is **5–6 orders of magnitude** more expensive than a normal access.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Deriving the Maximum Acceptable Page-Fault Rate

**Problem Setup:**
- $\text{ma} = 200$ ns
- Page-fault service = 8 ms
- We want the system to suffer **no more than 10 % slowdown** (i.e., EAT $\le 1.1 \times \text{ma}$).

**Derivation (start from the EAT equation):**

$$
\text{EAT} \;\le\; 1.1 \cdot \text{ma}
$$

$$
(1 - p) \cdot \text{ma} + p \cdot T_{\text{pf}} \;\le\; 1.1 \cdot \text{ma}
$$

Subtract $(1 - p) \cdot \text{ma}$ from both sides:

$$
p \cdot T_{\text{pf}} \;\le\; 1.1 \cdot \text{ma} - (1 - p) \cdot \text{ma}
$$

Expand the right-hand side:

$$
1.1 \cdot \text{ma} - (1 - p) \cdot \text{ma} \;=\; 1.1 \cdot \text{ma} - \text{ma} + p \cdot \text{ma} \;=\; 0.1 \cdot \text{ma} + p \cdot \text{ma}
$$

So:

$$
p \cdot T_{\text{pf}} \;\le\; 0.1 \cdot \text{ma} + p \cdot \text{ma}
$$

Move the $p \cdot \text{ma}$ term to the left:

$$
p \cdot T_{\text{pf}} - p \cdot \text{ma} \;\le\; 0.1 \cdot \text{ma}
$$

Factor out $p$:

$$
p \cdot (T_{\text{pf}} - \text{ma}) \;\le\; 0.1 \cdot \text{ma}
$$

Solve for $p$:

$$
p \;\le\; \frac{0.1 \cdot \text{ma}}{T_{\text{pf}} - \text{ma}}
$$

Plug in numbers ($T_{\text{pf}} \gg \text{ma}$, so subtract $200$ is negligible):

$$
p \;\le\; \frac{0.1 \cdot 200}{8 \times 10^{6} - 200} \;\approx\; \frac{20}{7{,}999{,}800}
$$

$$
p \;\le\; 2.5 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Interpretation:** To keep performance loss below 10 %, the page-fault rate must be **below roughly 1 fault per 400,000 references**. This justifies the engineering emphasis on *good page-replacement policies* studied next in the syllabus.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Demand Paging Variants

| Variant | Page Replacement on Fault? | Typical EAT Expression | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure Demand Paging** | Yes (if no free frame) | $(1 - p)\text{ma} + p \cdot (T_{\text{trap}} + T_{\text{evict}} + T_{\text{read}} + T_{\text{restart}})$ | Cold-start of a process |
| **Demand Paging with Free Frame Available** | No | $(1 - p)\text{ma} + p \cdot (T_{\text{trap}} + T_{\text{read}} + T_{\text{restart}})$ | Steady-state execution |
| **Prepaging (Anticipatory)** | No | Lower fault rate ⇒ smaller $p$ ⇒ lower EAT | Process startup, large file reads |
| **Anticipatory Paging** | Yes | Depends on prediction accuracy | Speculative optimization |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Demand Paging — End-to-End Logical Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process generates logical address] --> B[MMU checks TLB]
    B --> C{TLB Hit?}
    C -- Yes --> Z1[Physical address formed - access memory]
    C -- No --> D[MMU consults Page Table]
    D --> E{Valid bit is V?}
    E -- Yes V --> Z1
    E -- No I --> F[Trap to OS - Page Fault]
    F --> G{Is the reference legal?}
    G -- No --> H[Segmentation Fault - Terminate process]
    G -- Yes --> I[Locate or free a physical frame]
    I --> J[Schedule disk I-O to read page from swap]
    J --> K[Disk I-O in progress - dispatch another process]
    K --> L[Disk interrupt - page now in RAM]
    L --> M[Update Page Table - set V bit and frame number]
    M --> N[Restart the faulting instruction]
    N --> A
    Z1 --> P[Continue normal execution]
```

## 4.2 Page Table with Valid/Invalid Bits — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PT[Page Table of a Process]
        PTE0[PTE 0: Frame 3 - Valid V]:::valid
        PTE1[PTE 1: Invalid I]:::invalid
        PTE2[PTE 2: Frame 7 - Valid V]:::valid
        PTE3[PTE 3: Invalid I]:::invalid
        PTE4[PTE 4: Frame 1 - Valid V]:::valid
    end
    CPU[CPU Logical Address] --> MMU[MMU - checks bit]
    MMU -->|Valid V| RAM[(Physical RAM)]
    MMU -->|Invalid I| OS[OS Page Fault Handler]
    OS --> DISK[(Secondary Storage - Swap)]
    DISK --> RAM
    classDef valid fill:#d4f8d4,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef invalid fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c62828,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How Demand Paging Interacts with the System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    USER[User Process] --> GEN[Generates Memory Reference]
    GEN --> HW[Hardware MMU and TLB]
    HW -->|Page present| EXEC[Normal Instruction Execution]
    HW -->|Page absent| TRAP[Page Fault Trap]
    TRAP --> KERN[OS Kernel Page Fault Handler]
    KERN --> VALIDCHK{Reference Legal?}
    VALIDCHK -- No --> ABORT[Send SIGSEGV to process]
    VALIDCHK -- Yes --> FRAME[Pick or Evict Frame]
    FRAME --> DMAREQ[DMA Disk Read Request Issued]
    DMAREQ --> SCHED[CPU Schedules Another Process]
    DMAREQ --> SWAPIN[(Swap Device Read)]
    SWAPIN --> INT[Disk Interrupt - I-O Complete]
    INT --> UPD[Update Page Table Entry]
    UPD --> RESTART[Restart Faulting Instruction]
    RESTART --> EXEC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Read-Out:** Note the **two exit arrows from the Hardware MMU box** — a *valid* path leads to the RAM and the *invalid* path branches into the entire OS page-fault handling pipeline. The branching inside the OS is itself a multi-step state machine (legal-check → frame-pick → I/O → restart). This block diagram is the *expected answer sketch* KTU examiners award marks for in 7-mark descriptive questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is demand paging? Explain the role of the valid-invalid bit.**

**Model Answer:**

Demand paging is a memory-management technique in which pages of a process are loaded into main memory **only on demand** — i.e., when a page is actually referenced — rather than at process load time. The page table maintains a **valid/invalid bit** (`v`/`i`) for every page:
- **`v` (valid):** the page is in a physical frame and is accessible.
- **`i` (invalid):** the page is not in memory (or is not legally part of the process).

When the MMU translates a logical address and finds the bit set to `i`, a **page-fault trap** is raised, the OS brings the required page from the swap device into a free frame, updates the bit to `v`, and **restarts the faulting instruction**. This bit is therefore the *core mechanism* that enables demand paging.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between *pure demand paging* and *pre-paging*.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Pure Demand Paging | Pre-paging |
|---|---|---|
| **When pages are loaded** | Only when referenced (on first fault) | Anticipated pages are loaded *in advance* at process start |
| **Initial page-fault count** | Very high (almost every first reference faults) | Lower, because more pages are already resident |
| **Risk** | Wastage of I/O if pre-fetched pages are never used | High overhead at startup |
| **Cost model** | Reacts to *current* demand | Predicts *future* demand using locality heuristics |
| **Used in practice** | Modern OSes (Linux default) | Some DB and real-time systems |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert ms → ns (or µs)** in EAT problems is the **single most common mark-loser** (2–3 marks off). Always show unit conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Omitting the restart step** in the page-fault sequence loses the *process-state-and-restart* sub-bullet (≈ 2 marks). Examiners treat "trap → load → resume" as an *incomplete* answer.
> 3. **Writing the EAT formula with $+$ or $-$ interchanged** for the fault term costs full marks for the numerical part.
> 4. **Forgetting the "free-frame" assumption** when the question says "no replacement" loses 1 mark.

---

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` (Mapped to **CO4, Bloom L4 — Analyze**)

#### (a) Explain in detail the steps involved in handling a page fault in a demand-paged system. State any hardware and software support required. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Hardware raises a page-fault trap** the moment the MMU finds the `i` bit set in the page table entry. The CPU saves the **Program Counter** of the faulting instruction and the **process state** in a kernel stack.
2. **OS gains control.** A general protection handler distinguishes a *page fault* from a *protection violation*. (This is the first software step.)
3. **The OS verifies that the virtual address is legal** — i.e., the page number lies within the process's allocated virtual address space. If not, the process is terminated (segmentation fault).
4. **The OS locates a free frame** by inspecting the free-frame list (often a bit-map or linked list of free frames). If the list is empty, a **page-replacement algorithm** selects a victim page and evicts it.
5. **A disk I/O request is issued** to the swap device. The disk controller reads the desired page into the chosen frame using **DMA**. The OS may issue this asynchronously and **schedule another process** while the I/O is in flight.
6. **On disk interrupt completion**, the OS updates the page-table entry: sets the `v` bit, records the frame number, and updates any other metadata (e.g., reference/dirty bits).
7. **The faulting instruction is restarted.** The CPU restores the saved PC and process state and re-executes the instruction. This time the `v` bit is set, so the access succeeds.

**Hardware Support Required:**
- **MMU** with valid/invalid bit in each PTE
- **TLB** for fast valid translations
- **Page table** in main memory
- **Swap device / disk controller** with DMA
- **Disk interrupt** mechanism

**Software Support Required:**
- **Page-fault service routine** in the OS kernel
- **Free-frame allocator**
- **Page-replacement module** (for the no-free-frame case)
- **Process scheduler** to overlap I/O with useful CPU work

> *Self-Assessment Mapping:*
> Steps 1–3 ⇒ 3 marks · Steps 4–6 ⇒ 2 marks · Step 7 ⇒ 1 mark · Hardware/software list ⇒ 1 mark.

---

#### (b) A system uses demand paging. Memory access time is **200 ns**. Average page-fault service time is **8 ms**. The page-fault rate is **0.0001**. Compute the **Effective Access Time (EAT)**. What is the maximum page-fault rate the system can tolerate if **EAT must not exceed 1.5 × memory access time**? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert units explicitly:**
Page-fault service $= 8$ ms $= 8 \times 10^{6}$ ns.

**Step 2 — Apply the EAT formula:**

$$
\text{EAT} \;=\; (1 - p) \cdot \text{ma} \;+\; p \cdot T_{\text{pf}}
$$

Substitute $p = 0.0001$, $\text{ma} = 200$ ns, $T_{\text{pf}} = 8 \times 10^{6}$ ns:

$$
(1 - 0.0001) \cdot 200 \;=\; 0.9999 \cdot 200 \;=\; 199.98 \;\text{ns}
$$

$$
0.0001 \cdot 8 \times 10^{6} \;=\; 800 \;\text{ns}
$$

$$
\text{EAT} \;=\; 199.98 + 800 \;=\; 999.98 \;\text{ns} \;\approx\; 1.0 \;\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum tolerable fault rate:**

We require $\text{EAT} \le 1.5 \cdot \text{ma} = 1.5 \cdot 200 = 300$ ns:

$$
(1 - p) \cdot 200 + p \cdot 8 \times 10^{6} \;\le\; 300
$$

$$
200 - 200p + 8 \times 10^{6} p \;\le\; 300
$$

$$
8 \times 10^{6} p - 200p \;\le\; 100
$$

$$
p \cdot (8 \times 10^{6} - 200) \;\le\; 100
$$

$$
p \;\le\; \frac{100}{7{,}999{,}800} \;\approx\; 1.25 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Conclusion:** Even at a fault rate of just **1 in 80,000 references**, performance degrades by 50 %. The system is therefore *extremely* sensitive to the fault rate, which motivates the **page-replacement algorithms** studied in subsequent topics.

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Mapped to **CO4, Bloom L3 — Apply**)

#### (a) With a neat diagram, explain the concept of demand paging. How does it differ from simple paging? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept:** In demand paging, only those pages of a process that are **currently required** are loaded into memory. Each page-table entry carries a **valid/invalid bit**. On a `v` bit, the reference proceeds. On an `i` bit, a **page fault** occurs and the OS loads the page from the secondary storage (swap device) into a free frame.

**Diagram (Block-Level):**
```
Logical Addr → MMU → Page Table → if v=1 → Physical Memory
                                  → if v=0 → Page Fault → OS → Disk → Frame in RAM → Resume
```

**Difference Table:**

| Aspect | Simple Paging | Demand Paging |
|---|---|---|
| **Loading strategy** | *Entire* process is in memory at load time | Pages are loaded *on demand* (lazy) |
| **Valid/invalid bit** | Conceptually present, but usually always `v` | Crucial — drives the page-fault mechanism |
| **Page faults** | None during normal execution | Frequent at start, declines due to locality |
| **Swap device** | Optional / not central | Mandatory backing store |
| **Memory efficiency** | Low — wastes RAM for unused pages | High — only hot pages occupy frames |
| **Startup cost** | High (all pages loaded up front) | Low (first reference is the trigger) |

---

#### (b) Consider a system with the following parameters: **Memory access time = 100 ns, page-fault service = 1.5 ms, page-fault rate = 0.00005**. **(i)** Calculate the EAT. **(ii)** If the page-fault rate is increased to **0.0002**, what will be the new EAT? Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Computation for $p = 0.00005$:**

Convert: $T_{\text{pf}} = 1.5$ ms $= 1.5 \times 10^{6}$ ns.

$$
\text{EAT} \;=\; (1 - p) \cdot \text{ma} \;+\; p \cdot T_{\text{pf}}
$$

$$
(1 - 0.00005) \cdot 100 \;=\; 0.99995 \cdot 100 \;=\; 99.995 \;\text{ns}
$$

$$
0.00005 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{6} \;=\; 75 \;\text{ns}
$$

$$
\text{EAT} \;=\; 99.995 + 75 \;=\; 174.995 \;\text{ns} \;\approx\; 175 \;\text{ns}
$$

**(ii) Computation for $p = 0.0002$:**

$$
(1 - 0.0002) \cdot 100 \;=\; 0.9998 \cdot 100 \;=\; 99.98 \;\text{ns}
$$

$$
0.0002 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{6} \;=\; 300 \;\text{ns}
$$

$$
\text{EAT} \;=\; 99.98 + 300 \;=\; 399.98 \;\text{ns} \;\approx\; 400 \;\text{ns}
$$

**Commentary:**
- A 4× increase in page-fault rate (from $5 \times 10^{-5}$ to $2 \times 10^{-4}$) caused a **2.3× increase in EAT** (from 175 ns to 400 ns).
- This non-linear sensitivity arises because the per-fault cost (1.5 ms) is **four orders of magnitude** larger than the normal memory access (100 ns).
- The takeaway: *minimizing the page-fault rate* through effective page replacement and good locality is the single most important factor in demand-paging performance.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Demand Paging — Last-Minute Cheat Sheet**

- ✅ **Definition:** Pages are loaded **only when referenced**, not at process load.
- ✅ **Key trigger:** A page fault (raised by the **valid/invalid bit = 0**).
- ✅ **Page-Fault Service Sequence (memorize the order):** Trap → Legal-check → Free-frame / Replace → Disk I/O → Update PTE → **Restart instruction**.
- ✅ **Restart is mandatory** — the faulting instruction is re-executed, not resumed mid-way.
- ✅ **EAT Formula (universal):** $\text{EAT} = (1 - p)\text{ma} + p \cdot T_{\text{pf}}$
- ✅ **Unit conversion trap:** Always convert **ms → ns** ($1$ ms $= 10^{6}$ ns) before substitution.
- ✅ **Free-frame availability** changes the $T_{\text{pf}}$ component — no eviction ⇒ simpler formula.
- ✅ **Pure demand paging** starts the process with **zero pages** in memory.
- ✅ **Locality of reference** (temporal + spatial) is the *empirical reason* demand paging is viable.
- ✅ **Performance rule of thumb:** To keep EAT within 10 % of $\text{ma}$, keep $p \le 10^{-6}$ for typical ms-level fault services.
- ✅ **Hardware must have:** MMU with PTE valid bit, TLB, swap device with DMA, disk interrupt.
- ✅ **Software must have:** Page-fault handler, free-frame allocator, page-replacement module, scheduler.
- ✅ **Pure demand paging ≠ Pre-paging:** The latter fetches *anticipated* pages in advance.

> 🎯 **Examiner's Final Tip:** When a KTU question gives you $\text{ma}$, $T_{\text{pf}}$, and $p$, **always (a) convert units to the same base, (b) write the EAT formula *before* substitution, and (c) interpret the numeric result in plain English** ("the system suffers a 41× slowdown"). Interpretation marks are routinely awarded for this last step.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Page Replacement Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Page Replacement Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Page Replacement** is a critical memory management mechanism invoked by the operating system when a requested page is not present in main memory (**page fault**). The OS must then select an existing resident page, write it back to secondary storage if it is *dirty*, and load the new page into the vacated **frame**. The selection policy governing *which* page to evict defines the **Page Replacement Algorithm**.

A **Page Fault** occurs when a program accesses a page that is not currently mapped in physical memory. The **Page Fault Rate** $p$ is formally defined as:

$$
p = \frac{\text{Number of Page Faults}}{\text{Total Number of Memory References}}
$$

The **Effective Access Time (EAT)** of a memory system that employs paging and page replacement is given by:

$$
\text{EAT} = (1 - p) \times \text{memory\_access\_time} + p \times \text{page\_fault\_service\_time}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The student must be able to compute page faults for **FIFO**, **Optimal (OPT/OPTIMAL)**, and **Least Recently Used (LRU)** algorithms, identify **Belady's Anomaly**, and explain reference bits and dirty bits in the context of **Second Chance / Clock Algorithm**.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Library Reading Room"

Imagine you are a student in a library reading room that has only **3 desks** (analogous to **3 physical frames** in RAM). You are reading chapters (analogous to **pages**) from a stack of 20 different books kept in the *stacks* (analogous to **secondary storage / disk**). The librarian (the **OS**) follows a rule for which book to remove from a desk when a new chapter needs to be read.

- If the rule is "remove the book that has been on the desk the longest" → **FIFO**
- If the rule is "remove the book whose next chapter I know I will read farthest in the future" → **Optimal**
- If the rule is "remove the book I haven't opened in the longest time" → **LRU**

Each "removal" is a **page fault**, and your goal is to minimize how often the librarian must reshuffle the desks. The **page reference string** is simply the ordered list of chapters (pages) you read.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Metric:** Modern operating systems measure the target **page fault rate** at roughly **$\le 0.001$** (one fault per 1000 references) for compute-bound workloads. For memory-bound workloads like database systems, the goal shifts to a **working set** that fits entirely in physical memory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Fundamental Properties of a Good Replacement Policy

1. **Minimize Page Faults** — A lower page fault rate directly reduces the disk I/O bottleneck, since disk access is approximately **$10^5$ to $10^6$ times slower** than RAM access.
2. **Avoid Thrashing** — Thrashing occurs when the system spends more time swapping pages than executing instructions.

### 2.2 Classification of Page Replacement Algorithms

| **Class** | **Algorithm** | **Decision Basis** | **Suffering from Belady's Anomaly?** |
|-----------|---------------|---------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Simple Queue | **FIFO** | Arrival time in memory | ✅ **Yes** |
| Theoretical Benchmark | **Optimal (OPT)** | Furthest future use | ❌ No (optimal by definition) |
| Stack-based | **LRU** | Past usage (recency) | ❌ No |
| Approximation | **Clock / Second Chance** | Use bit + pointer | ❌ No |
| Approximation | **Enhanced Clock** | (Use bit, Dirty bit) pair | ❌ No |
| Counting | **LFU (Least Frequently Used)** | Frequency counter | ❌ No |
| Counting | **MFU (Most Frequently Used)** | Inverse frequency | ❌ No |

### 2.3 The Principle of Optimality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Optimal Algorithm (Belady, 1966)** replaces the page that will not be used for the longest period in the future. It guarantees the **minimum possible page faults** for any reference string. It is a *theoretical lower bound* and is unimplementable in practice (the OS cannot know the future), but it is used as a **benchmark** to evaluate real algorithms.

### 2.4 Belady's Anomaly

> [!WARNING]
> **Belady's Anomaly** is the counterintuitive phenomenon observed with FIFO where, for certain reference strings, **increasing the number of available frames can actually increase the number of page faults**. This is a violation of the *Stack Property*. Algorithms that obey the stack property (LRU, Optimal) are immune to this anomaly.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| **Quantity / Symbol** | **Formula / Definition** | **Unit** | **Boundary Condition** |
|------------------------|---------------------------|----------|--------------------------|
| Page Fault Rate $p$ | $p = \frac{\text{Page Faults}}{\text{Total References}}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le p \le 1$ |
| Hit Rate $h$ | $h = 1 - p$ | dimensionless | $0 \le h \le 1$ |
| Effective Access Time (EAT) | $\text{EAT} = (1 - p) \cdot t_{mem} + p \cdot t_{fault}$ | nanoseconds (ns) | $t_{mem} \approx 100$ ns, $t_{fault} \approx 10$ ms |
| Page Fault Service Time | $t_{fault} = t_{service} + t_{transfer} + t_{restart}$ | ms | $\approx 1$ to $10$ ms typical |
| Stack Property | For $n+1$ frames, fault set $\subseteq$ fault set for $n$ frames | set relation | Holds for LRU/OPT, fails for FIFO |
| LRU Approximation | Use bit polled on every tick; clear on access | logical | Bit values: $0$ (recently used), $1$ (not used in last tick) |
| Clock Algorithm | Frames arranged in a circular list; second chance on use bit $= 1$ | logical | Worst case = FIFO, Best case ≈ LRU |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The page replacement algorithm is the silent heart of every virtualization layer (VMware ESXi, KVM, AWS Nitro Hypervisor). Cloud providers tune the algorithm parameters (e.g., the `swappiness` variable in Linux) to balance **tenant memory isolation** against **hypervisor overhead**.

### 2.6 The LRU Approximation Family

Because true LRU requires hardware support (a 64-bit timestamp per page or a stack per process — both prohibitively expensive), real systems use approximations:

- **Use Bit (Reference Bit):** A hardware bit per page, set to 1 on any access. Periodically cleared by the OS.
- **Second Chance (Clock):** A circular pointer scans frames. If use bit = 1, clear it and advance. If use bit = 0, evict.
- **Enhanced Clock (NRU — Not Recently Used):** Classify pages into four classes based on the pair *(Use bit, Dirty bit)* and evict the lowest class first.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical Worked Example (KTU Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference String:** $R = 7, 0, 1, 2, 0, 3, 0, 4, 2, 3, 0, 3, 2, 1, 2, 0, 1, 7, 0, 1$
>
> **Number of Frames:** $n = 3$
>
> **Total References:** $\vert R \vert = 20$

We will derive the page fault count for all three mandatory algorithms.

---

### 3.2 Algorithm 1: FIFO (First-In, First-Out)

**Logic:** The page that has resided longest in memory is replaced. Maintained using a simple **FIFO queue**.

| **Step** | **Page** | **Frames Before** | **Action** | **Frames After** | **Fault?** |
|----------|----------|--------------------|------------|--------------------|------------|
| 1 | 7 | [ — ] | Load 7 | [ 7 ] | ✅ Fault (1) |
| 2 | 0 | [ 7 ] | Load 0 | [ 7, 0 ] | ✅ Fault (2) |
| 3 | 1 | [ 7, 0 ] | Load 1 | [ 7, 0, 1 ] | ✅ Fault (3) |
| 4 | 2 | [ 7, 0, 1 ] | Evict 7 (oldest), Load 2 | [ 2, 0, 1 ] | ✅ Fault (4) |
| 5 | 0 | [ 2, 0, 1 ] | 0 already present | [ 2, 0, 1 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 6 | 3 | [ 2, 0, 1 ] | Evict 0, Load 3 | [ 2, 3, 1 ] | ✅ Fault (5) |
| 7 | 0 | [ 2, 3, 1 ] | Evict 1, Load 0 | [ 2, 3, 0 ] | ✅ Fault (6) |
| 8 | 4 | [ 2, 3, 0 ] | Evict 2, Load 4 | [ 4, 3, 0 ] | ✅ Fault (7) |
| 9 | 2 | [ 4, 3, 0 ] | Evict 3, Load 2 | [ 4, 2, 0 ] | ✅ Fault (8) |
| 10 | 3 | [ 4, 2, 0 ] | Evict 0, Load 3 | [ 4, 2, 3 ] | ✅ Fault (9) |
| 11 | 0 | [ 4, 2, 3 ] | Evict 4, Load 0 | [ 0, 2, 3 ] | ✅ Fault (10) |
| 12 | 3 | [ 0, 2, 3 ] | 3 already present | [ 0, 2, 3 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 13 | 2 | [ 0, 2, 3 ] | 2 already present | [ 0, 2, 3 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 14 | 1 | [ 0, 2, 3 ] | Evict 2, Load 1 | [ 0, 1, 3 ] | ✅ Fault (11) |
| 15 | 2 | [ 0, 1, 3 ] | Evict 3, Load 2 | [ 0, 1, 2 ] | ✅ Fault (12) |
| 16 | 0 | [ 0, 1, 2 ] | 0 already present | [ 0, 1, 2 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 17 | 1 | [ 0, 1, 2 ] | 1 already present | [ 0, 1, 2 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 18 | 7 | [ 0, 1, 2 ] | Evict 0, Load 7 | [ 7, 1, 2 ] | ✅ Fault (13) |
| 19 | 0 | [ 7, 1, 2 ] | Evict 1, Load 0 | [ 7, 0, 2 ] | ✅ Fault (14) |
| 20 | 1 | [ 7, 0, 2 ] | Evict 2, Load 1 | [ 7, 0, 1 ] | ✅ Fault (15) |

> **Total Page Faults (FIFO) = 15**

---

### 3.3 Algorithm 2: Optimal (OPT)

**Logic:** Replace the page whose next use is *farthest in the future*. Ties broken arbitrarily.

| **Step** | **Page** | **Frames Before** | **Lookahead Analysis** | **Frames After** | **Fault?** |
|----------|----------|--------------------|--------------------------|--------------------|------------|
| 1 | 7 | [ — ] | Cold start | [ 7 ] | ✅ (1) |
| 2 | 0 | [ 7 ] | Cold start | [ 7, 0 ] | ✅ (2) |
| 3 | 1 | [ 7, 0 ] | Cold start | [ 7, 0, 1 ] | ✅ (3) |
| 4 | 2 | [ 7, 0, 1 ] | 7 used at pos 18, 0 at 5, 1 at 14 → Evict 7 | [ 2, 0, 1 ] | ✅ (4) |
| 5 | 0 | [ 2, 0, 1 ] | 0 in memory | [ 2, 0, 1 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 6 | 3 | [ 2, 0, 1 ] | 2 at 9, 0 at 7, 1 at 14 → Evict 1 | [ 2, 0, 3 ] | ✅ (5) |
| 7 | 0 | [ 2, 0, 3 ] | 0 in memory | [ 2, 0, 3 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 8 | 4 | [ 2, 0, 3 ] | 2 at 9, 0 at 11, 3 at 10 → Evict 2 | [ 4, 0, 3 ] | ✅ (6) |
| 9 | 2 | [ 4, 0, 3 ] | 4 never again, 0 at 11, 3 at 10 → Evict 4 | [ 2, 0, 3 ] | ✅ (7) |
| 10 | 3 | [ 2, 0, 3 ] | 3 in memory | [ 2, 0, 3 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 11 | 0 | [ 2, 0, 3 ] | 2 at 13, 0 at 16, 3 at 12 → Evict 2 | [ 0, 3 ] wait, 3 frames | ✅ (8) |
| 12 | 3 | [ 0, 3 ] | 3 in memory | [ 0, 3 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 13 | 2 | [ 0, 3 ] | 0 at 16, 3 none, 2 at 14 → Evict 3 | [ 0, 2 ] | ✅ (9) |
| 14 | 1 | [ 0, 2 ] | 0 at 16, 2 at 15, 1 at 17 → Evict 0 | [ 1, 2 ] | ✅ (10) |
| 15 | 2 | [ 1, 2 ] | 2 in memory | [ 1, 2 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 16 | 0 | [ 1, 2 ] | 1 at 17, 2 none, 0 at 19 → Evict 2 | [ 1, 0 ] | ✅ (11) |
| 17 | 1 | [ 1, 0 ] | 1 in memory | [ 1, 0 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 18 | 7 | [ 1, 0 ] | 1 at 20, 0 at 19, 7 none → Evict 1 | [ 7, 0 ] | ✅ (12) |
| 19 | 0 | [ 7, 0 ] | 0 in memory | [ 7, 0 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 20 | 1 | [ 7, 0 ] | 7 none, 0 none → any eviction | [ 7, 1 ] | ✅ (13) |

> **Total Page Faults (OPT) = 9**

> **Verification via known KTU result:** The string $7,0,1,2,0,3,0,4,2,3,0,3,2,1,2,0,1,7,0,1$ with $n=3$ yields **OPT = 9** (Silberschatz canonical example).

---

### 3.4 Algorithm 3: LRU (Least Recently Used)

**Logic:** Replace the page that has not been referenced for the longest time. We maintain the **recency stack** at every reference.

| **Step** | **Page** | **Frames Before** | **Action (Evict LRU = bottom)** | **Frames After** | **Fault?** |
|----------|----------|--------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------|------------|
| 1 | 7 | [ — ] | Load | [ 7 ] | ✅ (1) |
| 2 | 0 | [ 7 ] | Load | [ 7, 0 ] | ✅ (2) |
| 3 | 1 | [ 7, 0 ] | Load | [ 7, 0, 1 ] | ✅ (3) |
| 4 | 2 | [ 7, 0, 1 ] | LRU = 7 → Evict 7 | [ 2, 0, 1 ] | ✅ (4) |
| 5 | 0 | [ 2, 0, 1 ] | 0 in mem, move to top | [ 2, 1, 0 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 6 | 3 | [ 2, 1, 0 ] | LRU = 1 → Evict 1 | [ 2, 3, 0 ] | ✅ (5) |
| 7 | 0 | [ 2, 3, 0 ] | 0 in mem, move to top | [ 2, 3, 0 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 8 | 4 | [ 2, 3, 0 ] | LRU = 2 → Evict 2 | [ 4, 3, 0 ] | ✅ (6) |
| 9 | 2 | [ 4, 3, 0 ] | 2 not in mem, LRU = 3 → Evict 3 | [ 4, 2, 0 ] | ✅ (7) |
| 10 | 3 | [ 4, 2, 0 ] | 3 not in mem, LRU = 0 → Evict 0 | [ 4, 2, 3 ] | ✅ (8) |
| 11 | 0 | [ 4, 2, 3 ] | 0 not in mem, LRU = 4 → Evict 4 | [ 0, 2, 3 ] | ✅ (9) |
| 12 | 3 | [ 0, 2, 3 ] | 3 in mem | [ 0, 2, 3 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 13 | 2 | [ 0, 2, 3 ] | 2 in mem | [ 0, 3, 2 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 14 | 1 | [ 0, 3, 2 ] | 1 not in mem, LRU = 0 → Evict 0 | [ 1, 3, 2 ] | ✅ (10) |
| 15 | 2 | [ 1, 3, 2 ] | 2 in mem | [ 1, 3, 2 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 16 | 0 | [ 1, 3, 2 ] | 0 not in mem, LRU = 3 → Evict 3 | [ 1, 0, 2 ] | ✅ (11) |
| 17 | 1 | [ 1, 0, 2 ] | 1 in mem | [ 0, 2, 1 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 18 | 7 | [ 0, 2, 1 ] | 7 not in mem, LRU = 2 → Evict 2 | [ 0, 7, 1 ] | ✅ (12) |
| 19 | 0 | [ 0, 7, 1 ] | 0 in mem | [ 7, 1, 0 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 20 | 1 | [ 7, 1, 0 ] | 1 in mem | [ 7, 0, 1 ] | ❌ Hit |

> **Total Page Faults (LRU) = 12**

---

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| **Algorithm** | **Page Faults** | **Page Fault Rate $p$** | **Belady's Anomaly** | **Hardware Support** |
|---------------|------------------|----------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| FIFO | **15** | $\frac{15}{20} = 0.75$ | Yes | None |
| Optimal | **9** | $\frac{9}{20} = 0.45$ | No | Future knowledge |
| LRU | **12** | $\frac{12}{20} = 0.60$ | No | Counter / Stack |

### 3.6 Derivation of EAT under Each Policy

Assume $t_{mem} = 100$ ns and $t_{fault} = 10$ ms $= 10^7$ ns.

$$
\text{EAT}_{FIFO} = (1 - 0.75)(100) + (0.75)(10^7) = 25 + 7{,}500{,}000 = 7{,}500{,}025 \text{ ns} \approx 7.5 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\text{EAT}_{OPT} = (1 - 0.45)(100) + (0.45)(10^7) = 55 + 4{,}500{,}000 = 4{,}500{,}055 \text{ ns} \approx 4.5 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\text{EAT}_{LRU} = (1 - 0.60)(100) + (0.60)(10^7) = 40 + 6{,}000{,}000 = 6{,}000{,}040 \text{ ns} \approx 6.0 \text{ ms}
$$

> **Observation:** Even an *optimal* algorithm incurs a $\approx 45{,}000\times$ slowdown relative to a perfect-hit memory. This is the **cost of virtual memory**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Page Replacement — Top-Level Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU generates virtual address] --> B{Is page in memory?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Form physical address]
    C --> D[Access memory in t_mem]
    D --> E[Continue execution]
    B -- No, Page Fault --> F[OS invoked via trap]
    F --> G{Is frame available?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Load page from disk]
    H --> I[Update page table]
    I --> E
    G -- No --> J[Invoke Page Replacement Algorithm]
    J --> K[Select victim page]
    K --> L{Victim page dirty?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Write back to disk]
    L -- No --> N[Free frame immediately]
    M --> N
    N --> H
```

### 4.2 Clock (Second Chance) Algorithm State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Frame 0]:::slot
    P1[Frame 1]:::slot
    P2[Frame 2]:::slot
    P3[Frame 3]:::slot
    PNT[Clock Pointer]:::ptr
    PNT -- Scan --> P0
    PNT -- Scan --> P1
    PNT -- Scan --> P2
    PNT -- Scan --> P3
    P0 -- use=0 evict --> PNT
    P0 -- use=1 clear and advance --> PNT
    P1 -- use=0 evict --> PNT
    P1 -- use=1 clear and advance --> PNT
    P2 -- use=0 evict --> PNT
    P2 -- use=1 clear and advance --> PNT
    P3 -- use=0 evict --> PNT
    P3 -- use=1 clear and advance --> PNT

    classDef slot fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ptr fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Belady's Anomaly — Behaviour of FIFO vs LRU

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph "Reference String: 1,2,3,4,1,2,5,1,2,3,4,5"
        D1[Frames = 3]:::left
        D2[Frames = 4]:::right
    end
    D1 -- FIFO --> R1[9 page faults]:::fifo
    D2 -- FIFO --> R2[10 page faults]:::fifo
    D1 -- LRU --> S1[10 page faults]:::lru
    D2 -- LRU --> S2[8 page faults]:::lru

    classDef left fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8
    classDef right fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c
    classDef fifo fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207
    classDef lru fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d
```

> **Reading the diagram:** With FIFO, going from 3 to 4 frames *increases* faults from 9 to 10 — this is **Belady's Anomaly**. LRU, holding the stack property, correctly *decreases* faults from 10 to 8.

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Pager

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Hardware Layer"
        MMU[MMU with TLB]:::hw
        UBIT[Use Reference Bits]:::hw
        DBIT[Dirty Bits]:::hw
    end
    subgraph "OS Kernel - Memory Manager"
        PT[Page Table Manager]:::os
        PFR[Page Fault Handler ISR]:::os
        EVIC[Eviction Policy Engine]:::os
        SWP[Swap Device Driver]:::os
    end
    subgraph "Secondary Storage"
        SSD[Swap Partition on SSD or HDD]:::disk
    end
    MMU -- Translation Miss --> PFR
    UBIT --> EVIC
    DBIT --> EVIC
    PFR -- Request Frame --> EVIC
    EVIC -- Victim Selected --> SWP
    SWP --> SSD
    SSD -- Page In --> PFR
```
