```mermaid
    PFR -- Update --> PT
    PT -- Refresh TLB --> MMU

    classDef hw fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3
    classDef os fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d
    classDef disk fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**Q1. Define a *page fault*. Compute the page fault rate if a process makes 1500 memory references and incurs 75 page faults during its execution. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A *page fault* is a type of exception (trap) raised by the hardware Memory Management Unit (MMU) when a process attempts to access a page that is not currently resident in the main memory (RAM). The OS must then fetch the page from secondary storage, a costly disk I/O operation.

$$
p = \frac{\text{Number of Page Faults}}{\text{Total References}} = \frac{75}{1500} = 0.05
$$

Hence, the **page fault rate is $0.05$** (i.e., 5%). **(3 Marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**Q2. What is *Belady's Anomaly*? State which algorithm suffers from it. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Belady's Anomaly is the phenomenon in which, for certain page reference strings, **increasing the number of memory frames results in an *increase* (not a decrease) in the number of page faults**. This violates the intuitive assumption that more memory should never hurt performance.

It is exhibited by the **FIFO (First-In, First-Out)** algorithm. Algorithms based on the **stack property** (such as LRU and Optimal) are immune to this anomaly, as adding a frame can never cause a page that was previously faulted to be faulted again. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split into sub-parts (a) of 7 marks and (b) of 7 marks. You are required to attempt **ONE** full question (with both sub-parts).

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Consider the reference string: $R = 2, 6, 1, 5, 7, 7, 1, 6, 2, 1, 6, 3, 6, 4, 2, 7, 3, 6, 4, 2$

Compute the number of page faults for the **FIFO** and the **LRU** page replacement algorithm, assuming **3 frames** are allocated. Show the frame snapshot after every reference. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**FIFO Trace:**

| Step | Page | Frames After | Fault? |
|------|------|--------------|--------|
| 1 | 2 | [ 2 ] | ✅ (1) |
| 2 | 6 | [ 2, 6 ] | ✅ (2) |
| 3 | 1 | [ 2, 6, 1 ] | ✅ (3) |
| 4 | 5 | [ 5, 6, 1 ] evict 2 | ✅ (4) |
| 5 | 7 | [ 5, 7, 1 ] evict 6 | ✅ (5) |
| 6 | 7 | [ 5, 7, 1 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 7 | 1 | [ 5, 7, 1 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 8 | 6 | [ 5, 7, 6 ] evict 1 | ✅ (6) |
| 9 | 2 | [ 5, 2, 6 ] evict 7 | ✅ (7) |
| 10 | 1 | [ 1, 2, 6 ] evict 5 | ✅ (8) |
| 11 | 6 | [ 1, 2, 6 ] | ❌ Hit |
| 12 | 3 | [ 1, 2, 3 ] evict 6 | ✅ (9) |
| 13 | 6 | [ 6, 2, 3 ] evict 1 | ✅ (10) |
| 14 | 4 | [ 6, 4, 3 ] evict 2 | ✅ (11) |
| 15 | 2 | [ 6, 4, 2 ] evict 3 | ✅ (12) |
| 16 | 7 | [ 7, 4, 2 ] evict 6 | ✅ (13) |
| 17 | 3 | [ 7, 3, 2 ] evict 4 | ✅ (14) |
| 18 | 6 | [ 7, 3, 6 ] evict 2 | ✅ (15) |
| 19 | 4 | [ 7, 4, 6 ] evict 3 | ✅ (16) |
| 20 | 2 | [ 2, 4, 6 ] evict 7 | ✅ (17) |

> **FIFO Page Faults = 17**

**LRU Trace (Recency Stack):**

| Step | Page | Frames After | Fault? |
|------|------|--------------|--------|
| 1 | 2 | [ 2 ] | ✅ (1) |
| 2 | 6 | [ 6, 2 ] | ✅ (2) |
| 3 | 1 | [ 1, 6, 2 ] | ✅ (3) |
| 4 | 5 | [ 5, 1, 6 ] evict 2 | ✅ (4) |
| 5 | 7 | [ 7, 5, 1 ] evict 6 | ✅ (5) |
| 6 | 7 | [ 7, 5, 1 ] | ❌ |
| 7 | 1 | [ 1, 7, 5 ] | ❌ |
| 8 | 6 | [ 6, 1, 7 ] evict 5 | ✅ (6) |
| 9 | 2 | [ 2, 6, 1 ] evict 7 | ✅ (7) |
| 10 | 1 | [ 1, 2, 6 ] | ❌ |
| 11 | 6 | [ 6, 1, 2 ] | ❌ |
| 12 | 3 | [ 3, 6, 1 ] evict 2 | ✅ (8) |
| 13 | 6 | [ 6, 3, 1 ] | ❌ |
| 14 | 4 | [ 4, 6, 3 ] evict 1 | ✅ (9) |
| 15 | 2 | [ 2, 4, 6 ] evict 3 | ✅ (10) |
| 16 | 7 | [ 7, 2, 4 ] evict 6 | ✅ (11) |
| 17 | 3 | [ 3, 7, 2 ] evict 4 | ✅ (12) |
| 18 | 6 | [ 6, 3, 7 ] evict 2 | ✅ (13) |
| 19 | 4 | [ 4, 6, 3 ] evict 7 | ✅ (14) |
| 20 | 2 | [ 2, 4, 6 ] evict 3 | ✅ (15) |

> **LRU Page Faults = 15** **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b)** Explain **Belady's Anomaly** with a suitable reference string and frame count. Why does it occur in FIFO? Prove that LRU does not suffer from it. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Demonstration of Belady's Anomaly:**

Consider the reference string: $R = 1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ with FIFO:

*For 3 frames:* Faults = **9** (sequence: load 1,2,3, replace 1 with 4, replace 2 with 1, replace 3 with 2, replace 4 with 5, replace 1 with 3, replace 2 with 4, replace 5 with 5 already, replace 3 with 5).

The detailed trace yields **9 page faults** for 3 frames and **10 page faults** for 4 frames.

**Why It Occurs in FIFO:**
FIFO only considers the *arrival time* of a page in memory, not its actual usage. A frequently used page can be evicted simply because it arrived early. Adding more frames changes the replacement order of the existing frames in a non-monotonic way.

**Proof LRU is Immune (Stack Property):**
At any instant, the LRU algorithm maintains the set of the *n* most recently used pages as a **stack**. If we increase frames from $n$ to $n+1$, the set of pages kept for $n$ frames is a **subset** of the pages kept for $n+1$ frames. Therefore, every page that caused a fault with $n$ frames will still cause a fault with $n+1$ frames, and possibly fewer. Hence faults can only **decrease or stay equal** — never increase. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **Optimal Page Replacement Algorithm**. Why is it not implementable in a real system? What is its primary use? Consider the reference string $R = 1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 5, 6, 1, 2, 3, 7, 6, 3, 2, 1$ with 3 frames. Compute the page faults for Optimal. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** The Optimal Page Replacement Algorithm (proposed by Belady, 1966) replaces the page that will not be used for the **longest period in the future**. It yields the **minimum possible page faults** for any reference string, serving as the theoretical best-case.

**Why Unimplementable:** The OS cannot predict the future. It would require foreknowledge of the entire page reference string, which is only available *after* the program has executed.

**Primary Use:** It is used as a **benchmark** to evaluate the performance of practical algorithms (FIFO, LRU, Clock) by comparing their fault counts against the optimal lower bound.

**Trace for $R = 1,2,3,4,1,5,6,1,2,3,7,6,3,2,1$ with 3 frames:**

| Step | Page | Frames After | Evicted (Future Use) | Fault? |
|------|------|--------------|------------------------|--------|
| 1 | 1 | [ 1 ] | — | ✅ (1) |
| 2 | 2 | [ 1, 2 ] | — | ✅ (2) |
| 3 | 3 | [ 1, 2, 3 ] | — | ✅ (3) |
| 4 | 4 | [ 4, 2, 3 ] | 1 (next use: 5) | ✅ (4) |
| 5 | 1 | [ 4, 1, 3 ] | 2 (next use: 9) | ✅ (5) |
| 6 | 5 | [ 4, 1, 5 ] | 3 (next use: 11) | ✅ (6) |
| 7 | 6 | [ 4, 1, 6 ] | 5 (no future use) | ✅ (7) |
| 8 | 1 | [ 4, 1, 6 ] | 1 in memory | ❌ Hit |
| 9 | 2 | [ 2, 1, 6 ] | 4 (no future use) | ✅ (8) |
| 10 | 3 | [ 2, 1, 3 ] | 6 (next use: 12) | ✅ (9) |
| 11 | 7 | [ 2, 1, 7 ] | 3 (next use: 13) | ✅ (10) |
| 12 | 6 | [ 2, 1, 6 ] | 7 (no future use) | ✅ (11) |
| 13 | 3 | [ 3, 1, 6 ] | 2 (next use: 14) | ✅ (12) |
| 14 | 2 | [ 2, 1, 6 ] | 3 (no future) or 6 (no future) | ✅ (13) |
| 15 | 1 | [ 2, 1, 6 ] | Hit | ❌ Hit |

> **Optimal Page Faults = 11** **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b)** Describe the **Second Chance (Clock) Algorithm**. How does the use (reference) bit enable approximation of LRU? What happens if all use bits are set to 1? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Description:**
The Second Chance Algorithm (also called the **Clock Algorithm**) arranges all physical frames in a **circular list**, with a single **clock hand (pointer)** that traverses them. Each frame has an associated **use bit** (or reference bit) maintained by hardware.

**Decision Logic (step-by-step):**
1. When a new page must be loaded, advance the clock hand to inspect the frame.
2. If the **use bit = 0** → evict this page (it has not been used recently).
3. If the **use bit = 1** → clear it to 0 (give the page a "second chance") and advance the hand.

**Approximation of LRU:**
The use bit is set to 1 automatically on *any* read or write. The OS periodically clears all use bits (e.g., via a timer interrupt). Thus, a page whose use bit is 0 has not been accessed *since the last clear*, approximating "not used recently." Pages used recently retain use bit = 1 and survive eviction.

**If all use bits = 1:**
The clock hand will make a **full revolution**, clearing each bit in turn. On the second pass, all bits will be 0, and the page currently pointed to will be evicted. In the worst case (all bits = 1), the algorithm degrades to **FIFO**, because every page gets exactly one reprieve before eviction. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Costing Marks (Dec 2022 / June 2023 ESE Pattern):**
> 1. **FIFO Eviction Mistake:** Students often evict the *most recently loaded* page. **Correct rule:** Evict the *oldest* page (the one that arrived first, regardless of usage).
> 2. **LRU Recency Update Forgotten:** When a page is *hit*, its recency must be reset to the most recent position. Many students forget this and double-count faults.
> 3. **Confusing Belady's Anomaly Direction:** The anomaly says *more frames → more faults* (counterintuitive). Students often state the reverse.
> 4. **Optimal — Tie-Breaking:** If two pages have the same "farthest future use," pick either. The examiner expects you to *show the lookahead reasoning* explicitly.
> 5. **Units of EAT:** Always state the EAT in **ns or $\mu$s**, not "milliseconds" mixed with "nanoseconds" for $t_{mem}$.
> 6. **Page Table Updates:** When showing frames, you do **not** need to redraw the page table, but you must show *all three frame contents* after every step.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Page Fault** = trap when a referenced page is not in RAM.
- [x] **Page Fault Rate** $p$ = faults ÷ total references. Typical target $p \le 0.001$.
- [x] **EAT** = $(1-p) \cdot t_{mem} + p \cdot t_{fault}$. A $p = 0.001$ with $t_{fault} = 10$ ms destroys throughput.
- [x] **FIFO** = queue-based, suffers from **Belady's Anomaly**, no hardware needed.
- [x] **Optimal (OPT)** = theoretical benchmark, uses future knowledge, minimum faults guaranteed.
- [x] **LRU** = uses past usage, obeys **stack property**, immune to Belady's, but expensive (needs counter per page or a stack).
- [x] **Stack Property** = set of pages in $n$ frames $\subseteq$ set of pages in $n+1$ frames.
- [x] **Belady's Anomaly** = more frames can mean *more* faults under FIFO (e.g., string $1,2,3,4,1,2,5,1,2,3,4,5$).
- [x] **Clock (Second Chance)** = circular scan of use bits; gives recent pages a "second chance" by clearing use bit.
- [x] **Enhanced Clock** = uses (use bit, dirty bit) pair, four classes: $(0,0)$ best, $(0,1)$, $(1,0)$, $(1,1)$ worst.
- [x] **LFU (Least Frequently Used)** = evicts page with lowest access count. Suffers from "cache stagnation" — old high-count pages never evicted.
- [x] **MFU (Most Frequently Used)** = evicts page with highest count (rationale: the page with the most counts probably just arrived and has more to give). Rarely used in practice.
- [x] **Thrashing** = system spends more time swapping than executing; caused by too many processes competing for too few frames.
- [x] **Working Set Model** = $W(t, \Delta)$ = set of pages referenced in the time interval $[t - \Delta, t]$; OS allocates $\vert W \vert$ frames to each process to prevent thrashing.
- [x] **Linux `swappiness`** parameter (default 60) balances anonymous-page reclaim vs file-cache reclaim — a modern descendant of the page replacement design space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Pearl:** In the 14-mark questions, *always present the trace as a neat table*. Examiners reward clarity. If you run out of time, write "continuing in similar fashion..." and tabulate the remaining steps — this typically fetches 80%+ of the marks for that algorithm.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: File System: File Concept - Attributes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** File System: File Concept - Attributes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **File** is a named collection of related information that is recorded on secondary storage (such as magnetic disks, SSDs, or optical media) and treated as a single logical unit by the operating system. From the perspective of the OS, a file is the smallest logical storage entity to which the file system allocates a distinct set of attributes, permissions, and access controls.

A **File Concept** in operating systems abstracts the underlying physical storage (sectors, tracks, cylinders) into a logical, named, and user-friendly unit. The OS maintains a **File Control Block (FCB)** — or its modern equivalent, the **inode** — that stores the metadata (attributes) required to locate, manage, and protect the file.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Must Memorize):**
> *A file is a logical container of related information defined by its creator, typically representing data, source programs, object modules, executables, or simply text. The file is the central abstraction upon which the entire file system architecture is built.*

### 1.2 File Attributes — The Core Topic

**File Attributes** are the metadata entries stored by the operating system in the **File Control Block (FCB)** or **inode** structure. Attributes describe *what* a file is, *where* it resides, *who* owns it, and *how* it can be accessed — without containing the actual user data of the file itself.

The principal attributes mandated by the KTU 2024 Operating Systems syllabus are:

| # | Attribute | Purpose |
|---|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **Name** | Human-readable symbolic identifier |
| 2 | **Identifier** | Unique low-level tag (numeric) for OS internal use |
| 3 | **Type** | Required for systems supporting multiple file types |
| 4 | **Location** | Pointer to the device + location on the device |
| 5 | **Size** | Current size (and sometimes maximum size allowed) |
| 6 | **Protection** | Access control information (R/W/X permissions) |
| 7 | **Time, Date, and User ID** | For protection, security, and usage tracking |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Distinction:** Attributes ≠ Data. The actual *contents* of a file (text, binary, image) are NOT attributes. Attributes are *about* the file; data is *inside* the file.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Document Cabinet"

Imagine a large **office document cabinet** with thousands of folders:

- Each **folder** = one file
- The **label on the tab** = File Name (e.g., "Project_Report_2024")
- The **internal ID number printed in tiny font** = File Identifier (e.g., `inode #4012`)
- The **colored sticker** denoting type = File Type (e.g., red for confidential, blue for financial)
- The **cabinet number and shelf position** = Location (e.g., Cabinet 3, Shelf B, Position 7)
- The **thickness of the folder** = Size (e.g., 250 pages)
- The **"Do Not Copy" stamp** = Protection attribute
- The **date stamp + clerk signature** = Time, Date, User Identification

Just as a librarian never needs to read every page of a book to file it correctly, the OS never needs to read a file's contents to manage it — it only needs the **attributes** stored in the FCB/inode.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each File Attribute

#### 2.1.1 File Name
- A **human-readable string** chosen by the creator.
- Typically comprises two parts: a **name** and an **extension** separated by a dot (e.g., `report.pdf`, `main.c`).
- Case sensitivity is OS-dependent:
  - **Case-sensitive:** UNIX/Linux (`Report.txt` ≠ `report.txt`)
  - **Case-insensitive:** Windows (treats both as identical)
- Naming conventions are enforced by the directory structure, not the file itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exams, mention both *case-sensitive* vs *case-insensitive* OS examples — this is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

#### 2.1.2 File Identifier (inode number)
- A **unique numeric tag** (e.g., integer index) assigned by the OS upon file creation.
- The directory entry maps the *human-readable name* → *identifier*.
- The OS uses the identifier internally to locate the file's inode.
- This decoupling allows the file to be **renamed** without changing its physical location.

#### 2.1.3 File Type
- The OS distinguishes types to optimize handling (e.g., source code vs executable vs image).
- Stored as a magic number, extension, or a type field in the FCB.

**Common File Types in Modern Systems:**

| Type | Typical Extension | Nature |
|------|-------------------|--------|
| Source Code | `.c`, `.java`, `.py` | ASCII text |
| Object Code | `.obj`, `.o` | Binary |
| Executable | `.exe`, `.out`, `.app` | Binary executable |
| Batch/Script | `.bat`, `.sh` | Text commands |
| Multimedia | `.mp4`, `.mp3`, `.jpg` | Binary |
| Archive | `.zip`, `.tar`, `.rar` | Compressed binary |
| Document | `.pdf`, `.docx`, `.txt` | Mixed |

#### 2.1.4 File Location
- Points to the **physical device** and the **location on the device**.
- In modern systems, this is abstracted as a **pointer to the inode** (which itself contains disk block addresses).
- Historically stored as `(device, cylinder, track, sector)` tuple.

#### 2.1.5 File Size
- The **current actual size** of the file in bytes.
- Some systems also store the **maximum allocated size** to help with pre-allocation decisions.
- Directory listing commands (`ls -l` in Linux) display this attribute prominently.

#### 2.1.6 Protection
- Defines **who can do what** with the file.
- Typical modes include:
  - **Read (R)** — view file contents
  - **Write (W)** — modify file contents
  - **Execute (X)** — run the file as a program
  - **Append (A)** — add data to end only
  - **Delete (D)** — remove the file
- Permissions are categorized by user class: **Owner / Group / Others (World)**.
- The **UNIX rwx triplet** uses **3 bits per class**, giving the classic 9-bit permission mask:

$$ P_{\text{mask}} = \underbrace{r_o w_o x_o}_{\text{Owner}} \; \underbrace{r_g w_g x_g}_{\text{Group}} \; \underbrace{r_w w_w x_w}_{\text{Others}} $$

#### 2.1.7 Time, Date, and User Identification
Three timestamps are commonly maintained:
- **Creation time** — when the file was first created
- **Last modification time** — when the data was last changed
- **Last access time** — when the file was last read

User identification includes:
- **Owner (UID)** — the user who created the file
- **Group (GID)** — the group ownership for collaborative access

> [!NOTE]
> The Linux `stat` command displays all of these attributes simultaneously. KTU examiners often ask: *"List the file attributes maintained by a typical OS."* — This 7-part list is the **exact answer expected**.

### 2.2 File Control Block (FCB) — Where Attributes Live

The **File Control Block (FCB)** is the on-disk data structure that stores all attributes of a file. It is the kernel's internal representation of a file. The structure is typically:

$$
\text{FCB} = \{ \text{Name}, \text{ID}, \text{Type}, \text{Location}, \text{Size}, \text{Protection}, \text{Time/Date}, \text{UID}, \text{GID} \}
$$

In **UNIX/Linux**, the FCB equivalent is the **inode (index node)**. In **Windows NTFS**, it is the **Master File Table (MFT) entry**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **high-yield cheat sheet** — every KTU question on file attributes tests at least one of these.

| # | Concept | Key Expression / Value | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------|-----------------------|--------------|
| 1 | UNIX permission bits | $P = 3 \times 3 = 9$ | bits per file |
| 2 | Octal permission value | $P_{\text{octal}} = r \cdot 4 + w \cdot 2 + x \cdot 1$ | per class |
| 3 | Total files per directory | Limited by block size of directory | OS-dependent |
| 4 | Time attribute count | $T = 3$ (creation, modify, access) | typical |
| 5 | Inode size | $128$ bytes (ext4) or $256$ bytes (varies) | bytes |
| 6 | Max filename length | $255$ bytes (Linux) / $260$ chars (Windows) | bytes/chars |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** When writing $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) inside markdown tables, ALWAYS use `\vert` or `\mid` — never the bare pipe `|`. Bare pipes break table rendering.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Management Systems (DBMS):** Use file attributes (especially size, modification time, and protection) for transaction logging, recovery, and access control.
- **Cloud Storage Systems (AWS S3, Google Cloud Storage):** Each object stores metadata equivalent to OS file attributes — used for version control, access policies, and lifecycle management.
- **Digital Forensics:** Investigators reconstruct timelines using `mtime`, `ctime`, and `atime` attributes.
- **Version Control Systems (Git):** Internally track file hashes and modification times to detect changes — analogous to file attribute tracking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Breakdown: How a File Attribute is Stored and Retrieved

To consolidate understanding, we trace the lifecycle of a file attribute through the OS layers:

**Step 1: User Issues a Command**
A user types `touch report.txt` in a UNIX shell. The shell invokes the `touch` system call.

**Step 2: Kernel Allocates an Inode**
The kernel selects a free inode from the inode bitmap. The inode number is assigned — this becomes the **Identifier** attribute.

**Step 3: Kernel Populates the Inode**
The following attributes are initialized in the inode structure:
- Type: regular file (`S_IFREG`)
- Size: 0 bytes
- Protection: default umask applied (e.g., `0644` → `rw-r--r--`)
- Timestamps: creation, modification, and access time set to current system time
- UID: process's effective user ID
- GID: process's effective group ID
- Location: pointer to the first data block (initially empty)

**Step 4: Directory Entry Created**
The parent directory is updated with a new entry mapping `report.txt` → inode number. This couples the human-readable **Name** attribute to the **Identifier**.

**Step 5: Subsequent Read/Write**
When the file is later opened, the OS:
1. Searches the directory for `report.txt`
2. Retrieves the inode number
3. Loads the inode into main memory
4. Uses the inode to access the file's **Location**, applies **Protection** checks against the **UID/GID**, and updates **Access time**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Octal Permission Value from rwx Triplet

The UNIX octal permission encoding is derived as follows:

**Step 1:** Define a binary triplet per class. For one user class (Owner, Group, or Others), the triplet is $(r, w, x)$ where each bit is binary (0 or 1).

**Step 2:** Assign positional weights to each bit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Read bit weight}   &= 2^2 = 4 \\
\text{Write bit weight}  &= 2^1 = 2 \\
\text{Execute bit weight} &= 2^0 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the octal digit for each class as the weighted sum:

$$
P_{\text{class}} = r \cdot 4 + w \cdot 2 + x \cdot 1
$$

**Step 4:** Concatenate the three octal digits (Owner, Group, Others) to form the full permission string. For example:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Owner: } & r=1, w=1, x=1 \;\Rightarrow\; 1 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 7 \\
\text{Group: } & r=1, w=0, x=1 \;\Rightarrow\; 1 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 5 \\
\text{Others: }& r=1, w=0, x=0 \;\Rightarrow\; 1 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 + 0 \cdot 1 = 4 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Therefore the full permission mask is `754`, meaning:
- Owner: `rwx` (read, write, execute)
- Group: `r-x` (read, execute)
- Others: `r--` (read only)

**Verification:** This matches the standard UNIX symbolic representation `rwxr-xr--`. ✔

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Attribute Behavior Across Operating Systems

| Attribute | UNIX/Linux | Windows (NTFS) | macOS (APFS) |
|-----------|------------|----------------|--------------|
| Case-sensitive names | Yes | No (by default) | No (by default), configurable |
| Identifier type | Inode number (numeric) | MFT entry reference (numeric) | Inode-style object ID |
| Type storage | Magic number + extension | Extension-based | Magic number + UTI |
| Timestamps | 3 (atime, mtime, ctime) | 3 (accessed, modified, created) | 3 (atime, mtime, ctime) |
| Permission model | rwx + ACLs | ACLs only | POSIX + ACLs |
| Max filename length | 255 bytes | 255 chars | 255 UTF-16 chars |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
