## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*List the various file attributes maintained by a typical operating system.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A typical operating system maintains the following file attributes in the File Control Block (FCB):

1. **Name** – The symbolic human-readable identifier (e.g., `notes.txt`).
2. **Identifier** – A unique numeric tag (inode number) used internally by the OS to identify the file.
3. **Type** – Indicates the file format (text, binary, source, executable, etc.) for proper handling.
4. **Location** – A pointer to the storage device and the specific location on the device where the file resides.
5. **Size** – The current size of the file in bytes (and sometimes the maximum allowed size).
6. **Protection** – Access control information specifying who can read, write, or execute the file.
7. **Time, Date, and User ID** – Timestamps for creation, last modification, and last access; UID and GID of the owner.

> [!NOTE]
> All seven attributes must be listed. Missing even one costs a mark in KTU valuation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample]**
*What is a File Control Block (FCB)? What information does it contain?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **File Control Block (FCB)** is an on-disk data structure maintained by the operating system for every file. It is the kernel's internal representation of a file and stores all the file's attributes.

The FCB contains:
- File **name, identifier, type, location, size, protection**, and **time/date/UID** information.
- In UNIX/Linux, the FCB is called an **inode**; in Windows NTFS, it is a **Master File Table (MFT) entry**.
- The FCB is loaded into main memory when the file is opened and is used by the OS for all subsequent file operations.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a file in an operating system. List and explain the various file attributes maintained by the OS. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of the File Control Block (FCB) in file management. How are UNIX permissions encoded using the rwx triplet and octal notation? Derive the octal value for the permission `rwxr-xr--`. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**File Concept:**
A file is a named collection of information stored on secondary storage. It is the smallest logical unit of storage that the OS treats as a single entity. Files abstract away the physical details of disk organization (cylinders, tracks, sectors) and present a simple, named interface to users and programs.

**File Attributes (list and explain):**

1. **Name** – A symbolic string chosen by the user. May include an extension. Case sensitivity depends on the OS.
2. **Identifier** – A unique numeric tag (e.g., inode number) that the OS uses internally.
3. **Type** – Classifies the file (text, binary, source, executable, directory) so the OS can handle it appropriately.
4. **Location** – A pointer indicating the storage device and the disk blocks where the file data resides.
5. **Size** – The current length of the file in bytes. Updated dynamically as data is written.
6. **Protection** – Access control rules (read/write/execute) for owner, group, and others.
7. **Time, Date, and User ID** – Tracks when the file was created, modified, and last accessed, along with the owner's identity (UID) and group (GID).

> [!NOTE]
> All 7 attributes are essential; mark allocation: 1 mark for file concept + 1 mark per attribute group (covering all 7 in 6 marks).

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**FCB Role:**
The **File Control Block (FCB)** is the OS's internal data structure that stores all attributes of a file. When a file is opened, its FCB is loaded into a main-memory **open-file table**. The FCB is consulted for every operation (read, write, seek, close). It bridges the user-friendly filename and the physical disk blocks.

**UNIX Permission Encoding:**
Each file has three permission classes — Owner, Group, Others — and three operations per class — Read (r), Write (w), Execute (x). Each class forms a 3-bit binary pattern, which is represented as a single octal digit.

**Derivation for `rwxr-xr--`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Owner: } & r=1, w=1, x=1 \;\Rightarrow\; (1 \cdot 4) + (1 \cdot 2) + (1 \cdot 1) = 7 \\
\text{Group: } & r=1, w=0, x=1 \;\Rightarrow\; (1 \cdot 4) + (0 \cdot 2) + (1 \cdot 1) = 5 \\
\text{Others: }& r=1, w=0, x=0 \;\Rightarrow\; (1 \cdot 4) + (0 \cdot 2) + (0 \cdot 1) = 4 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Concatenating the three digits: **`754`**.

So the file is `chmod 754 filename`, granting the owner full access (`rwx`), the group read+execute (`r-x`), and others read-only (`r--`).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is a file? Discuss the seven key attributes of a file with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare file attribute handling in UNIX/Linux and Windows NTFS. What is an inode? How does the inode store file attributes? **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

A file is a logical container for related data, treated by the OS as a single addressable unit. The seven key attributes are:

1. **Name** – e.g., `project.c`
2. **Identifier** – e.g., inode `2073`
3. **Type** – e.g., C source file
4. **Location** – e.g., `/home/alice/project.c` on `/dev/sda1`
5. **Size** – e.g., `2.4 KB`
6. **Protection** – e.g., `0644` (`rw-r--r--`)
7. **Time, Date, UID/GID** – e.g., created `2024-10-15`, owner `alice` (UID 1000)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | UNIX/Linux | Windows NTFS |
|--------|------------|--------------|
| Attribute storage | Inode | Master File Table (MFT) entry |
| Name case sensitivity | Case-sensitive | Case-insensitive |
| Permission model | rwx bits + ACLs | ACLs only |
| Inode size | 128–256 bytes (typical) | MFT entry ~1 KB |
| Timestamps | atime, mtime, ctime | Created, Modified, Accessed |

**What is an Inode?**
An **inode (index node)** is a UNIX data structure that stores all file attributes except the filename. The filename is stored separately in the directory entry, which maps the name → inode number.

**How the inode stores attributes:**
The inode contains fields for: file type, permissions, UID, GID, file size, timestamps (atime, mtime, ctime), link count, and pointers to the data blocks (typically 12 direct, 1 indirect, 1 double-indirect, 1 triple-indirect pointer).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "filename" with "identifier":** Many students list "name" twice or merge name and identifier. They are distinct — name is human-readable (string), identifier is OS-internal (number).
> 2. **Forgetting Time/Date:** Out of the 7 attributes, students commonly list only 5 or 6. Always include **Time, Date, and User Identification** as a combined 7th item.
> 3. **Writing `|` for absolute value in tables:** This breaks the markdown table. Use `\vert` or `\mid`.
> 4. **Omitting examples in long answers:** KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for examples. Always give one example per attribute.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Last-Minute Revision — Memorize Before Exam:**

- **File** = named logical storage unit; smallest entity OS manages.
- **7 Attributes (MUST memorize in order):** Name, Identifier, Type, Location, Size, Protection, Time/Date/UID.
- **FCB** (File Control Block) stores all attributes = inode in UNIX = MFT entry in Windows.
- **Name ≠ Identifier:** Name is human string; Identifier is OS numeric tag.
- **3 Timestamps:** Creation (or ctime), Modification (mtime), Access (atime).
- **UNIX rwx Triplet:** 3 bits per class × 3 classes = 9 bits total.
- **Octal Conversion:** $r=4, w=2, x=1$; sum per class, concatenate three classes.
- **Case-sensitivity:** UNIX yes, Windows no (board-favorite question).
- **Identifier decoupling** enables renaming without moving data.
- **Protection bits** are checked against UID/GID on every file access.
- **Use `\vert` not `|`** for absolute value in any markdown/LaTeX table (engine formatting rule).
- **Inode does NOT store filename** — directory entry does. This is a classic KTU trick question.
- **Example permission:** `rwxr-xr--` → octal `754`.
- **Max filename length:** 255 bytes in Linux, 255 characters in Windows.
- **Forests of attributes** = {Identity, Physical, Security, Temporal} (4-way classification for advanced answers).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What are File Operations?

In the context of Operating Systems, **File Operations** refer to the fundamental, system-level primitives (function calls) invoked by user processes to interact with the file system — the on-disk and in-memory data structures maintained by the OS to organize, retrieve, and persist information across secondary storage. According to the KTU 2024 Operating Systems syllabus, file operations are classified into two broad categories:

1. **High-level / Logical Operations** — performed via system calls (open, read, write, close, etc.) at the user-process level.
2. **Low-level / Physical Operations** — performed by the OS on disk blocks, inodes, sectors, and cylinders (seek, transfer, rotational latency, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim-style):**
> *A file is a named collection of related information recorded on secondary storage. A file is the smallest logical unit of secondary storage that the system can manage and protect. Operations on a file are the abstract primitives that the operating system exposes to applications, masking the underlying physical geometry of the storage device.*

---

## 1.2 The 6 / 12 Standard File Operations

The most universally accepted set of file operations (per the Silberschatz, Galvin, and Gagne OS textbook — the de-facto reference for KTU) is:

| # | Operation | Purpose |
|---|-----------|---------|
| 1 | `Create` | Allocate a new file, register it in the directory |
| 2 | `Open` | Bring the file's metadata into memory |
| 3 | `Write` | Append or overwrite data into the file |
| 4 | `Read` | Transfer data from file to process memory |
| 5 | `Reposition (Seek)` | Move the current file pointer |
| 6 | `Delete` | Remove the file from the file system |
| 7 | `Truncate` | Empty the file but keep its attributes |
| 8 | `Close` | Deallocate the open file table entry |
| 9 | `Append` | Write at EOF without overwriting |
| 10 | `Rename` | Change file name without recreation |
| 11 | `Get-Attributes` | Read metadata (size, permissions, timestamps) |
| 12 | `Set-Attributes` | Modify metadata |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Locker Room" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Locker Room System):**
> Imagine your college has a locker room with 1,000 lockers (your **hard disk**). Each locker can hold one envelope (a **disk block**). Lockers are unlabeled from outside. The college maintains a **register book** (the **directory / inode table**) that records: *"Locker #457 belongs to student Arun, registered on 12-March."*

> 1. **Create** → Go to the office, fill out a form, get a new locker assigned. (Register a fresh entry in the directory.)
> 2. **Open** → Walk to the locker, unlock it, and place the key on the reception counter with a slip showing your name. (Load the inode into the **Open File Table**.)
> 3. **Read** → Open the envelope in your locker and read the letter. (Copy bytes from disk to RAM via a buffer.)
> 4. **Write** → Put a new letter into the envelope. (Copy bytes from RAM to disk.)
> 5. **Seek** → Skip to page 5 of the letter (move the **file pointer**).
> 6. **Close** → Return the key to the office. (Flush buffer, release the open file table slot.)
> 7. **Delete** → Tear up the form so no one knows which locker is yours. (Mark inode as free, deallocate blocks.)

> Without the register book, no one can find your locker — and without the open file table, the OS cannot quickly access your file's current state. This is **exactly** why the OS maintains a layered set of tables: **Per-Process Open File Table → System-Wide Open File Table → In-memory inode (vnode) → On-disk inode and data blocks**.

---

## 1.4 The Three-Layer Table Architecture

The OS keeps three synchronized structures to track file operations:

1. **Per-Process File Descriptor Table (FDT)** — Indexes the open files of *one* process; each entry points to a system-wide entry. Each entry is an integer called a **file descriptor** (e.g., `0 = stdin`, `1 = stdout`, `2 = stderr`).
2. **System-Wide Open File Table (SOFT)** — One entry per *actual* open instance of a file; contains current offset, access mode, and pointer to the in-memory inode.
3. **In-Memory inode / vnode Table** — A cached copy of the on-disk **inode** (index node), containing metadata: ownership, permissions, timestamps, size, and pointers to the actual data blocks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Exam:**
> When `fork()` is called, the **child inherits copies of the parent's file descriptors** that point to the *same* SOFT entry — so the child and parent share the current file offset. When `dup()` is called, a *new* FDT entry is created pointing to the *same* SOFT entry. When `open()` is called, a *new* SOFT entry is created (so the offset is *not* shared).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Life-Cycle of a File Operation

Every file operation can be decomposed into the following structured logic chain:

1. **User-Mode Call**: Application invokes a library wrapper (e.g., `fopen()` in C).
2. **Library Translation**: The C library converts the call to a system call (e.g., `open()` in POSIX).
3. **Mode Switch to Kernel**: Control transfers to the OS via a software interrupt (syscall / trap).
4. **Validation**: The kernel validates arguments (file descriptor range, buffer pointer, permissions, etc.).
5. **Lookup**: The kernel consults the per-process FDT → SOFT → in-memory inode.
6. **Buffer Cache / Page Cache Interaction**: Reads/writes are routed through the buffer cache (or page cache) to amortize slow disk I/O.
7. **Disk Driver Invocation**: Block I/O request is queued to the disk scheduler (FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, C-LOOK, F-SCAN).
8. **DMA Transfer**: Direct Memory Access controller moves data between disk and RAM without CPU intervention.
9. **Interrupt & Completion**: Disk raises an interrupt; OS updates the inode, timestamps, and SOFT entry.
10. **Return to User Mode**: System call returns a status code or byte count to the user process.

---

## 2.2 The Six Core System Calls — Detailed Semantics

### (a) `open(pathname, flags, mode)` → returns file descriptor `fd`
- Looks up the path component-wise (parent directory → child directory).
- If file does not exist and `O_CREAT` is set, allocates a new inode and data block.
- Allocates a new entry in the SOFT, initializes offset = 0, and returns the smallest unused FDT index.

### (b) `read(fd, buffer, count)` → returns bytes actually read
- Copies up to `count` bytes from the current offset into `buffer`.
- Advances the offset by the number of bytes read.
- Returns 0 at EOF.

### (c) `write(fd, buffer, count)` → returns bytes actually written
- Copies bytes from `buffer` to disk starting at the current offset.
- If offset is at EOF, the file grows; if it is past EOF, a *hole* (sparse file) is created in UNIX.
- May block until the data is **physically committed** (or only until it is in the buffer cache — depends on `O_SYNC`).

### (d) `lseek(fd, offset, whence)` → returns new offset
- `SEEK_SET` → absolute position = `offset`
- `SEEK_CUR` → position = current + `offset`
- `SEEK_END` → position = file size + `offset`
- Does **not** cause any I/O — it is purely a metadata update.

### (e) `close(fd)` → returns 0 on success
- Flushes the buffer cache for that file (if dirty).
- Writes back the in-memory inode to the on-disk inode.
- Releases the SOFT entry and FDT slot.

### (f) `unlink(pathname)` → removes a directory entry
- Decrements the link count in the inode.
- If link count → 0 *and* no process has it open, the file is physically deleted and blocks freed.
- If a process still has it open, the data persists until the last `close()`.

---

## 2.3 The `dup()` vs `fork()` Offset-Sharing Nuance (Most-Tested KTU Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram of Sharing Rules:**

| Call | New FDT entry? | New SOFT entry? | Offset shared? |
|------|---------------|-----------------|----------------|
| `fork()` | ✅ Yes (in child) | ❌ No (shared with parent) | ✅ Yes |
| `open()` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| `dup(fd)` | ✅ Yes (new fd value) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| `dup2(fd, newfd)` | ✅ Yes (overwrites `newfd`) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Disk I/O Performance)

These formulas are **directly tested** in KTU 2024 ESE numericals.

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| **Access Time (Ta)** | $T_a = T_s + T_r + T_{trans}$ | ms | Sum of seek, rotational, transfer |
| **Rotational Latency (Tr)** | $T_r = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{60}{RPM}$ | ms | Half a full rotation |
| **Transfer Time (Tt)** | $T_t = \dfrac{b}{N \times \dfrac{RPM}{60}}$ | ms | $b$=bytes, $N$=track capacity (bytes) |
| **Effective Transfer Time** | $T_t = \dfrac{1}{\text{bytes per revolution}} \times b$ | ms | $b / (N)$ rotations |
| **Rotations to read b bytes** | $R = \lceil b/N \rceil$ | rotations | Each rotation = one track pass |
| **Total Time for k blocks (contiguous)** | $T_{total} = T_s + T_r + k \cdot T_t$ | ms | One seek + one rotational delay |
| **Total Time for k blocks (random / linked)** | $T_{total} = k \cdot (T_s + T_r + T_t)$ | ms | One seek per block |
| **Throughput** | $\Theta = \dfrac{\text{Bytes read}}{T_{total}}$ | MB/s | Used in optimization comparisons |
| **Seek distance (SCAN / C-SCAN)** | $d_{seek} = \vert \text{head}_{end} - \text{head}_{start} \vert$ | cylinders | Sum across all movement phases |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** When a question gives you access time in **ms** and RPM, the rotational latency formula is $\dfrac{30{,}000}{RPM}$ ms = $\dfrac{0.5}{RPS}$ where $RPS = RPM/60$. **Do not** divide by 2 in seconds and forget to convert.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Databases (PostgreSQL, Oracle)** — call `open()` once and cache the file descriptor for the entire session to avoid repeated directory lookups.
- **Web Servers (Nginx, Apache)** — use `sendfile()` to copy data between two file descriptors entirely in kernel space, **avoiding the user-mode copy overhead** (zero-copy I/O).
- **Compilers (GCC)** — use `lseek()` for backpatching in object files.
- **Video Streaming (YouTube, Netflix)** — use `mmap()` to map files into virtual memory, allowing paging to act as demand-loading.
- **Embedded / IoT Systems** — use `O_DIRECT` to bypass the buffer cache and achieve deterministic I/O latency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked-Out Numerical 1 — Average Access Time

**Question (KTU 2024 style):**
A disk rotates at **10,000 RPM**, has an average seek time of **6 ms**, and a track capacity of **500 KB**. Calculate:
(a) Rotational latency
(b) Transfer time for a **100 KB** file
(c) Total access time

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step derivation follows. Read every line.**

### Part (a) — Rotational Latency

We know that the disk makes $\dfrac{RPM}{60}$ revolutions per second.

$$
RPS = \frac{10000}{60} = 166.667 \text{ revolutions / second}
$$

Time per revolution:

$$
T_{rev} = \frac{1}{RPS} = \frac{1}{166.667} = 0.006 \text{ s} = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

The **average** rotational latency is half a revolution (because on average the desired sector is halfway around):

$$
T_r = \frac{1}{2} \times T_{rev} = \frac{1}{2} \times 6 = 3 \text{ ms}
$$

### Part (b) — Transfer Time for 100 KB

The number of full track rotations required to read 100 KB (where one track = 500 KB):

$$
R = \left\lceil \frac{100 \text{ KB}}{500 \text{ KB}} \right\rceil = \lceil 0.2 \rceil = 1 \text{ rotation}
$$

Transfer time:

$$
T_t = R \times T_{rev} = 1 \times 6 \text{ ms} = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Note:** Even though we need only 20% of a track, the disk still spends one full rotation because data is read in sector units and the head cannot stop mid-rotation. (This is the **"rotational overhead"** that SSDs eliminate.)

### Part (c) — Total Access Time

$$
T_a = T_s + T_r + T_t
$$

$$
T_a = 6 \text{ ms} + 3 \text{ ms} + 6 \text{ ms} = 15 \text{ ms}
$$

**Answer Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Rotational Latency | **3 ms** |
| Transfer Time | **6 ms** |
| **Total Access Time** | **15 ms** |

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical 2 — Comparing Contiguous vs Linked Allocation

**Question:** A file has **2000 blocks**, each block is **1 KB**. The disk has:
- Average seek = 8 ms
- Rotational latency = 4 ms
- Transfer time per block = 0.1 ms
- Disk rotation = 7200 RPM

Find total I/O time to read the entire file in:
(a) **Contiguous allocation**
(b) **Linked allocation** (one seek + rotation per block)

### Setup Verification
Rotational latency cross-check:

$$
T_r = \frac{30{,}000}{RPM} = \frac{30000}{7200} = 4.1667 \text{ ms}
$$

For simplicity, we use $T_r \approx 4$ ms as given.

### Part (a) — Contiguous Allocation

In contiguous allocation, the file occupies 2000 *adjacent* blocks. Only **one** seek is needed to reach the start, and **one** rotational latency is incurred.

$$
T_{contig} = T_s + T_r + (k \times T_t)
$$

$$
T_{contig} = 8 + 4 + (2000 \times 0.1)
$$

$$
T_{contig} = 8 + 4 + 200 = 212 \text{ ms}
$$

### Part (b) — Linked Allocation

In linked allocation, each block points to the next via a pointer. The block may be anywhere on disk, so the head must seek (and wait for rotation) for **every** block.

$$
T_{linked} = k \times (T_s + T_r + T_t)
$$

$$
T_{linked} = 2000 \times (8 + 4 + 0.1)
$$

$$
T_{linked} = 2000 \times 12.1
$$

$$
T_{linked} = 24{,}200 \text{ ms} = 24.2 \text{ seconds}
$$

### Comparison

| Allocation | Total I/O Time | Speedup Factor |
|------------|---------------|----------------|
| Contiguous | **212 ms** | **114× faster** |
| Linked | **24,200 ms** | 1× (baseline) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** This is **the** classic KTU numerical. The conclusion is that linked allocation pays a heavy performance cost due to per-block seeks — the reason indexed allocation (single-level, multi-level) is preferred in modern file systems like **ext4** and **NTFS**.

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical 3 — SCAN Disk Scheduling

**Question (KTU-style):** A disk has 200 cylinders (0–199). The read/write head is currently at cylinder **75**, moving towards cylinder **0** (decreasing). The request queue is:

$$
\{ 25, 132, 18, 190, 40, 110, 65, 99 \}
$$

Compute the total head movement using the **SCAN (LOOK) algorithm** and the **C-SCAN (C-LOOK) algorithm**.

### SCAN (LOOK) Algorithm

Head direction: **towards 0 (decreasing)**.
Sorted requests ≤ 75: $\{ 25, 18, 40, 65 \}$ — these are served first, in the order encountered.
Sorted requests > 75: $\{ 99, 110, 132, 190 \}$ — these are served after the head reverses.

**Path of head movement:**

$$
75 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 40 \rightarrow 25 \rightarrow 18 \rightarrow 0 \rightarrow 99 \rightarrow 110 \rightarrow 132 \rightarrow 190
$$

**Head movements (absolute differences):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&|75 - 65| = 10 \\
&|65 - 40| = 25 \\
&|40 - 25| = 15 \\
&|25 - 18| = 7 \\
&|18 - 0| = 18 \\
&|0 - 99| = 99 \\
&|99 - 110| = 11 \\
&|110 - 132| = 22 \\
&|132 - 190| = 58
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total movement:**

$$
T_{SCAN} = 10 + 25 + 15 + 7 + 18 + 99 + 11 + 22 + 58 = 265 \text{ cylinders}
$$

### C-SCAN (C-LOOK) Algorithm

In C-SCAN, the head moves in **one direction only**, then jumps back to the beginning without servicing.

Sorted requests in **increasing** order, starting from 75:
$\{ 99, 110, 132, 190 \}$ (in direction of increasing cylinder)
Then a **jump** from 190 back to 0 (the lowest request, 18 — but C-LOOK jumps to the **lowest queued request** = 18, not 0).

**Path of head movement:**

$$
75 \rightarrow 99 \rightarrow 110 \rightarrow 132 \rightarrow 190 \rightarrow 18 \rightarrow 25 \rightarrow 40 \rightarrow 65
$$

**Head movements:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&|75 - 99| = 24 \\
&|99 - 110| = 11 \\
&|110 - 132| = 22 \\
&|132 - 190| = 58 \\
&|190 - 18| = 172 \text{ (jump)} \\
&|18 - 25| = 7 \\
&|25 - 40| = 15 \\
&|40 - 65| = 25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total movement:**

$$
T_{C-SCAN} = 24 + 11 + 22 + 58 + 172 + 7 + 15 + 25 = 334 \text{ cylinders}
$$

### Comparison Table

| Algorithm | Total Head Movement (cylinders) | Variability in Wait Time |
|-----------|--------------------------------|--------------------------|
| SCAN | **265** | Higher (innermost/outermost may wait) |
| C-SCAN | **334** | **Lower (more uniform)** — better for real-time |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The Three-Layer File Operation Architecture

This block-level functional architecture map shows the kernel's data structures touched by every file operation. It is a **high-yield** diagram that KTU examiners frequently test in 7-mark sub-parts.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Process A]
    P2[Process B]
    FDTA[Per Process FDT A]
    FDTB[Per Process FDT B]
    SOFT[System Wide Open File Table]
    INODEMEM[In Memory inode vnode Table]
    INODEDISK[On Disk inode]
    DBMETA[Directory Block]
    DB[Data Blocks 0 to N minus 1]
    BC[Buffer Cache Page Cache]

    P1 --> FDTA
    P2 --> FDTB
    FDTA --> SOFT
    FDTB --> SOFT
    SOFT --> INODEMEM
    INODEMEM -.flush.-> INODEDISK
    INODEMEM --> BC
    BC --> DB
    SOFT --> DBMETA
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** = active pointer reference in kernel memory.
- **Dotted arrow (flush)** = writeback to disk during `close()` or `sync()`.
- Every file operation (open, read, write, close) **traverses this entire graph**.

---

## 4.2 File Operation State Machine

This state diagram captures the legal transitions of a file during its lifecycle, as enforced by the kernel.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NonExistent
    NonExistent --> Open: create() OR open with O_CREAT
    Open --> Open: read() OR write() OR lseek()
    Open --> Closed: close()
    Open --> Deleted: unlink() with linkcount = 0
    Closed --> [*]: SOFT entry freed
    Deleted --> Closed: last close deallocates blocks
    Deleted --> [*]: data blocks returned to free list
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A file in the `Deleted` state continues to exist on disk as long as *any* process has it open — this is the famous "**unlinked-but-open**" file (e.g., `/tmp` files that survive process crash because they remain open in a daemon). Once the last descriptor is closed, blocks are released.

---

## 4.3 Disk Scheduler Family — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ[Request Queue] --> SCHED{Scheduler}
    SCHED -->|FCFS| R1[First Come First Serve]
    SCHED -->|SSTF| R2[Shortest Seek Time First]
    SCHED -->|SCAN| R3[Elevator Algorithm]
    SCHED -->|CSCAN| R4[Circular SCAN]
    SCHED -->|LOOK| R5[LOOK variant no end travel]
    SCHED -->|CLOOK| R6[Circular LOOK]
    SCHED -->|FSCAN| R7[F SCAN two snapshot queues]
    R1 --> HEAD[Disk Head]
    R2 --> HEAD
    R3 --> HEAD
    R4 --> HEAD
    R5 --> HEAD
    R6 --> HEAD
    R7 --> HEAD
```

**Key properties:**

| Algorithm | Starvation-Free? | Variance | Throughput |
|-----------|----------------|----------|------------|
| FCFS | ✅ Yes | High | Lowest |
| SSTF | ❌ No (far cylinders starve) | Medium | Higher |
| SCAN / LOOK | ✅ Yes | Medium | High |
| C-SCAN / C-LOOK | ✅ Yes | **Lowest (uniform wait)** | High |
| F-SCAN | ✅ Yes | Low | High (prevents arm-stick) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern OS Choice:** Linux uses the **CFQ, Deadline, and NOOP** schedulers. NOOP is used for SSDs. The classical SCAN family is the **textbook** answer for KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the "Open File Table" and the "Per-Process File Descriptor Table".**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> 1. **Per-Process File Descriptor Table (FDT)** is a per-process structure that contains integer indices (file descriptors) pointing to entries in the system-wide table. Each process has its **own** FDT, and the descriptors (0, 1, 2...) are local to that process. *(1 mark)*
> 2. **System-Wide Open File Table (SOFT)** is a **global** structure, shared across all processes. It contains one entry per *actual* open instance of a file, holding the current file offset, access mode, status flags, and a pointer to the in-memory inode. *(1 mark)*
> 3. The FDT acts as an **index of references**; the SOFT acts as a **metadata cache**. When `dup()` or `fork()` is called, the FDT entry is duplicated but the SOFT entry is shared — meaning the file offset is shared. When `open()` is called, a new SOFT entry is created, so the offset is *not* shared. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Explain the role of the buffer cache (page cache) in file operations.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> 1. The **buffer cache** (or page cache) is an in-memory region that holds recently read/written disk blocks. When a process issues `read()` or `write()`, the OS first consults the cache before issuing a physical disk I/O. *(1 mark)*
> 2. It exploits **temporal and spatial locality**: repeated reads of the same block (e.g., a frequently accessed file like `/etc/passwd`) are served entirely from RAM, avoiding the slow disk. *(1 mark)*
> 3. It also **de-bounces and merges** write requests, deferring them to a more efficient time (write-back) — this is why `write()` returns quickly even though the disk may not have been touched yet. `fsync()` forces the dirty pages to disk. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). You must answer **only one** of the two alternatives. Showing both forfeits marks.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **three layers of in-memory data structures** maintained by the OS to manage open files. Discuss how the sharing rules differ for `fork()`, `open()`, and `dup()`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A disk has the following specifications: **RPM = 7200**, average seek time = **8 ms**, track capacity = **256 sectors of 512 bytes** each.
   Compute:
   (i) Rotational latency
   (ii) Transfer time for reading **4096 bytes**
   (iii) Total access time to read the 4096-byte block **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

The three layers (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Per-Process File Descriptor Table (FDT):** Each process has its own private array indexed by integer descriptors `0, 1, 2, ...`. Each entry contains a pointer to a SOFT entry plus a set of close-on-exec flags. *Standard descriptors:* `0` = stdin, `1` = stdout, `2` = stderr. *(2 marks)*

2. **System-Wide Open File Table (SOFT):** A kernel-wide table with one entry per actual open file instance. Each entry contains:
   - Current file offset (read/write position)
   - Access mode (R, W, RW, Append)
   - Reference count (how many FDT entries point here)
   - Pointer to the in-memory inode *(2 marks)*

3. **In-Memory inode/vnode Table:** A cache of the on-disk inode containing metadata (UID, GID, mode bits, size, timestamps, block pointers). Data blocks themselves are read into the **buffer cache**, not into the inode table. *(2 marks)*

4. **Sharing rules (1 mark for table):**

| Call | New FDT? | New SOFT? | Offset Shared? |
|------|----------|-----------|----------------|
| `fork()` | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| `open()` | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `dup()` | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- RPM = 7200
- Average seek time $T_s = 8$ ms
- Track capacity = 256 sectors × 512 bytes = **131,072 bytes = 128 KB**
- Block to read = 4096 bytes = 4 KB

**(i) Rotational Latency:**

$$
T_r = \frac{30{,}000}{RPM} = \frac{30000}{7200} = 4.1667 \text{ ms} \approx 4.17 \text{ ms}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**(ii) Transfer Time for 4096 bytes:**

Number of rotations required:

$$
R = \left\lceil \frac{4096}{131072} \right\rceil = \lceil 0.03125 \rceil = 1 \text{ rotation}
$$

Time per rotation:

$$
T_{rev} = \frac{60}{RPM} = \frac{60}{7200} = 0.00833 \text{ s} = 8.33 \text{ ms}
$$

Transfer time:

$$
T_t = R \times T_{rev} = 1 \times 8.33 = 8.33 \text{ ms}
$$

*(2.5 marks)*

**(iii) Total Access Time:**

$$
T_a = T_s + T_r + T_t = 8 + 4.17 + 8.33 = 20.5 \text{ ms}
$$

*(2.5 marks)*

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, and C-LOOK** disk scheduling algorithms with a neat diagram. Compare their total head movement for the request queue: **98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67** with the head initially at cylinder **53** moving towards the **highest-numbered** cylinder. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of the `open()` and `close()` system calls** in detail, covering:
   - The data structures allocated/deallocated
   - The role of the buffer cache
   - The difference between mandatory and advisory locking **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Algorithm descriptions (3 marks):**
- **SCAN (Elevator):** The head moves in one direction servicing all requests in its path, then reverses direction at the disk end. Bounded waiting.
- **C-SCAN (Circular SCAN):** The head moves in one direction servicing requests, then **jumps back** to the beginning (without servicing) and repeats. Provides uniform wait time.
- **LOOK:** Like SCAN but the head **reverses at the furthest request**, not at the disk end.
- **C-LOOK:** Like C-SCAN but jumps back to the **lowest queued request**, not cylinder 0.

**Sorted request queue** (from the head at 53, moving upward):
$\{ 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183 \}$ (upward)
$\{ 37, 14 \}$ (after reversal, downward)

**SCAN path:** $53 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 183 \rightarrow 199 \rightarrow 37 \rightarrow 14$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{SCAN} &= |53-65| + |65-67| + |67-98| + |98-122| + |122-124| + |124-183| + |183-199| + |199-37| + |37-14| \\
&= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 16 + 162 + 23 = 331 \text{ cylinders}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**C-SCAN path:** $53 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 183 \rightarrow 14 \rightarrow 37$ (jumps back to lowest request, not 0)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{C\text{-}SCAN} &= |53-65| + |65-67| + |67-98| + |98-122| + |122-124| + |124-183| + |183-14| + |14-37| \\
&= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 169 + 23 = 322 \text{ cylinders}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks)*

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**`open()` system call (3.5 marks):**
1. **Pathname Resolution:** The kernel walks the directory hierarchy from the root (or CWD for relative paths), looking up each component. The final component must be a regular file or device.
2. **File-system Lookup:** Checks the directory entry — if `O_CREAT` is set and the file does not exist, a new inode and data block are allocated; if it exists, the existing inode is loaded into the in-memory inode table.
3. **Permission Check:** The effective UID/GID is compared against the inode's mode bits (rwx for owner, group, others).
4. **Open File Table Allocation:** A new SOFT entry is created (offset = 0, reference count = 1). A new FDT entry in the calling process points to this SOFT entry.
5. **Returns** the smallest unused file descriptor (integer).

**`close()` system call (2.5 marks):**
1. **Buffer Cache Flush:** All dirty (modified) pages of this file are written back to disk (or scheduled for writeback).
2. **Inode Writeback:** The in-memory inode (if modified) is written back to disk.
3. **Decrement Reference Count:** SOFT reference count is decremented; if it reaches 0, the SOFT entry is freed.
4. **FDT Cleanup:** The FDT entry is cleared; the descriptor number becomes available for reuse.

**Mandatory vs Advisory Locking (1 mark):**
- **Advisory locking:** Processes may ignore the lock and proceed. (Default in UNIX.)
- **Mandatory locking:** The kernel **enforces** the lock, blocking conflicting operations. (Must be enabled via mode bits on the file.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in Module 4 File Operations:**
> 1. **Confusing FDT and SOFT** — they are TWO DIFFERENT tables; do not merge them. Each is a separate data structure in the kernel.
> 2. **Forgetting the rotational latency's "half" factor** — average latency is **half a rotation**, not a full rotation. Easy to lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up `dup()` and `open()` offset sharing** — `dup()` shares the offset (same SOFT entry), `open()` does not (new SOFT entry).
> 4. **Forgetting the jump-back distance in C-SCAN** — students often compute only the serviced-cylinder movement, missing the **wrap-around jump** (which is the largest term).
> 5. **Mixing up `unlink()` and `delete()`** — `unlink()` removes the directory entry, not the file itself; the data persists until the last `close()`.
> 6. **Using `|x|` inside markdown tables** — this breaks the table layout. Use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX instead.
> 7. **Not mentioning the buffer cache** when describing `read()`/`write()` — the modern answer must reference the **page cache** to be marked complete.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **File operations** = the 6 / 12 standard system calls exposed by the OS to manipulate files.
- [x] The **three-layer table** (FDT → SOFT → in-memory inode) is the heart of file management.
- [x] `open()` creates a **new SOFT entry**; `fork()`/`dup()` **share** the existing SOFT entry.
- [x] `lseek()` is a **metadata-only** operation — no I/O is performed.
- [x] `close()` **flushes dirty buffers** and **writes back the inode** before releasing the SOFT entry.
- [x] `unlink()` removes the **directory entry**; the data lives on until the last `close()`.
- [x] **Access time** $T_a = T_s + T_r + T_t$ is the master equation for disk I/O cost.
- [x] **Rotational latency** $T_r = \dfrac{30{,}000}{RPM}$ ms (average = half-rotation).
- [x] **Contiguous allocation** is fastest; **linked allocation** is slowest (one seek per block).
- [x] **SCAN** reduces starvation vs SSTF; **C-SCAN** provides uniform wait time.
- [x] **Buffer cache** = temporal/spatial locality exploit; reduces real disk I/O.
- [x] **Mandatory locking** is kernel-enforced; **advisory locking** is cooperative.
- [x] **Sparse files** are created when `write()` extends offset past EOF (UNIX only).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Types # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Operating Systems (Module 4 — File Management / Secondary Storage), the term **"Types"** formally refers to the **Types of File Allocation Methods** on secondary storage (disk). A **File Allocation Method** (also called a *File Allocation Strategy* or *Disk Allocation Scheme*) is the on-disk layout policy that the operating system's file system uses to map a logical file (a sequential stream of bytes/blocks) onto the physical **sectors and tracks of a disk**. The three canonical types prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus are: 1. **Contiguous Allocation** 2. **Linked Allocation (Chained Allocation)** 3. **Indexed Allocation** > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > *File allocation methods are the techniques used by the file system to allocate disk space to files. The three standard schemes are Contiguous, Linked, and Indexed allocation — each trading off sequential access speed, random access speed, external fragmentation, and metadata overhead.* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module Context (PCERT601, Module 4)** > This topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO4** of the KTU 2024 scheme: *"Design and implement file system structures, directory schemes, and free-space management techniques."* Expect **2-mark to 14-mark questions** on this topic in the End Semester Examination (ESE). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Bookshelf Model Imagine a librarian is given the job of storing **500 novels** in a massive library with many empty shelves: - **Contiguous Allocation** → The librarian places all 500 novels *consecutively on a single long shelf*. Easy to read in order (read pages 1 → 500 in one sweep), but creating a 500-book "hole" is hard when someone wants to insert a new novel in the middle. - **Linked Allocation** → The librarian scatters the 500 novels across many different shelves, and writes on the back of each novel a sticky note saying *"The next novel is on Shelf 47, position 12."* You must follow the chain of sticky notes to read the whole book. Insertion is easy, but finding a specific page is slow. - **Indexed Allocation** → The librarian creates a **master index card** at the front desk listing the shelf location of every page (or chapter) of the novel. To read page 250, you look at the index card, jump to the right shelf, and pick it up directly. This is the most flexible, but the index card itself takes up shelf space. This analogy maps directly to the engineering trade-offs in disk allocation: - Disk seek time ≈ walking to a new shelf - Fragmentation ≈ wasted shelf space - Metadata overhead ≈ the size of the index card / sticky notes ## 1.3 Why These Three Types? — The Engineering Trade-off Triangle Every allocation method must balance three competing engineering goals: | Goal | Description | Best Method | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Sequential Access Speed** | Speed of reading file from start to end | Contiguous | | **Random Access Speed** | Speed of jumping to the *k*-th block | Indexed | | **Space Efficiency** | Minimal wasted space and metadata | Linked | > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU Metric** > Default block size on a typical UNIX/Linux file system is **4096 bytes (4 KB)**. All formulas in this module assume a fixed block size of **B bytes**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Type 1 — Contiguous Allocation ### Operational Logic Each file occupies a set of **contiguous (adjacent) blocks** on the disk. The directory entry stores only two numbers: the **starting block address** and the **length (in blocks)** of the file. **Directory Entry Structure (Contiguous):** $$\text{Directory Entry} = \{\text{Start\_Block},\ \text{Length}\}$$ **Block Address Computation (KTU 2024 High-Yield):** $$\text{Disk Address of Block } k = \text{Start\_Block} + (k - 1)$$ ### Advantages - **Excellent sequential access speed** — the read head does not need to seek between blocks. - **Excellent random access speed** — direct calculation of the *k*-th block using the formula above (only 1 seek). - **Simple implementation** — only `(start, length)` per file in the directory. ### Disadvantages - **External Fragmentation** — as files are created and deleted, free space breaks into small, non-adjacent holes. - **Difficult to grow a file** — the file may need to be moved if contiguous space is not available (requires a full copy). - **Must know the maximum file size at creation time** — wastes pre-allocated space. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact** > *Contiguous allocation suffers from external fragmentation, NOT internal fragmentation.* ## 2.2 Type 2 — Linked Allocation ### Operational Logic Each file is a **linked list of disk blocks** scattered anywhere on the disk. Each block contains a **pointer (typically 4 bytes)** to the next block. The directory entry stores only the **starting block address**. **Directory Entry Structure (Linked):** $$\text{Directory Entry} = \{\text{Start\_Block},\ \text{End\_Block (optional)}\}$$ **Block Structure on Disk:** $$\text{Block } = \{\text{Data\_Payload},\ \text{Next\_Pointer}\}$$ **Accessing Block *k* — Sequential Only:** To reach block *k*, the OS must traverse blocks 1, 2, 3, …, *k* by following pointers. ### Advantages - **No external fragmentation** — any free block can be used. - **Easy file growth** — simply allocate a new free block and update the previous block's pointer. - **Simple directory entry** — only the start address is needed. ### Disadvantages - **Slow random access** — to find the *k*-th block, the OS must read *k* blocks (linear traversal). - **Pointer overhead** — a portion of every block is wasted on the next pointer (e.g., 4 bytes out of 4096 = ~0.1%). - **Reliability issue** — a single corrupt pointer makes the rest of the file inaccessible (a bad sector on a critical link destroys the file). ### Variants (KTU 2024 — Frequently Asked) - **FAT (File Allocation Table) Variation**: All pointers are stored in a single table at the beginning of the disk. The disk blocks themselves contain only data. This solves the reliability problem and speeds up random access. ## 2.3 Type 3 — Indexed Allocation ### Operational Logic The directory entry stores a pointer to a special **index block**. The index block contains an **array of pointers** to all the data blocks of the file. **Directory Entry Structure (Indexed):** $$\text{Directory Entry} = \{\text{Index\_Block\_Pointer}\}$$ **Accessing Block *k* — Direct:** $$\text{Read Index\_Block} \rightarrow \text{Read pointer at slot } (k-1) \rightarrow \text{Read Data Block } k$$ This requires **2 disk seeks** (index block + data block) but supports **O(1) random access**. ### Advantages - **Fast random access** — direct pointer lookup. - **No external fragmentation** — blocks can be anywhere on the disk. - **Supports file growth** — until the index block is full. ### Disadvantages - **Index block overhead** — wastes an entire block for a small file. - **File size limited by index block size** — if block size is 4096 bytes and pointers are 4 bytes, the maximum file size is 4096/4 × 4096 = **4 MB**. ### Variants to Overcome the 4 MB Limit | Scheme | Solution | | :--- | :--- | | **Linked Index Block** | Chain multiple index blocks via a pointer in the last entry. | | **Multilevel Index (UNIX Inode)** | Use a 2-level or 3-level tree of index blocks. | | **Combined Scheme** | Use a few direct pointers + 1 single-indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect pointer (classic UNIX inode). | ## 2.4 KTU 2024 Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Use `\vert` notation for absolute value symbols in handwritten answers (e.g., $|x|$ → $\vert x \vert$) to avoid markdown table-breaking pipes.** | # | Parameter | Contiguous | Linked | Indexed | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Dir. Entry Size** | Start + Length (2 fields) | Start only (1 field) | Index Block Pointer (1 field) | | 2 | **Block *k* Address** | $\text{Start} + (k-1)$ | Traverse 1→k pointers | Index[k-1] | | 3 | **Random Access Time** | 1 seek | *k* seeks (linear) | 2 seeks | | 4 | **External Fragmentation** | **Yes** | No | No | | 5 | **Internal Fragmentation** | No | Pointer overhead (~0.1%) | Index block (wasted if small file) | | 6 | **Max File Size (KTU classic)** | $\text{Disk Size}$ | $\text{Disk Size}$ | $(B/S) \times B$ bytes | | 7 | **Reliability** | High | Low (broken pointer) | Medium | | 8 | **File Growth** | Hard | Easy | Easy (until index full) | Where: - $B$ = Block size in bytes - $S$ = Pointer size in bytes (typically **4 bytes** = 32 bits) - $B/S$ = Number of pointers per index block ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility These three allocation types are not merely academic — they appear in **every modern file system**: | Real File System | Allocation Type Used | | :--- | :--- | | **Optical Media (CD-ROM, DVD)** ISO 9660 | Contiguous Allocation | | **MS-DOS / FAT16 / FAT32** | Linked Allocation (with FAT variation) | | **UNIX / Linux (ext2, ext3, ext4)** | Indexed (Multi-level inode) | | **NTFS (Windows)** | Indexed (Master File Table) | | **Apple HFS+ / APFS** | Indexed (B-tree extents) | Understanding the trade-offs enables engineers to design **storage systems for embedded devices, databases, and SSDs** where the access patterns are predictable. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum File Size for Indexed Allocation **Problem Setup (KTU Classic):** Given a disk with block size $B$ = **4096 bytes** and a pointer size of $S$ = **4 bytes**, find the maximum file size supported by a **single-level** indexed allocation scheme. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Determine the number of pointers that fit in one index block.** Each pointer occupies $S$ bytes, so the number of pointers per index block is the block size divided by the pointer size: $$ N_{\text{pointers}} = \frac{B}{S} $$ **Step 2: Substitute the given values.** $$ N_{\text{pointers}} = \frac{4096\ \text{bytes}}{4\ \text{bytes/pointer}} = 1024\ \text{pointers} $$ **Step 3: Each pointer addresses one data block of size $B$ bytes.** The total data that can be addressed is the number of pointers times the block size: $$ \text{Max File Size} = N_{\text{pointers}} \times B $$ **Step 4: Substitute and compute.** $$ \text{Max File Size} = 1024 \times 4096\ \text{bytes} $$ $$ \text{Max File Size} = 4{,}194{,}304\ \text{bytes} = 4\ \text{MB} $$ ### Final Result (Boxed for KTU Exam) $$ \boxed{\text{Max File Size (Single-Level Indexed)} = \frac{B^2}{S} = 4\ \text{MB for } B=4096,\ S=4} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Seek Count for Linked Allocation **Problem Setup:** A file of $n$ blocks uses linked allocation. A user requests to read the **middle block** (block number $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ on average). How many disk seeks are required on average? ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: In linked allocation, reaching block $k$ requires reading blocks 1 through $k$ sequentially.** This is because each block only tells you the address of the next block. $$ \text{Seeks}(k) = k $$ **Step 2: The average position of an arbitrary block is the midpoint.** $$ k_{\text{avg}} = \frac{n + 1}{2} $$ **Step 3: The number of seeks to read this block is the block index itself.** $$ \text{Avg. Seeks} = k_{\text{avg}} = \frac{n + 1}{2} $$ **Step 4: For large $n$, approximate.** $$ \text{Avg. Seeks} \approx \frac{n}{2} $$ ### Final Result $$ \boxed{\text{Avg. Disk Seeks in Linked Allocation} \approx \frac{n}{2} \quad (\text{vs. } 1 \text{ for contiguous},\ 2 \text{ for indexed})} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Combined UNIX Inode Maximum File Size **Problem Setup (KTU Advanced):** The classic UNIX inode uses a **13-entry address array** in the inode: - 10 direct pointers - 1 single-indirect pointer - 1 double-indirect pointer - 1 triple-indirect pointer Given $B = 4096$ bytes and pointer size $S = 4$ bytes, derive the maximum file size. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Number of pointers per block.** $$ P = \frac{B}{S} = \frac{4096}{4} = 1024 $$ **Step 2: Data addressed by 10 direct pointers.** $$ S_{10} = 10 \times B = 10 \times 4096 = 40{,}960\ \text{bytes} = 40\ \text{KB} $$ **Step 3: Data addressed by the single-indirect pointer.** The single-indirect block contains 1024 pointers, each pointing to a 4096-byte data block: $$ S_{1} = P \times B = 1024 \times 4096 = 4\ \text{MB} $$ **Step 4: Data addressed by the double-indirect pointer.** The double-indirect block points to 1024 single-indirect blocks, each pointing to 1024 data blocks: $$ S_{2} = P \times P \times B = 1024 \times 1024 \times 4096 = 4\ \text{GB} $$ **Step 5: Data addressed by the triple-indirect pointer.** $$ S_{3} = P^3 \times B = 1024^3 \times 4096 = 4\ \text{TB} $$ **Step 6: Total maximum file size.** $$ S_{\text{total}} = S_{10} + S_{1} + S_{2} + S_{3} $$ $$ S_{\text{total}} \approx 40\ \text{KB} + 4\ \text{MB} + 4\ \text{GB} + 4\ \text{TB} $$ $$ \boxed{S_{\text{total}} \approx 4\ \text{TB}} $$ (The 4 KB and 40 KB terms are negligible compared to 4 TB.) --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use Which | Use Case | Best Allocation Type | Reason | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Read-mostly large media files (video, audio) | **Contiguous** | Maximum sequential throughput | | Append-only logs, streaming writes | **Linked** | Easy growth, no fragmentation | | General-purpose OS (programs, documents) | **Indexed** | Balance of random + sequential access | | CD/DVD burning | **Contiguous** | Media is write-once, sequential | | Solid State Drives (SSDs) | **Indexed (Log-structured)** | Random access cost is uniform | | Database systems | **Contiguous (extents)** | Predictable I/O patterns | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Comparison Diagram — All Three Types ```mermaid flowchart TB Start([KTU 2024 Module 4: File Allocation Types]) --> Q1{Is the file stored
in adjacent blocks?} Q1 -- Yes --> CA[Contiguous Allocation] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is each block linked
to the next via pointer?} Q2 -- Yes --> LA[Linked Allocation] Q2 -- No --> IA[Indexed Allocation] CA --> CAP[Directory: start + length
Access: direct math
Problem: external fragmentation] LA --> LAP[Directory: start block
Access: follow chain
Problem: slow random access] IA --> IAP[Directory: index block ptr
Access: 2 seeks O1
Problem: index block overhead] CAP --> Out([Choose Contiguous:
CD-ROM, DVD, backup tapes]) LAP --> Out2([Choose Linked:
FAT, append-only logs]) IAP --> Out3([Choose Indexed:
UNIX ext4, NTFS, APFS]) ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Contiguous Allocation ```mermaid graph LR DIR[Directory Entry
File X: start=100, length=4] B100[Block 100
Data Part 1] B101[Block 101
Data Part 2] B102[Block 102
Data Part 3] B103[Block 103
Data Part 4] DIR -.->|start=100| B100 B100 -->|consecutive| B101 B101 -->|consecutive| B102 B102 -->|consecutive| B103 style DIR fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309 style B100 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d style B101 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d style B102 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d style B103 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Linked Allocation ```mermaid graph LR DIR[Directory Entry
File X: start=205] B205[Block 205
Data + ptr to 12] B12[Block 12
Data + ptr to 901] B901[Block 901
Data + ptr to 47] B47[Block 47
Data + ptr to NIL] DIR -.->|start| B205 B205 -- next --> B12 B12 -- next --> B901 B901 -- next --> B47 B47 -- next --> NIL([End of File]) style DIR fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309 style B205 fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3 style B12 fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3 style B901 fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3 style B47 fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3 style NIL fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Indexed Allocation ```mermaid graph TB DIR[Directory Entry
File X: index_block=300] IDX[Index Block 300
ptr0=205, ptr1=12, ptr2=901, ptr3=47] DIR -.->|index_block| IDX IDX -->|slot 0| B205[Block 205
Data Part 1] IDX -->|slot 1| B12[Block 12
Data Part 2] IDX -->|slot 2| B901[Block 901
Data Part 3] IDX -->|slot 3| B47[Block 47
Data Part 4] style DIR fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309 style IDX fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d style B205 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d style B12 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d style B901 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d style B47 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d ``` ## 4.5 Decision Flow — Selecting the Right Type ```mermaid flowchart TB Start([Design Decision:
Choose Allocation Type]) --> A{Primary access pattern?} A -- Sequential only --> B{Need to minimize
fragmentation?} A -- Random + Sequential --> C[Use Indexed Allocation
e.g., UNIX ext4, NTFS] A -- Append only, unknown size --> D[Use Linked Allocation
e.g., FAT, log files] B -- Yes --> E[Use Linked Allocation
but with FAT variant] B -- No, max throughput required --> F[Use Contiguous Allocation
e.g., CD-ROM, DVD] C --> Out([Output: Indexed]) D --> Out2([Output: Linked]) E --> Out3([Output: Linked/FAT]) F --> Out4([Output: Contiguous]) ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]** *List the three file allocation methods. Which one supports direct access? Justify.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The three file allocation methods are: 1. **Contiguous Allocation** — file occupies a set of consecutive disk blocks. 2. **Linked Allocation** — file is a linked list of scattered disk blocks. 3. **Indexed Allocation** — file has a dedicated index block containing pointers to data blocks. **Direct Access Support:** - Contiguous supports direct access via the address formula: Address of block *k* = start + (*k* − 1). (1 mark) - Linked allocation does **not** support efficient direct access — it requires sequential traversal of *k* blocks to reach block *k*. (1 mark) - Indexed supports direct access by reading the index block and using the *k*-th pointer to jump directly to the data block (2 disk seeks). (1 mark) **Most efficient for direct access:** Contiguous (only 1 seek) > Indexed (2 seeks) > Linked (k seeks). **(1 mark for clear conclusion)** ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Compare contiguous and linked allocation with respect to external fragmentation and access time.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Criterion | Contiguous | Linked | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **External Fragmentation** | Yes — causes holes on disk as files are created/deleted (1 mark) | No — blocks are scattered; any free block can be used (1 mark) | | **Access Time** | Fast — 1 seek for any block using direct calculation (0.5 mark) | Slow — *k* seeks to reach the *k*-th block (0.5 mark) | **Conclusion:** Contiguous offers faster access but suffers from external fragmentation; linked avoids fragmentation but is slow for random access. **(KTU expects a balanced comparative conclusion for full 3 marks.)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern** > Part B questions on file allocation are typically 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (full choice). ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 4]** **(a)** Explain the three file allocation methods — contiguous, linked, and indexed — with neat diagrams. Highlight the directory entry structure of each. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Contiguous Allocation (≈ 2.5 marks)** - Each file occupies a **set of consecutive disk blocks**. - Directory entry stores: `{ start_block, length }` - Address of *k*-th block: `start + (k − 1)` → supports O(1) random access. - **Advantages:** Fastest access (1 seek), simple. - **Disadvantages:** External fragmentation; difficult to grow file. **Diagram (ASCII for handwritten answer):** ``` File X: start=100, length=4 | 100 | 101 | 102 | 103 | 104 | 105 | ... | X | X | X | X | ? | ? | ... ``` **(ii) Linked Allocation (≈ 2 marks)** - File is a **linked list of disk blocks** scattered anywhere on disk. - Each block contains: `{ data, next_block_pointer }` - Directory entry stores: `{ start_block }` only. - To reach block *k*, traverse 1 → 2 → ... → k pointers. - **Advantages:** No external fragmentation; easy growth. - **Disadvantages:** Slow random access; pointer overhead; reliability issue. **Diagram:** ``` File X: start=205 [205: data→12] → [12: data→901] → [901: data→47] → [47: data→NIL] ``` **(iii) Indexed Allocation (≈ 2.5 marks)** - A dedicated **index block** stores all data block pointers. - Directory entry stores: `{ index_block_pointer }` only. - Accessing block *k*: read index block, then jump via pointer at slot (*k* − 1). → 2 seeks. - **Advantages:** Fast random access; no external fragmentation. - **Disadvantages:** Index block overhead; max file size = $B^2 / S$. **Diagram:** ``` Directory: File X → index_block=300 Index block 300: [205, 12, 901, 47, ...] Each pointer → data block on disk ``` **(b)** Consider a disk with block size **B = 1024 bytes** and pointer size **S = 4 bytes**. A file of size **48 KB** is stored using indexed allocation. **(7 marks)** - **(i)** How many data blocks are needed for the file? - **(ii)** How many index blocks are needed? - **(iii)** What is the maximum file size supported? **Model Solution:** **(i) Number of data blocks:** $$ N_{\text{data}} = \frac{\text{File Size}}{B} = \frac{48 \times 1024}{1024} = 48\ \text{blocks} $$ **(ii) Number of index blocks needed:** Number of pointers per index block: $$ P = \frac{B}{S} = \frac{1024}{4} = 256\ \text{pointers/index block} $$ Since 48 < 256, only **1 index block** is sufficient. **(1.5 marks)** **(iii) Maximum file size (single-level indexed):** $$ \text{Max Size} = \frac{B^2}{S} = \frac{1024 \times 1024}{4} = \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{4} = 262{,}144\ \text{bytes} = 256\ \text{KB} $$ **Conclusion:** A 48 KB file easily fits (uses only 48/256 = ~19% of index capacity), and the maximum supported file size is **256 KB**. **(1 mark for interpretation/conclusion)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]** **(a)** Explain the **UNIX inode structure** as a combined index scheme. Why is the maximum file size effectively 4 TB? Derive step by step. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The classic UNIX inode uses a **combined (multi-level) index scheme** with 13 address fields: - **10 direct pointers** — each points to a data block (1 seek each). - **1 single-indirect pointer** — points to an index block of pointers. - **1 double-indirect pointer** — points to an index block of single-indirect blocks. - **1 triple-indirect pointer** — points to an index block of double-indirect blocks. **Derivation (KTU expects full steps):** Let $B = 4096$ bytes (block size) and $S = 4$ bytes (pointer size). **Step 1:** Pointers per block: $P = B/S = 4096/4 = 1024$ pointers. **Step 2:** Direct data: $$ S_{10} = 10 \times 4096 = 40\ \text{KB} $$ **Step 3:** Single-indirect data: $$ S_1 = P \times B = 1024 \times 4096 = 4\ \text{MB} $$ **Step 4:** Double-indirect data: $$ S_2 = P^2 \times B = 1024^2 \times 4096 = 4\ \text{GB} $$ **Step 5:** Triple-indirect data: $$ S_3 = P^3 \times B = 1024^3 \times 4096 = 4\ \text{TB} $$ **Step 6:** Total maximum file size: $$ S_{\text{total}} = 40\ \text{KB} + 4\ \text{MB} + 4\ \text{GB} + 4\ \text{TB} \approx 4\ \text{TB} $$ The terms 40 KB and 4 MB are **negligible** compared to 4 TB, so the **effective** maximum is **4 TB**. **(1 mark for the engineering interpretation)** **(b)** Compare the three allocation methods in a **single table** covering: directory entry overhead, external fragmentation, access time, and reliability. Which method is used by **NTFS and UNIX ext4**? Why? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Comparison Table (≈ 4 marks):** | Criterion | Contiguous | Linked | Indexed | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Dir. Entry** | Start + Length (2 fields) | Start (1 field) | Index pointer (1 field) | | **Ext. Fragmentation** | Yes | No | No | | **Access Time** | 1 seek (fastest) | *k* seeks (slowest for random) | 2 seeks (fast for random) | | **Reliability** | High | Low (broken pointer = lost file) | Medium (index block critical) | | **File Growth** | Hard | Easy | Easy | | **Max File Size** | Disk size | Disk size | $B^2/S$ (single-level) | **Real-World Use (≈ 3 marks):** - **NTFS (Windows)** and **UNIX ext2/ext3/ext4** both use **indexed allocation** (specifically, multi-level indexed / inode). - **Reason:** They are **general-purpose** operating systems that must support both random access (e.g., loading a program) and sequential access (e.g., reading a file). Indexed allocation provides the **best trade-off** — fast random access (2 seeks) without external fragmentation, and the multi-level scheme overcomes the 4 MB limit to support files up to 4 TB. - **Contiguous** is unsuitable due to external fragmentation on long-running systems. - **Linked** (or FAT) is unsuitable because random access is too slow for typical workloads (program loading, database queries). **(1 mark for the engineering reasoning)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Confusing fragmentation types:** Many students write "contiguous has internal fragmentation" — this is **WRONG**. Contiguous has **external** fragmentation. Internal fragmentation is what occurs in indexed allocation when a small file wastes most of an index block. (−1 mark) > 2. **Forgetting the formula for max file size:** Always write the full derivation: $P = B/S$, then $\text{Max} = P \times B = B^2/S$. A bare answer of "4 MB" without derivation gets only 1 of 2 marks. (−1 mark) > 3. **Mixing up access times:** Random access in indexed is **2 seeks**, not 1. (Contiguous is the only one with 1 seek.) (−1 mark) > 4. **Diagram clarity:** Use clear box-and-arrow diagrams. Do not draw "wavy lines" for linked allocation — show **distinct blocks with labeled pointers**. Ambiguous diagrams lose up to 2 marks. > 5. **Ignoring the question's specified values:** If the question says $B = 1024$ bytes, you must use 1024 in all calculations, not 4096. (−2 marks) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Types of File Allocation** - ☐ **Three types:** Contiguous, Linked, Indexed. (Always list all three in any "explain the types" question.) - ☐ **Contiguous** = adjacent blocks; directory = `{start, length}`; **1 seek** random access; **suffers external fragmentation**. - ☐ **Linked** = scattered blocks chained by pointers; directory = `{start}`; **k seeks** random access; **no external fragmentation**; **pointer overhead**; FAT variant uses a central table. - ☐ **Indexed** = index block of pointers; directory = `{index_ptr}`; **2 seeks** random access; **no external fragmentation**; max file size = $B^2/S$. - ☐ **Max file size derivation:** $P = B/S$ → $\text{Max} = P \times B = B^2/S$. For $B=4096, S=4$ → **4 MB** (single-level). - ☐ **UNIX inode** = 10 direct + 1 single + 1 double + 1 triple indirect → **effective max ≈ 4 TB** for $B=4096, S=4$. - ☐ **External vs Internal Fragmentation:** Contiguous = external; Indexed (small files) = internal; Linked = neither (but pointer waste). - ☐ **Real-world mapping:** CD/DVD = Contiguous; FAT32 = Linked; ext4/NTFS/APFS = Indexed (multi-level). - ☐ **Average seeks in linked allocation** = $n/2$ for the *k*-th block of an *n*-block file. - ☐ **Address of *k*-th block (contiguous):** $\text{Start} + (k - 1)$ — must show this in any "show the address calculation" question. - ☐ **Draw diagrams for every method** — KTU awards up to 2 marks for clear block-and-pointer diagrams. - ☐ **Always justify the choice** when a question asks "which is best for …" — name the engineering trade-off (e.g., random access speed, fragmentation tolerance, file size). - ☐ **Combined schemes** overcome the 4 MB single-level index limit — know all three (linked index, multilevel, combined UNIX inode).

topics.title: Structure - Access Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Structure - Access Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a File?

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme Operating Systems syllabus (Module 4: File Systems)**, a **file** is a named collection of related information that is recorded on secondary storage (such as magnetic disk, SSD, or optical media). From the operating system's perspective, a file is the smallest logical unit of secondary storage allocation. A file is identified by its **name**, **location**, and **attributes** (metadata such as size, type, creation time, permissions, and owner).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Silberschatz-Galvin-Gagne Standard):**
> "A file is a logical container defined by the file system abstraction, representing a sequence of bytes, records, or other structured information that the OS treats as a single, addressable entity for storage, retrieval, and manipulation."

## 1.2 What is a File Access Method?

A **File Access Method** is the technique or mechanism by which an operating system (and the application programs running on it) reads from and writes to the records stored within a file. The access method defines the **logical order in which records of a file can be processed** and is dictated by both the **file structure** and the **storage device characteristics**.

The three classical access methods specified in the KTU syllabus are:

1. **Sequential Access**
2. **Direct Access** (also called Random Access or Relative Access)
3. **Indexed Access**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The syllabus explicitly categorizes file concepts into:
> - **File Structure** — Internal organization (Byte stream, Record stream, Tree)
> - **Access Methods** — *How* the records within a file are accessed
> These two are often confused but are distinct concepts. *Structure* = internal layout; *Access Method* = pattern of retrieval.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **library**:

- **Sequential Access** is like watching a movie on a **VHS tape or a playlist** — you must read the file from the beginning and play through the records in order to reach a particular one. To get to minute 45, you must fast-forward through minutes 1 to 44.

- **Direct Access** is like a **book with chapter tabs** — you can flip directly to Chapter 7 without reading Chapters 1–6. The OS calculates the physical location (like a chapter) of the desired record and jumps to it.

- **Indexed Access** is like a **book's index at the back** — you look up a topic in the index page, which gives you the page number, and then you jump directly to that page. The index is itself a small, fast-searchable structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Examiners frequently award 2 marks in 3-mark questions for clearly distinguishing these three with analogies. A well-drawn analogy is often the difference between full and partial marks.

## 1.4 Standard Physical Metrics (Bold for Memorization)

The following constants and metrics are essential for solving KTU numerical problems on file systems:

- **Block Size (B)** — Fixed-size logical unit of disk transfer, typically **512 bytes, 1 KB, 4 KB, or 8 KB**.
- **Record Size (R)** — Logical size of one application-level record, measured in **bytes**.
- **Records per Block (RPB)** = floor(B / R), dimensionless.
- **Seek Time (Ts)** — Time to position the read/write head over the correct track, typically **3–10 ms** for HDDs.
- **Rotational Latency (Tr)** — Time for the desired sector to rotate under the head, typically **4–8 ms** (half a rotation for 7200 RPM disks).
- **Transfer Time (Tt)** — Time to actually move bytes from disk to memory, **0.05–0.5 ms** per block.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Access Methods — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Sequential Access

**Operational Logic:**
- The file is processed as an ordered sequence of records.
- A single **read pointer** advances automatically after every read.
- A `read_next()` operation reads the current record and advances the pointer.
- A `reset()` operation rewinds the pointer to the beginning of the file.
- A `write_next()` operation appends at the current pointer location.
- **No arbitrary jumps are possible** (in pure sequential access).

**Why it is used:**
- Simple to implement in both hardware and software.
- Excellent for **batch processing** (e.g., payroll runs, log file scanning).
- Optimal for **magnetic tape** storage, which was the original use case.
- Lowest overhead per access since no index or address computation is needed.

**How it works internally:**
The OS maintains a current file position pointer (often called the **file offset** or **file position**). Each I/O system call increments this pointer by exactly one record length. The kernel translates the logical record number to a physical disk block using:

$$\text{Block Number} = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{Record Number} \times R}{B} \right\rfloor$$

and a byte offset within the block:

$$\text{Offset in Block} = (\text{Record Number} \times R) \mod B$$

---

### 2.1.2 Direct Access (Random Access)

**Operational Logic:**
- The file is viewed as a numbered sequence of **logical blocks** (or records) indexed from 0 to N−1.
- A `read(n)` operation jumps directly to the nth record/block **without traversing records 0 to n−1**.
- A `write(n, data)` operation overwrites or appends at position n.
- A `seek(n)` operation positions the read pointer at record n.
- **No implicit pointer movement** — the application explicitly specifies the position.

**Why it is used:**
- Essential for **databases, hash tables, transaction systems** where any record may be needed instantly.
- Required for **interactive systems** where response time is critical.
- Maps naturally to **disk storage**, where the seek-rotate-transfer model allows approximate random access.

**How it works internally:**
For a direct-access file of size *S* bytes divided into blocks of size *B* bytes, the user-visible logical record *i* maps to a physical disk block as:

$$P(i) = \left\lfloor \frac{i \times R}{B} \right\rfloor$$

where *P(i)* is the physical block number. The OS uses the disk's **cylinder-track-sector geometry** (or **LBA — Logical Block Addressing** on modern drives) to compute the actual (cylinder, head, sector) tuple.

> [!NOTE]
> **Direct Access does NOT mean "instantaneous access" in terms of latency.** The disk head must still physically seek to the correct track. It is "direct" only in the *logical* sense — the OS does not need to read intervening records.

---

### 2.1.3 Indexed Access

**Operational Logic:**
- A separate **index file** is maintained alongside the main data file.
- Each entry in the index contains a **search key** and a **pointer** (block number) to the corresponding data record.
- To access a record, the OS first searches the index for the key, obtains the pointer, then performs a direct read on the data file.
- The index itself can be **single-level, multi-level (hierarchical), or tree-based (B+ tree)**.

**Why it is used:**
- Supports **fast key-based retrieval** without scanning the entire data file.
- The index is much smaller than the data file, so it can be kept (or partially kept) in main memory.
- Crucial for **large databases** and **file systems** (e.g., NTFS MFT, ext4 inode tables use indexed concepts).

**How it works internally:**
Given a key *K* to search for, the OS performs:

1. Binary search (if index is sorted) over the index blocks to locate the entry for *K*.
2. The entry returns a physical block number *P* in the data file.
3. A direct read is performed on block *P* of the data file.

The number of index entries is:

$$N_{\text{index}} = \left\lceil \frac{S_{\text{data}}}{R_{\text{data}} \cdot \text{Fan Out}} \right\rceil$$

where *Fan Out* is the number of data records pointed to by one index entry.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).** Every numerical and short-answer question on Module 4 file access methods is solvable using these formulas.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables | Units |
|---|---------|----------------------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Block containing record *i* | $\left\lfloor \frac{i \cdot R}{B} \right\rfloor$ | $i$ = record index, $R$ = record size, $B$ = block size | dimensionless |
| 2 | Byte offset inside the block | $(i \cdot R) \mod B$ | same as above | bytes |
| 3 | Records per block (RPB) | $\left\lfloor \frac{B}{R} \right\rfloor$ | $B$, $R$ | records/block |
| 4 | Total blocks for a file | $\left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil$ | $S$ = file size in bytes | blocks |
| 5 | Total file access time (sequential) | $n \cdot (T_s + T_r + T_t)$ | $n$ = number of records | milliseconds |
| 6 | Random access time (single block) | $T_s + T_r + T_t$ | typical HDD: **3 + 4 + 0.1 ≈ 7.1 ms** | milliseconds |
| 7 | Number of index entries | $\left\lceil \frac{N_{\text{records}}}{k} \right\rceil$ | $k$ = fan-out (records/index entry) | entries |
| 8 | Indexed access time (one record) | $T_{\text{index search}} + T_{\text{data fetch}}$ | sum of two direct accesses | milliseconds |
| 9 | Index file size | $N_{\text{index}} \times (\text{Key Size} + \text{Pointer Size})$ | $N_{\text{index}}$ from formula 7 | bytes |
| 10 | Effective access time (binary search over index) | $\log_2(N_{\text{index}}) \cdot T_{\text{block}} + T_{\text{data}}$ | $T_{\text{block}}$ = one block access time | milliseconds |

> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> These formulas are used in:
> - **Database Management Systems (DBMS)** — B+ tree fan-out and height calculations.
> - **Storage Engineering** — RAID array sizing, SSD wear-leveling algorithms.
> - **Compiler Design** — Symbol table indexing.
> - **Network File Systems (NFS, CIFS)** — Block allocation and access optimization.
> - **Embedded Systems** — Flash Translation Layer (FTL) for NAND flash access.

---

## 2.3 Choosing the Right Access Method (Engineering Decision Matrix)

| Criterion | Sequential | Direct | Indexed |
|-----------|------------|--------|---------|
| Implementation Complexity | **Lowest** | Medium | **Highest** |
| Storage Overhead | **None** | None | Significant (index file) |
| Best for | Batch jobs, logs, tape | Databases, real-time | Large databases, key-search |
| Read Time (record *i*) | $O(n)$ — must read *i* records | $O(1)$ — direct jump | $O(\log N_{\text{index}}) + O(1)$ |
| Write Time (record *i*) | $O(1)$ if at end, else $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(\log N_{\text{index}}) + O(1)$ |
| Hardware Suitability | Magnetic tape, SSD stream | HDD, SSD | HDD with large files |
| Update Friendliness | Poor (overwrites cascade) | Good | **Best** (with B+ tree) |
| KTU Exam Frequency | Very High | Very High | **Very High** |
