# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum File Size in UNIX Inode Scheme

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A UNIX file system uses block size $S_b = 1 \text{ KB}$ (1024 bytes) and 32-bit (4-byte) disk block pointers. The inode contains 12 direct pointers, 1 single-indirect, 1 double-indirect, and 1 triple-indirect pointer. Compute the **maximum possible file size**.

### Step 1 — Compute the number of pointers per block ($P$)

$$
P = \frac{S_{block}}{S_{pointer}} = \frac{1024 \text{ bytes}}{4 \text{ bytes}} = 256 \text{ pointers/block}
$$

**Explanation:** A single index block is 1024 bytes wide. Each pointer consumes 4 bytes. Therefore, the block can hold exactly $1024 / 4 = 256$ pointers.

### Step 2 — Direct blocks contribution

The 12 direct pointers each address **1 data block** of 1 KB:

$$
L_{direct} = 12 \times S_{block} = 12 \times 1 \text{ KB} = 12 \text{ KB}
$$

**Explanation:** No indirection overhead — these pointers directly name data blocks.

### Step 3 — Single-indirect contribution

The single-indirect block contains $P = 256$ pointers, each naming a data block:

$$
L_{single} = P \times S_{block} = 256 \times 1 \text{ KB} = 256 \text{ KB}
$$

**Explanation:** One level of indirection → one index block names 256 data blocks.

### Step 4 — Double-indirect contribution

A double-indirect block points to $P$ index blocks, each of which points to $P$ data blocks:

$$
L_{double} = P^{2} \times S_{block} = 256^{2} \times 1 \text{ KB} = 65{,}536 \times 1 \text{ KB} = 64 \text{ MB}
$$

**Explanation:** Two levels of indirection → $P \times P = P^{2} = 65{,}536$ data blocks addressable, totalling 64 MB.

### Step 5 — Triple-indirect contribution

Three levels of indirection:

$$
L_{triple} = P^{3} \times S_{block} = 256^{3} \times 1 \text{ KB} = 16{,}777{,}216 \times 1 \text{ KB} = 16 \text{ GB}
$$

**Explanation:** $256^{3} = 16{,}777{,}216$ data blocks addressable.

### Step 6 — Total maximum file size

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{max} &= L_{direct} + L_{single} + L_{double} + L_{triple} \\
&= 12 \text{ KB} + 256 \text{ KB} + 64 \text{ MB} + 16 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to a common unit (KB):

$$
L_{max} = 12 + 256 + 65{,}536 + 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ KB}
$$

$$
L_{max} = 16{,}843{,}020 \text{ KB}
$$

Converting to GB:

$$
L_{max} = \frac{16{,}843{,}020}{1024 \times 1024} \text{ GB} \approx 16.0625 \text{ GB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $L_{max} \approx 16.0625 \text{ GB}$ (or exactly $16 \text{ GB} + 12 \text{ KB} + 256 \text{ KB} + 64 \text{ MB}$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum File Size with 64-bit Pointers

**Modified Problem:** Same as above, but the system uses 64-bit (8-byte) pointers and 4 KB blocks.

### Step 1 — Compute $P$

$$
P = \frac{4 \text{ KB}}{8 \text{ B}} = \frac{4096}{8} = 512 \text{ pointers/block}
$$

### Step 2 — Total data blocks addressable

$$
N_{max} = 12 + 512 + 512^{2} + 512^{3}
$$

$$
512^{2} = 262{,}144
$$

$$
512^{3} = 134{,}217{,}728
$$

$$
N_{max} = 12 + 512 + 262{,}144 + 134{,}217{,}728 = 134{,}480{,}396 \text{ blocks}
$$

### Step 3 — Maximum size

$$
L_{max} = 134{,}480{,}396 \times 4 \text{ KB} = 537{,}921{,}584 \text{ KB}
$$

Converting:

$$
L_{max} \approx \frac{537{,}921{,}584}{1024 \times 1024} \text{ GB} \approx 513.0 \text{ GB}
$$

**Why this matters:** Modern ext4 / NTFS use 64-bit pointers and large blocks, supporting files up to 16 TB or more.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bitmap Storage Requirement

**Problem:** A 1 TB disk uses 4 KB blocks. Compute the size of the bitmap required to track free/allocated blocks.

### Step 1 — Total number of blocks

$$
N_{blocks} = \frac{D}{S_{block}} = \frac{1 \text{ TB}}{4 \text{ KB}} = \frac{2^{40}}{2^{12}} = 2^{28} = 268{,}435{,}456 \text{ blocks}
$$

### Step 2 — Bits required for the bitmap

$$
B_{bits} = N_{blocks} = 268{,}435{,}456 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to bytes

$$
B_{bytes} = \frac{268{,}435{,}456}{8} = 33{,}554{,}432 \text{ bytes} = 32 \text{ MB}
$$

### Step 4 — Number of 4 KB blocks needed to store the bitmap

$$
N_{bitmap\_blocks} = \frac{33{,}554{,}432}{4096} = 8192 \text{ blocks} = 8 \text{ KB blocks per 1 TB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The bitmap consumes only $\frac{1}{N_{blocks}}$ fraction of the disk — extremely efficient. This is one reason why bitmaps are the preferred free-space management technique in modern FS.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — External Fragmentation in Contiguous Allocation

**Problem:** A disk has the following free regions: 30 KB, 25 KB, 15 KB, 10 KB. A request arrives for **35 KB**. Compute external fragmentation and explain what happens.

### Step 1 — Identify the largest free chunk

$$
\text{Largest free} = 30 \text{ KB}
$$

### Step 2 — Total free space

$$
F_{total} = 30 + 25 + 15 + 10 = 80 \text{ KB}
$$

### Step 3 — External fragmentation ratio

$$
Frag_{ext} = 1 - \frac{30}{80} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625 \; (62.5\%)
$$

**Explanation:** Even though 80 KB is free, the request for 35 KB **fails** because no single contiguous region is large enough. The free space is unusable for this request → external fragmentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap Question:** "Total free space is 80 KB and request is 35 KB — will the request succeed under *contiguous* allocation?" The correct answer is **NO**, because of fragmentation. This is the single most common conceptual error.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Linked Allocation Access Time

**Problem:** A file occupies $n = 1000$ blocks. Average seek + rotation latency per block is $t_{s} = 8$ ms. Transfer time per block is $t_{t} = 0.1$ ms. Compute the time to read the **last** block of the file from start.

### Step 1 — Time to traverse $n - 1$ pointers (sequential)

$$
T_{traverse} = (n - 1) \times (t_{s} + t_{t}) = 999 \times (8 + 0.1) = 999 \times 8.1
$$

$$
T_{traverse} = 8091.9 \text{ ms} \approx 8.09 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 2 — Time to read the last block

$$
T_{last} = t_{s} + t_{t} = 8.1 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 3 — Total access time

$$
T_{total} = T_{traverse} + T_{last} = 8091.9 + 8.1 = 8100 \text{ ms} = 8.1 \text{ seconds}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison with Indexed Allocation:** For indexed allocation, the time to access the last block is:
> $$T_{indexed} = t_{s}(\text{read index block}) + t_{s}(\text{read data block}) + t_{t} = 2 \times 8 + 0.1 = 16.1 \text{ ms}$$
> Indexed allocation is **~500× faster** for direct access in this example.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Pros/Cons at a Glance

| Allocation Method | External Frag. | Direct Access | Sequential Access | File Size Limit | Reliability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Contiguous** | High (severe) | Excellent O(1) | Excellent | Known at creation | High |
| **Linked** | None | Poor O(n) | Good | Disk size | Low (bad pointer) |
| **FAT (Linked variant)** | None | Good via table | Good | Disk size | Medium (table backup) |
| **Indexed (single-level)** | None | Excellent O(1) | Good | $P \times S_{block}$ | High |
| **Indexed (multi-level)** | None | Excellent O(1) amortized | Good | Very large (TBs) | High |

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Directory Implementation

| Method | Lookup Time | Insertion Time | Deletion Time | Space Overhead |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear List** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ at end | $O(n)$ to find, $O(1)$ to remove | Low |
| **Sorted Linear List** | $O(\log n)$ via binary search | $O(n)$ to shift | $O(n)$ | Low |
| **Hash Table** | $O(1)$ average; $O(n)$ worst | $O(1)$ average | $O(1)$ average | Higher |

---

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Free-Space Management

| Technique | Find Free Block | Allocation Cost | Space Overhead | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bit Vector** | $O(1)$ with HW find-first-bit | $O(1)$ | 1 bit per block | General purpose (ext2/3/4) |
| **Linked List** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Pointer per free block | Tiny disks |
| **Grouping** | $O(1)$ per group of $n$ | $O(1)$ | 1 pointer per $n$ blocks | Variable workloads |
| **Counting** | $O(\text{number of runs})$ | $O(1)$ | 2 numbers per run | Contiguous-alloc FS |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 On-Disk File System Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["BOOT BLOCK VOL 0"] --> B["SUPERBLOCK FS METADATA"]
    B --> C["FREE SPACE MGMT BITMAP OR FAT"]
    C --> D["INODE TABLE PER FILE METADATA"]
    D --> E["ROOT DIRECTORY ANCHOR"]
    E --> F1["DATA BLOCKS FILE 1"]
    E --> F2["DATA BLOCKS FILE 2"]
    E --> F3["DATA BLOCKS FILE 3"]
    E --> F4["DATA BLOCKS FILE N"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** A typical UNIX-style file system partition is laid out linearly on disk. The first block (Block 0) is the boot block containing the bootstrap code. The superblock follows, holding critical FS parameters. The free-space manager (bitmap or FAT) tracks allocation. The inode table contains one inode per file. The root directory anchors the hierarchy, and the remaining blocks store actual file data.

## 4.2 UNIX Inode Hybrid Scheme

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["INODE STRUCTURE"] --> D0["DIRECT POINTER 0"]
    IN --> D1["DIRECT POINTER 1"]
    IN --> D2["DIRECT POINTER 2"]
    IN --> D3["DIRECT POINTER 3"]
    IN --> D4["DIRECT POINTER 4"]
    IN --> D5["DIRECT POINTER 5"]
    IN --> D6["DIRECT POINTER 6"]
    IN --> D7["DIRECT POINTER 7"]
    IN --> D8["DIRECT POINTER 8"]
    IN --> D9["DIRECT POINTER 9"]
    IN --> D10["DIRECT POINTER 10"]
    IN --> D11["DIRECT POINTER 11"]
    IN --> SI["SINGLE INDIRECT POINTER"]
    IN --> DI["DOUBLE INDIRECT POINTER"]
    IN --> TI["TRIPLE INDIRECT POINTER"]

    SI --> IB1["INDEX BLOCK 1"]
    IB1 --> DB1["DATA BLOCK A"]
    IB1 --> DB2["DATA BLOCK B"]
    IB1 --> DB3["DATA BLOCK C"]
    IB1 --> DBN["DATA BLOCK N"]

    DI --> IB2["INDEX BLOCK 2"]
    IB2 --> IB3["INDEX BLOCK 3"]
    IB2 --> IB4["INDEX BLOCK 4"]
    IB3 --> DBB1["DATA BLOCK X"]
    IB3 --> DBB2["DATA BLOCK Y"]

    TI --> IT1["INDEX BLOCK T1"]
    IT1 --> IT2["INDEX BLOCK T2"]
    IT2 --> IT3["INDEX BLOCK T3"]
    IT3 --> DBT1["DATA BLOCK Z"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The inode starts with 12 direct pointers. The single-indirect pointer leads to an index block that points to many data blocks. The double-indirect pointer leads to an index of indices, and the triple-indirect pointer goes three levels deep. This hierarchy allows very large files while keeping small files fast (most accesses go through direct pointers).

## 4.3 File-System Architecture (VFS Layer)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP["USER APPLICATION OPEN READ WRITE CLOSE"] --> SYSCALL["SYSTEM CALL INTERFACE"]

    SYSCALL --> VFS["VIRTUAL FILE SYSTEM VFS LAYER"]

    VFS --> EXT4["EXT4 FS DRIVER"]
    VFS --> NTFS["NTFS FS DRIVER"]
    VFS --> FAT["FAT32 FS DRIVER"]
    VFS --> NFS["NETWORK FS NFS"]

    EXT4 --> DC1["DISK CONTROLLER 1"]
    NTFS --> DC2["DISK CONTROLLER 2"]
    FAT --> DC3["USB CONTROLLER"]
    NFS --> NET["NETWORK STACK"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Applications invoke generic POSIX system calls. The VFS layer intercepts these calls and dispatches them to the appropriate concrete file-system driver. Each driver then talks to its respective storage controller. This indirection is what allows Linux/Windows to mount heterogeneous file systems transparently.

## 4.4 Allocation Methods — Comparative Block Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CONTIGUOUS["CONTIGUOUS ALLOCATION"]
        direction LR
        C1["BLOCK 10 FILE A"] --> C2["BLOCK 11 FILE A"] --> C3["BLOCK 12 FILE A"] --> C4["BLOCK 13 FILE A"]
    end

    subgraph LINKED["LINKED ALLOCATION"]
        direction LR
        L1["BLOCK 5 FILE B"] -->|NEXT| L2["BLOCK 9 FILE B"] -->|NEXT| L3["BLOCK 2 FILE B"] -->|NEXT| L4["BLOCK 17 FILE B"]
    end

    subgraph INDEXED["INDEXED ALLOCATION"]
        direction TB
        IB["INDEX BLOCK POINTERS 100 250 7 999"]
        IB --> ID1["BLOCK 100 FILE C"]
        IB --> ID2["BLOCK 250 FILE C"]
        IB --> ID3["BLOCK 7 FILE C"]
        IB --> ID4["BLOCK 999 FILE C"]
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:** Contiguous allocation places all blocks of a file side-by-side. Linked allocation scatters them across the disk and chains them with pointers. Indexed allocation uses a single index block as a lookup table to find scattered data blocks.

## 4.5 Journaling / Log-Structured File System Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX["TRANSACTION START"] --> WJ["WRITE INTENT TO JOURNAL"]
    WJ --> WD["WRITE DATA TO DISK"]
    WD --> WM["WRITE METADATA TO DISK"]
    WM --> CM["COMMIT JOURNAL ENTRY"]
    CM --> CR["ON CRASH: REPLAY JOURNAL"]
    CR --> AP["APPLY COMPLETED TXS"]
    AP --> DD["DISCARD INCOMPLETE TXS"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** A journaling file system writes the *intent* of a transaction to a log *before* modifying actual data. If a crash occurs, the recovery process replays committed transactions and discards incomplete ones — guaranteeing consistency in milliseconds rather than hours.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between contiguous allocation and linked allocation of disk space.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Contiguous Allocation | Linked Allocation |
|---|---|---|
| **Block Placement** | Sequential / adjacent on disk | Scattered, chained via pointers |
| **Directory Entry** | Stores starting block + length | Stores only starting block |
| **External Fragmentation** | Yes — severe | No |
| **Direct/Random Access** | Yes — O(1) | No — must traverse chain O(n) |
| **File Growth** | Difficult (relocation) | Easy (allocate & link) |
| **Reliability** | High | Low (one bad pointer → file lost) |
| **Example Use** | CD-ROM, ISO 9660 | FAT (variant), early UNIX |

**Key memory line for 3 marks:** Mention starting block, pointer mechanism, fragmentation status, and access method. (3 marks are awarded for clarity and coverage.)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**What is a bitmap in the context of free-space management? Why is it preferred over a linked list?**

**Model Answer:**

A **bitmap** (or bit vector) is a sequence of bits, one per disk block, where each bit indicates whether the corresponding block is free (0) or allocated (1).

**Why preferred over linked list:**

1. **Speed of finding a free block**: With hardware *find-first-zero* instruction, finding the first free block is **O(1) to O(n/sizeof(word))**. Linked list requires **O(n)** traversal of the entire chain.
2. **Reliability**: A single corrupted pointer in a linked list of free blocks can orphan many blocks. The bitmap has no such vulnerability.
3. **Random access to any block's status**: Constant time. Linked list gives only sequential access.
4. **Space efficiency**: 1 bit per block, regardless of disk size.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Valuation):** Many students write "bitmap is faster" but fail to specify *why* (HW instruction support). The hardware find-first-bit instruction is the key phrase. **−1 mark** if missing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the on-disk structure of a UNIX file system with a neat diagram. List the contents of the superblock and inode.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A UNIX file system uses 1 KB block size and 32-bit (4-byte) disk block pointers. An inode has 10 direct pointers, 1 single-indirect, 1 double-indirect, and 1 triple-indirect pointer. Calculate the maximum file size supported.

---

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**On-Disk Structure (write in order from low to high block address):**

1. **Boot Block (Block 0)**: Contains the bootstrap code to load the OS. Present even on non-bootable partitions for uniformity.
2. **Superblock (Block 1)**: The most critical metadata block. Contains:
   - FS size and block size
   - Free block count
   - Free inode count
   - Inode table location
   - Magic number (FS type)
   - Mount count, maximum mount count
   - State flag (clean, errors)
3. **Free-Space Management Block(s)**: Either a **bitmap** or a **FAT** tracking free blocks.
4. **Inode Table**: An array of inodes, one per file. Each inode contains:
   - File type and permissions (mode)
   - Owner ID, group ID
   - File size, timestamps (creation, modification, access)
   - Link count
   - 15 block pointers (12 direct + 1 single-indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect)
5. **Root Directory**: An inode pointing to data blocks that contain directory entries.
6. **Data Blocks**: The remaining disk space where actual file contents are stored.

**Diagram (write in answer):**

```
| Boot Block | Superblock | Free-Space Mgmt | Inode Table | Root Dir | Data Blocks ... |
```

**Mark Distribution for A(a):**
- Structure order & description: 3 marks
- Superblock contents: 2 marks
- Inode contents: 1 mark
- Diagram: 1 mark

---

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Given:**
- $S_{block} = 1 \text{ KB} = 1024 \text{ bytes}$
- $S_{pointer} = 4 \text{ bytes}$
- Direct pointers = 10, Single-indirect = 1, Double-indirect = 1, Triple-indirect = 1

**Step 1 — Pointers per block ($P$):**

$$
P = \frac{1024}{4} = 256 \text{ pointers/block}
$$

**Step 2 — Direct blocks contribution:**

$$
L_{direct} = 10 \times 1 \text{ KB} = 10 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 3 — Single-indirect contribution:**

$$
L_{single} = P \times S_{block} = 256 \times 1 \text{ KB} = 256 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 4 — Double-indirect contribution:**

$$
L_{double} = P^{2} \times S_{block} = 256^{2} \times 1 \text{ KB} = 65{,}536 \text{ KB} = 64 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 5 — Triple-indirect contribution:**

$$
L_{triple} = P^{3} \times S_{block} = 256^{3} \times 1 \text{ KB} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ KB} = 16 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 6 — Total maximum file size:**

$$
L_{max} = 10 \text{ KB} + 256 \text{ KB} + 64 \text{ MB} + 16 \text{ GB}
$$

Converting all to GB:

$$
L_{max} = 16 \text{ GB} + \frac{64}{1024} \text{ GB} + \frac{256}{1024 \times 1024} \text{ GB} + \frac{10}{1024 \times 1024} \text{ GB}
$$

$$
L_{max} \approx 16.0627 \text{ GB}
$$

**Mark Distribution for A(b):**
- Correct $P$ calculation: 1 mark
- Direct contribution: 1 mark
- Single-indirect: 1 mark
- Double-indirect: 1 mark
- Triple-indirect: 1 mark
- Final summation with correct unit: 1 mark
- Working presentation: 1 mark

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the three disk allocation methods: contiguous, linked, and indexed. Use a comparison table and explain the role of FAT as a variation of linked allocation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of journaling file systems. Differentiate between the data, ordered, and full journaling modes.

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Comparison Table (write in answer):**

| Parameter | Contiguous | Linked | Indexed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Disk block layout | Adjacent | Scattered (chained) | Scattered (indexed) |
| Directory entry | Start + length | Start only | Index block pointer |
| External fragmentation | High | None | None |
| Internal fragmentation | Low | Pointer per block (small) | Index block per file (small) |
| Random access | O(1) | O(n) | O(1) (with index in mem) |
| Sequential access | Excellent | Good | Good |
| File size flexibility | Limited at creation | Unlimited | Limited by index size |
| Reliability | High | Low (bad pointer) | High |

**Role of FAT (File Allocation Table):**

- A **FAT** is a centralized linked-list implementation. Instead of storing the *next* pointer inside each data block, the pointers are stored in a separate **table** in memory (and mirrored on disk).
- The directory entry points to the **first block** of the file; the FAT entry for that block contains the index of the next block, and so on. A special value (e.g., `0xFFFF`) marks the end of the file.
- **Advantages over plain linked allocation:**
  1. The per-block pointer overhead in the data block is eliminated → the entire block can hold file data.
  2. Random access becomes possible: to reach block $k$, the OS reads the FAT entries 0, 1, ..., $k$ — still $O(n)$ in worst case but the FAT is **cached in memory** and reads are extremely fast.
  3. The FAT can be backed up to a second location for reliability (FAT32 stores two copies).
- FAT12, FAT16, FAT32 differ in the size of the block pointer (12, 16, 32 bits).

**Mark Distribution for B(a):**
- Comparison table covering at least 6 parameters: 4 marks
- FAT explanation (table-based, in-memory caching, double copy): 2 marks
- Example/variant (FAT12/16/32): 1 mark

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Working of Journaling File Systems:**

A journaling file system maintains a **log (journal)** on disk that records metadata changes *before* they are applied. The procedure for any metadata-modifying operation:

1. **Tx Begin**: Mark the start of a transaction in the journal.
2. **Log Metadata Changes**: Write the *intended* changes to the journal.
3. **Flush Journal**: Force the journal entries to disk (synchronous).
4. **Apply to FS**: Update the actual FS metadata.
5. **Tx Commit**: Mark the transaction as completed in the journal.
6. **Discard Journal**: Free journal space for reuse.

**On Crash Recovery:**

- On reboot, the recovery process scans the journal.
- **Committed transactions** are *replayed* (their effects are guaranteed to be either fully applied or fully re-applied).
- **Uncommitted transactions** are *discarded*.
- This guarantees **atomicity** and **consistency** in **seconds**, even after a mid-transaction crash.

**Three Journaling Modes:**

| Mode | What's Logged | Performance | Safety |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Write-back (Journal)** | Metadata only | Fastest | Lowest (data may be lost) |
| **Ordered (Default in ext3)** | Metadata only; data flushed **before** metadata | Medium | Good (data guaranteed before metadata) |
| **Full (Data=journal)** | Both data and metadata | Slowest | Highest (no data loss) |

- **Write-back**: Metadata in journal; file data written directly to disk. Risk: data blocks may belong to a different file after recovery.
- **Ordered**: Ensures file data is written *before* the corresponding metadata is committed. This guarantees that the metadata never points to garbage data. Default in ext3.
- **Full**: Both file data and metadata are journaled. The most robust — supports atomic recovery of file contents.

**Mark Distribution for B(b):**
- 5-step journaling procedure: 3 marks
- Crash recovery explanation: 2 marks
- Three modes with at least 1 example each: 2 marks

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in File-System Implementation Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the inode pointer count.** The standard UNIX inode has **15** pointers (12 direct + 3 indirect). Many variants exist (ext4 uses a different layout). Always re-read the question carefully.
> 2. **Mixing up $P$ vs $P^2$ vs $P^3$.** In a double-indirect scheme, the data blocks are $P^2$, *not* $P \times 2$. The exponent equals the *depth of indirection*.
> 3. **Forgetting the index blocks themselves are disk blocks.** The single-indirect block, double-indirect blocks, etc. consume disk space and must be allocated.
> 4. **Wrong unit conversion.** $1 \text{ MB} = 1024 \text{ KB}$ (not 1000). $1 \text{ GB} = 1024 \text{ MB}$. KTU uses **binary** convention.
> 5. **Omitting the bitmap/FAT location.** When asked about on-disk structure, students often forget to mention the free-space management area. **−1 mark** typical.
> 6. **No diagram for 7-mark allocation questions.** Always include a block-level diagram; it secures at least **1 mark** in valuation.
> 7. **Writing "VFS is a file system"** — it is **not** a file system; it is an *abstraction layer* that sits *above* concrete file systems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **On-disk structure** (in order): Boot Block → Superblock → Free-Space Manager → Inode Table → Root Directory → Data Blocks.
- **Superblock** holds the FS's vital metadata. Losing the superblock is catastrophic.
- **Inode** is per-file metadata; does not store filename (which lives in the directory).
- **VFS** is an abstraction layer, not a file system; it provides a uniform API via the `vnode`/`inode` object.
- **Mounting** attaches a partition's FS to the global directory tree at a *mount point*.
- **Contiguous allocation**: Best for sequential access; suffers external fragmentation; needs file size known a-priori.
- **Linked allocation**: No external fragmentation; no direct access; FAT variant fixes the per-block pointer problem.
- **Indexed allocation**: Supports direct access; multi-level indexing handles large files; ext2/3/4 and NTFS use variations of this.
- **UNIX inode max file size formula** (with 12 direct pointers, 32-bit ptrs, 1 KB blocks): $L_{max} = (12 + 256 + 256^{2} + 256^{3}) \text{ KB} \approx 16.06 \text{ GB}$.
- **Bitmap** is the modern free-space management technique of choice (1 bit per block, $O(1)$ free block find with HW).
- **Directory implementation**: Linear list = $O(n)$; Hash table = $O(1)$ average.
- **Journaling** guarantees atomicity and quick recovery; modes are *write-back* (fastest, unsafe), *ordered* (default, safe for data), and *full* (safest, slowest).
- **fsck** (UNIX) and **chkdsk** (Windows) are consistency checkers run on non-journaling or corrupted journaling FS.
- **Buffer cache / page cache** is critical for performance; it is in-memory and volatile.
- **Open-file table** exists in two levels: *system-wide* and *per-process*; the per-process table contains a pointer (file descriptor) to the system-wide entry.
- **Key formulas to memorize**: $P = S_{block}/S_{pointer}$, $L_{max} = (12 + P + P^{2} + P^{3}) \times S_{block}$, $Frag_{ext} = 1 - (\text{largest free})/(\text{total free})$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Directory Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Directory Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Directory Implementation** refers to the data structures and algorithms used by the operating system to organize, store, retrieve, and maintain the mapping between **human-readable file names** and their corresponding **low-level identifiers (inodes / disk addresses / FCB pointers)**. It defines *how* a directory entry is physically and logically laid out on secondary storage so that file lookup, insertion, deletion, and renaming can be performed efficiently.

In a File System, a **directory** is itself a special file (often called a *directory file* or *catalog file*) whose contents are a collection of **directory entries**. Each entry contains at minimum:
- The **file name** (variable length, human-readable)
- A pointer/reference to the **File Control Block (FCB)** or **inode number**
- Optional attributes: file type, protection bits, size, timestamps

The **implementation scheme** decides:
1. The **storage layout** of these entries (linear, hashed, hierarchical).
2. The **lookup complexity** (worst-case and average).
3. The **disk access cost** to locate a file given its name.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Library Analogy:** Imagine a huge library with millions of books. The "directory" is the *card catalog* (or the modern database) that lets you find a book from its title. Now, imagine two designs of that catalog:
>
> - **Linear list** → you walk through every card one-by-one until you find the title (slow for large libraries).
> - **Hash table** → you use a magic formula on the title to jump directly to a specific drawer (very fast, but if two titles hash to the same drawer, there's a small collision).
> - **Tree (B-Tree)** → the cards are organized like a phone book, alphabetically in a balanced tree, so you can find anything in only a few page-flips.
>
> Choosing a directory scheme is exactly this trade-off: **search speed vs. memory overhead vs. disk I/O cost**.

### Key Metrics & Standard Terms

- **Search Time** measured in *disk accesses* (each access ≈ **5–10 ms** for HDD).
- **Directory file size** is often quantified in **blocks** (typically **4 KB** each in modern UNIX-like systems).
- **Hash function collision rate** is given as a percentage.
- The standard **directory entry size** in classic UNIX (v7) is **16 bytes**: *2-byte inode number* + *14-byte file name*.

### Three Standard Schemes Taught in KTU Module 4

| Scheme | Data Structure | Typical Complexity | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear List | Unsorted/Sorted Array | $O(n)$ / $O(\log n)$ | MS-DOS, FAT (early) |
| Hash Table | Hash + Overflow Buckets | $O(1)$ average | Legacy UNIX |
| Tree-Structured | B-Tree / Red-Black Tree | $O(\log n)$ | NTFS, ext2/3/4, ReFS |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Scheme A: Linear List (The Simplest Approach)

**Data Structure:** A straightforward, sequential array of directory entries.

**Operational Steps for `search(name)`:**
1. Read the directory file block-by-block from disk into memory.
2. Linearly scan each entry, comparing `name` field with the target.
3. Return pointer to FCB if matched, else `NULL` (file not found).

**Operational Steps for `create(file, attributes)`:**
1. Search the entire directory for the requested filename.
2. If found → return error (*file already exists*).
3. If not found → locate a free slot (or append at the end).
4. Write the new entry (name + attributes + FCB pointer).

**Operational Steps for `delete(file)`:**
1. Search for the entry.
2. Release the FCB back to free-FCB pool.
3. Either mark entry as free (a *hole*) **or** compact the list by shifting subsequent entries upward.

**Why & How — The Underlying Logic:**

> [!NOTE]
> - **Why is deletion hard?** Because removing entry $k$ creates a "hole" of length $\vert E \vert$ bytes. Two options exist: leave the hole (wastes space) or shift all entries $k+1, k+2, \dots, n$ leftward (wastes CPU + extra disk I/O).
> - **Why is search slow?** Because the directory is on disk; each block read costs a disk seek. For a directory of $n$ entries spread across $b$ blocks, the worst case is $b$ disk accesses.
> - **Sorting the list** converts the search into a binary search ($\log_2 n$) — but insertion/deletion now require maintaining sorted order, which is $O(n)$ for shifting.

**Disadvantages of Linear List:**
- Inefficient for large directories (thousands of files).
- Sorted variant complicates `insert` and `delete`.
- File names of variable length waste space if fixed-size entries are used.

### 2.2 Scheme B: Hash Table (The Speed-Optimized Approach)

**Data Structure:** A hash function $h(name) \rightarrow [0, m-1]$ maps filenames to a fixed-size table; collisions are resolved via **chaining** (linked list of overflow entries) or **open addressing**.

**Operational Steps for `search(name)`:**
1. Compute $h(name) = k$.
2. Access table slot $k$.
3. Walk the collision chain at slot $k$ to find the entry.

**Operational Steps for `create(file, attributes)`:**
1. If $h(name)$ slot occupied → chain a new entry; else create new entry.
2. If chain length exceeds a threshold → directory is full; rebuild with a larger table.

**Collision Resolution — Chaining Visualization:**

$$
\text{slot index} = h(\text{filename}) \bmod m
$$

**Why & How — The Underlying Logic:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Hash function choice** is critical: a poor hash function creates many collisions, degenerating the table into a linear list.
> - **Fixed table size $m$** must be carefully chosen: too small → many collisions; too large → wasted memory and poor cache locality.
> - **Rehashing cost** is non-trivial — when load factor $\alpha = n/m$ exceeds a threshold (commonly $0.75$), the entire table must be rebuilt on a larger $m$.

**Disadvantages of Hash Table:**
- Fixed-size $m$ is a hard limit on directory capacity.
- Collisions degrade performance unpredictably.
- Difficult to enumerate all files in alphabetical order (no inherent ordering).

### 2.3 Scheme C: Tree-Structured (The Scalable Approach)

**Data Structure:** A **balanced search tree** — typically a **B-Tree** (generalized for disk blocks) or **Red-Black Tree** (in-memory).

**Why a B-Tree specifically?** A B-Tree is **disk-optimized**: each node has size ≈ one disk block, minimizing the number of block reads for any lookup.

**Properties of a B-Tree of order $d$:**
- Every node has at most $2d$ keys and $2d+1$ children.
- Every internal node (except root) has at least $d$ keys.
- All leaves appear at the same depth $\Rightarrow$ balanced.
- A node with $k$ keys has exactly $k+1$ children.

**Search complexity:**

$$
T_{\text{search}} = O(\log_d n)
$$

For $n = 1{,}000{,}000$ files and $d = 100$:

$$
T_{\text{search}} \approx \log_{100}(1{,}000{,}000) = 3 \text{ disk accesses}
$$

**Why & How — The Underlying Logic:**

> [!NOTE]
> - **Why not a binary tree?** A binary tree has depth $\log_2 n$ ≈ 20 for 1M files → 20 disk accesses. A B-Tree of order 100 has depth ≈ 3.
> - **Why is the node size = block size?** Because one I/O brings in $d$ keys, and the next comparison decides which child to fetch → exactly **1 I/O per tree level**.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Linear list search (unsorted) | $T_s = n$ | Comparisons |
| Linear list search (sorted, binary) | $T_s = \lceil \log_2(n+1) \rceil$ | Comparisons |
| Hash table average search | $T_s \approx 1 + \alpha$ | Where $\alpha$ = load factor |
| Hash table worst-case search | $T_s = O(n)$ | All keys collide |
| Hash table load factor | $\alpha = n / m$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| B-Tree search | $T_s = O(\log_d n)$ | $d$ = order of tree |
| B-Tree node size | $B \ge (2d+1) \cdot p + (2d) \cdot k$ | $p$ = ptr size, $k$ = key size |
| Disk access cost (HDD) | $\approx \mathbf{5 \text{ to } 10 \text{ ms}}$ per seek | $SSD \approx \mathbf{50 \text{ to } 100 \,\mu s}$ |
| Standard block size | $\mathbf{4 \text{ KB}}$ | Modern UNIX defaults |

> **Real-World Utility:** Modern journaling file systems (**ext4, NTFS, APFS, XFS, Btrfs**) all use B-Tree variants (often B+Tree) for their directory implementations. This is why your laptop can hold 10 million files in `C:\Windows` and still open a folder in under 10 milliseconds.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: B-Tree Search Cost vs. Binary Search Tree

**Problem:** Given a directory of $n$ files, compare the worst-case number of disk accesses for a binary tree vs. a B-Tree of order $d$.

**Step 1 — Establish the block model.**

Assume one disk block holds the data of **one tree node** in either scheme. The cost of comparing a key and choosing a child is $O(1)$ once the block is in memory. The dominant cost is therefore the number of blocks read, i.e., the **tree height $h$**.

**Step 2 — Height of a balanced binary tree.**

For a balanced binary tree containing $n$ keys:

$$
h_{\text{BST}} = \lceil \log_2 (n+1) \rceil
$$

For $n = 1{,}048{,}576$ (= $2^{20}$):

$$
h_{\text{BST}} = \lceil \log_2 (1{,}048{,}576 + 1) \rceil = 20 \text{ disk accesses}
$$

**Step 3 — Height of a B-Tree of order $d$.**

A B-Tree of order $d$ has at most $2d$ keys per node. The minimum number of keys at the root is $1$, and at all other internal nodes is $d$.

The number of keys $n$ is bounded by:

$$
n \le (2d)^{h} - 1
$$

Solving for the minimum height $h$:

$$
(2d)^{h} \ge n + 1
$$

$$
h \ge \log_{2d}(n+1)
$$

For $n = 1{,}048{,}576$ and $d = 100$ (i.e., 200 keys per node):

$$
h_{\text{BTree}} = \lceil \log_{200}(1{,}048{,}577) \rceil
$$

$$
h_{\text{BTree}} = \left\lceil \frac{\ln(1{,}048{,}577)}{\ln(200)} \right\rceil
$$

$$
h_{\text{BTree}} = \left\lceil \frac{13.86}{5.30} \right\rceil
$$

$$
h_{\text{BTree}} = \lceil 2.615 \rceil = 3 \text{ disk accesses}
$$

**Step 4 — Speedup factor.**

$$
S = \frac{h_{\text{BST}}}{h_{\text{BTree}}} = \frac{20}{3} \approx 6.67 \times
$$

**Step 5 — Time saved (assuming 8 ms per disk access on HDD).**

$$
\Delta t = (h_{\text{BST}} - h_{\text{BTree}}) \times 8 \text{ ms} = 17 \times 8 = 136 \text{ ms}
$$

> **Conversion Logic Summary:** Each tree level costs one full disk-block read. B-Trees pack $\approx 2d$ keys per block, so depth shrinks by a factor of $\log(2d)$ relative to a binary tree. For a 1-million-file directory, this means the difference between 20 disk seeks and 3 — a difference the human eye cannot perceive.

### 3.2 Derivation: Hash Table Load Factor & Average Chain Length

**Given:** $n$ entries, table size $m$, uniform hash function.

**Step 1 — Definition of load factor.**

$$
\alpha = \frac{n}{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Average chain length under simple uniform hashing.**

By the **Birthday Paradox / occupancy principle**, the expected chain length at any slot is:

$$
E[L] = \alpha
$$

**Step 3 — Average search cost (successful search, chaining).**

$$
T_{\text{avg}} = 1 + \frac{\alpha}{2} = 1 + \frac{n}{2m}
$$

The "+1" accounts for the initial table-slot access, and $\alpha/2$ is the average traversal halfway through a chain.

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**

Let $n = 750$, $m = 1000$:

$$
\alpha = \frac{750}{1000} = 0.75
$$

$$
T_{\text{avg}} = 1 + \frac{0.75}{2} = 1.375 \text{ accesses}
$$

**Step 5 — Worst case (all keys collide to one slot).**

$$
T_{\text{worst}} = n = 750 \text{ accesses}
$$

> **Conversion Logic Summary:** The load factor $\alpha$ is the *single most important tunable knob* in a hash-table directory. KTU examiners love this formula because it directly maps a design decision (table size $m$) to a measurable performance metric ($T_{\text{avg}}$).

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Linear List | Hash Table | Tree-Structured (B-Tree) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Search (avg) | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(\log_d n)$ |
| Search (worst) | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log_d n)$ |
| Insert | $O(1)$ at end, $O(n)$ to keep sorted | $O(1)$ avg | $O(\log_d n)$ |
| Delete | $O(n)$ (shifting) or hole | $O(1)$ avg | $O(\log_d n)$ |
| Space overhead | Low (just array) | Medium (table + chains) | Medium-High (pointers) |
| Sorted enumeration | Easy if sorted | Hard | In-order traversal natural |
| Alphabetical listing | Possible (sorted list) | Not natural | Yes (in-order traversal) |
| Scalability to millions | Poor | Good (with good hash) | Excellent |
| Real-world example | MS-DOS FAT (small) | legacy UNIX UFS dirs | NTFS, ext4, APFS |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Directory File

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef blk fill:#E8F1FF,stroke:#1F4E8C,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B2545
    classDef dir fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#B35900,stroke-width:2px,color:#3D1F00
    classDef io fill:#E8FFE8,stroke:#1F7A1F,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B3D0B
    classDef mem fill:#FFE5F1,stroke:#8C1F4E,stroke-width:2px,color:#3D0B25

    disk[Disk: Directory File]:::blk
    blk0[Block 0: entries 0 to 31]:::blk
    blk1[Block 1: entries 32 to 63]:::blk
    blk2[Block 2: entries 64 to 95]:::blk

    app[Application: open foo txt]:::io
    fs[File System Layer]:::io
    cache[In Memory Directory Cache]:::mem

    fcb[FCB Inode for foo txt]:::dir
    fcbdata[File Data Blocks]:::dir

    disk --> blk0
    disk --> blk1
    disk --> blk2

    app --> fs
    fs --> cache
    cache -.miss.-> blk0
    cache -.miss.-> blk1
    blk1 --> fcb
    fcb --> fcbdata
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow: Choosing a Directory Implementation Scheme

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef start fill:#1F4E8C,stroke:#0B2545,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef q fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#B35900,stroke-width:2px,color:#3D1F00
    classDef opt fill:#E8FFE8,stroke:#1F7A1F,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B3D0B
    classDef end fill:#FFE5F1,stroke:#8C1F4E,stroke-width:2px,color:#3D0B25

    A[Start: Design Directory Structure]:::start
    B{Directory size small under 100 files}:::q
    C{Requires constant time lookup}:::q
    D{Need alphabetical listing of files}:::q
    E{Directory size millions of files}:::q
    F[Linear List: simplest possible]:::opt
    G[Hash Table: O 1 average lookup]:::opt
    H[B Tree: scalable, sortable]:::opt
    I[B Tree: industry standard NTFS ext4 APFS]:::end

    A --> B
    B -- Yes --> F
    B -- No --> C
    C -- Yes --> G
    C -- No --> D
    D -- Yes --> H
    D -- No --> E
    E -- Yes --> I
    E -- No --> H
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Mapping of Each Scheme

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef lin fill:#E8F1FF,stroke:#1F4E8C,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B2545
    classDef hash fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#B35900,stroke-width:2px,color:#3D1F00
    classDef tree fill:#E8FFE8,stroke:#1F7A1F,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B3D0B

    L1[File name]:::lin
    L2[File name]:::lin
    L3[File name]:::lin
    L4[File name]:::lin
    Ld[...n entries]:::lin
    Lin[Linear List in array]:::lin

    H1[h k = 0]:::hash
    H2[h k = 1]:::hash
    H3[h k = 2]:::hash
    H4[h k = 3 with chain]:::hash
    H5[h k = m minus 1]:::hash
    HashT[Hash Table]:::hash

    R[root node]:::tree
    C1[child left keys K1 to K100]:::tree
    C2[child mid keys K101 to K200]:::tree
    C3[child right keys K201 to K300]:::tree
    TreeB[B Tree]:::tree

    L1 --- Lin
    L2 --- Lin
    L3 --- Lin
    L4 --- Lin
    Ld --- Lin

    H1 --- HashT
    H2 --- HashT
    H3 --- HashT
    H4 --- HashT
    H5 --- HashT

    R --- TreeB
    C1 --- TreeB
    C2 --- TreeB
    C3 --- TreeB
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Q1. List any three advantages and two disadvantages of using a linear list for directory implementation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Advantages:** (i) Simple to implement, requiring only an array of entries. (ii) Low memory overhead, no extra data structure. (iii) Sequential files are easy to enumerate in insertion order.
- **Disadvantages:** (i) Search is slow — $O(n)$ in the unsorted variant, requiring many disk accesses for large directories. (ii) Insertion into a sorted linear list is $O(n)$ because subsequent entries must be shifted.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
> **Q2. What is the role of a hash function in directory implementation? Why is collision resolution necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **hash function** maps a variable-length filename to a fixed integer index in a bounded table, enabling direct access without scanning. **Collision resolution** is necessary because the pigeonhole principle guarantees that two distinct filenames will eventually map to the same index when $n > m$. Common resolution techniques include **chaining** (linked list of overflow entries) and **open addressing** (probing for the next free slot).

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often **forget to mention the disk-access cost** when comparing schemes. KTU examiners specifically award marks for linking *complexity class* to *actual I/O operations*.
> - For B-Tree derivations, students must **explicitly state the value of $d$ (order)**; otherwise the numerical comparison with binary tree is considered incomplete.
> - Hash-table questions: do **not** confuse the load factor formula $\alpha = n/m$ with the average chain length formula $1 + \alpha/2$. They are different.
> - Always label your diagrams (Linear list, Hash, Tree) — unlabeled diagrams receive **partial credit only**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Linear List & Hash Table Comparison**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **linear list** scheme for directory implementation. Describe in detail the algorithms for `search`, `create`, and `delete` operations, and discuss how variable-length filenames are handled.

**Model Answer:**

A **linear list** stores all directory entries sequentially in a file, with each entry containing the filename, FCB pointer, and (optionally) attributes.

**1. Data structure of an entry:**

```
+----------+----------------+------------------+
| inode no |  filename (14B) | other attributes |
+----------+----------------+------------------+
   2 bytes        14 bytes            ...
```

**2. Search algorithm:**

1. Read the directory file block-by-block into a memory buffer.
2. For each entry, compare stored filename with the target.
3. If match → return FCB pointer; else continue.
4. After end-of-file → return *not found*.

**3. Create algorithm:**

1. Invoke *search* to confirm the filename does not already exist (else error).
2. Find a free slot (or append a new entry at the end).
3. Write the new entry's filename, attributes, and the freshly allocated FCB pointer.

**4. Delete algorithm:**

1. Invoke *search* to locate the entry.
2. Release the FCB back to the free pool.
3. Two choices:
   - **Mark as free (hole):** leaves a gap, but is fast.
   - **Compact the list:** shift all subsequent entries left by one slot, preserving contiguity but expensive ($O(n)$).

**5. Handling variable-length filenames:**

Three strategies are used in practice:
- **Fixed-size entries** (e.g., 16 bytes in classic UNIX): the filename is truncated to 14 characters; simple but limits naming.
- **Variable-size entries** with explicit length field: avoids truncation but complicates the array layout, requiring a pointer or offset to the next entry.
- **Hybrid** (used in Windows NT, modern Linux): a small fixed-size directory entry holds the most-used fields; for longer names, additional *overflow blocks* chained via pointers are used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **hash table** scheme with the linear list. Derive the average search cost when chaining is used and the load factor is 0.8.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Comparison table:**

| Property | Linear List | Hash Table |
|---|---|---|
| Search | $O(n)$ unsorted, $O(\log n)$ sorted | $O(1)$ average, $O(n)$ worst |
| Insert | $O(1)$ end / $O(n)$ sorted | $O(1)$ average |
| Delete | $O(n)$ with shift / hole | $O(1)$ average |
| Memory overhead | Low | Higher (table + chains) |
| Sorted listing | Easy if sorted | Difficult |
| Scalability | Poor for large dirs | Good with good hash |

**2. Derivation — Average search cost under chaining:**

Given $n$ keys uniformly hashed into $m$ slots, the load factor is:

$$
\alpha = \frac{n}{m}
$$

The expected number of keys in any single chain equals $\alpha$. For a **successful** search, the expected position of the target is the middle of its chain, giving:

$$
T_{\text{avg}} = 1 + \frac{\alpha}{2}
$$

(The first "1" accounts for accessing the table slot itself; $\alpha/2$ is the expected traversal distance.)

**3. Numerical substitution with $\alpha = 0.8$:**

$$
T_{\text{avg}} = 1 + \frac{0.8}{2} = 1.4 \text{ accesses}
$$

Thus on average, a file lookup requires only **1.4 accesses** — effectively constant time.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Tree-Structured Directory (B-Tree)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **tree-structured** directory scheme. Why is a **B-Tree** preferred over a binary search tree for directories stored on disk?

**Model Answer:**

A **tree-structured directory** organizes entries in a balanced tree. The directory becomes a *hierarchical* index, where internal nodes contain *separator keys* and leaf nodes contain the actual entries.

**1. B-Tree properties (order $d$):**
- Every node stores at most $2d$ keys and $2d+1$ child pointers.
- Every internal node (except the root) has at least $d$ keys.
- All leaves are at the **same depth** (perfect balance).
- Root has between 1 and $2d$ keys.

**2. Why B-Tree over binary search tree for disk directories:**

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Disk-block alignment** | A B-Tree node is sized to fit exactly one disk block, so each node read is a single I/O. |
| **Higher fan-out** | With $2d$ keys per node, depth drops from $\log_2 n$ to $\log_{2d} n$. For $d=100$ and $n=10^6$, depth ≈ 3. |
| **Guaranteed balance** | Insertions and deletions trigger *split* and *merge* operations that maintain perfect balance, so worst-case cost is $O(\log n)$ always. |
| **Cache-friendly range scans** | A B+Tree (a B-Tree variant) links leaves in a linked list, allowing efficient alphabetical directory listing — a key feature of `ls`. |

A binary search tree, by contrast, has height up to $n$ in the worst case (unbalanced) and only 2 children per node, making it expensive for disk-resident directories.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A directory contains $n = 1{,}000{,}000$ files. (i) Compute the worst-case number of disk accesses using a balanced binary search tree. (ii) Compute the same for a B-Tree of order 100. (iii) Comment on the engineering significance.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Binary Search Tree (balanced):**

$$
h_{\text{BST}} = \lceil \log_2 (n+1) \rceil
$$

$$
h_{\text{BST}} = \lceil \log_2 (1{,}000{,}001) \rceil
$$

$$
h_{\text{BST}} = \lceil 19.93 \rceil = 20 \text{ disk accesses}
$$

**(ii) B-Tree of order 100:**

$$
h_{\text{BTree}} = \lceil \log_{2d} (n+1) \rceil = \lceil \log_{200}(1{,}000{,}001) \rceil
$$

$$
h_{\text{BTree}} = \left\lceil \frac{\ln(1{,}000{,}001)}{\ln(200)} \right\rceil
$$

$$
h_{\text{BTree}} = \left\lceil \frac{13.8155}{5.2983} \right\rceil
$$

$$
h_{\text{BTree}} = \lceil 2.607 \rceil = 3 \text{ disk accesses}
$$

**(iii) Engineering significance:**

The B-Tree reduces disk accesses from 20 to 3 — a **6.67× speedup**. On a traditional HDD (≈ 8 ms per seek), this is a difference of:

$$
\Delta t = (20 - 3) \times 8 \text{ ms} = 136 \text{ ms}
$$

On SSDs (≈ 0.1 ms per access), the absolute saving is smaller (~1.7 ms) but still meaningful for latency-sensitive workloads such as web servers, database lookups, and OS file system traversal. This is precisely why **NTFS, ext4, APFS, XFS, and Btrfs** all use B+Tree variants for directory implementation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Directory Implementation**

- ☐ A **directory entry** minimally contains: *filename* + *pointer to FCB/inode*.
- ☐ **Linear list** has search cost $O(n)$ unsorted, $O(\log n)$ sorted; uses fixed-size 16-byte entries in classic UNIX (2-byte inode + 14-byte name).
- ☐ **Hash table** average search cost under chaining:

$$
T_{\text{avg}} = 1 + \frac{\alpha}{2}, \quad \alpha = \frac{n}{m}
$$

- ☐ **Hash table worst case** degenerates to $O(n)$ on total collision.
- ☐ **B-Tree of order $d$** stores at most $2d$ keys per node; node = one disk block.
- ☐ **B-Tree height formula:**

$$
h \ge \lceil \log_{2d}(n+1) \rceil
$$

- ☐ For $n = 10^6$: BST ≈ **20** accesses, B-Tree ($d=100$) ≈ **3** accesses.
- ☐ **Variable-length filenames** are handled via fixed-size truncation, length-prefixed entries, or chained overflow blocks.
- ☐ **Real-world systems** (NTFS, ext4, APFS, XFS, Btrfs) all use B+Tree variants.
- ☐ **Disk access cost is the dominant metric** — always express complexity in terms of I/O operations, not CPU cycles.
- ☐ **Sorted enumeration** is natural for linear (sorted) and B-Tree, but **not** for hash table.
- ☐ **Trade-off mantra:** *Linear list = simplicity; Hash table = speed for known size; B-Tree = scalable + sortable + industry standard.*
- ☐ Examiner's mantra: **never confuse $O(1)$ average with $O(1)$ worst-case** in hash-table answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Allocation Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Allocation Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are File Allocation Methods?

**File Allocation Methods** are the strategies used by an Operating System to determine how individual files are physically placed and organized on the secondary storage device (typically a hard disk or SSD). The OS must decide *where* on the disk the bytes of a file will be stored, *how* that location will be tracked in the file system's data structures, and *how* the disk head will move (seek) when reading or writing the file sequentially or randomly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Allocation methods define the mapping between the logical view of a file (a contiguous stream of bytes) and its physical location on the disk. The three classical methods taught are **Contiguous Allocation**, **Linked Allocation**, and **Indexed Allocation**, each offering different trade-offs between access speed, disk space utilization, and the ability to grow/shrink files dynamically.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Library Bookshelf

Imagine a large library with thousands of books (the *files*) and hundreds of long, empty bookshelves (the *disk blocks*). The librarian (the *Operating System*) must decide where to place each new book.

- **Contiguous Allocation** is like forcing every book to occupy one continuous, unbroken stretch of shelf space (e.g., books 1-300 on shelf A). Easy to find if you know the start, but a nightmare if the book grows or if the shelf is broken.
- **Linked Allocation** is like scattering a book's pages randomly across the library, but writing a "next page location" pointer at the bottom of every page (like a treasure hunt). You cannot jump to chapter 5 directly; you must follow the chain from page 1.
- **Indexed Allocation** is like keeping a separate "Table of Contents" (an index block) for every book, listing exactly which shelf each logical page lives on. You look up the page number in the index, then jump directly to it.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Block / Sector** | The smallest addressable unit of a disk (typically **512 bytes** or **4 KB**). |
| **Logical Address** | The byte offset the user process requests (e.g., "give me byte 5000"). |
| **Physical Address** | The actual cylinder/track/sector on the disk. |
| **File Allocation Table (FAT)** | A linked-list variant used historically in MS-DOS/Windows. |
| **Inode** | The data structure used in UNIX/Linux to hold allocation metadata. |
| **Seek Time (Ts)** | Time taken to move the disk arm to the correct track. |
| **Rotational Latency (Tr)** | Time waiting for the sector to rotate under the read/write head. |

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of allocation method is one of the most fundamental **design trade-offs** in file system engineering. There is no single "best" method — UNIX (indexed), MS-DOS (FAT/linked), and CD-ROMs (contiguous) all made different choices for good reasons.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Primary Allocation Methods

### A. Contiguous Allocation
Each file occupies a set of **consecutive** disk blocks.

**Operational Logic:**
1. When a file is created, the OS specifies its **starting block** and **length** (in blocks).
2. The directory entry stores: `| start_address | length |`
3. To access block `i` of a file starting at `start`: **physical_block = start + i**
4. Disk address calculation requires only **one** arithmetic operation — extremely fast.

**Advantages:**
- **Excellent Sequential Access:** Reading a file requires minimal head movement.
- **Excellent Random Access:** Direct calculation of any block's location.
- Simple to implement; only `start` and `length` are needed.

**Disadvantages:**
- **External Fragmentation:** Free blocks become scattered into small holes over time.
- **Difficult to Grow Files:** A file cannot easily extend if the next block is already occupied.
- **Pre-allocation Problem:** The maximum file size must often be declared at creation time.

### B. Linked Allocation
Each file is a **linked list of disk blocks** scattered anywhere on the disk.

**Operational Logic:**
1. The directory entry stores only the **pointer to the first block** of the file.
2. Each disk block contains a **pointer to the next block** (typically in the last few bytes of the block — e.g., last 4 bytes of a 4 KB block are the link).
3. To find block `i`, the OS must traverse `i` pointers from the start (**sequential access only**).
4. **FAT Variant:** A separate table in memory holds all pointers, eliminating the per-block link overhead.

**Advantages:**
- **No External Fragmentation:** Any free block can be used.
- **Easy to Grow:** Just allocate the next free block and update the previous block's pointer.
- Directory entry is very small (only a starting pointer).

**Disadvantages:**
- **No Random Access:** To reach block `N`, you must read `N-1` blocks first.
- **Pointer Overhead:** A portion of every block is consumed by the next pointer.
- **Reliability Issue:** If one pointer is corrupted, the rest of the file is lost.
- Slow if blocks are scattered far apart on disk (high seek overhead).

### C. Indexed Allocation
Each file has its own **index block** (or set of index blocks) that holds an array of pointers to the file's data blocks.

**Operational Logic:**
1. The directory entry stores the address of the file's **index block**.
2. The index block is a small array of disk block pointers (e.g., 128 pointers × 4 bytes = 512-byte index block).
3. To read logical block `i`, the OS reads the index block, looks up entry `i`, and goes directly to the data block.
4. **UNIX Solution (inode):** Uses a multi-level index — 12 direct pointers, 1 single-indirect, 1 double-indirect, 1 triple-indirect pointer.

**Advantages:**
- **Excellent Random Access:** Any block can be located in one index lookup.
- **No External Fragmentation:** Data blocks can be anywhere on the disk.
- Supports dynamic file growth up to a defined maximum.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Index Block Size Limits** the maximum file size unless multi-level indexing is used.
- **Extra Disk I/O:** Reading a small file now requires two disk accesses (one for the index, one for the data).
- Wastes space for very small files (the entire index block is allocated even for a 1-byte file).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Block Number from Logical Address** | `block_number = floor(logical_byte_offset / block_size)` | Used in all methods |
| **Byte Offset within Block** | `offset = logical_byte_offset mod block_size` | Range: `[0, block_size - 1]` |
| **Contiguous: Physical Block** | `physical_block = start_block + i` | O(1) calculation |
| **FAT: Max File Size** | `max_size = (block_size - 4) × num_FAT_entries` | 4 bytes reserved for next pointer per block |
| **UNIX inode: Max File Size (Single Indirect)** | `(128 - 13) × 4096 bytes` | 128 pointers/block, 4-byte pointers |
| **Fragmentation (Contiguous)** | `External Fragmentation` | Yes, significant |
| **Fragmentation (Linked/Indexed)** | `External Fragmentation = 0` | But internal fragmentation in index blocks |
| **Average Seek Time** | `Ts_avg ≈ 1/3 × full_stroke_seek` | Approx. **3–10 ms** for HDD |
| **Rotational Latency (avg)** | `Tr_avg = 1/2 × (60 / RPM)` | For 7200 RPM: ≈ **4.16 ms** |
| **Total Access Time** | `T_total = Ts + Tr + T_transfer` | For 4 KB block, transfer ≈ 0.1 ms |

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU exam, remember the classic comparison: **Contiguous is best for sequential**, **Indexed is best for random**, **Linked is a compromise** that wastes space in pointers but avoids fragmentation. UNIX's **inode** is the canonical example of indexed allocation and is the most frequently asked diagram in board exams.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CD-ROMs and DVD Video** use **contiguous allocation** because the data is written once and read sequentially.
- **MS-DOS / Windows FAT file systems** historically used **linked allocation** (the FAT itself being the in-memory linked list).
- **Linux ext2/ext3/ext4, UNIX UFS, and NTFS** all use **indexed allocation** (via inodes or B+ tree variants) for its superior random-access performance.
- Modern **log-structured file systems (LFS)** and **SSDs** further optimize these ideas by writing all updates sequentially to free segments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Logical-to-Physical Address Translation in Contiguous Allocation

**Problem Statement:**
Given a file starting at block `S` on disk, with a block size of `B` bytes, derive the physical block number `P` and byte offset `W` for a user-requested logical byte address `L`.

**Step 1 — Define the Input.**
The user process issues a `read(fd, buffer, n)` system call requesting a logical byte offset `L` within the file (where `L = 0` is the first byte).

**Step 2 — Compute the Block Index Relative to the File Start.**

$$
i = \left\lfloor \frac{L}{B} \right\rfloor
$$

*Explanation:* Integer division tells us *how many complete blocks* into the file the requested byte lies. If `B = 4096` and `L = 8192`, then `i = 2` (the 3rd block of the file, zero-indexed).

**Step 3 — Compute the Byte Offset Within That Block.**

$$
W = L \mod B
$$

*Explanation:* The modulo operation gives the byte's position *inside* the identified block. If `L = 8192` and `B = 4096`, then `W = 0` (the very first byte of that block).

**Step 4 — Translate to a Physical Disk Block.**

$$
P = S + i
$$

*Explanation:* In contiguous allocation, the `i`-th block of the file is simply `i` blocks after the starting address `S`. No further computation or disk lookup is needed.

**Step 5 — Combine into the Final Physical Address.**

$$
\text{Physical Address} = (P, W) = \left( S + \left\lfloor \frac{L}{B} \right\rfloor,\; L \mod B \right)
$$

*Final Result:* The entire translation is **O(1)** — a single division, a single modulo, and a single addition. This is why contiguous allocation is theoretically the fastest for random access.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum File Size in UNIX Inode (Indexed Allocation)

**Problem Statement:**
A UNIX inode has 12 direct pointers, 1 single-indirect pointer, 1 double-indirect pointer, and 1 triple-indirect pointer. Each pointer is **4 bytes**, the block size is **4096 bytes**, and the inode fits in 128 bytes. Derive the maximum file size.

**Step 1 — Pointers per Block.**
Since each pointer is 4 bytes, the number of pointers that fit in one 4096-byte block is:

$$
N_{ptr} = \frac{B}{P_{size}} = \frac{4096}{4} = 1024 \text{ pointers}
$$

**Step 2 — Data Accessible via Direct Pointers.**

$$
S_{direct} = 12 \times B = 12 \times 4096 = 49{,}152 \text{ bytes} = 48 \text{ KB}
$$

*Explanation:* Each of the 12 direct pointers points to one 4096-byte data block, giving 12 full blocks of immediate access.

**Step 3 — Data Accessible via the Single-Indirect Pointer.**

$$
S_{single} = N_{ptr} \times B = 1024 \times 4096 = 4{,}194{,}304 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ MB}
$$

*Explanation:* The single-indirect pointer points to a block containing 1024 pointers, each pointing to a 4096-byte data block.

**Step 4 — Data Accessible via the Double-Indirect Pointer.**

$$
S_{double} = (N_{ptr})^2 \times B = 1024^2 \times 4096 = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ GB}
$$

*Explanation:* The double-indirect block points to 1024 single-indirect blocks, each pointing to 1024 data blocks.

**Step 5 — Data Accessible via the Triple-Indirect Pointer.**

$$
S_{triple} = (N_{ptr})^3 \times B = 1024^3 \times 4096 = 4{,}398{,}046{,}511{,}104 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 6 — Total Maximum File Size.**

$$
S_{max} = S_{direct} + S_{single} + S_{double} + S_{triple}
$$

$$
S_{max} \approx 48 \text{ KB} + 4 \text{ MB} + 4 \text{ GB} + 4 \text{ TB} \approx 4 \text{ TB}
$$

*Final Result:* The triple-indirect level dominates; the maximum file size is approximately **4 TB** (the sum of the smaller terms is negligible by comparison).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **classic KTU board question**. Students are often asked to recompute with a different block size (e.g., 512 bytes or 8192 bytes) or a different pointer size (e.g., 8 bytes for 64-bit addressing). Practice substituting different values.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Contiguous | Linked | Indexed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Random Access Speed** | Excellent (O(1) math) | Poor (must traverse chain) | Excellent (one index lookup) |
| **Sequential Access Speed** | Excellent | Good (with FAT caching) | Good |
| **External Fragmentation** | Yes — major problem | No | No |
| **Internal Fragmentation** | No (except at end of file) | Yes — pointer overhead per block | Yes — full index block per file |
| **File Growth** | Hard | Trivial (extend chain) | Moderate (may need new index) |
| **Directory Entry Size** | `start + length` (medium) | `start pointer` (small) | `index block pointer` (small) |
| **Reliability** | High (no chain to break) | Low (one bad pointer loses file) | Moderate (corrupted index = lost file) |
| **Used In** | CD-ROM, early systems | FAT (MS-DOS) | UNIX, Linux ext*, NTFS |
| **Disk I/O for Small File** | 1 | 1 (or 2 without FAT) | 2 (index + data) |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the single most important comparison for KTU exams. Be prepared to draw it from memory in a 7-mark question and explain *why* each cell has its value.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Contiguous Allocation — Directory + Disk Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Directory Entry: File 'A'] -->|start = 0, length = 4| A1[Block 0]
    A1 --> A2[Block 1]
    A2 --> A3[Block 2]
    A3 --> A4[Block 3]
    A1 -.empty.-> X1[Block 4: FREE]
    X1 --> X2[Block 5: FREE]
    B[Directory Entry: File 'B'] -->|start = 6, length = 3| B1[Block 6]
    B1 --> B2[Block 7]
    B2 --> B3[Block 8]
```

*Interpretation:* The directory stores `start` and `length` for every file. The disk has a free hole at blocks 4–5 (external fragmentation). A new file requesting 3 contiguous blocks cannot fit there.

## 4.2 Linked Allocation — Single-File Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DIR[Directory: start = 9] --> N9[Block 9: DATA + ptr to 16]
    N9 --> N16[Block 16: DATA + ptr to 1]
    N16 --> N1[Block 1: DATA + ptr to 8]
    N1 --> N8[Block 8: DATA + ptr to 0]
    N8 --> NIL[NULL = End of File]
```

*Interpretation:* The directory only knows the start block (9). Each block carries a small pointer to the next. Blocks can be scattered across the disk in any order. To reach the 4th data block, the OS must read blocks 9 → 16 → 1 → 8 in sequence.

## 4.3 Indexed Allocation — The Inode Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D[Directory: index block = 20] --> IB[Index Block 20]
    IB --> P0[ptr 0 -> Data Block 5]
    IB --> P1[ptr 1 -> Data Block 12]
    IB --> P2[ptr 2 -> Data Block 7]
    IB --> P3[ptr 3 -> Data Block 33]
    IB --> PD[ptr 4 to 11 -> Direct Data Blocks]
    IB --> SI[ptr 12 -> Single-Indirect Block]
    SI --> SIP[SIP holds 1024 pointers to data]
    IB --> DI[ptr 13 -> Double-Indirect Block]
    DI --> DIL[DIB points to 1024 Single-Indirect Blocks]
```

*Interpretation:* The directory points to one index block. The index block's first 12 entries are direct pointers to data; the 13th points to a block of 1024 more pointers (single-indirect), the 14th points to a block of 1024 single-indirect blocks (double-indirect). This is the classic UNIX inode.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Comparing the Three Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER[User Process View]
        U[Logical Byte Stream: file of N bytes]
    end

    subgraph FS[File System Layer]
        DIR[Directory Entry]
        METADATA[Allocation Metadata]
    end

    subgraph DISK[Physical Disk]
        BLK[Disk Blocks 0 to M-1]
    end

    U -->|syscall read| FS
    FS --> METADATA
    METADATA -->|Contiguous: start, len| BLK
    METADATA -->|Linked: head pointer + chain| BLK
    METADATA -->|Indexed: index block| BLK

    style USER fill:#e1f5ff
    style FS fill:#fff4e1
    style DISK fill:#e8f5e9
```

*Interpretation:* A unified view showing how all three methods sit between the user's logical file view and the physical disk. Only the *metadata structure* changes between methods; the user always sees a flat byte stream.

## 4.5 Growth Pattern Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CONT[Contiguous: Cannot Grow In-Place]
        C1[File at blocks 5-7] -->|block 8 occupied| C2[FAIL: Must Copy Entire File]
    end

    subgraph LINK[Linked: Trivial Growth]
        L1[File ends at block 12] -->|allocate free block 99| L2[Update block 12 pointer to 99]
        L2 --> L3[File now ends at block 99]
    end

    subgraph IND[Indexed: Moderate Growth]
        I1[Index block has 10/128 slots used] -->|allocate new block 200| I2[Set index slot 10 = 200]
        I2 --> I3[File now uses 11 blocks]
    end

    style CONT fill:#ffebee
    style LINK fill:#e8f5e9
    style IND fill:#fff9c4
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Differentiate between contiguous and linked file allocation. List one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

In **contiguous allocation**, every file is stored in a set of consecutive disk blocks, and the directory entry stores the starting block address and the file's length. Its main **advantage** is excellent random and sequential access (any block can be computed by a single addition: `start + i`). Its main **disadvantage** is external fragmentation and the difficulty of growing an existing file.

In **linked allocation**, a file is a linked list of scattered disk blocks; the directory stores only a pointer to the first block, and each block contains a pointer to the next. Its main **advantage** is the complete absence of external fragmentation and easy file growth (just allocate the next free block and update the previous block's pointer). Its main **disadvantage** is that random access is not possible — block `N` requires `N` disk reads to reach.

---

### **Q2. What is an inode? How does it support indexed file allocation in UNIX?**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

An **inode (index node)** is a data structure used by UNIX/Linux file systems to store all metadata about a file — except its name and its actual data. It is the on-disk representation of a file's *index block*.

The inode contains 15 block pointers: **12 direct pointers** (each pointing directly to a data block), **1 single-indirect pointer** (pointing to a block of 1024 more pointers), **1 double-indirect pointer** (pointing to a block of 1024 single-indirect blocks), and **1 triple-indirect pointer** (for very large files). The directory entry contains only the inode number, and the inode itself holds the file's size, timestamps, permissions, and the 15 block pointers. This structure supports indexed allocation by allowing the OS to look up the address of *any* logical block of the file with at most three disk accesses (one for the inode, one for the indirect-block level, one for the data).

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **🔹 Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the three main file allocation methods in detail. Compare them on the basis of access time, space efficiency, and file growth flexibility.**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

The three main file allocation methods are:

**1. Contiguous Allocation:**
Each file occupies a set of consecutive disk blocks. The directory entry stores `start` and `length`. Accessing block `i` of the file requires only the calculation `start + i` — a single arithmetic operation. This makes it the **fastest** method for both sequential and random access. However, it suffers from **external fragmentation** (free blocks become non-contiguous holes), and files cannot grow without potentially relocating the entire file. Space efficiency is high (no per-block overhead), but a file's maximum size must be known in advance in many implementations.

**2. Linked Allocation:**
Each file is a linked list of scattered disk blocks. The directory stores only a pointer to the first block. Each data block contains a pointer to the next block in its last few bytes. There is **zero external fragmentation** — any free block can be used. Files can grow indefinitely by simply extending the chain. However, random access is **not supported**: reaching block `N` requires `N` sequential reads. There is also **pointer overhead** (a few bytes per block consumed by the link) and a **reliability concern** (corruption of one pointer loses the rest of the file). The FAT variant solves the random-access issue by keeping the entire linked list in memory.

**3. Indexed Allocation:**
Each file has a dedicated **index block** that is an array of pointers to its data blocks. The directory stores the index block's address. Random access is **excellent** — block `i` is found by a single array lookup. There is no external fragmentation. The drawbacks are: an extra disk I/O to read the index, space wastage for small files (a full index block is allocated even for 1-byte files), and a maximum file size bounded by the index block size (solved in UNIX by multi-level indexing).

**Comparative Table:**

| Aspect | Contiguous | Linked | Indexed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Access Time | Best (O(1) math) | Worst (O(N) traversal) | Good (O(1) array lookup) |
| Space Efficiency | Best (no overhead) | Moderate (pointer per block) | Moderate (index block per file) |
| File Growth | Poor (may need full copy) | Best (extend chain) | Good (add to index until full) |

**(b) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the indexed allocation method with the UNIX inode scheme. Calculate the maximum file size for a 4 KB block size and 4-byte pointers.**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**UNIX Inode Scheme:**
A UNIX inode has 15 block pointers (4 bytes each):
- **12 direct pointers** → point to 12 data blocks (48 KB)
- **1 single-indirect pointer** → points to a block of 1024 pointers → 1024 × 4 KB = 4 MB
- **1 double-indirect pointer** → points to a block of 1024 single-indirect blocks → 1024 × 1024 × 4 KB = 4 GB
- **1 triple-indirect pointer** → points to a block of 1024 double-indirect blocks → 1024³ × 4 KB ≈ 4 TB

**Diagram:**

```
          ┌─────────────────────────┐
          │  Directory (inode #)    │
          └────────────┬────────────┘
                       │
                       ▼
    ┌────────────────────────────────────┐
    │           INODE (Index Block)      │
    │  ptr0  ptr1  ptr2  ...  ptr11      │  ← Direct
    │  ptr12 ─────────────► [SIP block]  │  ← Single-Indirect
    │  ptr13 ─────────────► [DIB]        │  ← Double-Indirect
    │  ptr14 ─────────────► [TIB]        │  ← Triple-Indirect
    └────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Maximum File Size Calculation:**

Pointers per block = 4096 / 4 = 1024

$$
S_{max} = 12 \times 4KB + 1024 \times 4KB + 1024^2 \times 4KB + 1024^3 \times 4KB
$$

$$
S_{max} = 48\text{ KB} + 4\text{ MB} + 4\text{ GB} + 4\text{ TB} \approx 4\text{ TB}
$$

The maximum file size is approximately **4 TB**.

---

### **🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the linked allocation method with a diagram. What is the File Allocation Table (FAT) and how does it improve upon basic linked allocation?**

`[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Linked Allocation:**
In linked allocation, a file is stored as a linked list of disk blocks scattered anywhere on the disk. The directory entry stores only a pointer to the first block of the file. Each block contains a small pointer (typically the last 4 bytes) that gives the address of the next block in the file. The chain ends when a block's pointer contains a special `NULL` or `-1` value.

**Diagram:**

```
Directory:  [ File X | start_block = 9 ]
                                          
Block 9: [ Data (4088 bytes) | next = 16 ]  ──►  Block 16: [ Data | next = 1 ]  ──►
Block 1:  [ Data | next = 8 ]  ──►  Block 8: [ Data | next = NULL ]
```

**File Allocation Table (FAT):**
The basic linked scheme stores the `next` pointer inside each data block, which has two problems: (1) it wastes a few bytes per block, and (2) random access requires reading every preceding block. The **FAT** solution moves the entire linked list into a **separate table kept in main memory**.

In FAT, each entry in the table corresponds to one disk block and stores the block number of the next block in the file (or `EOF` / `FREE`). The directory entry stores the file's starting block number. To find block `N` of a file, the OS simply follows `N` entries in the in-memory FAT — no disk reads needed for the pointers themselves, because the FAT is cached in RAM. This dramatically improves both the random-access speed (no extra disk I/O for the chain) and the usable space per data block (no per-block pointer overhead).

**(b) [7 Marks] A disk has 1000 blocks, block size 4 KB. A file 'Exam.txt' is stored using contiguous allocation starting at block 100 with length 50 blocks. Another file 'Notes.txt' uses linked allocation starting at block 300. After some operations, blocks 145, 150, 155, 200, 300, 305 are free. Can a new file of 20 blocks be created? Discuss with respect to each allocation method.**

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Scenario Setup:**
- Total blocks: 1000, block size: 4 KB
- `Exam.txt`: contiguous, blocks **100–149** (start=100, length=50)
- `Notes.txt`: linked, occupies blocks **300, 301, 302, 303, 304, 306** (since 305 is free per the problem) — actually 5 blocks. Assume the chain is: 300 → 301 → 302 → 303 → 304 → 306 → EOF.
- Free blocks: 145, 150, 155, 200, 300, 305

**Decision for Each Method:**

**1. Contiguous Allocation:**
A new file of 20 blocks needs **20 consecutive free blocks**. The free blocks are at 145, 150, 155, 200, 300, 305 — none of these are adjacent (the largest consecutive run is just 1 block). Therefore, **the new file CANNOT be created using contiguous allocation**, despite there being 6 free blocks (24 KB) of total free space. This illustrates the classic problem of **external fragmentation**.

**2. Linked Allocation:**
A new file of 20 blocks needs 20 free blocks. The total free blocks available is only 6 — far less than 20. Therefore, **the new file CANNOT be created using linked allocation** either, because there simply are not enough free blocks in the system, regardless of contiguity. (If the total free space were ≥ 20, linked allocation would have no problem, since it can use any scattered blocks.)

**3. Indexed Allocation:**
Same constraint — 20 free blocks are needed. Only 6 are free, so **the new file CANNOT be created** under indexed allocation either. The difference is that if we had 20 free blocks scattered anywhere, indexed allocation would succeed, just like linked.

**Conclusion:** The new file **cannot be created** under any of the three methods because the **total free space (6 blocks) is less than the required space (20 blocks)**. The allocation method only matters once there is *enough* total free space — and at that point, contiguous would still fail (due to non-contiguity) while linked and indexed would succeed. This problem is a classic illustration of why **pre-allocation and external-fragmentation-awareness** matter in OS design.

---
