## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Allocation-Method Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing internal vs. external fragmentation** — Linked/indexed has NO external fragmentation but DOES have internal fragmentation (in index blocks or pointers). Many students write "indexed has no fragmentation" and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the FAT variant's improvement** — When asked about linked allocation, many students ignore the FAT and lose 2 marks. Always mention that the FAT is a *memory-resident* linked list.
> 3. **Wrong max-file-size calculation** — A frequent error: students add `12 + 1024 + 1024² + 1024³` *without* multiplying each by the block size. Always include the `× B` term.
> 4. **Missing the "growth" trade-off** — When comparing, always mention that contiguous cannot grow without relocation, and indexed grows only until the index is full.
> 5. **Diagram without labels** — In 7-mark questions, an unlabeled diagram is marked down 1–2 marks. Always label the start block, the index block, and the data blocks.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three classical file allocation methods:** Contiguous, Linked, Indexed.
- **Contiguous** = best speed, worst fragmentation, hardest growth.
- **Linked** = no fragmentation, no random access, FAT variant fixes the access problem.
- **Indexed** = random access + no fragmentation, but extra disk I/O and index-block overhead.
- **UNIX inode** = 12 direct + 1 single-indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect.
- **Max file size formula (UNIX):** `(12 + 1024 + 1024² + 1023³) × B` for 4-byte pointers.
- **FAT** = entire linked list kept in RAM → eliminates per-block pointer overhead and enables O(1) random access.
- **Trade-off principle:** Sequential workloads favor contiguous; mixed/random workloads favor indexed.
- **CD/DVD** = contiguous (write-once, read-many).
- **MS-DOS / FAT file systems** = linked (with in-memory FAT).
- **Linux ext2/ext3/ext4, NTFS** = indexed (with multi-level inodes or B+ trees).
- **Block size is typically 4 KB** in modern systems; **512 bytes** historically.
- **External fragmentation** = free space exists but is non-contiguous.
- **Internal fragmentation** = allocated block has unused space inside it.
- **For KTU exams:** Always draw a diagram, always include a comparison table, and always state the trade-off in one sentence.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage Management: Magnetic Disks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Storage Management: Magnetic Disks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Magnetic Disk?

A **Magnetic Disk** is a non-volatile, electromechanical secondary storage device that stores data on one or more rapidly rotating circular platters coated with a magnetic film. Data is read/written by read/write heads mounted on a moving actuator arm. In the KTU 2024 OS syllabus (Module 4 — Mass-Storage Structure), it is the **primary on-line secondary storage** used by the OS for file systems, virtual memory swap, and I/O buffering.

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Definition (Standardized):** "A magnetic disk provides the bulk of secondary storage for modern computer systems. Each disk platter has a flat circular shape, like a CD, and its surfaces are coated with a magnetic material. Data is stored on the surfaces in concentric circles called tracks."

## 1.2 Physical Architecture (Layered View)

A modern disk is a stack of physical layers, working from the surface inward:

| Layer | Component | Function |
|------|-----------|----------|
| Outer | Platter | Aluminum/glass disk coated with ferromagnetic material. |
| Surface | Tracks | Concentric rings where data is magnetically encoded. |
| Radial | Cylinders | Set of tracks at the same radius across all platters. |
| Arc | Sectors | Smallest addressable unit; typically **512 bytes** (modern: **4 KiB**). |
| Block | Disk Block | Logical unit of I/O; usually 1 sector or a small group. |
| Mechanical | R/W Heads | One per surface; mounted on common actuator arm. |
| Control | Disk Controller | Manages head movement, rotation, caching, and DMA. |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A *cylinder* is significant because switching between tracks in the same cylinder requires **no seek** (only head switch latency). This is heavily exploited by file-system allocators.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]  
> **Analogy: The Vinyl Record Library**  
> Imagine a vinyl record as one **platter**. Each song on the record is a **track** (a spiral in vinyl, but concentric rings on a disk). The **needle** is the read/write head. To play a different song, you slide the needle sideways (this is a **seek**). The time for the record to spin until the right groove is under the needle is the **rotational latency**. The time for the needle to slide physically across is the **seek time**. The speed at which music plays once the needle is positioned is the **transfer rate**. Now stack many records on a turntable (multiple platters) and you have a hard disk!

## 1.4 Connection to the Operating System

The OS views the magnetic disk through a layered abstraction:

```text
Application Program
        |
   File System API        (open, read, write)
        |
   Logical File System    (file → FCB/inode)
        |
   File Organization Module
        |
   Basic File System      (issues generic block requests)
        |
   I/O Scheduler / Disk Scheduling Algorithm   <-- THIS IS OUR FOCUS
        |
   Disk Controller (Hardware)
        |
   Physical Disk (Platters, Heads, Spindle)
```

The **Disk Scheduling Layer** is where most of Module 4's theory resides — its job is to **re-order** pending I/O requests to minimize total head movement and improve throughput.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Latencies of Disk Access

Every disk I/O request, no matter how small, must wait for **three sequential mechanical events**. This is the most important concept in Module 4.

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Exam Mantra:** "A disk access consists of three components. A disk access time = seek time + rotational latency + transfer time. Of these three, the seek time is the dominant factor."

### 2.1.1 Seek Time ($T_s$)

The time for the actuator arm to move the read/write head from its current cylinder to the target cylinder.

- **Minimum:** 0 (head already on target cylinder)
- **Maximum:** $T_{s,\max}$ (full-stroke seek, e.g., **3 ms** to **10 ms** for modern HDDs)
- **Average (used in KTU problems):** Typically **½ of $T_{s,\max}$** or given explicitly as $T_{s,\text{avg}}$

For an **average random request**, the OS assumes:
$$T_{s,\text{avg}} = \frac{T_{s,\max}}{2}$$

If a problem gives a per-cylinder seek time $t_c$ and a seek of $n$ cylinders:
$$T_s = n \times t_c$$

### 2.1.2 Rotational Latency ($T_r$)

The time waiting for the correct sector to rotate under the head after the head is positioned.

- For a disk spinning at $R$ revolutions per minute (RPM):
  - One full rotation = $\frac{60}{R}$ seconds
- Since the desired sector can be anywhere, the **average** rotational latency is half a rotation:
$$T_{r,\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{60}{R} = \frac{30}{R} \text{ seconds}$$

> [!EXAMPLE]  
> A 7200 RPM disk → $T_{r,\text{avg}} = \frac{30}{7200} = 4.17$ ms

### 2.1.3 Transfer Time ($T_t$)

The actual time to read/write the data once the head is on the right sector.

If we need to transfer $B$ bytes and each track holds $N_t$ bytes:
$$T_t = \frac{B}{N_t} \times \frac{60}{R}$$

> [!EXAMPLE]  
> Reading 4 KiB from a 7200 RPM disk with 256 sectors of 512 bytes per track → one full rotation reads a whole track (128 KiB). Transfer time = $\frac{4}{128} \times 8.33$ ms = 0.26 ms.

## 2.2 The Master Equation

The **total time to service one I/O request**:
$$T_{\text{access}} = T_s + T_r + T_t$$

For **random single-sector I/O** of 1 sector:
$$T_{\text{access}} = T_{s,\text{avg}} + \frac{30}{R} + \frac{60}{R \times N_s}$$

where $N_s$ is the number of sectors per track.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why this matters for OS designers:** A typical 7200 RPM HDD has average access around **8–13 ms**, while RAM access is ~**100 ns**. That is a factor of **80,000× to 130,000×** slower — the OS must minimize disk I/O aggressively.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Average Seek Time | $T_{s,\text{avg}} = \frac{T_{s,\max}}{2}$ | milliseconds (ms) |
| 2 | Per-Cylinder Seek | $T_s = n \cdot t_c$ | n = cylinders crossed, $t_c$ in ms/cyl |
| 3 | Rotational Period | $T_{\text{rot}} = \frac{60}{R}$ | R in RPM, result in seconds |
| 4 | Average Rotational Latency | $T_{r,\text{avg}} = \frac{T_{\text{rot}}}{2} = \frac{30}{R}$ | in seconds; × 1000 for ms |
| 5 | Transfer Time (whole track) | $T_t = \frac{60}{R}$ | one full rotation |
| 6 | Transfer Time (b bytes) | $T_t = \frac{b}{N_t} \cdot \frac{60}{R}$ | $N_t$ = bytes per track |
| 7 | Total Access Time | $T = T_s + T_r + T_t$ | sum of all three |
| 8 | Transfer Rate | $R_t = \frac{N_t}{60/R} = \frac{N_t \cdot R}{60}$ | bytes/second |
| 9 | Rotational Speed in Hz | $f = \frac{R}{60}$ | revolutions per second |
| 10 | Throughput of Scheduler | $\Theta = \frac{\text{requests served}}{\text{total head movement time}}$ | qualitative metric |

> [!NOTE]  
> **CRITICAL Markdown Rule:** All absolute values and set delimiters in the formulas above use $\vert$ or $\mid$ — never the bare pipe character `|` — so the markdown table renders correctly.

## 2.4 Disk Scheduling Algorithms — The "Why"

The OS receives many concurrent I/O requests. Serving them in arrival order (FCFS) makes the head zig-zag wildly across the disk. A **disk scheduling algorithm** re-orders requests to:

1. **Minimize total head movement** (less mechanical work → lower average $T_s$).
2. **Maximize throughput** (more requests served per second).
3. **Reduce average response time** (faster turnaround for individual requests).
4. **Maintain fairness** (avoid indefinite starvation of edge cylinders).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The OS cannot reduce $T_r$ or $T_t$ — those are physical. The **only** lever a scheduler pulls is **$T_s$** by reordering cylinder visits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Disk Access Time

> **[KTU Numerical Pattern — Dec 2022 / Jun 2023 Style]**  
> Consider a magnetic disk with the following specifications:
> - 4 platters, 8 surfaces (one head per surface)
> - 1024 cylinders (numbered 0 to 1023)
> - 256 sectors per track
> - Sector size = 512 bytes
> - Rotation speed = 7200 RPM
> - Average seek time = 8 ms
> - Controller overhead = 0.5 ms per I/O
>
> Calculate: (a) Total disk capacity, (b) Average rotational latency, (c) Total time to read a file of 64 KB starting at a random location, (d) Data transfer rate.

### 3.1.1 (a) Total Capacity

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bytes per track} &= 256 \text{ sectors} \times 512 \text{ bytes} = 131\,072 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Bytes per cylinder} &= 8 \text{ surfaces} \times 131\,072 = 1\,048\,576 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Total capacity} &= 1024 \text{ cylinders} \times 1\,048\,576 \\
&= 1\,073\,741\,824 \text{ bytes} = 1 \text{ GiB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** 1 GiB of formatted raw storage (1 decimal GB = 1.07 × 10⁹ bytes). Note: KTU problems often state 1 GB = 2³⁰ bytes.

### 3.1.2 (b) Average Rotational Latency

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{rot}} &= \frac{60}{R} = \frac{60}{7200} = 0.00833 \text{ s} = 8.33 \text{ ms} \\
T_{r,\text{avg}} &= \frac{T_{\text{rot}}}{2} = \frac{8.33}{2} = 4.17 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 (c) Time to Read 64 KB Randomly

64 KB = 65 536 bytes. Number of sectors = $\frac{65536}{512} = 128$ sectors.

Since sectors can span at most 2 tracks worst-case (we'll assume worst-case crossing, KTU typical approach):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_s &= 8 \text{ ms (given average)} \\
T_r &= 4.17 \text{ ms (average)} \\
\text{Controller overhead} &= 0.5 \text{ ms} \\
\text{Data per rotation} &= 8 \times 256 \times 512 = 1\,048\,576 \text{ bytes} = 1 \text{ MiB} \\
T_t &= \frac{65536}{1\,048\,576} \times 8.33 \text{ ms} = 0.52 \text{ ms} \\
T_{\text{total}} &= 8 + 4.17 + 0.5 + 0.52 = 13.19 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 (d) Data Transfer Rate

$$
R_t = \frac{N_t \cdot R}{60} = \frac{131\,072 \times 7200}{60} = 15\,728\,640 \text{ bytes/s} \approx 15 \text{ MB/s}
$$

> [!TIP]  
> **Sanity check:** Modern SATA HDDs deliver 100–200 MB/s. Our 15 MB/s reflects a single-track burst rate ignoring track skew, caching, and inter-sector gaps — which is the KTU exam convention.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Comparing Disk Schedulers

> **[KTU Numerical Pattern — Most Common 14-Mark Problem]**  
> Consider a disk with **200 tracks (0–199)**. The read/write head is initially at **track 53**, moving in the direction of **increasing track numbers**. The I/O request queue (in arrival order) is:  
> `98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67`
>
> Compute the **total head movement** for FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, and C-LOOK.

### 3.2.1 FCFS (First-Come, First-Served)

Service in arrival order; head movement = sum of absolute differences between consecutive tracks.

| From | To | Movement |
|------|----|----------|
| 53 | 98 | 45 |
| 98 | 183 | 85 |
| 183 | 37 | 146 |
| 37 | 122 | 85 |
| 122 | 14 | 108 |
| 14 | 124 | 110 |
| 124 | 65 | 59 |
| 65 | 67 | 2 |
| **Total** | | **640 cylinders** |

### 3.2.2 SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First)

At each step, pick the **nearest** pending request (tie: any). Start at 53.

```
53 → 65 (12) → 67 (2) → 37 (30) → 14 (23) → 98 (84) → 122 (24) → 124 (2) → 183 (59)
```

Sequence: 65, 67, 37, 14, 98, 122, 124, 183

| From | To | Movement |
|------|----|----------|
| 53 | 65 | 12 |
| 65 | 67 | 2 |
| 67 | 37 | 30 |
| 37 | 14 | 23 |
| 14 | 98 | 84 |
| 98 | 122 | 24 |
| 122 | 124 | 2 |
| 124 | 183 | 59 |
| **Total** | | **236 cylinders** |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Starvation hazard:** SSTF may indefinitely delay a request at a far cylinder if requests keep arriving near the head. KTU examiners often deduct marks if you call SSTF "fair".

### 3.2.3 SCAN (Elevator Algorithm)

Head moves toward higher tracks first, servicing all requests on the way, then reverses.

- Going up: from 53 → 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183 (then continue to track 199, the end)
- Then reverse and go down: 37, 14

```
53 → 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 199 (end) → 37 → 14
```

| From | To | Movement |
|------|----|----------|
| 53 | 65 | 12 |
| 65 | 67 | 2 |
| 67 | 98 | 31 |
| 98 | 122 | 24 |
| 122 | 124 | 2 |
| 124 | 183 | 59 |
| 183 | 199 | 16 |
| 199 | 37 | 162 |
| 37 | 14 | 23 |
| **Total** | | **331 cylinders** |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Definition of SCAN:** "The disk arm starts at one end of the disk and moves toward the other end, servicing requests as it goes. At the other end, the direction of head movement is reversed and servicing continues." — Silberschatz (KTU reference)

### 3.2.4 C-SCAN (Circular SCAN)

Head moves toward higher tracks, services, jumps to **track 0** (no service during return), then continues up again.

```
53 → 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 199 (end) → [jump to 0] → 14 → 37
```

| From | To | Movement |
|------|----|----------|
| 53 | 65 | 12 |
| 65 | 67 | 2 |
| 67 | 98 | 31 |
| 98 | 122 | 24 |
| 122 | 124 | 2 |
| 124 | 183 | 59 |
| 183 | 199 | 16 |
| 199 | 0 | 199 |
| 0 | 14 | 14 |
| 14 | 37 | 23 |
| **Total** | | **382 cylinders** |

> [!TIP]  
> **C-SCAN's "C" stands for "Circular"** — it provides a **more uniform wait time** than SCAN, because the distance from the last to the first request is always the same (full stroke). KTU often tests this in MCQ.

### 3.2.5 LOOK (SCAN without going to the end)

Head reverses at the **last request** in each direction, not at the disk end.

```
53 → 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 (highest) → 37 → 14 (lowest)
```

| From | To | Movement |
|------|----|----------|
| 53 | 65 | 12 |
| 65 | 67 | 2 |
| 67 | 98 | 31 |
| 98 | 122 | 24 |
| 122 | 124 | 2 |
| 124 | 183 | 59 |
| 183 | 37 | 146 |
| 37 | 14 | 23 |
| **Total** | | **299 cylinders** |

### 3.2.6 C-LOOK (C-SCAN without going to the end)

Head services all in current direction, jumps to lowest pending, then continues up.

```
53 → 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 (highest) → [jump to 14] → 37
```

| From | To | Movement |
|------|----|----------|
| 53 | 65 | 12 |
| 65 | 67 | 2 |
| 67 | 98 | 31 |
| 98 | 122 | 24 |
| 122 | 124 | 2 |
| 124 | 183 | 59 |
| 183 | 14 | 169 |
| 14 | 37 | 23 |
| **Total** | | **322 cylinders** |

### 3.2.7 Summary Comparison Table

| Algorithm | Total Head Movement (cyl) | Variance | Starvation? | Fairness |
|-----------|---------------------------|----------|-------------|----------|
| FCFS | 640 | High | No | High (FIFO) |
| SSTF | **236** | Medium | **Yes** | Low |
| SCAN | 331 | Low | No | Medium |
| C-SCAN | 382 | **Lowest** | No | High (uniform) |
| LOOK | 299 | Low | No | Medium |
| C-LOOK | 322 | Very Low | No | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Typical 14-Mark Question Pattern:** You will be asked to (i) compute head movement for a given queue under 2-3 algorithms, (ii) state which is best, and (iii) explain the trade-off (throughput vs fairness, starvation in SSTF, etc.).

## 3.3 RAID Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Definition:** "RAID — Redundant Arrays of Independent Disks — is a set of physical disk drives viewed by the OS as a single logical drive. Data is distributed across the drives to improve performance and/or reliability."

| Level | Technique | Min Disks | Fault Tolerance | Read Perf | Write Perf | KTU Use Case |
|------|-----------|-----------|-----------------|-----------|------------|--------------|
| RAID 0 | Striping (no parity) | 2 | **None** | Excellent (parallel) | Excellent | High-perf, non-critical data |
| RAID 1 | Mirroring | 2 | 1 disk failure | Good (read from either) | Slower (write both) | Critical data, OS volumes |
| RAID 2 | Bit-level striping + Hamming ECC | 3 | 1 (Hamming) | OK | Poor | Obsolete; concept question only |
| RAID 3 | Byte-level striping + 1 parity disk | 3 | 1 disk | OK for large transfers | Poor for small writes | High-bandwidth apps |
| RAID 4 | Block-level striping + 1 parity disk | 3 | 1 disk | Good | **Write bottleneck** (parity disk) | Rarely used |
| **RAID 5** | **Block striping + distributed parity** | 3 | 1 disk | Good | Good (parallel parity) | **Most common in KTU** |
| RAID 6 | Block striping + double distributed parity | 4 | 2 disks | Good | Slower (2 parities) | High-availability arrays |

### 3.3.1 RAID 5 Write Penalty Derivation (Frequently Asked)

For RAID 5, every logical write to a data block requires **4 physical I/O operations**:

1. Read the **old data block** (D_old)
2. Read the **old parity block** (P_old)
3. Compute **new parity**: $P_{new} = P_{old} \oplus D_{old} \oplus D_{new}$
4. Write the **new data block** and the **new parity block** in parallel

$$
T_{\text{write, RAID5}} = 2 \times T_{\text{read}} + 2 \times T_{\text{write}} = 4 \times T_{\text{access}}
$$

> [!TIP]  
> This is the "**RAID-5 write penalty**". KTU loves to ask: "Why does RAID 5 have a write penalty even though it has no dedicated parity disk?" — Answer above.

### 3.3.2 Effective Capacity Formulas

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RAID 0 effective} &= N \times D \\
\text{RAID 1 effective} &= \frac{N \times D}{2} \\
\text{RAID 5 effective} &= (N-1) \times D \\
\text{RAID 6 effective} &= (N-2) \times D
\end{aligned}
$$

where $N$ = number of disks, $D$ = capacity of one disk.

> [!EXAMPLE]  
> 4 disks × 1 TB each: RAID 0 → 4 TB, RAID 1 → 2 TB, RAID 5 → 3 TB, RAID 6 → 2 TB.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Disk Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    spindleA[Spindle Motor 5400 7200 15000 RPM] --> platter1[Platter 1 Top Surface]
    spindleA --> platter2[Platter 2]
    spindleA --> platter3[Platter 3 Bottom Surface]

    armA[Actuator Arm Assembly] --> headA1[Read Write Head Surface 1]
    armA --> headA2[Read Write Head Surface 2]
    armA --> headA3[Read Write Head Surface 3]
    armA --> headA4[Read Write Head Surface 4]
    armA --> headA5[Read Write Head Surface 5]
    armA --> headA6[Read Write Head Surface 6]

    platter1 -. magnetically accessed by .-> headA1
    platter2 -. magnetically accessed by .-> headA2
    platter2 -. magnetically accessed by .-> headA3
    platter3 -. magnetically accessed by .-> headA4
    platter3 -. magnetically accessed by .-> headA5
    platter3 -. magnetically accessed by .-> headA6

    ctrlA[Disk Controller] --> armA
    ctrlA --> spindleA
    busA[SATA or SAS or NVMe Bus] --> ctrlA
```

> **Block-level interpretation:** The spindle spins all platters together at fixed RPM. The actuator arm moves all heads radially in lockstep — meaning at any moment, all heads sit on the **same cylinder number**. The controller exposes this geometry to the OS through LBA (Logical Block Addressing).

## 4.2 Disk Geometry — Tracks, Cylinders, Sectors (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    surfaceA[Platter Surface] --> t0[Track 0 outer]
    surfaceA --> t1[Track 1]
    surfaceA --> t2[Track 2]
    surfaceA --> tn[Track N inner]

    t0 --> s0a[Sector 0]
    t0 --> s0b[Sector 1]
    t0 --> s0c[Sector 2]
    t0 --> s0d[Sector M1]

    cylinderX["Cylinder K = All Tracks at Radius K across all platters"] -.aligned across platters.-> t2
```

> **Reading aid:** A **cylinder** is a vertical column cutting through all platters at one radius. Reading sequentially within a cylinder requires **no seek** — only a head switch. This is why file systems co-locate logically related blocks in the same cylinder (e.g., inode + small file body).

## 4.3 I/O Request Service Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Issues read or write System Call] --> B[File System Layer Resolves to Block Number]
    B --> C[Block Number Converted to Cylinder Head Sector via Disk Geometry or LBA]
    C --> D[Request Enqueued in Disk Scheduler Queue]
    D --> E{Disk Scheduler Applies FCFS SSTF SCAN C-SCAN LOOK C-LOOK}
    E --> F[Selected Request Issued to Disk Controller]
    F --> G[Seek Time Move Arm to Target Cylinder]
    G --> H[Rotational Latency Wait for Target Sector]
    H --> I[Transfer Time Read or Write Data]
    I --> J[Data DMA Transferred to Memory or Disk]
    J --> K[Interrupt Raised to CPU]
    K --> L[Process Resumed]
```

> **Key insight:** The scheduler's only role is **choosing the next request at step E**. All later steps (G, H, I) are pure hardware physics the OS cannot speed up.

## 4.4 RAID 5 Data + Parity Distribution (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D0A[Disk 0] --- D1A[Disk 1] --- D2A[Disk 2] --- D3A[Disk 3]
    D0A --> strip0A[Strip 0 Data A]
    D1A --> strip1A[Strip 1 Data B]
    D2A --> strip2A[Strip 2 Data C]
    D3A --> strip3A[Strip 3 Parity P0]
    D0A --> strip0B[Strip 4 Data D]
    D1A --> strip1B[Strip 5 Parity P1]
    D2A --> strip2B[Strip 6 Data E]
    D3A --> strip3B[Strip 7 Data F]
```

> **Reading aid:** Notice how the **parity block rotates** between disks across successive strips. This rotation is what eliminates RAID 4's single-parity-disk write bottleneck.

## 4.5 Algorithm Comparison Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ALG[Disk Scheduling Algorithm] --> A1[FCFS]
    ALG --> A2[SSTF]
    ALG --> A3[SCAN]
    ALG --> A4[C-SCAN]
    ALG --> A5[LOOK]
    ALG --> A6[C-LOOK]

    A1 --> P1[Pros Simple Fair No Starvation]
    A1 --> C1[Cons Worst Average Seek Time]

    A2 --> P2[Pros Best Local Throughput]
    A2 --> C2[Cons Starvation Possible Not Globally Optimal]

    A3 --> P3[Pros Bounded Wait Good Throughput]
    A3 --> C3[Cons Biased Toward Middle Tracks]

    A4 --> P4[Pros Uniform Wait Time]
    A4 --> C4[Cons Longer Total Travel Than SCAN]

    A5 --> P5[Pros Less Wasted Travel Than SCAN]
    A5 --> C6[Cons Slightly More Variance Than C-SCAN]

    A6 --> P6[Pros Best of C-SCAN with Less Travel]
    A6 --> C6c[Cons Most Complex Implementation]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Differentiate between seek time and rotational latency. How do they contribute to the average disk access time?**

**Model Answer (10–12 lines):**

- **Seek Time ($T_s$):** The time taken by the disk's actuator arm to position the read/write head over the target cylinder. It is a mechanical motion that depends on the number of cylinders traversed. For a random access, the average seek time is taken as **half the maximum seek time**, i.e., $T_{s,\text{avg}} = \frac{T_{s,\max}}{2}$.
- **Rotational Latency ($T_r$):** The time spent waiting for the correct sector on the chosen track to rotate under the head. Since the desired sector is uniformly distributed, the average rotational latency is **half of one full rotation**, $T_{r,\text{avg}} = \frac{30}{R}$ seconds for an $R$-RPM disk.
- **Contribution to Total Access Time:** The total disk access time is the sum of the three components: $T_{\text{access}} = T_s + T_r + T_t$. Of these, the **seek time is the dominant factor** because it is a coarse mechanical motion across the entire disk radius, while rotational latency is bounded by one rotation and transfer is bounded by the amount of data read. Hence, disk scheduling algorithms aim primarily to **minimize $T_s$** by reordering requests.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 2024]`
**What is swap-space management? Why is the location and performance of swap space critical?**

**Model Answer (8–10 lines):**

- **Definition:** Swap space is a reserved area on a **secondary storage device (typically a magnetic disk or SSD)** that the OS uses to back virtual memory pages that do not fit in physical RAM. It extends the apparent memory of the system.
- The OS creates a **swap partition** (raw) or a **swap file** (within a file system) during installation/configuration.
- **Criticality of location:** If the swap is on the same disk that holds the file system, heavy paging causes **thrashing** and disk-arm contention. Hence, swap is often placed on a **separate, dedicated disk** to allow parallel access.
- **Performance:** Swap I/O has the same access-time profile as any disk read/write ($T_s + T_r + T_t$). Modern OSes use **swap partition over swap file** because partitions avoid file-system metadata overhead and allow **raw block I/O**, reducing swap latency by 20–30%.
- Swap management algorithms (e.g., Linux's LRU-2Q, Windows' standby list) are tightly integrated with the VM page-replacement policy.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Pattern, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **physical structure of a magnetic disk**. Explain the terms *platter, track, sector, cylinder,* and *actuator arm*. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Consider a magnetic disk with **500 tracks (0–499)**. The read/write head is initially at **track 215**, moving in the direction of **increasing track numbers**. The disk request queue (in arrival order) is:  
`85, 339, 41, 432, 121, 270, 366, 21`

Compute the total head movement for **(i) FCFS, (ii) SSTF, (iii) SCAN, (iv) C-SCAN, (v) LOOK, (vi) C-LOOK**. State which algorithm is most efficient and why. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a)

A magnetic disk is a **secondary storage device** that uses rigid, rotating platters coated with magnetic material to store data. Its physical structure (as shown in Section 4.1) consists of:

1. **Platters:** Flat circular disks made of aluminum alloy or glass, coated on both sides with a thin ferromagnetic layer. Data is stored as tiny magnetized regions.
2. **Spindle:** A central motor that rotates all platters at a constant angular velocity, typically **5400, 7200, 10000, or 15000 RPM**.
3. **Tracks:** Concentric circles on each platter surface along which data is stored. Each track is divided into **sectors** (commonly 256 per track).
4. **Sectors:** The smallest individually addressable unit of storage on a disk. Modern disks use **512 bytes** (emulated) or **4096 bytes** (4K native).
5. **Cylinders:** A 3-D concept: the set of all tracks on all platters that lie at the **same radius** from the spindle. Operating on the same cylinder requires no radial movement, only a **head switch** (electronically, microseconds).
6. **Read/Write Heads:** Tiny electromagnetic devices mounted on the **actuator arm**, one per surface. They do not touch the platter — they float on a microscopic air cushion (~5 nm) to avoid damage.
7. **Actuator Arm:** A movable arm that carries all the heads as a single rigid assembly, controlled by a **voice-coil actuator**.
8. **Disk Controller:** Embedded electronics that translate logical block requests into mechanical motion, handle DMA, maintain the on-disk cache, and report SMART data.

> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Platter/Spindle (1), Track/Sector (1.5), Cylinder (1.5), Head/Arm (1.5), Controller (0.5), Diagram (1).

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b)

**Given:** Head start = 215, Direction = towards higher tracks, Queue = `[85, 339, 41, 432, 121, 270, 366, 21]`.

**(i) FCFS — service in arrival order**

| From | To | Movement |
|------|----|----------|
| 215 | 85 | 130 |
| 85 | 339 | 254 |
| 339 | 41 | 298 |
| 41 | 432 | 391 |
| 432 | 121 | 311 |
| 121 | 270 | 149 |
| 270 | 366 | 96 |
| 366 | 21 | 345 |
| **Total FCFS** | | **1974 cylinders** |

**(ii) SSTF — at each step, nearest pending**

From 215: nearest is 270 (55). From 270: 339 (69). From 339: 366 (27). From 366: 432 (66). From 432: 121 (311). From 121: 85 (36). From 85: 41 (44). From 41: 21 (20).

Sequence: `215 → 270 → 339 → 366 → 432 → 121 → 85 → 41 → 21`

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{SSTF}} &= (270-215) + (339-270) + (366-339) + (432-366) + (432-121) + (121-85) + (85-41) + (41-21) \\
&= 55 + 69 + 27 + 66 + 311 + 36 + 44 + 20 \\
&= 628 \text{ cylinders}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) SCAN — go up to 499 (last track), then down**

Going up: 215 → 270 → 339 → 366 → 432 → 499 (no more pending in upper half)  
Going down: 499 → 121 → 85 → 41 → 21

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{SCAN}} &= (270-215) + (339-270) + (366-339) + (432-366) + (499-432) + (499-121) + (121-85) + (85-41) + (41-21) \\
&= 55 + 69 + 27 + 66 + 67 + 378 + 36 + 44 + 20 \\
&= 762 \text{ cylinders}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) C-SCAN — go up to 499, jump to 0, then up to remaining**

Going up: 215 → 270 → 339 → 366 → 432 → 499  
Jump: 499 → 0  
Going up: 0 → 21 → 41 → 85 → 121

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{C-SCAN}} &= 55 + 69 + 27 + 66 + 67 + 499 + 21 + 20 + 44 + 36 \\
&= 904 \text{ cylinders}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(v) LOOK — reverse at last pending (no 499 or 0 trips)**

Going up: 215 → 270 → 339 → 366 → 432 (last in upper)  
Going down: 432 → 121 → 85 → 41 → 21

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{LOOK}} &= 55 + 69 + 27 + 66 + (432-121) + (121-85) + (85-41) + (41-21) \\
&= 55 + 69 + 27 + 66 + 311 + 36 + 44 + 20 \\
&= 628 \text{ cylinders}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(vi) C-LOOK — reverse at last pending, jump to first pending, then up**

Going up: 215 → 270 → 339 → 366 → 432  
Jump: 432 → 21 (lowest pending)  
Going up: 21 → 41 → 85 → 121

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{C-LOOK}} &= 55 + 69 + 27 + 66 + (432-21) + 20 + 44 + 36 \\
&= 55 + 69 + 27 + 66 + 411 + 20 + 44 + 36 \\
&= 728 \text{ cylinders}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Summary and Verdict

| Algorithm | Total Movement (cyl) | Rank |
|-----------|---------------------|------|
| FCFS | 1974 | 6 (worst) |
| **SSTF** | **628** | **1 (best in this run)** |
| SCAN | 762 | 4 |
| C-SCAN | 904 | 6 |
| LOOK | 628 | 1 (tie) |
| C-LOOK | 728 | 5 |

> **Examiner's Note:** The most efficient is **SSTF/LOOK (tied at 628)**. In general, SCAN-family algorithms (SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, C-LOOK) are preferred in real systems because they **guarantee no starvation**, while SSTF can starve edge cylinders. The "best" algorithm is workload-dependent; KTU accepts any one with proper justification.

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Frequent Mark Deductions:**  
> 1. **Direction confusion:** In SCAN, the head moves in the **current direction first** until end, then reverses. Students often reverse the order. → **Lose 2 marks**.  
> 2. **End-of-disk travel:** SCAN/C-SCAN go to tracks 0 and 499 (or max), while LOOK/C-LOOK stop at the **last requested track**. Mixing these up is the #1 error. → **Lose 2–3 marks**.  
> 3. **Neglecting the return jump in C-SCAN:** Forgetting the 499→0 jump invalidates the total. → **Lose 1 mark**.  
> 4. **Mixing absolute values:** Use $|x-y|$ or check up/down explicitly. KTU wants the sum of positive movements. → **Lose 1 mark**.  
> 5. **Forgetting to state the conclusion:** "Which is best and why?" is mandatory. → **Lose 1 mark**.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Pattern, 14 Marks, Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **RAID (Redundant Array of Independent Disks)**. Compare **RAID 0, RAID 1, RAID 4, and RAID 5** in terms of striping, mirroring, parity, performance, and fault tolerance. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A system uses a **RAID 5 array with 5 disks of 200 GB each**. Calculate:  
(i) Effective usable capacity.  
(ii) If one disk fails, how is data recovered?  
(iii) What is the **RAID-5 write penalty** and how many disk I/Os occur per logical write?  
(iv) If a sequential write workload reads 1 MB of data per request from a 7200 RPM disk with 5 ms average seek, 256 sectors of 512 bytes per track, what is the total time to read 100 such requests? Compare it with a single-disk scenario. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**RAID Definition:** RAID is a technology that combines multiple physical disk drives into one or more logical units for the purposes of **data redundancy, performance improvement, or both**. It provides fault tolerance through redundancy (mirroring or parity) and improves performance through striping.

**Comparative Table:**

| Feature | RAID 0 | RAID 1 | RAID 4 | RAID 5 |
|---------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| Technique | Block striping, no redundancy | Mirroring (full copy) | Block striping + dedicated parity disk | Block striping + distributed parity |
| Min disks | 2 | 2 | 3 | 3 |
| Fault tolerance | **0 disks** (any loss = data loss) | $N/2$ disks (1 of 2) | 1 disk | 1 disk |
| Read performance | Excellent (parallel) | Good (read from either mirror) | Good | Good (parallel like RAID 0) |
| Write performance | Excellent | Slower (write to both) | **Bottleneck** at parity disk | Good (distributed parity) |
| Effective capacity | $N \times D$ | $N \times D / 2$ | $(N-1) \times D$ | $(N-1) \times D$ |
| Use case | Temp/scratch, video editing | OS volumes, databases | Rare | **Most general-purpose** |

> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Definition (1), RAID 0 (1), RAID 1 (1.5), RAID 4 (1.5), RAID 5 (2).

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**(i) Effective Capacity (RAID 5 with 5 × 200 GB):**

$$
C_{\text{eff}} = (N-1) \times D = (5-1) \times 200 = 800 \text{ GB}
$$

**(ii) Recovery on single disk failure:**

When one disk fails, the data on it is **reconstructed on-the-fly** by XOR-ing the corresponding blocks from the surviving disks:

$$
D_{\text{recovered} \, i} = D_j \oplus D_k \oplus \ldots \oplus P_{\text{stripe}}
$$

where $P_{\text{stripe}}$ is the parity block of that stripe. The reconstruction is **compute-bound** (CPU performs XOR), and during reconstruction the array runs in **degraded mode** with reduced performance and **no second-fault tolerance**.

**(iii) RAID 5 Write Penalty:**

A logical write of a single block requires **4 physical I/Os**:

1. **Read** old data block
2. **Read** old parity block
3. **Write** new data block
4. **Write** new parity block

$$
P_{\text{new}} = P_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{\text{new}}
$$

For a **full-stripe write** (all blocks in a stripe written at once), the penalty is **reduced to 1 read + 1 write per block** because the new parity is computed in memory — no read of old values is needed.

**(iv) Time for 100 sequential reads on RAID 5 (5 disks) vs. single disk:**

- Bytes per request: 1 MB = 1024 × 1024 = 1 048 576 bytes
- Sectors per request: $\frac{1048576}{512} = 2048$ sectors
- Tracks per request (with 256 sectors/track): $\frac{2048}{256} = 8$ tracks
- **Single disk — worst case (8 tracks, 1 seek + 8 rotations):**

$$
T_{\text{1-disk}} = (5 \text{ ms} + \frac{8.33}{2} \text{ ms}) + 8 \times 8.33 \text{ ms} = 9.17 + 66.64 = 75.81 \text{ ms per request}
$$

For 100 requests: $100 \times 75.81 = 7581$ ms = **7.58 s**

- **RAID 5 — stripe across 5 disks, so 1 request touches 5/5 disks = 1 track per disk, but still 1 seek per disk (8 sectors per request per disk? No, with 5 disks and 8 tracks, 1.6 tracks per disk ≈ 2 tracks). Worst case: 2 tracks per disk = 1 seek + 2 rotations per disk:**

$$
T_{\text{1-disk, RAID5}} = (5 + 4.17) + 2 \times 8.33 = 9.17 + 16.66 = 25.83 \text{ ms per request}
$$

But the **5 disks operate in parallel** for a single request (1 MB split into 5 × 200 KB, each on a separate disk). So per-request time = **25.83 ms** (limited by the slowest disk). For 100 requests (no contention): same.

**However**, if the 100 requests are issued sequentially and the data is striped round-robin, the requests can be **pipelined** across disks — disk 0 handles requests 1, 6, 11, ... while disk 1 handles 2, 7, 12, etc. Total time then approaches:

$$
T_{\text{RAID5, pipelined}} \approx \frac{100 \times 25.83}{5} = 516.6 \text{ ms} \approx 0.52 \text{ s}
$$

**Comparison:**

| Scenario | Total Time | Speedup |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| Single disk (worst) | 7.58 s | 1× |
| RAID 5, no pipeline | 2.58 s | ~2.9× |
| RAID 5, pipelined | 0.52 s | ~14.5× |

> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Capacity (1.5), Recovery (1), Write penalty (1.5), Read time (3).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST (Read before the exam):**

- ✅ **Three latencies of disk I/O:** $T = T_s + T_r + T_t$. Of these, **seek time is dominant** and is the only one an OS can reduce.
- ✅ **Average seek** = $\frac{T_{s,\max}}{2}$ for random requests.
- ✅ **Average rotational latency** = $\frac{30}{R}$ seconds = $\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{60}{R}$.
- ✅ **Transfer time** = $\frac{\text{bytes}}{\text{bytes per track}} \times \frac{60}{R}$.
- ✅ **FCFS:** Simple, fair, no starvation, but **worst throughput**. No reordering.
- ✅ **SSTF:** Locally optimal, but **causes starvation** at edge cylinders.
- ✅ **SCAN:** "Elevator" — goes to one end then reverses. **No starvation**, but biased toward middle.
- ✅ **C-SCAN:** Goes one way, jumps back, continues. **Most uniform wait time** — used in real systems.
- ✅ **LOOK:** Like SCAN but reverses at the last request. Saves travel.
- ✅ **C-LOOK:** Like C-SCAN but reverses at last request. **Best general-purpose** in KTU problems.
- ✅ **RAID levels to memorize:** 0 (striping, no redundancy), 1 (mirroring, full redundancy), 4 (block striping + dedicated parity), **5 (block striping + distributed parity)**, 6 (double parity).
- ✅ **RAID 5 write penalty** = 4 I/Os per logical write (read old data, read old parity, write new data, write new parity).
- ✅ **Effective capacity formulas:** $C_0 = N D$, $C_1 = ND/2$, $C_4 = C_5 = (N-1)D$, $C_6 = (N-2)D$.
- ✅ **Cylinder concept:** Set of tracks at the same radius; switching within a cylinder is **head-switch latency (~ms)** not seek.
- ✅ **Modern sector size:** 4 KiB (Advanced Format), not the legacy 512 B.
- ✅ **Zoned bit recording (ZBR):** Outer tracks have more sectors than inner tracks → higher transfer rate on outer cylinders.
- ✅ **Swap space:** Should be on a **separate disk** to avoid file-system contention. Raw partition preferred over swap file.
- ✅ **Disk cache/buffer:** Disk controllers have on-board RAM cache; OS layer cache (page cache) further reduces physical I/O.
- ✅ **NCQ (Native Command Queuing) / Tagged Command Queuing:** Modern disks allow the OS to send **multiple outstanding requests** and let the disk's internal controller re-order them based on rotational position — this is hardware-assisted disk scheduling that has largely replaced OS-level scheduling for SSDs and even modern HDDs.
- ✅ **For SSD-based exam questions (bonus point):** SSDs have **no seek time and no rotational latency**; their I/O model is purely on **page-read/page-write** of NAND flash, so traditional disk scheduling is irrelevant for SSDs. The OS-level scheduler still helps with **request merging and fairness** even on SSDs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid-State Disks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Solid-State Disks ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Solid-State Disk (SSD)** is a non-volatile, semiconductor-based secondary storage device that uses **NAND flash memory** (or, less commonly, 3D XPoint/NOR flash) to persistently store data. Unlike electromechanical Hard Disk Drives (HDDs), an SSD contains **no moving parts** — data is stored in arrays of floating-gate transistors arranged into **blocks, pages, and planes**. The SSD is managed by an embedded controller running the **Flash Translation Layer (FTL)**, which maps logical block addresses to physical NAND locations and performs critical housekeeping functions such as **wear leveling, garbage collection, and bad block management**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Mass Storage):** > The Operating Systems syllabus (PCERT601) categorizes SSDs under *secondary storage structures*, explicitly emphasizing the following sub-topics: **(a) SSD architecture vs HDD, (b) Read/Write/Erase primitives of NAND flash, (c) Wear-leveling techniques, (d) TRIM command, and (e) Performance benchmarking parameters.** ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Without a Librarian > [!NOTE] > **Intuition for First-Time Learners:** > Imagine a massive **library** with no librarian and no card catalog: > - In an **HDD**, you must physically *walk* to the shelf, *spin* the book carousel, and *read* the page. Every retrieval is mechanical — slow, but you can write on the page with a pencil and erase it freely. > - In an **SSD**, the library is replaced by **millions of tiny electrical switches (transistors)**. To read a value, you simply *apply a voltage* and check whether current flows. There is no mechanical motion — everything happens at the speed of electricity, but the catch is: **you can only flip a switch from 1→0 freely (write/program), and to flip it back to 1 you must erase a whole *roomful* of books (a block) at once.** ### 1.3 Why SSDs Matter in Modern OS Design | Property | HDD (Legacy) | SSD (Modern) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Access Mechanism | Spinning platters + moving head | Pure electronic | | Random Read Latency | **5–10 ms** | **20–100 μs** | | Random IOPS (4K) | ~100–200 | ~10,000–500,000 | | Power Draw (Active) | 6–8 W | 2–4 W | | Failure Mode (AFR) | Mechanical wear | Write-cycle wear-out | | Acoustic Noise | Yes (clicks, hum) | Silent | > [!TIP] > **For KTU Board Exams:** Always mention the three killer advantages of SSDs — **(i) Random access performance (no seek time), (ii) Shock resistance (no head crash), and (iii) Lower latency variance** — these are favorite 3-mark questions. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 SSD Internal Architecture (Layered View) The SSD is not a "magic black box." It is a **four-layer hierarchy**: **Layer 1 — NAND Flash Array (Physical Layer)** - The raw memory: billions of **floating-gate MOSFETs** organized hierarchically as: $$\text{Die} \rightarrow \text{Plane} \rightarrow \text{Block} \rightarrow \text{Page}$$ - A typical page is **4 KB – 16 KB**; a typical block contains **128 – 256 pages**. **Layer 2 — SSD Controller (FTL – Flash Translation Layer)** - A RISC/DSP processor (e.g., ARM Cortex) running firmware. - Owns the **logical-to-physical (L2P) mapping table** (often kept in DRAM). - Implements **wear leveling, garbage collection, read disturb handling**. **Layer 3 — DRAM Cache (Volatile Buffer)** - A small onboard DDR/DDR2/DDR3 buffer (typically 256 MB – 4 GB). - Holds the L2P table and **temporarily coalesces random writes** into aligned sequential block writes (write coalescing). **Layer 4 — Host Interface** - SATA III (6 Gbps), PCIe Gen3/4/5 NVMe, SAS, or U.2 — speed scaling dramatically. ### 2.2 NAND Flash Cell Types — The Density vs Endurance Trade-off | Cell Type | Bits per Cell | P/E Cycles (Endurance) | Read Latency | Typical Use | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | | **SLC** (Single-Level Cell) | 1 | **100,000** | 25 μs | Enterprise | | **MLC** (Multi-Level Cell) | 2 | **10,000** | 50 μs | Prosumer | | **TLC** (Triple-Level Cell) | 3 | **3,000** | 75 μs | Consumer | | **QLC** (Quad-Level Cell) | 4 | **1,000** | 100 μs | Read-heavy archive | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *bits per cell* with *bytes per page*. Remember: a *cell* stores 1–4 *bits* via charge levels; a *page* is the smallest readable unit and contains many cells (e.g., 16 KB = 16,384 bytes). ### 2.3 The Three Primitives of NAND Flash Unlike a hard disk where "write" is a single atomic operation, NAND flash has **three distinct primitives**, and this asymmetry is the heart of every SSD algorithm. 1. **READ (Page)**: Sensing the charge level of a page. Fastest of the three. **Latency ≈ 25–100 μs**. 2. **PROGRAM / WRITE (Page)**: Forcing charge into the floating gate of each cell in a page. Requires the block to be *already erased*. **Latency ≈ 200–500 μs**. 3. **ERASE (Block)**: Resetting all cells in a block to "1" state by quantum tunneling (Fowler–Nordheim). **Slowest, latency ≈ 1,500–3,000 μs**. **Cannot erase individual pages — only whole blocks.** > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Write-Before-Erase" Rule:** > NAND flash can only flip bits from 1 → 0 (program) directly. To flip 0 → 1, the OS/FTL must **erase the entire containing block** (set all bits to 1), then **rewrite** the updated page back. This asymmetry is the root cause of **garbage collection** and **write amplification**. ### 2.4 Core SSD Algorithms (OS / FTL Level) **A) Wear Leveling (Dynamic and Static):** - NAND cells degrade with each P/E cycle. Without wear leveling, frequently updated files (e.g., swap, logs) would burn out certain blocks while others remain pristine. - *Dynamic wear leveling*: Maps new writes to the *least-used* free block. - *Static wear leveling*: Periodically relocates *cold* (read-only) data from rarely-erased blocks to heavily-erased blocks, freeing the worn blocks for new writes. **B) Garbage Collection (GC):** - Because erasure is block-granular but writes are page-granular, a block may contain a mix of *valid* and *stale (invalid)* pages. - GC picks a block with few valid pages, copies valid pages to a new block, and erases the old block. **This is the dominant background cost of SSDs.** **C) TRIM Command (ATA-8 / NVMe equivalent: Dataset Management):** - When the OS **deletes a file**, the filesystem merely marks the space as free in its bitmap — but the SSD doesn't know those pages are now invalid. - The **TRIM command** (issued by the OS via `fstrim` / mount option `discard`) tells the SSD: *"These LBAs are no longer in use; you may mark their physical pages as invalid for GC."* - **Result:** Faster GC, lower write amplification, sustained steady-state performance. ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **IOPS (Theoretical Peak)** | $IOPS = \dfrac{1}{t_{latency}}$ | ops/sec | $t_{latency}$ in seconds | | 2 | **Steady-State IOPS** | $IOPS_{ss} = \dfrac{N_{writes} \cdot 1024}{WA \cdot (t_{prog} + t_{read} + t_{erase}\cdot f)}$ | ops/sec | $f$ = fraction of valid pages | | 3 | **Write Amplification Factor (WAF)** | $WAF = \dfrac{\text{Data written to NAND}}{\text{Data written by host}}$ | dimensionless | $WAF \ge 1$; ideal $= 1$ | | 4 | **Drive Writes Per Day (DWPD)** | $DWPD = \dfrac{TBW}{C \cdot D}$ | writes/day | $C$ = capacity, $D$ = warranty days | | 5 | **Terabytes Written (Endurance)** | $TBW = DWPD \cdot C \cdot D$ | TB | Five-year warranty typical | | 6 | **SSD Lifetime (Years)** | $T_{life} = \dfrac{N_{PE} \cdot N_{blocks}}{WAF \cdot W_{host}}$ | years | $N_{PE}$ = P/E cycles per cell | | 7 | **Over-Provisioning (OP)** | $OP\% = \dfrac{C_{raw} - C_{usable}}{C_{usable}} \times 100$ | % | Enterprise SSDs: 7–28% | | 8 | **Effective Throughput** | $T = \dfrac{B}{t_{avg}} = \dfrac{B}{\alpha \cdot t_{r} + (1-\alpha)\cdot t_{w}}$ | MB/s | $\alpha$ = read fraction | | 9 | **Mean Time Between Failures** | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{AFR} \cdot 8{,}760$ | hours | $AFR$ = Annualized Failure Rate | | 10 | **Cost-per-GB** | $C_{GB} = \dfrac{P_{drive}}{C_{usable}}$ | USD/GB | $C_{usable}$ in GB | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** For a full 14-mark derivation, the two most-tested formulas are **WAF** and **DWPD/TBW**. Memorize the *definitions* (not just the symbols) — examiners award 2 marks for a precise definition. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility SSDs are critical in: - **Databases** (OLTP workloads): Random read/write IOPS bound. - **AI/ML training pipelines**: High-throughput streaming of large tensor files. - **Aerospace & Defense**: Vibration tolerance. - **Mobile / Embedded**: Low power draw (smartphones, IoT). - **Hybrid storage (SSHD)**: SSD as cache fronting HDD — the OS scheduler must decide placement. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Computing the Steady-State Random Write IOPS **Problem Statement:** Consider an SSD with the following manufacturer specifications: - Page size: $S_p = 16$ KB - Pages per block: $N_{p/b} = 128$ (so block size = 2 MB) - Read latency: $t_r = 80$ μs - Program (write) latency: $t_p = 500$ μs - Erase latency: $t_e = 2{,}000$ μs - Free block fraction reserved for GC: $f = 0.25$ (i.e., 25% of blocks held in reserve for garbage collection) - Host workload: 4 KB random writes **Step 1 — Determine the data movement per GC cycle.** Each GC cycle must copy the **valid pages** of a victim block to a free block, then erase the victim. With fraction $f$ reserved, the average victim block has $(1 - f) \cdot N_{p/b}$ valid pages that must be migrated. $$ \text{Valid pages to copy} = (1 - 0.25) \cdot 128 = 0.75 \cdot 128 = 96 \text{ pages} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the time cost of one GC cycle.** Each migrated page requires a read + program operation. Then the victim must be erased. $$ t_{GC} = N_{valid}\cdot(t_r + t_p) + t_e $$ $$ t_{GC} = 96 \cdot (80 + 500) \text{ μs} + 2{,}000 \text{ μs} $$ $$ t_{GC} = 96 \cdot 580 \text{ μs} + 2{,}000 \text{ μs} = 55{,}680 \text{ μs} + 2{,}000 \text{ μs} = 57{,}680 \text{ μs} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the equivalent data written by GC (in pages).** GC itself writes the 96 valid pages to new locations, equivalent to 96 page-programs. $$ N_{writes,GC} = 96 \text{ page-programs} $$ **Step 4 — Translate to per-second equivalents for IOPS formula.** Using the standard steady-state model (simplified): $$ IOPS_{ss} = \frac{N_{writes,GC} \cdot 1024 \text{ (per second normalization)}}{t_{GC}} $$ We need a 1-second budget. For each GC cycle consuming $57{,}680$ μs, the fraction of the second spent is $\frac{57{,}680}{1{,}000{,}000} \approx 0.0577$ s. In one full second, the number of GC cycles possible: $$ N_{GC,sec} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{57{,}680} \approx 17.34 \text{ cycles/sec} $$ Each cycle sustains 96 page-programs, so the **GC-allowed host write bandwidth** is: $$ B_{host} = 17.34 \cdot 96 \text{ pages/sec} \cdot 16 \text{ KB/page} \approx 26{,}643 \text{ MB/s} \cdot 10^{-3} $$ Wait — this is for one block; in reality we use the IOPS formulation. The **per-host-IO time** is dominated by GC duty cycle. For a 4 KB host write triggering partial block copy: $$ IOPS_{4K} = \frac{1}{t_{hostIO}} \approx \frac{N_{p/b}}{t_{GC}} \cdot \frac{S_p}{S_{hostIO}} $$ $$ IOPS_{4K} = \frac{128}{57{,}680 \times 10^{-6}} \cdot \frac{16}{4} = 2{,}219.4 \cdot 4 = 8{,}877 \text{ IOPS} $$ **Step 5 — State the steady-state IOPS in standard form.** $$ \boxed{IOPS_{ss} \approx 8{,}877 \text{ random-write IOPS at 4 KB}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** Notice how the *erase latency* ($2{,}000$ μs) and the *fraction of valid pages* dominate the cost. This is why **over-provisioning** (more free blocks) and **TRIM** (lowering valid-page fraction) are engineering imperatives. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — SSD Lifetime (Years) from TBW and Workload **Problem Statement:** A 1 TB enterprise TLC SSD is rated for **600 TBW** under a 5-year warranty. Compute: (a) the **DWPD** rating, (b) the **years to wear-out** under a sustained 30 GB/day write workload. **Step 1 — Compute DWPD.** $$ DWPD = \frac{TBW}{C \cdot D} $$ Where $C = 1$ TB, $D = 5 \cdot 365 = 1{,}825$ days. $$ DWPD = \frac{600 \text{ TB}}{1 \text{ TB} \cdot 1{,}825 \text{ days}} = \frac{600}{1{,}825} \approx 0.3288 \text{ writes/day} $$ $$ \boxed{DWPD \approx 0.33 \text{ writes/day}} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the daily write load in TB.** $$ W_{daily} = 30 \text{ GB/day} = 0.03 \text{ TB/day} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the equivalent DWPD of the workload.** $$ DWPD_{workload} = \frac{0.03 \text{ TB/day}}{1 \text{ TB}} = 0.03 \text{ writes/day} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the years to wear-out.** Total data the SSD can absorb: 600 TB. Total data written per year: $0.03 \cdot 365 = 10.95$ TB/yr. $$ T_{life} = \frac{600 \text{ TB}}{10.95 \text{ TB/yr}} \approx 54.79 \text{ years} $$ $$ \boxed{T_{life} \approx 54.8 \text{ years}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The SSD can *theoretically* last 54.8 years on this workload — but the *5-year warranty* assumes a much heavier workload (~0.33 DWPD = 109.5 GB/day). Always quote the **warranty-limited lifetime** in OS exam answers, not the theoretical lifetime. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Write Amplification Factor (WAF) for Sequential vs Random Workloads **Given:** A 256 GB SSD with raw capacity 256 GB (no over-provisioning, for simplicity). WAF for sequential writes = 1.0; WAF for random 4 KB writes = 4.0 (typical for consumer SSDs without TRIM). **Step 1 — Host writes 1 GB of data in each case.** **Sequential (WAF = 1):** $$ \text{NAND writes} = 1 \text{ GB} \cdot 1.0 = 1 \text{ GB} $$ **Random 4 KB (WAF = 4):** $$ \text{NAND writes} = 1 \text{ GB} \cdot 4.0 = 4 \text{ GB} $$ **Step 2 — Endurance impact (TLC: 3,000 P/E cycles).** $$ \text{Blocks available} = \frac{256 \text{ GB}}{2 \text{ MB/block}} = 131{,}072 \text{ blocks} $$ $$ \text{Max total NAND writes} = 131{,}072 \text{ blocks} \cdot 2 \text{ MB} \cdot 3{,}000 = 786{,}432 \text{ GB} \approx 768 \text{ TB} $$ **Step 3 — Compute host-writable TBW for each workload.** Sequential: $$ TBW_{seq} = \frac{768 \text{ TB}}{1.0} = 768 \text{ TB} $$ Random: $$ TBW_{rand} = \frac{768 \text{ TB}}{4.0} = 192 \text{ TB} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Trap:** Random 4 KB writes consume **4× the endurance budget** of sequential writes — even though the *host* wrote the same amount of data! This is why **filesystems align partitions to 4 KB boundaries** and why **TRIM** is non-optional on consumer SSDs. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — SSD vs HDD Across OS-Relevant Dimensions | OS-Relevant Dimension | HDD | SSD | Winner | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Seek Time | 3–10 ms | None (electrical) | SSD | | Rotational Latency | 0–half-rotation | None | SSD | | Transfer Rate (Sequential) | 100–200 MB/s | 500–7,000 MB/s | SSD | | Random IOPS | 75–200 | 10K–500K | SSD | | Latency Variance (Jitter) | High (mechanical) | Low (deterministic) | SSD | | Cost per GB | $0.02–0.04 | $0.06–0.15 | HDD | | Capacity (Single Drive) | Up to 22 TB | Up to 100 TB (QLC) | Tie | | Power (Idle) | 4–6 W | 0.05–0.5 W | SSD | | Endurance | Unlimited (mechanical wear) | Finite (P/E cycles) | HDD | | OS Scheduler Design | C-SCAN, LOOK (minimize seeks) | FCFS / NOOP (no seeks) | — | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 SSD Internal Block Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB hostA[Host CPU] --> ifaceA[Host Interface SATA NVMe SAS] ifaceA --> ctrlA[SSD Controller FTL Firmware ARM DSP] ctrlA --> dcacheA[DRAM Cache L2P Table Write Coalescing] ctrlA --> wearA[Wear Leveling Engine Dynamic Static] ctrlA --> gcA[Garbage Collector] ctrlA --> trimA[TRIM Handler] ctrlA --> eccA[ECC Engine LDPC BCH] dcacheA --> nandA[NAND Flash Array] wearA --> nandA gcA --> nandA trimA --> nandA eccA --> nandA nandA --> planeA[Plane 0 to Plane N] planeA --> blockA[Block 0 to Block M] blockA --> pageA[Page 0 to Page K 16 KB] ``` ### 4.2 NAND Flash Hierarchy: Die → Plane → Block → Page ```mermaid flowchart TB die1[Die Chip 1] --> plane1A[Plane 0] die1 --> plane1B[Plane 1] plane1A --> block1A[Block 0 2 MB 128 pages] plane1A --> block1B[Block 1] plane1B --> block1C[Block 128] block1A --> page1A[Page 0 16 KB] block1A --> page1B[Page 1] block1A --> page1C[Page 2] block1A --> page1D[Page 127] page1A --> cell1A[Floating Gate Cells 16384 bytes plus metadata] ``` ### 4.3 Read / Program / Erase Flow (Asymmetric Primitives) ```mermaid flowchart LR startA[Host Issues Read] --> readOpA[FTL Translates LBA to PPN] readOpA --> senseA[Apply Gate Voltage Sense Threshold] senseA --> eccA2[LDPC Decode Correct Errors] eccA2 --> returnA[Return Page to Host] startB[Host Issues Write] --> progOpA[FTL Allocates Free Page] progOpA --> checkA{Block Erased?} checkA --> yesA[Program Page Set 1 to 0] checkA --> noA[Schedule GC Move Valid Pages Erase Block] noA --> yesA yesA --> commitA[Update L2P Map Mark Old PPN Invalid] startC[Garbage Collection Trigger] --> pickA[Pick Victim Block Few Valid Pages] pickA --> copyA[Copy Valid Pages to Free Block] copyA --> eraseA[Block Erase Fowler Nordheim Tunneling] eraseA --> readyA[Block Added to Free Pool] ``` ### 4.4 TRIM Lifecycle in the OS-SSD Contract ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant UserApp as User App participant FS as Filesystem ext4 NTFS participant OS as OS Block Layer participant SSD as SSD Controller participant NAND as NAND Flash UserApp->>FS: rm file.dat delete syscall FS->>FS: Mark inodes and bitmap as free FS->>OS: Return success OS->>SSD: TRIM LBA range 1000 to 2000 SSD->>SSD: Mark pages 1000 to 2000 as INVALID Note over SSD: Future GC skips these pages
Lower WAF Higher sustained IOPS SSD->>NAND: Garbage Collection moves only valid pages NAND-->>SSD: Block erased freed ``` ### 4.5 Decision Flow — Should the OS Issue TRIM? ```mermaid flowchart TD q1A{Filesystem mounted with discard?} q1A --> yesA2[Yes: Continuous TRIM on every delete] q1A --> noA2[No: Periodic batched fstrim via cron] noA2 --> q2A{Workload write heavy random?} q2A --> yesA3[Yes: Run fstrim weekly to maintain WAF] q2A --> noA3[No: Monthly is sufficient] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **"Differentiate between SLC, MLC, TLC, and QLC NAND flash. State one typical application of each."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Cell Type | Bits/Cell | P/E Cycles | Application | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | | **SLC** | 1 | ~100,000 | Enterprise servers, military | | **MLC** | 2 | ~10,000 | Prosumer notebooks, NAS | | **TLC** | 3 | ~3,000 | Consumer SSDs, smartphones | | **QLC** | 4 | ~1,000 | Read-heavy archive, cold storage | *(1 mark for correct bits/cell, 1 mark for P/E cycles trend, 1 mark for application mapping)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]** **"What is the TRIM command? Why is it essential for SSD performance?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** TRIM is an ATA/SATA command (with NVMe equivalent: *Dataset Management*) that informs the SSD of LBAs no longer in use by the filesystem. - **Mechanism (1 mark):** The OS issues TRIM after a file delete; the SSD's FTL marks the corresponding physical pages as *invalid*, making them eligible for garbage collection. - **Why essential (1 mark):** Without TRIM, the SSD must copy *stale* pages during GC, raising **Write Amplification Factor (WAF)**, dropping sustained IOPS, and shortening endurance. --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of a Solid-State Disk (SSD)**. Identify the role of the Flash Translation Layer (FTL). **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **asymmetric read, program, and erase operations** of NAND flash. Show how this asymmetry forces the use of **garbage collection** and **wear leveling**. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) SSD Architecture (7 marks):** The SSD is a four-layer stack: 1. **NAND Flash Array (Physical Layer) – 2 marks:** Organized as Die → Plane → Block → Page. A typical block is 2 MB (128 pages × 16 KB). The floating-gate transistor is the storage primitive. 2. **SSD Controller / FTL – 2 marks:** The on-board microcontroller runs the Flash Translation Layer firmware. The FTL: - Maintains the **L2P (Logical-to-Physical) mapping table** in DRAM. - Performs **wear leveling, garbage collection, bad block management, and ECC**. 3. **DRAM Cache – 1 mark:** Volatile buffer (256 MB – 4 GB) holding the L2P table and **coalescing random writes** into aligned block writes. 4. **Host Interface – 1 mark:** SATA III, NVMe over PCIe, SAS — bandwidth scales from 600 MB/s (SATA) to 7+ GB/s (PCIe Gen4 NVMe). 5. **Block Diagram – 1 mark:** (Draw or describe the four-layer block diagram as in Section 4.1 above.) --- **(b) Asymmetric Primitives, Garbage Collection & Wear Leveling (7 marks):** **1. The Asymmetry (2 marks):** | Operation | Granularity | Latency | Asymmetry Reason | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Read | Page (16 KB) | 80 μs | Sense charge via gate voltage | | Program | Page (16 KB) | 500 μs | Quantum tunneling into floating gate (1→0 only) | | Erase | Block (2 MB) | 2,000 μs | Fowler–Nordheim tunneling of whole block (0→1 only) | The asymmetry: **write is page-granular, but erase is block-granular.** A page cannot be individually overwritten — it must be erased as part of its containing block. **2. Why Garbage Collection Becomes Necessary (3 marks):** - Random writes leave a block with a mix of *valid* (still referenced) and *invalid* (overwritten/deleted) pages. - To reclaim space, the GC picks a victim block, reads the valid pages, writes them to a free block, and erases the victim. - This causes **Write Amplification (WAF > 1)** and **read-modify-write overhead**, which is why SSDs show degraded steady-state performance vs burst performance. **3. Wear Leveling (2 marks):** - Both SLC/MLC/TLC cells wear out after a finite number of P/E cycles (e.g., 3,000 for TLC). - **Dynamic wear leveling** spreads writes across free blocks. - **Static wear leveling** relocates *cold* (read-only) data so the *hot* blocks can be cycled, equalizing wear. - Without wear leveling, log files and swap partitions would prematurely burn out their blocks. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Define **Write Amplification Factor (WAF)** and **Terabytes Written (TBW)**. A 512 GB TLC SSD is rated at 300 TBW. Calculate the **DWPD** under a 5-year warranty. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **TRIM command** in detail. With a sequence diagram, show how TRIM interacts with the filesystem, OS block layer, and SSD controller. State two consequences of *not* issuing TRIM. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) WAF, TBW & DWPD Calculation (7 marks):** **Definitions (3 marks):** - **WAF** = (Data written to NAND flash) / (Data written by host). Always $\ge 1$. Ideal WAF = 1 (only achievable for large sequential writes with TRIM). - **TBW** = Total terabytes the SSD can write over its service life before cells wear out. It is a direct measure of endurance. **Calculation (4 marks):** Given: $C = 512$ GB, $TBW = 300$ TB, Warranty $D = 5 \cdot 365 = 1{,}825$ days. $$ DWPD = \frac{TBW}{C \cdot D} $$ $$ DWPD = \frac{300 \text{ TB}}{0.512 \text{ TB} \cdot 1{,}825 \text{ days}} $$ $$ DWPD = \frac{300}{934.4} \approx 0.321 \text{ writes/day} $$ $$ \boxed{DWPD \approx 0.32 \text{ writes/day}} $$ --- **(b) TRIM Command (7 marks):** **1. Definition (2 marks):** TRIM is an ATA command (issued by the OS) that informs the SSD's FTL of LBAs the filesystem no longer needs. The NVMe equivalent is the **Dataset Management** command. **2. Mechanism (3 marks):** Sequence of events: - User runs `rm file.dat` → filesystem updates its inode table and free-space bitmap. - OS (or batched `fstrim`) issues TRIM to the SSD specifying the LBA range. - The SSD's FTL marks the corresponding Physical Page Numbers (PPNs) as *invalid* in its L2P table. - During future **garbage collection**, the SSD skips these invalid pages — they do not need to be copied. **3. Two Consequences of Not Issuing TRIM (2 marks):** - **(i) WAF inflation:** The GC must copy *stale* pages that the host considers deleted but the SSD does not. WAF can rise to 4–10×. - **(ii) Performance degradation:** Sustained write IOPS drop dramatically (steady-state IOPS may fall to 10–20% of peak) because GC is constantly busy migrating useless data. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on SSD Questions:** > 1. **Confusing page with cell:** A *cell* stores 1–4 bits; a *page* is the smallest I/O unit (4–16 KB). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this slip. > 2. **Forgetting the "write-before-erase" rule:** Saying "SSD supports random writes" without noting the **page-program then block-erase** asymmetry is incomplete — lose 1 mark. > 3. **Skipping the role of the FTL:** The Flash Translation Layer is the *heart* of the SSD; any 7-mark architecture question that omits the FTL loses 2 marks. > 4. **Units in DWPD/TBW:** Capacity must be in *TB* (not GB) when using the standard DWPD formula. A 512 GB drive = 0.512 TB. Mixing units is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. > 5. **TRIM ≠ "secure erase":** Students sometimes describe TRIM as a security command. It is a *performance* command; the secure-erase ATA command is different. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **SSD = no moving parts, NAND flash based**, controlled by an embedded FTL. - [x] Three primitives: **Read (page, ~80 μs), Program (page, ~500 μs), Erase (block, ~2000 μs).** - [x] **Asymmetry:** writes are page-granular; erases are block-granular → forces garbage collection. - [x] **Cell types:** SLC (1 bit, 100K P/E) → MLC (2, 10K) → TLC (3, 3K) → QLC (4, 1K). - [x] **WAF** = NAND writes / Host writes; always $\ge 1$; ideal = 1. - [x] **DWPD** = $\dfrac{TBW}{C \cdot D}$; **TBW** = DWPD × C × D. - [x] **TRIM** is an OS-to-SSD command marking LBAs as invalid; lowers WAF, sustains IOPS. - [x] **Wear leveling** has two flavors: *dynamic* (on free blocks) and *static* (on cold data). - [x] **FTL** owns the L2P mapping table — without it, the SSD cannot emulate a block device. - [x] **Over-provisioning** (7–28% extra raw NAND) is enterprise SSD best practice; improves WAF and endurance. - [x] **OS scheduler implication:** SSDs do not benefit from seek-minimizing algorithms (C-SCAN, LOOK); the **NOOP** or **deadline** scheduler is preferred. - [x] **Filesystem alignment to 4 KB** is mandatory to avoid read-modify-write penalties. - [x] **Killer advantage triad:** no seek time, no rotational latency, no mechanical failure.

topics.title: Disk Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Disk Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disk Structure** refers to the physical and logical organization of a secondary storage device (magnetic hard disk), describing how data is stored, addressed, and retrieved through concentric circles called **tracks**, pie-shaped segments called **sectors**, and the 3D cylindrical abstraction known as a **cylinder**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Operating Systems syllabus, *Disk Structure* is the foundational concept that bridges **secondary storage hardware** and the **file system layer** of the OS. The OS views the disk as a linear array of logical blocks (also called **disk blocks** or **sectors**), each of fixed size — typically **512 bytes** in older disks and **4096 bytes (4 KB)** in modern **Advanced Format (AF)** drives.

The structure can be analyzed at **two levels**:

| Level | Description | Granularity |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical Structure** | Real geometry of platters, tracks, sectors, heads | Spinning mechanical layout |
| **Logical Structure** | Linearized block address space presented to the OS | Uniform `LBA` block numbers |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Vinyl Record Library"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a vast circular library building with multiple **floors** (platters), where each floor has **concentric circular bookshelves** (tracks). Every shelf is divided into identical **cubby holes** (sectors) holding one "page" of data. A robotic arm with a camera on each floor (read/write head) moves radially to pick the right shelf and reads the right cubby.

- The **platter** = floor of the library
- The **track** = circular bookshelf
- The **sector** = cubby hole (the smallest addressable unit)
- The **read/write head** = robotic arm with a camera
- The **spindle** = central rotating pillar
- The **cylinder** = same-radius bookshelves across **all** floors (you can read them without moving the arm)

This mental model makes the terms *track*, *sector*, *cylinder*, and *seek* instantly intuitive for first-time learners.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (KTU Board Emphasis)

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Numerical Values to Memorise:**
> - **Sector size** = **512 bytes** (legacy) or **4096 bytes (4 KB)** (modern AF)
> - **Track-to-track seek time** ≈ **1–2 ms**
> - **Average seek time** ≈ **8–10 ms** (modern HDD)
> - **Average rotational latency** = **$\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{60}{RPM}$ seconds**
> - **For 7200 RPM** → average rotational latency ≈ **4.16 ms**
> - **Platter rotation speed** = **5400, 7200, 10000, 15000 RPM** (typical)

These constants appear regularly in KTU numerical problems, especially in the **disk scheduling** module that follows.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Anatomy of a Magnetic Disk

A modern HDD is a precision mechanical device. From bottom-up:

1. **Spindle Motor** — Rotates the platters at constant angular velocity (CAV).
2. **Platter(s)** — Rigid circular substrates coated with magnetic material. Standard disks have **1 to 5 platters**.
3. **Read/Write Head(s)** — One per platter surface (top + bottom). A 3-platter disk has **6 heads**.
4. **Actuator Arm** — Moves heads radially in/out across the platter.
5. **Controller / Interface** — SATA, NVMe, SAS bridges the disk to the system bus.

### 2.1.1 Track
A **track** is a single concentric circle on a platter surface where data is magnetically written. Numbered from the **outer edge (Track 0)** to the **innermost track**.

### 2.1.2 Sector
A **sector** is the smallest physical storage unit on a track. The platter's circumference is divided into fixed angular wedges, each holding **512 or 4096 bytes** of user data plus **syndrome bytes** (ECC).

### 2.1.3 Cylinder
A **cylinder** is the collection of all tracks at the **same radius** on **all platters** simultaneously. Since all heads move in unison, accessing any track within one cylinder requires **no seek time** — only rotational latency and head-switching (≈ 0.5–1 ms).

### 2.1.4 Cluster / Block
The OS may group consecutive sectors into a **cluster** (Windows terminology) or **block** (Unix terminology) for allocation efficiency. The cluster is the **smallest unit the file system allocates to a file**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why ZBR (Zone Bit Recording) matters:** Outer tracks have a longer circumference → can hold more sectors. Modern disks partition the surface into **zones**, where each zone uses a constant sectors-per-track value. Outer zones are faster (more data per revolution) — the OS places frequently-accessed data on outer tracks when possible.

---

## 2.2 Logical Disk Structure (The OS's View)

The OS does **not** see tracks, sectors, or cylinders. It sees a **linear array of logical blocks**, each addressable by a **Logical Block Address (LBA)**.

$$\text{LBA} = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$

where $N$ is the total number of addressable sectors. The disk controller translates LBA → (cylinder, head, sector) — historically called **CHS addressing**.

### 2.2.1 CHS → LBA Conversion

$$\text{LBA} = ((C \times H_{per\_cyl}) + H) \times S_{per\_track} + (S - 1)$$

- $C$ = cylinder number
- $H$ = head number
- $S$ = sector number (1-based)
- $H_{per\_cyl}$ = total number of heads
- $S_{per\_track}$ = sectors per track

### 2.2.2 LBA → CHS Conversion

$$C = \frac{\text{LBA}}{S_{per\_track} \times H_{per\_cyl}}$$

$$H = \frac{\text{LBA}}{S_{per\_track}} \mod H_{per\_cyl}$$

$$S = (\text{LBA} \mod S_{per\_track}) + 1$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_r$ | **Rotational Latency (avg)** | $\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{60}{RPM}$ | seconds |
| $T_s$ | **Seek Time** | Given (depends on cylinder distance) | ms |
| $T_t$ | **Transfer Time** | $\frac{\text{Bytes Transferred}}{\text{Transfer Rate}}$ | ms |
| $T_a$ | **Total Access Time** | $T_s + T_r + T_t$ | ms |
| $b$ | **Bytes per sector** | Typically 512 or 4096 | bytes |
| $L$ | **LBA address** | Linear index of sector | unitless |
| $C$ | **Capacity** | $Cyl \times Heads \times SPT \times b$ | bytes |
| $T_{rot}$ | **Time for one full rotation** | $\frac{60}{RPM}$ | seconds |
| $Z$ | **Zone transfer rate** | $\frac{S_{per\_track\_in\_zone} \times b}{T_{rot}}$ | bytes/sec |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** The sector number $S$ in CHS is **1-based**, but in LBA it is **0-based**. This is a common KTU exam trap.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **RAID Arrays** — Disk structure knowledge is essential to compute stripe sizes and parity placement.
- **SSD vs HDD** — SSDs have **no platters, no heads, no rotational latency** — only NAND flash pages and blocks. Structure concepts still apply logically.
- **Disk Scheduling (FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK)** — Heavily relies on cylinder/track numbers as inputs.
- **Defragmentation Tools** — Use cylinder locality to minimize seek time.
- **Database Design** — DBAs place hot tables on outer cylinders for higher throughput.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Time to Read a File from Disk

**Problem Context:** Read $K$ sectors of a file. The relevant head starts at cylinder $C_1$ (after the previous operation) and must reach cylinder $C_2$.

**Step 1 — Identify the three time components:**

> The total access time to retrieve data from disk is the sum of three sequential, non-overlapping delays. We model the disk access as a pipeline: position the head, wait for the sector to spin under the head, then read the bytes.

$$T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Seek Time:**

> The seek time depends on how far the head must travel. KTU problems either (a) give the actual seek time, (b) provide a formula $T_s = a + b \cdot d$ where $d = \vert C_2 - C_1 \vert$, or (c) assume $T_s$ = average seek time.

$$T_{seek} = a + b \cdot \vert C_2 - C_1 \vert$$

where $a$ is the startup/cell-overhead time and $b$ is the per-cylinder travel time.

**Step 3 — Compute Rotational Latency (Average Case):**

> The disk rotates at constant angular velocity. The desired sector's angular position is uniformly random relative to the head. Therefore, on average the head waits for **half a revolution**.

$$T_{rot} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot T_{one\_rotation} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{60}{RPM} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Transfer Time:**

> Transfer time is bytes-read divided by the disk's transfer rate. If consecutive sectors are read in a single rotation, $T_{transfer}$ scales with the number of sectors.

$$T_{transfer} = \frac{K \cdot b}{\text{Transfer Rate}}$$

For a track of $S_{per\_track}$ sectors read in one full revolution:

$$T_{transfer} = \frac{K}{S_{per\_track}} \cdot T_{one\_rotation}$$

**Step 5 — Combine:**

$$\boxed{T_{access} = (a + b \cdot \vert C_2 - C_1 \vert) + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{60}{RPM} + \frac{K \cdot b}{TransferRate}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Numerical Style

**Problem:** A disk has **4 platters (8 surfaces)**, **4096 cylinders**, and an **average of 1024 sectors per track**. Each sector is **4096 bytes**. The disk rotates at **7200 RPM**, average seek time is **8 ms**, and the controller transfer rate is **200 MB/s**. Calculate:

(a) Total disk capacity.
(b) Time to read a 64 KB file assuming the head is at the correct cylinder.
(c) Total access time including an 8 ms seek.

**Solution:**

**(a) Capacity:**

$$C = 4096 \text{ cyl} \times 8 \text{ heads} \times 1024 \text{ sectors} \times 4096 \text{ bytes}$$

$$C = 4096 \times 8 \times 1024 \times 4096$$

Let us evaluate step by step.

$$4096 \times 8 = 32768$$

$$32768 \times 1024 = 33{,}554{,}432$$

$$33{,}554{,}432 \times 4096 = 137{,}438{,}953{,}472 \text{ bytes}$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 137.44 \text{ GB}}$$

**(b) Read time (no seek, head on cylinder):**

64 KB file → number of sectors:

$$K = \frac{64 \times 1024}{4096} = 16 \text{ sectors}$$

Rotational latency at 7200 RPM:

$$T_{rot} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{60}{7200} = \frac{1}{240} \text{ s} = 4.1667 \text{ ms}$$

Transfer time:

$$T_{transfer} = \frac{16 \times 4096}{200 \times 10^6} = \frac{65536}{2 \times 10^8} = 3.2768 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.328 \text{ ms}$$

Total:

$$T_{access} = 0 + 4.1667 + 0.328 = 4.494 \text{ ms}$$

$$\boxed{T_{access} \approx 4.49 \text{ ms}}$$

**(c) With 8 ms seek:**

$$T_{access} = 8 + 4.49 = 12.49 \text{ ms}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Component Matters

| Component | Magnitude (7200 RPM) | % of typical access | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Seek Time | 3–12 ms | **~50%** | Disk Scheduling (SCAN, LOOK) |
| Rotational Latency | ~4.16 ms | **~30%** | Higher RPM, page placement |
| Transfer Time | 0.1–1 ms | **~5%** | Larger block reads, prefetch |
| Controller overhead | < 1 ms | **~5%** | NCQ, AHCI, NVMe |
| OS / Queueing | 0–20 ms | **~10%** | Better I/O schedulers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Seek time dominates → KTU's emphasis on disk scheduling algorithms in the next section is **not** academic — it is the single biggest performance lever.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Physical Disk Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ACTUATOR["ACTUATOR ASSEMBLY"]
        ARM["Actuator Arm"]
```
