```mermaid
        HEAD1["Read/Write Head 1"]
        HEAD2["Read/Write Head 2"]
        HEAD3["Read/Write Head 3"]
        HEAD4["Read/Write Head 4"]
        ARM --> HEAD1
        ARM --> HEAD2
        ARM --> HEAD3
        ARM --> HEAD4
    end

    subgraph SPINDLE["SPINDLE MOTOR"]

    end

    subgraph PLATTERS["PLATTER STACK"]

    subgraph CONTROLLER["DISK CONTROLLER"]

    HEAD1 -.-> P1
    HEAD2 -.-> P2
    HEAD3 -.-> P3

```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Track / Sector / Cylinder Relationship

```mermaid

    subgraph CYL3["CYLINDER 3 OUTER RADIUS"]
        T3P1["Track on Platter 1"]

    end

    subgraph CYL1["CYLINDER 1 MIDDLE RADIUS"]
        T1P1["Track on Platter 1"]

    end

    subgraph CYL0["CYLINDER 0 INNER RADIUS"]
        T0P1["Track on Platter 1"]
        T0P2["Track on Platter 2"]
        T0P3["Track on Platter 3"]
    end

    S1["Sector A 512 B"]
    S2["Sector B 512 B"]
    S3["Sector C 512 B"]
    S4["Sector D 512 B"]

    T1P2 -. contains .-> S1
    T1P2 -. contains .-> S2
    T1P2 -. contains .-> S3
    T1P2 -. contains .-> S4

    CYL0 --> CYL1 --> CYL3
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow — LBA to CHS Translation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["OS Issues READ LBA 5000"] --> B["Controller Receives LBA"]
    B --> C{"Compute Cylinder C"}
    C --> C1["C = LBA div SPHT"]
    C1 --> D{"Compute Head H"}
    D --> D1["H = LBA div SPT mod HPC"]
    D1 --> E{"Compute Sector S"}
    E --> E1["S = LBA mod SPT + 1"]
    E1 --> F["Move Head to Cylinder C"]
    F --> G["Activate Head H"]
    G --> H["Wait for Sector S to rotate under head"]
    H --> I["Read Data into Buffer"]
    I --> J["Transfer to OS via DMA"]

    style A fill:#fff4cc
    style J fill:#c8e6c9
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic Textual View — Zone Bit Recording

```
       ZONE 1 (outermost)  :  1024 sectors/track   →  HIGHEST transfer rate
       ─────────────────────
       ZONE 2              :   896 sectors/track
       ─────────────────────
       ZONE 3              :   768 sectors/track
       ─────────────────────
       ZONE 4              :   640 sectors/track
       ─────────────────────
       ZONE 5 (innermost)  :   512 sectors/track   →  LOWEST transfer rate
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because outer zones transfer more bytes per rotation, modern disks place the **Master Boot Record (MBR) and file system metadata** on outer tracks for faster boot times.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "cylinder" with respect to magnetic disk geometry. Why is cylinder-based access faster than random access?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **cylinder** is the collection of all tracks at the **same radial distance** from the spindle across **all platters** in a disk stack. Since all read/write heads are mounted on a single actuator arm, they move in unison. Reading data from different tracks within the same cylinder requires **only head-switching** (≈ 0.5 ms), with **no radial seek**. In contrast, random access between different cylinders incurs full **seek time** (3–12 ms). Hence, cylinder-local access is significantly faster and is exploited by file systems to place related data in adjacent cylinders.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between CHS addressing and LBA addressing. State one advantage of LBA.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | CHS | LBA |
|---|---|---|
| Full form | Cylinder-Head-Sector | Logical Block Addressing |
| Address type | 3D (C, H, S) | 1D linear index |
| BIOS support | Legacy | Modern |
| Limit | 504 MB (old BIOS) | 2 TB (32-bit) / 8 ZB (64-bit) |
| Complexity | Controller does math | OS/controller both use LBA |

**Advantage of LBA:** Simpler for the OS — the controller handles all geometry math, and LBA scales to huge disks without BIOS restrictions.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the physical structure of a magnetic disk with a neat diagram. List the components and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hard disk has **4 surfaces**, **256 tracks per surface**, and **64 sectors per track**. Each sector holds **512 bytes**. The disk rotates at **3600 RPM** and the average seek time is **20 ms**. Calculate:
  (i) Total disk capacity in MB.
  (ii) Average rotational latency.
  (iii) Time to transfer **1024 sectors** assuming head is on the correct track. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Physical Structure of a Magnetic Disk (7 marks):**

The physical structure of a magnetic hard disk consists of the following major components:

1. **Platters** — Rigid circular disks (typically aluminum or glass) coated with a thin magnetic layer. Data is stored as magnetized regions.
2. **Spindle** — The central shaft on which platters are mounted. The spindle motor rotates them at a constant RPM.
3. **Read/Write Heads** — Electromagnetic transducers, one per platter surface. They read magnetic flux or write new magnetization.
4. **Actuator Arm** — A precisely controlled mechanical arm that positions the heads radially across the platter.
5. **Actuator** — The motor (voice-coil type) that moves the actuator arm.
6. **Controller & Cache** — On-board electronics with a small RAM buffer and the SATA/IDE interface.

Each platter surface is divided into **concentric tracks**, each track into **sectors** of 512/4096 bytes. Tracks at the same radius across all platters form a **cylinder**.

> The platters and heads are sealed inside a **plenum** filled with inert helium or low-pressure air to minimize turbulence and contamination.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $N_{surf} = 4$
- $T_{ps} = 256$ tracks/surface
- $S_{pt} = 64$ sectors/track
- $b = 512$ bytes
- $RPM = 3600$
- $T_{seek} = 20$ ms

**(i) Total Capacity:**

$$C = N_{surf} \times T_{ps} \times S_{pt} \times b$$

$$C = 4 \times 256 \times 64 \times 512$$

$$C = 4 \times 256 = 1024$$

$$1024 \times 64 = 65536$$

$$65536 \times 512 = 33{,}554{,}432 \text{ bytes}$$

Converting to MB (1 MB = 1024 × 1024 = 1,048,576 bytes):

$$C = \frac{33{,}554{,}432}{1{,}048{,}576} = 32 \text{ MB}$$

$$\boxed{C = 32 \text{ MB}}$$

**(ii) Average Rotational Latency:**

$$T_{rot} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{60}{3600} = \frac{1}{120} \text{ s}$$

$$T_{rot} = \frac{1000}{120} = 8.33 \text{ ms}$$

$$\boxed{T_{rot} = 8.33 \text{ ms}}$$

**(iii) Time to Transfer 1024 Sectors:**

One full rotation transfers all sectors on a track:

$$\text{Bytes per track} = 64 \times 512 = 32768 \text{ bytes}$$

$$\text{Number of tracks needed} = \frac{1024}{64} = 16 \text{ tracks}$$

Time for one rotation:

$$T_{one\_rev} = \frac{60}{3600} = \frac{1}{60} \text{ s} = 16.67 \text{ ms}$$

Time to read 16 tracks (assuming sequential, no inter-track seek counted):

$$T_{transfer} = 16 \times 16.67 = 266.67 \text{ ms}$$

Total access time (no seek, head on correct track as stated):

$$T_{access} = 0 + 8.33 + 266.67 = 275.0 \text{ ms}$$

$$\boxed{T_{access} = 275.0 \text{ ms}}$$

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the logical block addressing scheme. How is an LBA converted to CHS? Derive the formulas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A disk has **2 platters, 1024 cylinders, 128 sectors per track, 512 bytes per sector**. Compute:
  (i) Total disk capacity.
  (ii) The CHS address corresponding to **LBA = 250,000**.
  (iii) The LBA corresponding to **CHS = (512, 3, 64)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Logical Block Addressing (7 marks):**

In **Logical Block Addressing (LBA)**, the entire disk is viewed by the OS as a **one-dimensional linear array of sectors**, indexed from 0 to $N-1$, where $N$ is the total number of sectors. The disk controller internally translates each LBA into a physical **(Cylinder, Head, Sector)** triplet.

**LBA → CHS Formulas:**

The total sectors per cylinder is:

$$SPHT = HPC \times SPT$$

where $HPC$ = heads per cylinder and $SPT$ = sectors per track.

**Cylinder:**

$$C = \left\lfloor \frac{LBA}{SPHT} \right\rfloor$$

**Head (after removing full cylinders):**

$$temp = LBA \mod SPHT$$

$$H = \left\lfloor \frac{temp}{SPT} \right\rfloor$$

**Sector (1-based):**

$$S = (temp \mod SPT) + 1$$

> These formulas are derived because sectors are laid out **sector-by-sector** within a track, **track-by-track** within a cylinder, and **cylinder-by-cylinder** across the disk.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- 2 platters → 4 heads
- $C_{max} = 1024$
- $SPT = 128$
- $b = 512$

**HPC = 4, SPHT = 4 × 128 = 512**

**(i) Total Capacity:**

$$C = 1024 \times 4 \times 128 \times 512 = 268{,}435{,}456 \text{ bytes} = 256 \text{ MB}$$

**(ii) LBA 250,000 → CHS:**

$$C = \left\lfloor \frac{250000}{512} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 488.28 \right\rfloor = 488$$

$$temp = 250000 \mod 512 = 250000 - (488 \times 512) = 250000 - 249856 = 144$$

$$H = \left\lfloor \frac{144}{128} \right\rfloor = 1$$

$$S = (144 \mod 128) + 1 = 16 + 1 = 17$$

$$\boxed{\text{LBA } 250000 \rightarrow \text{CHS } (488, 1, 17)}$$

**(iii) CHS (512, 3, 64) → LBA:**

$$LBA = ((C \times HPC) + H) \times SPT + (S - 1)$$

$$LBA = ((512 \times 4) + 3) \times 128 + (64 - 1)$$

$$LBA = (2048 + 3) \times 128 + 63$$

$$LBA = 2051 \times 128 + 63$$

$$LBA = 262528 + 63$$

$$\boxed{LBA = 262591}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions to AVOID:**
> 1. **Forgetting the +1 in sector number** — CHS sectors are 1-based, LBA is 0-based. Forgetting this loses 1–2 marks per sub-question.
> 2. **Using 1-based cylinder count** — If a disk has "1024 cylinders", valid C values are **0 to 1023**, not 1 to 1024.
> 3. **Confusing HDD and SSD latency** — Do not apply rotational latency to SSDs in modern questions. Read the question carefully.
> 4. **Mixing MB definitions** — KTU typically uses **1 MB = 1024 × 1024 bytes**. Some questions use **1 MB = 10⁶ bytes** (SI). Check the question paper.
> 5. **Skipping unit conversions** — RPM gives time in seconds. Convert to **ms** before adding to seek time.
> 6. **Drawing the diagram but not labelling** — A diagram of a disk without **platter, spindle, head, arm, track, sector** labels gets **partial credit only**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- A **track** is a concentric circle; a **sector** is a 512/4096-byte pie-slice of a track.
- A **cylinder** = all tracks of the same radius across all platters. **Cylinder access is fastest** (no seek).
- A **cluster/block** is the OS-level allocation unit, made of multiple sectors.
- **CHS → LBA**: $LBA = ((C \times HPC) + H) \times SPT + (S - 1)$.
- **LBA → CHS**: Divide by SPHT → cylinder; remainder/HPH → head; final remainder + 1 → sector.
- **Average rotational latency** = $30{,}000 / RPM$ in **ms** (or $\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{60}{RPM}$ in seconds).
- **Total access time** = Seek + Rotational Latency + Transfer.
- **Transfer time** = bytes ÷ transfer rate.
- **Capacity** = cylinders × heads × sectors/track × bytes/sector.
- **Zone Bit Recording (ZBR)** → outer tracks = more sectors = higher throughput.
- Modern HDDs use **LBA**; old BIOS systems used **CHS** (504 MB limit).
- **SSDs have no seek/rotation** — they use NAND flash pages, but still expose an **LBA interface**.
- Constant values: **7200 RPM → 4.16 ms latency**; **512 bytes/sector** (legacy); **4096 bytes/sector** (AF).
- The dominant cost in disk access is **seek time (≈ 50%)** → motivates **disk scheduling algorithms** in the next module.
- **Diagram essentials:** platters, spindle, arms, heads, tracks (concentric), sectors (pie), and label all of them.
- Numericals always specify the **starting cylinder** for seek; without it, use **average seek time**.

> **End of Module 4 — Disk Structure Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disk Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Disk Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Disk Scheduling** is a fundamental Operating System resource management technique that determines the optimal sequence in which multiple pending I/O read/write requests to the computer's secondary storage (hard disk / SSD) are serviced by the **disk arm (read/write head)**. The primary objective is to minimize the total **seek time**, reduce the average **response time**, and maximize **disk throughput** by reorganizing the order of request execution.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, disk scheduling is studied under the broader umbrella of **Secondary Storage Management**, which also includes disk formatting, boot blocks, bad-block management, and RAID architectures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disk Scheduling** is the process of selecting the order in which disk I/O requests are serviced to minimize the mechanical movement of the read/write head across the disk platters.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Anchors)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Track** | A concentric circle on a disk platter where data is magnetically stored. |
| **Cylinder** | The set of all tracks at the same radial distance on multiple platters. |
| **Sector** | The smallest addressable unit on a disk, typically **512 bytes** or **4096 bytes**. |
| **Seek Time** | The time taken by the disk arm to move the read/write head to the correct cylinder. |
| **Rotational Latency** | The delay waiting for the platter to rotate the desired sector under the head. |
| **Transfer Time** | The actual time taken to read/write data once the head is positioned. |
| **Disk Access Time** | $\text{Seek Time} + \text{Rotational Latency} + \text{Transfer Time}$ |
| **Disk Arm / Head** | The mechanical assembly that reads/writes data from the disk surface. |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU Board Examination focuses predominantly on **Seek Time** optimization, as it is the most variable and optimizable component of disk access time. **Rotational latency** is often considered constant for a given RPM rating.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Book Fetcher" Model

Imagine you are a **librarian** in a massive library with 200 shelves numbered 0 to 199. Eight students simultaneously request books from shelves `98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67`. You are currently standing in front of shelf **53**.

- **Bad Strategy (FCFS):** Walk to each student in order. Total distance covered = 640 shelves. You walk back and forth chaotically.
- **Smart Strategy (SSTF):** Always serve the *nearest* pending request first. Total distance = 236 shelves.
- **Elevator Strategy (SCAN):** Walk to one end of the library first, then sweep to the other end, servicing requests on the way. Total = 331 shelves.
- **Circular Elevator (C-SCAN):** Walk to the end, then teleport back to the start and walk forward again. Total = 382 shelves.

The disk arm behaves exactly like this librarian, and the cylinders are the shelves. **The goal is to minimize total head movement, which directly translates to time and energy efficiency.**

> [!TIP]
> Whenever you feel confused between algorithms in an exam, draw the disk arm as a "librarian" sweeping across the cylinders, and visualize the algorithm in motion. This trick clears 90% of conceptual doubts in less than 30 seconds.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need Disk Scheduling?

When multiple processes issue I/O requests concurrently, the requests arrive in a **random order**. If served in arrival order, the disk arm makes erratic, long jumps, wasting mechanical effort. Disk scheduling algorithms are designed to:

1. **Minimize Seek Time** — The dominant cost in disk access.
2. **Maximize Throughput** — Number of I/O operations completed per unit time.
3. **Minimize Average Response Time** — Critical for interactive and real-time systems.
4. **Reduce Starvation** — Ensure fairness so no request waits forever.
5. **Decrease Wear and Tear** — Less mechanical movement extends disk life.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Seek Time | $\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \vert H_{i+1} - H_i \vert$ | Cylinders traversed | Performance evaluation of any algorithm |
| Average Seek Time | $\dfrac{\text{Total Seek Time}}{n-1}$ | Cylinders/request | Comparative analysis across algorithms |
| Disk Access Time | $T_s + T_r + T_{transfer}$ | milliseconds (ms) | Theoretical access latency |
| Rotational Latency | $\dfrac{60}{2 \times RPM} \times 1000$ | ms | For **7200 RPM**, $T_r \approx 4.17$ ms |
| Transfer Time | $\dfrac{\text{Bytes to transfer}}{\text{Bytes per track} \times \text{RPS}}$ | ms | Sequential read/write speed |
| Throughput | $\dfrac{\text{Number of requests served}}{\text{Total time}}$ | Requests/sec | System-level I/O capacity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU numerical problems, you are **only** required to compute the **Total Seek Distance** (in cylinders). The phrase "Total Head Movement" is the examiner's way of saying "sum of absolute differences between successive head positions."

## 2.3 The Six Core Disk Scheduling Algorithms

### 2.3.1 FCFS — First-Come, First-Served
- **Logic:** Service requests in the order they arrive in the queue.
- **Pros:** Simple, inherently fair, zero starvation.
- **Cons:** Performs poorly — no optimization of head movement.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$
- **Use Case:** Baseline algorithm for comparison; not used in production systems.

### 2.3.2 SSTF — Shortest Seek Time First
- **Logic:** Always select the request closest to the current head position (similar to SJF scheduling).
- **Pros:** Significantly reduces total seek time compared to FCFS.
- **Cons:** Can cause **starvation** for requests at far ends of the disk.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(n^2)$ if naïvely implemented.
- **Use Case:** Older systems; rarely used in modern OS due to starvation.

### 2.3.3 SCAN (Elevator Algorithm)
- **Logic:** The head moves in one direction (e.g., towards higher cylinders), servicing all requests along the way, until it hits the **end of the disk**. Then it reverses direction.
- **Pros:** Good performance, bounded waiting time (no starvation).
- **Cons:** Requests at the end just serviced must wait for a full sweep.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$
- **Use Case:** Classic elevator algorithms; used in many early OS designs.

### 2.3.4 C-SCAN — Circular SCAN
- **Logic:** Like SCAN, but when the head reaches the end, it **jumps back to the beginning** (cylinder 0) without servicing any requests, then resumes servicing in the original direction.
- **Pros:** Provides more **uniform wait time** for all requests.
- **Cons:** Slightly longer total seek time than SCAN due to the return jump.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$
- **Use Case:** Real-time systems requiring predictable latency.

### 2.3.5 LOOK Algorithm
- **Logic:** A practical variant of SCAN. The head only goes as far as the **last request** in each direction, not all the way to the disk end.
- **Pros:** Avoids unnecessary travel to disk boundaries — more efficient than SCAN.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$
- **Use Case:** Modern OS; default in many Linux kernels.

### 2.3.6 C-LOOK Algorithm
- **Logic:** Combines C-SCAN with LOOK — the head only goes to the last request in each direction and jumps back to the lowest pending request.
- **Pros:** Most efficient among all six algorithms in most practical scenarios.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$
- **Use Case:** High-performance systems and database servers.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud Storage Systems (AWS EBS, Google Persistent Disk):** Use variants of C-LOOK with deadline-based I/O schedulers (e.g., Linux's `mq-deadline` and `Kyber`).
- **Database Engines (MySQL InnoDB, PostgreSQL):** Reorder I/O to cluster sequential reads, mimicking SCAN behavior.
- **SSDs and NVMe Devices:** Traditional algorithms become irrelevant since seek time is constant. Instead, **NCQ (Native Command Queuing)** reorders I/O based on NAND flash geometry and channel utilization.
- **Embedded Systems (RTOS):** Use deterministic FCFS or fixed-priority scheduling for predictable behavior.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Master Reference Problem (Used Universally by KTU)

> **Given:**
> - Total cylinders: 0 to 199 (so the disk has **200 cylinders**)
> - Request Queue: `98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67`
> - Initial Head Position: **53**
> - Direction of Movement: **Towards the higher cylinder numbers (ending end = 199)**

We will compute the total head movement for all six algorithms using this common input set.

## 3.2 FCFS — Exhaustive Calculation

The head simply visits requests in their original queue order.

$$\text{Sequence: } 53 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 183 \rightarrow 37 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 14 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67$$

Now compute the absolute differences step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Movement}_1 &= \vert 98 - 53 \vert = 45 \text{ cylinders} \\
\text{Movement}_2 &= \vert 183 - 98 \vert = 85 \text{ cylinders} \\
\text{Movement}_3 &= \vert 37 - 183 \vert = 146 \text{ cylinders} \\
\text{Movement}_4 &= \vert 122 - 37 \vert = 85 \text{ cylinders} \\
\text{Movement}_5 &= \vert 14 - 122 \vert = 108 \text{ cylinders} \\
\text{Movement}_6 &= \vert 124 - 14 \vert = 110 \text{ cylinders} \\
\text{Movement}_7 &= \vert 65 - 124 \vert = 59 \text{ cylinders} \\
\text{Movement}_8 &= \vert 67 - 65 \vert = 2 \text{ cylinders}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Total Head Movement (FCFS)} = 45 + 85 + 146 + 85 + 108 + 110 + 59 + 2 = \mathbf{640 \text{ cylinders}}
$$

## 3.3 SSTF — Exhaustive Calculation

From **53**, compute distance to every request:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert 98-53 \vert &= 45, \quad \vert 183-53 \vert = 130, \quad \vert 37-53 \vert = 16, \\
\vert 122-53 \vert &= 69, \quad \vert 14-53 \vert = 39, \quad \vert 124-53 \vert = 71, \\
\vert 65-53 \vert &= 12, \quad \vert 67-53 \vert = 14
\end{aligned}
$$

**Minimum = 12 → Serve 65 first.** Head now at 65.

From **65**, recompute distances to pending `{98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 67}`:

$$
\vert 67-65 \vert = 2 \text{ (minimum)} \Rightarrow \text{Serve 67 next.}
$$

From **67**, recompute for `{98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124}`:

$$
\vert 98-67 \vert = 31,\ \vert 183-67 \vert = 116,\ \vert 37-67 \vert = 30,\ \vert 122-67 \vert = 55,\ \vert 14-67 \vert = 53,\ \vert 124-67 \vert = 57
$$

**Minimum = 30 → Serve 37 next.** Head now at 37.

From **37**, recompute for `{98, 183, 122, 14, 124}`:

$$
\vert 98-37 \vert = 61,\ \vert 183-37 \vert = 146,\ \vert 122-37 \vert = 85,\ \vert 14-37 \vert = 23,\ \vert 124-37 \vert = 87
$$

**Minimum = 23 → Serve 14.** Head now at 14.

From **14**, recompute for `{98, 183, 122, 124}`:

$$
\vert 98-14 \vert = 84,\ \vert 183-14 \vert = 169,\ \vert 122-14 \vert = 108,\ \vert 124-14 \vert = 110
$$

**Minimum = 84 → Serve 98.** Head now at 98.

From **98**, recompute for `{183, 122, 124}`:

$$
\vert 183-98 \vert = 85,\ \vert 122-98 \vert = 24,\ \vert 124-98 \vert = 26
$$

**Minimum = 24 → Serve 122.** Head now at 122.

From **122**, recompute for `{183, 124}`:

$$
\vert 183-122 \vert = 61,\ \vert 124-122 \vert = 2
$$

**Minimum = 2 → Serve 124.** Head now at 124.

From **124**, the only remaining request is 183.

$$
\vert 183-124 \vert = 59
$$

**SSTF Sequence:** $53 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67 \rightarrow 37 \rightarrow 14 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 183$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Head Movement} &= 12 + 2 + 30 + 23 + 84 + 24 + 2 + 59 \\
&= \mathbf{236 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 SCAN — Exhaustive Calculation (Direction: Higher)

Head moves towards higher cylinders, servicing all requests $\geq 53$ in ascending order, hits 199, reverses, services remaining.

**Phase 1 (Forward Sweep):** 53 → 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 199

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Movement} &= (65-53) + (67-65) + (98-67) + (122-98) + (124-122) + (183-124) + (199-183) \\
&= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 16 = 146
\end{aligned}
$$

**Phase 2 (Reverse Sweep):** 199 → 37 → 14

$$
\text{Movement} = (199-37) + (37-14) = 162 + 23 = 185
$$

$$
\text{Total Head Movement (SCAN)} = 146 + 185 = \mathbf{331 \text{ cylinders}}
$$

## 3.5 C-SCAN — Exhaustive Calculation (Direction: Higher)

C-SCAN services requests $\geq 53$ going up to 199, then **jumps to 0** (no servicing), then services the rest going up again.

**Phase 1 (Forward Sweep):** 53 → 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 199

$$
\text{Movement} = 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 16 = 146
$$

**Phase 2 (Circular Jump + Service):** 199 → 0 → 14 → 37

$$
\text{Movement} = (199-0) + (14-0) + (37-14) = 199 + 14 + 23 = 236
$$

$$
\text{Total Head Movement (C-SCAN)} = 146 + 236 = \mathbf{382 \text{ cylinders}}
$$

## 3.6 LOOK — Exhaustive Calculation (Direction: Higher)

LOOK stops at the **last request** in each direction — no travel to disk boundaries.

**Phase 1 (Forward):** 53 → 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183

$$
\text{Movement} = 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 = 130
$$

**Phase 2 (Reverse):** 183 → 37 → 14

$$
\text{Movement} = (183-37) + (37-14) = 146 + 23 = 169
$$

$$
\text{Total Head Movement (LOOK)} = 130 + 169 = \mathbf{299 \text{ cylinders}}
$$

## 3.7 C-LOOK — Exhaustive Calculation (Direction: Higher)

C-LOOK services requests $\geq 53$ in ascending order up to the last request, then jumps to the lowest request and services in ascending order again.

**Phase 1 (Forward):** 53 → 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183

$$
\text{Movement} = 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 = 130
$$

**Phase 2 (Jump + Service):** 183 → 14 → 37

$$
\text{Movement} = (183-14) + (37-14) = 169 + 23 = 192
$$

$$
\text{Total Head Movement (C-LOOK)} = 130 + 192 = \mathbf{322 \text{ cylinders}}
$$

## 3.8 The KTU Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Algorithm | Service Sequence | Total Head Movement | Starvation Risk | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FCFS** | 98 → 183 → 37 → 122 → 14 → 124 → 65 → 67 | **640** | None | Fairness baseline |
| **SSTF** | 65 → 67 → 37 → 14 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 | **236** | High | Throughput maximization |
| **SCAN** | 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 199 → 37 → 14 | **331** | Low | Elevator systems |
| **C-SCAN** | 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 199 → 0 → 14 → 37 | **382** | Very Low | Real-time systems |
| **LOOK** | 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 37 → 14 | **299** | Low | Modern general-purpose OS |
| **C-LOOK** | 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 14 → 37 | **322** | Very Low | High-performance I/O |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For this specific input set, the ranking (best to worst) is:**
> $$\text{SSTF (236)} < \text{LOOK (299)} < \text{C-LOOK (322)} < \text{SCAN (331)} < \text{C-SCAN (382)} < \text{FCFS (640)}$$
> Note: SSTF is best here, but the **expected** textbook answer for "best general-purpose algorithm" remains **C-LOOK** or **LOOK** because of starvation avoidance.

## 3.9 Worked Example #2 — Numerical Practice

> **Given:** Cylinders 0–199, Queue = `176, 79, 32, 140, 36, 195, 47, 65`, Head starts at **50**, Direction: **towards higher cylinders.**

| Algorithm | Sequence | Total Movement |
|---|---|---|
| **FCFS** | 176 → 79 → 32 → 140 → 36 → 195 → 47 → 65 | 488 |
| **SSTF** | 65 → 79 → 47 → 36 → 32 → 140 → 176 → 195 | 248 |
| **SCAN** | 65 → 79 → 140 → 176 → 195 → 199 → 47 → 36 → 32 | 321 |
| **C-SCAN** | 65 → 79 → 140 → 176 → 195 → 199 → 0 → 32 → 36 → 47 | 385 |
| **LOOK** | 65 → 79 → 140 → 176 → 195 → 47 → 36 → 32 | 299 |
| **C-LOOK** | 65 → 79 → 140 → 176 → 195 → 32 → 36 → 47 | 309 |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Disk Scheduling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Layer: Multiple I/O Requests] --> B[Operating System I/O Scheduler Queue]
    B --> C{Scheduling Algorithm Selector}
    C --> D[FCFS Module]
    C --> E[SSTF Module]
    C --> F[SCAN / C-SCAN Module]
    C --> G[LOOK / C-LOOK Module]
    D --> H[Ordered Request Sequence]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Disk Driver Subsystem]
    I --> J[Disk Controller Hardware]
    J --> K[Physical Disk Arm Movement]
    K --> L[Track / Sector Located]
    L --> M[Data Transfer Completion]
    M --> N[Acknowledge to OS]
    N --> O[Process Resumed]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow for Algorithm Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Select Scheduling Algorithm] --> Q{Priority: Fairness?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Use FCFS]
    Q -- No --> S{Priority: Throughput?}
    S -- Yes --> T[Acceptable Starvation Risk?]
    T -- Yes --> U[Use SSTF]
    T -- No --> V{Predictable Latency?}
    V -- Yes --> W[Use C-SCAN or C-LOOK]
    V -- No --> X[Use SCAN or LOOK]
```

## 4.3 Disk Geometry Schematic (Mermaid Block Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DISK[Physical Disk Geometry]
        A0[Cylinder 0: Outer Edge]
        A1[Cylinder 50: Mid Region]
        A2[Cylinder 100: Mid Region]
        A3[Cylinder 150: Inner Region]
        A4[Cylinder 199: Inner Edge]
    end
    subgraph ARM[Disk Arm Assembly]
        R1[Read/Write Head mounted on Actuator Arm]
    end
    R1 -.-> A0
    R1 -.-> A1
    R1 -.-> A2
    R1 -.-> A3
    R1 -.-> A4
```

## 4.4 Request Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Issued: Process issues read/write
    Issued --> Queued: Enters OS I/O queue
    Queued --> Selected: Scheduler picks request
    Selected --> Seek: Disk arm moves to cylinder
    Seek --> Rotate: Platter rotates to sector
    Rotate --> Transfer: Data read/written
    Transfer --> Completed
    Completed --> [*]: Process notified
    Queued --> Queued: Waiting for selection
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**What is disk scheduling? Why is it required in modern operating systems?**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

> Disk scheduling is the technique used by the operating system to determine the **order in which pending disk I/O requests are serviced** by the read/write head.
>
> **It is required to:**
> 1. **Minimize seek time**, which is the dominant cost in disk access.
> 2. **Improve throughput** by reducing mechanical movement of the disk arm.
> 3. **Reduce average response time** for I/O-bound processes.
> 4. **Prevent starvation** of requests located at extreme cylinder positions.
> 5. **Extend disk life** by minimizing wear and tear on mechanical components.

*(Keyword coverage: seek time, throughput, response time, starvation — 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Differentiate between SSTF and SCAN disk scheduling algorithms.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

> | Aspect | SSTF | SCAN |
> |---|---|---|
> | Selection Criterion | Request with **shortest seek distance** from current head | Head sweeps in **one direction**, servicing all requests |
> | Starvation | **Yes** — far-end requests may starve | **No** — bounded waiting time |
> | Movement Pattern | Erratic, jumps to nearest request | Linear, elevator-like sweep |
> | Best For | Throughput-optimal scenarios | Systems needing fairness |
>
> **SSTF is greedy and may be locally optimal but globally suboptimal**, whereas **SCAN guarantees bounded delay** for every request.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> - **Mark Loss Reason #1:** Forgetting to include the **boundary travel** in SCAN/C-SCAN (going up to 199) — costs **7 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> - **Mark Loss Reason #2:** Incorrectly drawing the **circular jump** in C-SCAN/C-LOOK as a regular sweep — examiners will deduct **3-4 marks**.
> - **Mark Loss Reason #3:** Computing only the **forward sweep** and ignoring the **reverse sweep** — a classic 5-mark error.
> - **Mark Loss Reason #4:** Mixing up LOOK with SCAN — remember LOOK **stops at last request**, not at the disk end.
> - Always **show the sequence as a numbered list** and then **sum the absolute differences explicitly**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]

Consider a disk with **200 cylinders** (numbered 0 to 199). The disk arm starts at cylinder **50**. The pending request queue in order of arrival is: **`82, 170, 40, 110, 28, 130, 60, 35`**. The head moves initially **towards the higher-numbered cylinders**.

**Compute the total head movement for:**

**(a)** FCFS and SSTF algorithms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** SCAN and C-SCAN algorithms. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — FCFS and SSTF**

**FCFS:**
Sequence: 50 → 82 → 170 → 40 → 110 → 28 → 130 → 60 → 35

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Movement} &= (82-50) + (170-82) + (170-40) + (110-40) + (110-28) \\
&\quad + (130-28) + (130-60) + (60-35) \\
&= 32 + 88 + 130 + 70 + 82 + 102 + 70 + 25 \\
&= \mathbf{599 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**SSTF:**

From 50: distances to requests are 32, 120, 10, 60, 22, 80, 10, 15.
Minimum = 10 (tie between 40 and 60). Conventionally, take the **first encountered** = **40**.
Head at 40. Distances: 42, 130, 70, 12, 90, 20, 5. Min = **5** → **35**.
Head at 35. Distances: 47, 135, 75, 7, 95, 25. Min = **7** → **28**.
Head at 28. Distances: 54, 142, 82, 2, 102, 32. Min = **2** → **60**? Wait — recheck: 82-28=54 for 110, 130-28=102, 60-28=32, **35 already done**. The 2 is wrong; let me recompute: from 28, remaining = {82, 170, 110, 130, 60}. |82-28|=54, |170-28|=142, |110-28|=82, |130-28|=102, |60-28|=32. Min = 32 → **60**.
Head at 60. Remaining = {82, 170, 110, 130}. |82-60|=22, |170-60|=110, |110-60|=50, |130-60|=70. Min = 22 → **82**.
Head at 82. Remaining = {170, 110, 130}. |170-82|=88, |110-82|=28, |130-82|=48. Min = 28 → **110**.
Head at 110. Remaining = {170, 130}. |170-110|=60, |130-110|=20. Min = 20 → **130**.
Head at 130. Remaining = {170}. Movement = 40.

**SSTF Sequence:** 50 → 40 → 35 → 28 → 60 → 82 → 110 → 130 → 170

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Movement} &= 10 + 5 + 7 + 32 + 22 + 28 + 20 + 40 = \mathbf{164 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — SCAN and C-SCAN**

**SCAN (towards higher first):**

Forward sweep: 50 → 60 → 82 → 110 → 130 → 170 → 199
Reverse sweep: 199 → 40 → 35 → 28

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Forward} &= (60-50) + (82-60) + (110-82) + (130-110) + (170-130) + (199-170) \\
&= 10 + 22 + 28 + 20 + 40 + 29 = 149 \\
\text{Reverse} &= (199-40) + (40-35) + (35-28) = 159 + 5 + 7 = 171 \\
\text{Total (SCAN)} &= 149 + 171 = \mathbf{320 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**C-SCAN (towards higher first):**

Forward sweep to end: 50 → 60 → 82 → 110 → 130 → 170 → 199
Circular jump: 199 → 0
Then service lower requests in ascending order: 0 → 28 → 35 → 40

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Forward} &= 10 + 22 + 28 + 20 + 40 + 29 = 149 \\
\text{Jump + Service} &= (199-0) + (28-0) + (35-28) + (40-35) = 199 + 28 + 7 + 5 = 239 \\
\text{Total (C-SCAN)} &= 149 + 239 = \mathbf{388 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]

For the same disk and request queue as in Question A:

**(a)** Compute the total head movement using **LOOK** and **C-LOOK** algorithms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the total head movement of **all six algorithms** in a single table and justify which algorithm is most suitable for a **real-time database system**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — LOOK and C-LOOK**

**LOOK (towards higher first):**

Forward: 50 → 60 → 82 → 110 → 130 → 170
Reverse: 170 → 40 → 35 → 28

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Forward} &= 10 + 22 + 28 + 20 + 40 = 120 \\
\text{Reverse} &= (170-40) + (40-35) + (35-28) = 130 + 5 + 7 = 142 \\
\text{Total (LOOK)} &= 120 + 142 = \mathbf{262 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**C-LOOK (towards higher first):**

Forward: 50 → 60 → 82 → 110 → 130 → 170
Jump to lowest: 170 → 28
Then service ascending: 28 → 35 → 40

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Forward} &= 10 + 22 + 28 + 20 + 40 = 120 \\
\text{Jump + Service} &= (170-28) + (35-28) + (40-35) = 142 + 7 + 5 = 154 \\
\text{Total (C-LOOK)} &= 120 + 154 = \mathbf{274 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Comparative Analysis Table**

| Algorithm | Total Head Movement | Starvation | Predictable Latency |
|---|---|---|---|
| FCFS | 599 | None | Poor |
| SSTF | 164 | High | Poor |
| SCAN | 320 | Low | Moderate |
| C-SCAN | 388 | Very Low | Excellent |
| LOOK | 262 | Low | Moderate |
| C-LOOK | 274 | Very Low | Excellent |

**Justification for Real-Time Database System:**

> **Best Choice: C-LOOK or C-SCAN**
>
> 1. **Bounded Worst-Case Latency:** C-LOOK guarantees that no request waits longer than one full sweep + one jump — critical for real-time SLAs.
> 2. **No Starvation:** Unlike SSTF, every request will eventually be serviced regardless of its position.
> 3. **Uniform Service:** C-LOOK treats the disk as a circular list, ensuring newer requests at the high end do not suffer from biased delays.
> 4. **Trade-off Accepted:** Higher total head movement (274 vs SSTF's 164) is acceptable because real-time systems prioritize **predictability over raw throughput**.
>
> **SSTF is rejected** because of its starvation vulnerability — a single far-end request could be delayed indefinitely under heavy load.
> **FCFS is rejected** because its erratic movement causes unacceptable latency variance.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Disk Scheduling (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Six Algorithms:** FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, C-LOOK — memorize in this order of increasing sophistication.
- ✅ **FCFS = Arrival Order = No Optimization = 640 cylinders in master problem.**
- ✅ **SSTF = Greedy nearest-neighbor = Best total movement but STARVATION-prone.**
- ✅ **SCAN = Elevator = Goes to disk END (199) before reversing.**
- ✅ **C-SCAN = Goes to end, JUMPS to 0, then sweeps forward again = Most uniform latency.**
- ✅ **LOOK = SCAN but stops at LAST REQUEST, not disk end.**
- ✅ **C-LOOK = C-SCAN but stops at LAST REQUEST in each direction = Modern preferred algorithm.**
- ✅ **Key Formulas:** Total Movement = $\sum \vert H_{i+1} - H_i \vert$, Average = Total / (n-1).
- ✅ **Disk Access Time = Seek Time + Rotational Latency + Transfer Time.**
- ✅ **Real-Time Systems → C-LOOK / C-SCAN. Throughput Systems → SSTF. General Purpose → LOOK.**
- ✅ **For SSDs, traditional disk scheduling is largely irrelevant; use NCQ-based schedulers instead.**
- ✅ **Always show your service sequence as a numbered list and the absolute differences as a sum — this is the KTU mark allocation pattern.**
- ✅ **Boundary travel (e.g., to 199) must be explicitly added in SCAN and C-SCAN — examiners check this carefully.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disk Formatting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT601 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Disk Formatting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Disk Formatting** is the preparatory procedure executed on a secondary storage device (magnetic hard disk, SSD, optical media, or USB flash storage) to establish a usable storage environment. In Operating System theory, it is the process of laying down the magnetic/optical markers that divide the disk surface into addressable units (sectors and tracks), and then overlaying an empty file system structure (boot record, allocation tables, root directory, free-space bitmap) that the OS can subsequently mount and use.

KTU 2024 syllabus treats disk formatting as a two-stage procedure:

- **Low-Level Formatting (Physical Formatting):** Carried out by the disk manufacturer. Creates the concentric tracks and pie-shaped sectors on the platter surface. Defines the physical geometry of the disk.
- **High-Level Formatting (Logical Formatting):** Performed by the user using an OS utility (e.g., `format`, `mkfs.ext4`, `newfs`). Creates the file system structures on top of an already physically formatted disk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCERT601 Module 4):**
> Disk formatting logically precedes file system installation, partition management, and disk scheduling. The student must clearly distinguish **low-level** vs. **high-level** formatting, identify the structures created, and understand the role of **sector sparing** and **sector slipping** in handling bad blocks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Empty Notebook and the Indexed Filing Cabinet**
>
> Imagine you have bought a brand new, completely empty filing cabinet (your raw disk). Before you can store any files inside it, two things must happen:
>
> 1. **Low-Level Formatting** is like a carpenter dividing every drawer into uniform small compartments (the "sectors"), and stamping a number on each compartment (the "sector address"). The cabinet now has a fixed physical structure — every drawer (track) is split into identical sized boxes.
> 2. **High-Level Formatting** is like sticking a **cover page (boot block)**, an **index register (FAT / inode table)**, and a **"Files" tab on the first drawer (root directory)** so the librarian (the OS) knows where to look for any file you put in.
>
> Until both steps are done, the cabinet is "physically there but logically unusable." Similarly, until a disk is low-level and high-level formatted, the OS cannot read or write a single byte reliably.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Standard Definition |
|---|---|
| **Sector** | Smallest physical addressable unit; traditionally **512 bytes**, modern drives use **4096 bytes (4 KiB)**. |
| **Track** | A concentric ring on a single platter surface. |
| **Cylinder** | Stack of tracks at the same radial position across multiple platters. |
| **Cluster / Block** | Logical grouping of consecutive sectors; the file system's allocation unit. |
| **Platter** | Rigid magnetic disk surface inside the HDD. |
| **Spindle / Arm** | Rotational axis / actuator that positions the R/W head. |
| **Bad Block** | A sector that cannot reliably store data (manufacturing defect or wear-out). |

> [!NOTE]
> All physical storage constants in KTU numerical problems assume:
> - **Sector size = 512 bytes** (unless the problem specifies 4 KiB Advanced Format).
> - **Rotation speeds** are in RPM; **transfer rates** in MB/s; **seek times** in milliseconds (ms).
> - The OS addresses data at the **block/cluster level**, not at the **sector level** directly.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Stages of Formatting — Operational Logic

#### 2.1.1 Low-Level Formatting (Physical Formatting)

1. **Drive Platters Initialization** — The disk controller lays down servo information and sector markers using the factory servo writer.
2. **Track Creation** — Concentric circular paths are defined on every platter surface. A typical 3.5″ HDD has thousands of tracks per surface.
3. **Sector Division** — Each track is sliced into fixed-size **sectors** separated by inter-sector gaps (used for synchronization and address marks).
4. **Cylinder Formation** — A vertical column of tracks across all platters at the same radius is grouped as a cylinder — this is the addressing unit for multi-platter seek operations.
5. **Header / Address Mark Writing** — Each sector receives a *Sector ID* and *synchronization pattern* (preamble, address mark) so the head can locate it during reads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is low-level formatting usually NOT a user operation today?**
> Modern drives use **zoned bit recording (ZBR)** with variable sector counts per track. The geometry is hidden behind the **Logical Block Address (LBA)** interface, and low-level formatting is **destructive** — it overwrites servo tracks. Hence it is restricted to the factory. KTU may ask a 3-mark question on *why user-initiated low-level format is not recommended*.

#### 2.1.2 High-Level Formatting (Logical Formatting)

1. **Boot Sector / Superblock Writing** — The very first sector(s) get a small program and parameters describing the file system (block size, free-block count, etc.).
2. **Allocation Table / Inode Bitmap Creation** — Structures that track which blocks are free or occupied.
3. **Root Directory Creation** — The top-level directory entry is initialized empty.
4. **Free-Space Bitmap / FAT Initialization** — Marks every block as available.
5. **Reserved / Bad-Block Tables** — A reserved area where the OS notes factory-detected bad sectors.

> [!NOTE]
> High-level formatting **does not** erase the actual user data area in many implementations — it merely rewrites the metadata. Forensic tools can often recover files from a "quick-formatted" drive. This is a frequent KTU conceptual question.

### 2.2 Handling Bad Blocks

A robust OS must deal with defective sectors permanently.

- **Sector Sparing (Forward Remapping):** When the controller detects a bad sector during formatting or operation, it transparently maps the LBA of that sector to a spare sector from the **spare-areas pool** (e.g., grown-bad table). The original LBA is never used again.
- **Sector Slipping:** During a *full* reformat, the formatter shifts all subsequent sector numbers down by one whenever it hits a bad sector, preserving sequential LBA ordering. This works only during formatting — sector sparing is used at runtime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap Question:** "Sector slipping is performed *at format time*, sector sparing is performed *at run time*." Memorize this distinction.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bytes per track | $B_t = S_t \times S_s$ | $S_t$ = sectors/track, $S_s$ = sector size (bytes) |
| 2 | Bytes per cylinder | $B_c = B_t \times N_s$ | $N_s$ = number of surfaces (= number of heads) |
| 3 | Total disk capacity | $C = B_c \times N_c$ | $N_c$ = number of cylinders |
| 4 | Formatted capacity (with overhead) | $C_f = C \times (1 - \text{overhead fraction})$ | Overhead = boot block, FAT, root dir, gaps |
| 5 | Average rotational latency | $T_{rot} = \dfrac{30\,000}{N_{rpm}}$ | **ms** (half a rotation) |
| 6 | Average access time | $T_{acc} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer}$ | All in **ms** |
| 7 | Transfer time of $D$ bytes | $T_{tr} = \dfrac{D}{R_{tr}}$ | $R_{tr}$ = transfer rate (bytes/ms) |
| 8 | Number of blocks (clusters) | $N_{blk} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{C_f}{B_{blk}} \right\rfloor$ | $B_{blk}$ = block/cluster size |
| 9 | Block offset of byte $b$ | $b_{blk} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{b}{B_{blk}} \right\rfloor$ | Used to translate logical byte to block address |
| 10 | Spare pool fraction (typical) | $\approx 1\% - 3\%$ | Reserved for sector sparing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Disk formatting is the foundation on which **disk partitioning (MBR/GPT)**, **RAID initialization**, **LVM provisioning**, **SSD TRIM and wear-leveling**, and **filesystem journaling (ext4, NTFS, XFS)** all depend. Industrial storage arrays (SAN/NAS) run automated low-level + high-level formats on every replacement drive before inclusion in a RAID group.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Formatted Disk Capacity from First Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (KTU-style numerical):**
> A hard disk has **6 platters**, **12 read/write heads (one per surface)**, **16 383 cylinders**, **average 256 sectors per track**, and **512 bytes per sector**. A high-level format reserves **5% of the raw capacity** for boot block, FAT, root directory, and inter-sector gaps. Compute:
> (a) the **raw (unformatted) capacity**,
> (b) the **formatted (usable) capacity**,
> (c) the **number of 4 KiB blocks** available to the file system.

#### Step 1 — Identify the parameters

$$\text{Platters} = 6$$

$$\text{Surfaces} = N_s = 2 \times 6 = 12 \quad \text{(one head per surface)}$$

$$\text{Cylinders} = N_c = 16\,383$$

$$\text{Sectors / Track} = S_t = 256$$

$$\text{Bytes / Sector} = S_s = 512$$

#### Step 2 — Compute bytes per track

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_t &= S_t \times S_s \\
    &= 256 \times 512 \\
    &= 131\,072 \text{ bytes} \\
    &= 128 \text{ KiB per track}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Every track is divided into 256 sectors and each sector stores 512 bytes; multiplying gives the storage capacity of a single track.

#### Step 3 — Compute bytes per cylinder

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_c &= B_t \times N_s \\
    &= 131\,072 \times 12 \\
    &= 1\,572\,864 \text{ bytes per cylinder} \\
    &\approx 1.5 \text{ MiB per cylinder}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** A cylinder is the vertical stack of tracks at the same radius across all 12 surfaces; total cylinder capacity is therefore track capacity × number of recording surfaces.

#### Step 4 — Compute raw (unformatted) capacity

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{raw} &= B_c \times N_c \\
        &= 1\,572\,864 \times 16\,383 \\
        &= 25\,769\,803\,776 \text{ bytes} \\
        &\approx 24.00 \text{ GiB (using } 1 \text{ GiB} = 2^{30} \text{ bytes)} \\
        &\approx 25.77 \text{ GB (using } 1 \text{ GB} = 10^9 \text{ bytes)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Every cylinder is repeated 16 383 times along the radial axis from the spindle to the outer rim, giving the total raw capacity of the platter stack.

#### Step 5 — Apply 5 % formatting overhead

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overhead bytes} &= 0.05 \times 25\,769\,803\,776 \\
                      &= 1\,288\,490\,189 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{fmt} &= C_{raw} \times (1 - 0.05) \\
        &= 25\,769\,803\,776 \times 0.95 \\
        &= 24\,481\,313\,587 \text{ bytes} \\
        &\approx 22.80 \text{ GiB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The high-level format consumes 5 % of the raw space to host the boot block, FAT, root directory, inodes, and inter-sector gaps; subtracting this fraction yields the user-visible capacity.

#### Step 6 — Number of 4 KiB blocks

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{blk} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{C_{fmt}}{B_{blk}} \right\rfloor \\
        &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{24\,481\,313\,587}{4096} \right\rfloor \\
        &= \left\lfloor 5\,976\,883.20 \right\rfloor \\
        &= 5\,976\,883 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** A block (cluster) is the smallest addressable unit the file system grants to a file; dividing formatted capacity by block size and discarding the remainder gives the integer number of addressable blocks.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns

| # | Pattern | Key Formula | Typical Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bytes per track / cylinder | $B_t = S_t \times S_s$, $B_c = B_t \times N_s$ | 3 |
| 2 | Raw vs formatted capacity | $C_f = C \times (1 - \text{overhead})$ | 4 |
| 3 | Number of file-system blocks | $N_{blk} = \left\lfloor C_f / B_{blk} \right\rfloor$ | 3 |
| 4 | Block-number of a given byte offset | $\text{block} = \lfloor b / B_{blk} \rfloor$ | 2 |
| 5 | Block-fragmentation / internal slack | $\text{slack} = B_{blk} - (b \bmod B_{blk})$ | 2 |
| 6 | Spare pool sizing | $S_{pool} = N_{blk} \times f_{spare}$ | 2 |

### 3.3 KTU Pitfall Callout — Why Students Lose Marks

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Trap #1 — Unit Conversion:**
> KTU examiners expect **1 MiB = 2²⁰ = 1 048 576 bytes**, **1 GiB = 2³⁰**, **1 KB = 1000 B** (manufacturer) but **1 KiB = 1024 B** (OS). A capacity stated as "500 GB" by the manufacturer is "≈ 465.66 GiB" to the OS. Wrong unit conversion = **2 marks lost** in numerical answers.
>
> **Examiner's Pitfall Trap #2 — Distinction between low-level and high-level:**
> A student who writes "formatting creates the file system" without mentioning **partition table / boot block / FAT / root directory** loses the depth-of-understanding marks (KTU CO3 mapping: *Apply*).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Disk Geometry — Track / Sector / Cylinder Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hard Disk Platter Stack] --> B[Platter 1 Top Surface]
    A --> C[Platter 1 Bottom Surface]
    A --> D[Platter 2 Top Surface]
    A --> E[Platter 2 Bottom Surface]
    A --> F[Platter N Surfaces]

    B --> B1[Track 0 Outer Ring]
    B --> B2[Track 1 Mid Ring]
    B --> B3[Track 2 Inner Ring]

    B1 --> S1[Sector 0 of Track 0]
    B1 --> S2[Sector 1 of Track 0]
    B1 --> S3[Sector 2 of Track 0]
    B1 --> S4[Sector 3 of Track 0]
    B1 --> S5[Sector N of Track 0]

    B2 --> T2S1[Sector 0 of Track 1]
    B2 --> T2S2[Sector 1 of Track 1]

    C --> C1[Track 0 of Bottom Surface]
    D --> D1[Track 0 of Next Platter]

    C1 --> CYL1[Cylinder Group 0]
    D1 --> CYL1
    B1 --> CYL1
```

**Interpretation:** A cylinder is the vertical alignment of the **same-numbered track** on every recording surface. Reading/writing sequentially within a cylinder needs **no seek** (only rotational latency), which is why file systems try to keep logically contiguous blocks in the same cylinder.

### 4.2 Two-Stage Formatting Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RAW[Raw Unformatted Disk Media] --> LLF[Stage 1 Low Level Formatting]
    LLF --> SERVO[Servo Tracks Sector Markers Address IDs]
    SERVO --> CHK1{Quality Check Verify Every Sector}
    CHK1 -->|Pass| GEOFIX[Disk Geometry Established Cylinders Heads Sectors]
    CHK1 -->|Fail Bad Sectors| PDEF[Permanent Defect List Updated]
    PDEF --> GEOFIX
    GEOFIX --> HLF[Stage 2 High Level Formatting]
    HLF --> BOOT[Write Boot Block Partition Table]
    BOOT --> FS[Create File System Structures FAT or Inode Table Root Directory Free Bitmap]
    FS --> RESV[Reserve Spare Sectors for Sector Sparing]
    RESV --> READY[Disk Ready for Mount and Use]
```

**Interpretation:** The first stage is the **physical** carving of the disk (mostly factory work). The second stage is the **logical** installation of an empty file system, after which the OS can create, read, write, and delete files.

### 4.3 Bad-Block Recovery — Sector Sparing vs. Sector Slipping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BBDET[Bad Block Detected] --> AT{When Did It Happen}
    AT -->|At Format Time| SLIP[Sector Slipping]
    AT -->|At Run Time| SPARE[Sector Sparing Forward Remap]

    SLIP --> SLIP1[Skip Bad Sector]
    SLIP1 --> SLIP2[Shift All Subsequent Sector LBAs Down by 1]
    SLIP2 --> SLIP3[Preserve Sequential LBA Ordering]
    SLIP3 --> OKSLIP[Logical Disk Geometry Intact]

    SPARE --> SP1[Map Bad LBA to Spare Sector from Spare Pool]
    SP1 --> SP2[Update Grown Bad Block Table in Controller Firmware]
    SP2 --> SP3[All Future I/O Transparent to OS Uses New LBA]
    SP3 --> OKSPARE[OS Continues to Issue Old LBA Address]
```

**Interpretation:** *Slipping* fixes the geometry permanently during formatting by re-numbering later sectors; *sparing* fixes the disk during operation by secretly redirecting reads/writes to a hidden reserve sector. The OS never knows the difference in the sparing case.

### 4.4 High-Level Format — File System Structure Built on the Disk

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HFS[High Level Format Output] --> BS[Boot Block Sector 0 Contains Boot Loader and FS Identifier]
    HFS --> SB[Superblock FS Parameters Block Size Total Blocks Free Count]
    HFS --> IMAP[Inode Bitmap or FAT Region Tracks Free vs Allocated Inodes]
    HFS --> IDATA[Inode Table or FAT Continuation File Metadata Owner Size Timestamps]
    HFS --> RDIR[Root Directory Created Empty Ready for First Files]
    HFS --> FREE[Free Block Bitmap Marks All Data Blocks as Available]
    HFS --> DATA[Data Block Region Where Actual File Content Is Stored]
```

**Interpretation:** A formatted disk has clearly partitioned regions — the first few sectors are *system metadata*, and the bulk is *user data*. The OS consults the metadata first to translate a logical file path into physical block addresses.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]`
**Differentiate between low-level formatting and high-level formatting of a disk.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Low-Level Formatting | High-Level Formatting |
|---|---|---|
| **Also called** | Physical formatting | Logical formatting |
| **Performed by** | Disk manufacturer (factory) | User / OS using `format`, `mkfs` |
| **Result** | Physical sectors, tracks, servo info are laid down on the platter | File system structures (boot block, FAT/inode table, root directory, free bitmap) are written |
| **Effect on geometry** | Establishes the disk geometry | Does not change geometry; only writes metadata |
| **Frequency** | Once at the factory (destructive, not recommended later) | Every time a new file system is installed |
| **User data effect** | Erases everything permanently | Erases only metadata; data may be recoverable |

*(1 mark for correct definition of each type, 1 mark for tabulated comparison, 1 mark for an extra valid distinguishing point.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]`
**What is a bad block? Explain sector sparing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **bad block** is a sector on the disk that has developed a permanent defect (manufacturing flaw, wear-out, or physical damage) and can no longer reliably store data.

**Sector sparing** is the technique by which the disk controller:
1. Maintains a small **spare-sectors pool** reserved at format time.
2. When a bad sector is detected at *runtime*, the controller **remaps** the failing sector's LBA to a healthy spare sector.
3. Updates a **grown defect list** stored in the controller's firmware/EEPROM.
4. All future I/O to the original LBA is transparently redirected to the new spare sector.

*(1 mark definition of bad block, 1 mark sector-sparing mechanism, 1 mark for the LBA-transparency point.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Note (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Each Part-B question is 14 marks, with internal choice — the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set 1]`

**(a)** Explain the physical structure of a magnetic hard disk in detail. With a neat diagram, describe tracks, sectors, cylinders, and platters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hard disk has 4 platters, 16 384 cylinders, 200 sectors/track, 512 bytes/sector, and rotates at 7 200 RPM. Calculate:
(i) raw capacity,
(ii) formatted capacity if 4 % is reserved as overhead,
(iii) the average rotational latency in ms. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

- **Platters, surfaces, heads** (2 marks): The hard disk contains multiple rigid platters stacked on a common spindle. Each platter has two recording surfaces (top and bottom). A movable **read/write head** is mounted on an actuator arm for each surface. With 4 platters, the disk has $N_s = 2 \times 4 = 8$ recording surfaces and therefore 8 heads.
- **Tracks** (2 marks): Each surface is divided into thousands of concentric circles called **tracks**. The same-numbered track on every surface is called a **cylinder** — 16 384 cylinders in this example.
- **Sectors** (2 marks): Each track is divided into fixed-size **sectors** (typically 512 B), separated by inter-sector gaps. A sector is the smallest addressable unit on the disk.
- **Cylinder** (1 mark): A cylinder is the set of all tracks at the same radial position across all platters. It is the geometric unit for seek-free sequential access.

*(Diagram as in Section 4.1 above — 1 mark for the labelled diagram, distributed across the points above.)*

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Raw capacity** (3 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_t &= 200 \times 512 = 102\,400 \text{ bytes/track} \\
B_c &= 102\,400 \times 8 = 819\,200 \text{ bytes/cylinder} \\
C_{raw} &= 819\,200 \times 16\,384 \\
        &= 13\,421\,772\,800 \text{ bytes} \\
        &\approx 12.50 \text{ GiB (binary)} \\
        &\approx 13.42 \text{ GB (decimal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Formatted capacity** (2 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{fmt} &= 0.96 \times 13\,421\,772\,800 \\
        &= 12\,884\,901\,888 \text{ bytes} \\
        &\approx 12.00 \text{ GiB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Average rotational latency** (2 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{rot} &= \frac{30\,000}{N_{rpm}} = \frac{30\,000}{7\,200} = 4.1667 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Each sub-part — 1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark final value where applicable.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set 2]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **high-level formatting** procedure. Mention all file system structures created. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **sector slipping** and **sector sparing** with diagrams. When is each technique used? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

- **Step 1 – Boot Block / Superblock** (1 mark): The first sector(s) of the partition receive a small boot loader and the file system identifier and parameters (block size, free count, etc.).
- **Step 2 – Allocation Table / Free Bitmap** (2 marks): A table tracks which blocks are free vs. allocated. In FAT-family file systems, this is the **File Allocation Table**; in Unix file systems, this is the **inode bitmap** + **free-block bitmap**.
- **Step 3 – Root Directory** (1 mark): The top-level directory entry is created empty, ready to receive the first file and subdirectory names.
- **Step 4 – Reserved / Spare Region** (1 mark): A small percentage of sectors is reserved for replacing defective sectors in the future.
- **Step 5 – Data Region** (1 mark): The remaining sectors form the data area, marked as completely free in the bitmap.
- **Block diagram** (1 mark): As in Section 4.4 of these notes.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

- **Sector Slipping** (3 marks): Used **at format time only**. When the formatter encounters a bad sector, it **shifts all subsequent sector numbers down by one**, preserving sequential LBA ordering. The bad sector is removed from the LBA sequence entirely. *Limitation:* cannot be applied after the disk is in use, because files already address the original LBAs.
- **Sector Sparing** (3 marks): Used **at run time**. The controller maintains a **spare-sectors pool**. When a read/write fails, the LBA is **remapped to a healthy spare** and the grown defect list is updated. All future I/O using the old LBA is redirected transparently. *Advantage:* works continuously during the lifetime of the disk.
- **Comparison / When to use** (1 mark): Slipping — only at format time, preserves LBA geometry. Sparing — at any time, transparent to the OS, the typical mechanism in modern drives.

*(Diagram as in Section 4.3 above — 1 mark distributed within the above for the labelled diagram.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways KTU students lose marks on Disk Formatting questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "format" with "erase":** Writing *"formatting erases the disk"* is wrong. High-level formatting only rewrites the metadata; data may be recovered. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 2. **Skipping the overhead calculation:** Many numericals deduct marks if the 5 %–10 % formatting overhead is ignored.
> 3. **Mixing SI and binary units:** Always specify whether you used **GiB (2³⁰)** or **GB (10⁹)** in the final answer.
> 4. **Confusing cylinder capacity with track capacity:** A cylinder capacity = track capacity × number of surfaces, *not* track capacity alone.
> 5. **Sparing vs. slipping:** Saying both techniques "do the same thing" loses 2 marks — they operate at *different times* in the disk's life.
> 6. **No diagram:** KTU awards 1 mark for a neat, labelled diagram in 7-mark sub-parts. Skipping it = automatic 1-mark loss.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Disk Formatting (PCERT601 Module 4):**
>
> - **Two stages:** Low-level (physical, factory) → High-level (logical, OS utility).
> - **Disk geometry primitives:** Platter → Surface → Track → Sector → Cylinder. Sectors are the smallest addressable units (~512 B or 4 KiB).
> - **Capacity cascade:** $B_t = S_t \times S_s$ → $B_c = B_t \times N_s$ → $C_{raw} = B_c \times N_c$ → $C_{fmt} = C_{raw} \times (1 - \text{OH})$.
> - **Block = cluster = allocation unit** of the file system; OS never reads/writes a single sector in user code.
> - **High-level format creates:** Boot block, partition table, superblock, FAT/inode table, root directory, free-bitmap, spare-area.
> - **Bad-block handling:**
>   - *Sector slipping* = at format time, shifts LBAs.
>   - *Sector sparing* = at run time, remaps to a spare pool; OS unaware.
> - **Quick format** = rewrites only metadata, leaves user data (recoverable).
> - **Full format** = rewrites every sector, detects new bad blocks, slower.
> - **Spare pool = ~1–3 %** of total capacity; reserved for sparing.
> - **MBR vs GPT:** MBR supports ≤ 4 primary partitions, 2 TiB; GPT supports up to 128 partitions, > 2 TiB.
> - **Modern drives hide geometry** behind LBA — cylinder/head/sector (CHS) addressing is legacy.
> - **SSD-specific note:** SSD firmware performs *internal* block management (FTL), wear-leveling, TRIM; OS-level format is a *logical* operation only.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
